gloss_en: The maxim of the she-goat and the sword.
explanation: The maxim of the she-goat and the sword. It is founded on some story of a goat's being suddenly killed by accidental contact with a sword, and is used to illustrate any surprising event happening altogether by chance. It therefore belongs to the same class as काकताखीय, खस्वाटबिल्वीय and others of a similar kind. An excellent illustration of its use is found on page 229 of S'riharsha’s Khandanakhandakhddya:—“quit पञ्च वराटकान्पिधाय कश्चिस्पृच्छति कति वराटका इति । पृष्टश्चाजाकरपाणीयन्यायेन aa पञ्चेति"? ॥ In a footnote the maxim is thus explained :—“‘qugaary स्तंभादौ शिथिरूबन्धखङ्गे छागी भ्रीवां प्रसारयति यदच्छया च रीवा छिद्यते aaraatsareपाणीयन्यायः काकतारीयन्यायसमः'? ॥ Vardhamana puts it differently in his comment on Ganaratnamahodadhi iii. 196 :— “ध्यथाजया भूमि खनन्स्यात्मवधाय कृपाणो दशितस्तत्तल्यं वृत्तं केनचिदात्मवि- नाद्याय कतमजाकृपाणीयम्‌'? ॥ For another variety, see 0420120 - jar? on तक 5. 3. 106. The nydya, with the same illustration, is found also in the Khandanoddhdra, page 52; and the illustration, without the nydya, in Siddhdntales'a, page 95. Then on page 96, it is again referred to in the expression “काकताङीयसंवादिवराटकसंख्या- विश्ेषाहायंज्ञानवत्‌, ” It is interesting to note that the Mar&thi-speaking folk of Western India have adopted the maxim, but with a changed meaning. Molesworth defines it as “The maxim of the sword upon the neck of the goat, Expressive of meekness and absoInte helplessness.” 2
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 1

gloss_en: The maxim of a lamp in a central position.
explanation: The maxim of a lamp in a central position. Applied to something which fulfils a double purpose. It occurs in S’ankara’s bhishya on the Mundaka Upanishad 3. 1. 5. (“सव्येन weaeaपसा द्येष आत्मा सम्यग्न्नानेन ब्रह्मचर्येण faq” । ) on which he says:— “Pet सर्वदा । नित्यं ada नित्यं तपसा नित्यं सम्यग्तानेनेति स्त्र निलयशब्दो ऽन्तर्दीपिकान्यायेनानुषक्तव्यः ” ॥ 1८ isakin 10 देहरीदीपन्याय and मध्य दीपन्याय. Jivananda’s edition of the Mundakabhdshya reads aradiqeeataa. The reading given above is from the Anandds'rama edition. ` अन्धकवतंकीयन्यायः ॥ The maxim, of the blind man and the quail. Like ag पाणीय and many others, it is used to express a wholly fortuitous occurrence. Vardhamana, on Ganaratnamahodadhi iii. 195, explains it thus -- ^“ अन्धकश्च वतका च अन्धकवतैकम्‌ | अन्धकस्य वर्तकाया उपर्यतर्कितः पादन्यास उच्यते | तत्तल्यमन्धकवर्वकीयम्‌ ” This authority brings the quail under the blind man’s foot; but the commentator S'ris'rutasdgara, who expounded the work Yas’astilaka* and who in the colophon is described as “ तरकव्याकरणछन्दोरंकारसिद्धान्तसादहिव्यादिश्ाखनिपुणमतिः प्राङकृतव्याकरणाद्यनेकडाखरचaga, ” brings the bird into the man’s hands. And, surely, such a prodigy of learning must be right! The verse in which the expression अन्धकवर्वकीय occurs is Yas‘astilaka 1. 153. “garg. सागरमिमं अमता नितान्तं जीवेन मानवभवः समवापि दैवात्‌ । तत्रापि यद्धवनमान्यङुरे safe: सत्सङ्गतिश्च तदिहान्धकवतैकीयम्‌? “It is altogether by chance that a soul wandering about in this ocean of repeated births is born as a man; and that he should be born into a family of repute in the world, and enjoy the society of the good, is likewise as accidental as in the case of the blind man and the * This important work, described by Dr. Peterson at considerable length in his Second Report, was published in the Advyamdid, a valuable periodical issued by the Proprietor of the Nirnayasigar Press, 3 quail.” S'ris‘rutasigara’s explanation of the last term is as follows:—“ अन्धकवर्तकीयो दृष्टान्तः | यथा कश्चिद्न्धकः पुमान्‌ करतरेन करं ताडयन्वर्तेते तस्य करद्वयमध्ये ade: पक्षिविशेषः समायाति स तु दुरुभस्तथा मानवभवे सस्सङ्गतिुरैभा वैते ° ॥ 4 अन्धगजन्थायः ॥ The maxim of the blind men and the elephant. A number of blind men desired to form an idea of the shape of an elephant. One touched his trunk and thought he must be like a snake; another took hold of a leg and supposed that he was like a post, and so on. Tardnatha tells us that it is used to illustrate the divergence of views held by the ignorant in regard to isvara. The story is found in the Buddhist work Udédnam (vi. 4, pages 66-69) published by the Pali Text Society in 1885. It is referred to in Sures’vara’s large Vdrtika 4. 4. 566 (page 1813) as follows:— एकमेवैकरूपं सद्रस्स्वक्तातं AAT | जात्यन्धगजदष्ट AT कोटिश्चः seaa wat” "Also in his Naiskarmyasiddhi 7. 93 :-- - “तदेतदद्वयं aa निर्विकारं ऊुबुदधिभिः। जात्यन्धगजद्टयेव कोट्शिः परि- कर्प्यते ” ॥ 1 have met with the nydya again on pages 107 and 160 of Syddvddamanjart. It occurs also in the Jainadars‘ana of Saddars'anasamuccaya (page 46 ); but the passage in which it is found was taken verbatim from Mallisena’s work, without any acknowledgment.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 2

gloss_en: The maxim of the blind man and the cow’s tail.
explanation: The maxim of the blind man and the cow’s tail. The story is that an evil-disposed fellow found a blind man who, having lost his way, was wandering about helplessly. Expressing great sympathy for him, and promising to help him, the man led him to a young and frisky cow, and putting her tail into his hand told 4 him to hold on, and that she would certainly lead him to the village to which he wished to go. The result was, of course, most disastrous, Sankara, in his bhishya on Vedéntasdtra 1. 1. 7, applies the maxim to the case of a teacher who wrongly instructs his pupil in regard to emancipation and so leads him to destruction! These are his words:— «यदि चाज्ञस्य सतो सुुक्षोरचेतनमा- त्मानमात्मेत्युपदिजेलपमाणभूतं शाखं स श्रदधानतयान्धगोलाङ्गलन्यायेन तदात्म्टं न परिलजेत्तब्यतिरिक्तं चात्मानं न प्रतिपद्येत तथा सति पुरूषाथा- दविदन्येतान्थं च ऋच्छेत्‌? ॥ 1116 nyfya is found, too, in Pancapadikavivarana, page 170. अन्धपरम्परान्यायः ॥ + The maxim of च continuous series of blind men. It would seem to be in this sense that S’ankarachfrya uses it in his Brahmasitrabhashya 2. 2. 30,37. The passages stand thus :— ५“अनादित्वेऽप्यन्धपरस्परान्यायेनाप्रतिष्टैवानवस्था व्यवहारलोपिनी स्यान्नाभिप्रा- यसिद्धिः*“'व्तमानकारवद तीतेष्वपि कारेष्वितरेतराश्रयदोषाविरोषादन्धपरम्प- „+ रान्यायापत्तेः? ॥ In his rendering of the former passage, Dr. Thibaut has overlooked the maxim, but the latter he translates as follows:—‘“For in past time as well as in the present, mutual interdependence of the two took place, so that the beginningless series is like an endless chain of blind men leading other blind men.” I should add that, in the former case, S’ankara is arguing against the Buddhist theory of a beginningless series of mental impressions, and, in the latter, against the Sankhya notion of a similar chain of human actions and divine interpositions. Dr. Thibaut’s explanation of the maxim is quite in accord with that of Dr. Garbe in his rendering of the Sdnkhyasitra iii. 81 ““इतरथान्धपरम्परा” “Else there would be a tradition [ comparable to a row ] of blind men [ leading each other ].” A very apt quotation from one of Coleridge's Lay Sermons is given in that useful work A Rational Refutation of Hindu Philosophical Systems (now quite out of print), from which I 5 extract the following:—“The old man talked much and vehemently concerning an infinite series of causes and effects, which he explained to be a string of blind men, the last of whom caught hold of the skirt of the one before him, he of the next, and so on till they were all out of sight; and that they all walked infallibly straight, without making one false step, though all were alike blind. Methought I borrowed courage from surprise, and asked him, ‘Who, then, is at the head to guide them? He looked at me with ineffable contempt, not unmixed with an angry suspicion and then replied, “No one; the string of blind men goes on for ever without any beginning, for although one blind man cannot move without stumbling, yet infinite blindness supplies the want of sight.” In the opening part of the Padamaiijart ( Pandit x. 248 ) we find the expression अन्धपरम्पराप्रसङ्ग used with reference to testimony received through a series of blind men, and therefore of doubtful value. It is part of an interesting discussion regarding different forms of a word, why some are considered correct and others not, ^ तच्र ये साधवस्ते शाखेणानुशिष्यन्तेऽसाधुभ्यो विविक्ता; प्रकृतिप्रययविभागेन क्ताप्यन्त इमे साधव इति । कथं पुनरिदमा- चर्येण पाणिनिनावगतमेते साधव इति । आपिशेन पूर्वव्याकरणेन । आपि- शक्िना तर्हिं केनावगतम्‌ | ततः पूर्वेण व्याकरणेन | यद्ेवमन्धपरम्पराप्रसङ्गः | तद्यथा शङ्कं क्षीरमिलयन्धेनोक्ते केनेदमवगतमिति षष्टो यदान्धान्तरं मूलं निर्दि- शति सोऽप्यन्धान्तरं तदा haga: शौय प्रमाणं भवति तादगेतत्‌”' ॥ That the nydya is in very general use will be apparent from the following additional references. Zantravdrtika, pages 11, 72, 75, 232, 799, 877. Pancapddikd, page 98. Bhdmatt, pages 254, 464. Nydyamanjaré pages 234, 249, 251, 425, 492,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 3

gloss_en: The maxim of the pointing out of the star Arundhatt.
explanation: The maxim of the pointing out of the star Arundhatt. The idea here is that of gradual instruction, on the principle of the semtaataeata for which see the Second Handful. Its 6 ‘usage is explained by S'ankara in Brahmasdtrabhdshya 1. 1. 8, as follows:—‘ यथारुन्धतीं दिदद्वीयिषुस्तत्समीपस्थां equi तारामसुख्यां श्रथममरन्धतीति भराहयित्वा तां प्रत्याख्याय पश्चादरन्धतीमेव ग्राहयति तद्वन्नायमात्मेति gata” ॥ Similarly, too, in 1. 1. 12, we read :--“4यथारन्धती- निदश्ैने बहीष्वपि तारास्वसुख्यास्वरन्धतीषु द्ितासु यान्त्या प्रदद्यते सा सुख्ये- वारुन्धती भवव्येवमिहाप्यानन्दमयस्य सवौन्तरत्वान्मुख्यमात्मस्वम्‌' ॥ 116 maxim is sometimes styled स्थूलारन्धतीन्यायः and it appears under this name in Nrisimhasarasvati’s commentary on section 20 of the Veddntasdra—that section which gives the views of Charvakas and others as to the dtman. A 5 qad ~ अके चेन्मधु विन्देत किमथ पर्वतं व्रजेत्‌ |. If one can find honey on the Arka-tree [ close at hand ] then why go to the mountain for it? That 18, if an object can be accomplished by simple means, don’t adopt a more complicated method. This is well illustrated by S’ankara in his Veddntasitrabhdshya 3. 4. 3, a8 follows:— केवलाचचेऽत्तानात्पुरुषार्थसिद्धि स्याक्किमथेमनेकायाससमन्वितानि BA ते BA | ae चेन्मधु विन्देत किमर्थं yaa बजेदिति न्यायात्‌ ॥ ` Its source, however, is S’abara on Jaimini 1. 2. 4, Here, the ptrvapakshin, after taking exception to certain Vedic injunctions as useless says—“ तद्यथा पथि mash ayeasa तेनेव पथा मध्वर्थिनः wad न गच्छेयुस्तादशं हि तत्‌। अपि चाहुः । अकै चेन्मधु विन्देत किमथे wd व्रजेत्‌ । gear संसिद्धौ को विद्रान्यलमाचरेत्‌" ॥ The same couplet is quoted by Aniruddha in his comment on Sdnkhyasétra i. 1; also by Vachaspati Mis‘ra in his Sénkhyatattvakaumudt 1. and again in his Nydyavdrtikatdtparyatihd, page 220. Instead of a, however, we have in the former instance अक्त and in the latter qm. Raghunath reads am and explains it by गृहकोणे. One more example of the application of this nydya may be found in Kumarila’s Tantravdrtika 1.2.17: — 4 “ यदचस्पान्महतश्च कर्मणः समं BS AA ततोऽकरे चेन्मधु aaa न्यायेनाख्पेन सिद्धे महति न afar” ॥ ` अर्धजरतीयन्यायः ॥ The maxim of the semi-senile woman. It is very difficult to , fix on the exact force of this saying. Authorities differ so much as to make it almost an instance of quot homines tot sententic, As expounded by Vardhaména (iii. 195 ) it seems to imply indefiniteness, half-and-half-ness, the being neither one thing nor the other. He says:—‘‘ यथा खी न तरुणी छथस्तनत्वात्‌ छष्णकेशत्वान्न जरती ay शक्यते तद्रस्सिद्धासिद्धं प्रयोजनम्‌” ॥ (11118 seems to accord with the meaning assigned to it by Marfthas, as shown by Molesworth in his Marathi dictionary where he defines it as ^ Action of indeterminate character ; speech vague and indefinite; a proceeding void of decided leaning or bearing.” The maxim is cited by Sankara in Brahmasdtrabhdshya 1. 1. 19, and againin 1. 2. 8. In the former, after quoting Taittiriya-upanishad ii. 1-4 in regard to अन्नरसमयः, प्राणमय &९. he says :—“qfe विकारार्थे मयट्प्रवाहे सत्यानन्दमय एवाकस्मादधजरतीयन्यायेन कथमिव मयटः भ्राचुयाँथैसवं ब्रह्मविषयत्वं वाश्रीयत इति? ॥ The commentators Anandagiri and Ramananda (erroneously styled Govindananda* ) ignore the maxim entirely, and so does Dr, Thibaut in his translation. In the second instance, S’ankara says:—“aatae तरिं weftatss: प्रतिपत्तव्यो न तत्राधजरतीयं wea,” which is rendered by Dr. Thibaut, “Very well, then, it appears that the truth about scriptural matters is to be ascertained from scripture, and that scripture is not sometimes to be appealed to and on other occasions to be disregarded.’ Anandagiri’s comment is:—a हि कु्ुटादेरेकदेशो भोगाय पच्यत एकदेशस्तु प्रसवाय Hera विरो- धात्‌", “You cannot take one part of a fowl for cooking and leave the other part to lay eggs;” that is, you must take a * 806 Dr, Fitzedward Hall’s Index, page 90, | 4 thing in its entirety, or else leave it altogether. Ramananda, on the other hand, says:—‘ a} सुखमात्रं जरला agra: कामयते नाङ्गानीति सोऽयमर्धज
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 5

gloss_en: I am too weak to construct a house, but I am well able to destroy one.
explanation: I am too weak to construct a house, but I am well able to destroy one. This is found in Dhundhirdja’s commentary on Mudrérékshasa iii. 11. Chinakya says—“@4 स्पद्धेते मया सह दुरात्मा WA” | and then follows the verse “Harm: कौटिल्यो सुजग * Vol. ii. page 231 of the edition by Dr. F, Kielhorn, who himself most kindly gave me the reference,’ 9 इव निर्याय नगरात्‌ &.,” on which Dhundhirdja remarks:—“‘aaa नास्य बुद्धिबलं परंतु BATA अशक्तोऽहं गृहारम्भे शक्तोऽहं गरहभञ्जन इति न्यायेन मैोर्यापकारमात्राय प्रयतमानः सन्केवरं पौरषवरमवष्टभ्य AES: TH मतिशयितुं व्यवसित इत्यहो राक्षसस्य दुव्यैवसितमिति"' ॥ I am indebted for this passage to the late learned Librarian at the India Office, C. H. Tawney Esqr., who has also pointed out to me Pancatantra i. 363 as illustrating the nyaya. The verse reads thus :— «८ घातयितुमेव नीचः wert वेत्ति न प्रसाधयितुम्‌ | पातयितुमेव शक्तिनौखोरुदध तमन्नपिटम्‌ ” ॥ अशोकवनिकान्यायः ॥ The maxim of the grove of Asoka trees. Apte says “Ravana . kept Sita in the grove of As‘oka trees, but it is not easy to account for his preference of that particular grove to any other one; so when a man finds several ways of doing a thing, any one of them may be considered as good as another, and the preference of any particular one cannot be accounted for.” For the As‘oka-grove see Rdimdyana 1. 1. 73. The only example of this nyéya that I have met with is in S’esinanta’s tika on S‘as’adhara’s Nydyasiddhdntadipa, page 11. This work was commenced in the Pandit for April 1903, but is still incomplete,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 8

gloss_en: The maxim of the stone and clod of earth.
explanation: The maxim of the stone and clod of earth. Apte, following the Vdcaspatyam, explains it thus:—“A clod may be considered to be hard when compared with cotton, but is soft as compared with a stone. So a person may be considered to be very important as compared with his inferiors, but sinks into insignificance when compared with his betters” Térindtha adds that when it is intended to indicate that there is very little difference between two things or persons compared, the kindred 2 10 maxim पाषाणेष्टकान्याय is used. With these Apte compares the Marathi proverb “gaettaidizax’ “Brick is softer than stone.” ‘The sense, however, is not quite the same; for, according to Molesworth, the Marathi saying is used “in ironical softening of a difficulty or hardship but barely surmountable or sufferable, by comparing it with a matter utterly impracticable or intolerable.” प Is it not much more likely however that the maxim is based on Brihaddéranyaka Upanishad 1. 3.7, “‘aargaraaat wet favdaa” which S’ankara expounds thus:—‘“‘qyy लोकेऽदमानं पाषा- nat गत्वा प्राप्य wie: पांसुपिण्डः पाषाणचूणैनायादमनि ie: स्वयं विध्वंसेत aaa विचूर्णभिवेत्‌ 9 This is referred to in Brahmasitrabhdshya 3. 3. 6. as the “‘agqetefaaata’ and it seems to remind one of the Scripture saying “Whosover shall fall upon that stone shall be broken; but on whomsoever it shall fall, it will grind him to powder.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 9

gloss_en: A weapon is silenced by a weapon.
explanation: A weapon is silenced by a weapon. Perhaps analogous to the saying “Diamond cuts diamond,” or, “Set a thief to catch a thief.” It occurs in Jnénottama’s commentary on Sures’vara’s Naiskarmyasiddhi i. 81, where he says :—“‘azqeanan शाम्यतीति न्यायेन काम्यैः काम्यानां निषिदधर्निषिद्धानां fafaefeaar आह न च wreak’ ॥ Compare with this Nitisdra viii. 67 :-- “विषं विषेण व्यथते ad ado भिद्यते । गजेन्द्रो दष्टसारेण गजेन्दरेणेव बध्यते" ॥ AAAI: ॥ The simile of a Lamp without oil [ that is, from which the oil has burnt out}, Raghundthavarma explains it thus:— « अरसिमश्चाध्यासेऽत्ञानं कारणम्‌ | तत्व्ञानेन वातदीपन्यायेन तज्निटृत्तावसेहदीपल्यायेन तदध्यासोऽपि fade. न च ज्ञानेनैवोभयनिडृत्तिः कतो न ः 3 11 स्यात्‌ । ज्ञानमज्ञानस्यैव निवर्तकमिति न्यायविरोधात्‌ । द्विविधा हि निचृत्ति- विरोधिना सामग्रीनिन्रृत्या च । यथा वातादिना deat यथा च तेरुवल्यीदि- निन्रत्या दीपनिष्ृत्तिः । तत्राद्या निदृत्तिरक्तानस्य द्वितीया कायैव्गस्येति बोध्यम्‌ "` ॥ I have met with the following example of the nyAya in Yogavdsistha 2. 1. 44, a chapter entitled शुकनियीणम्‌ :-- “aa वषैसहसराणि निर्विकल्यसमाधिना । aa स्थित्वा शशामासावात्मन्यज्ञेह दीपवत्‌ ° ॥ For the "धै ्ञानम्ञानस्यैव निवर्तकम्‌ see the Third Handful of Popwlar Mawims; and for fiffaeqaanta see Vedanta. séra, pp. 55, 57 &, and Manual of Hindu Pantheism, pages 109, 110. अहिकुण्डरन्यायः ॥ | The maxim of the snake and its coils, The expression occurs in Brahmasétra 3, 2. 27, and is explained in the bhashya. They read पाप :--““उभयव्यपदेशाचहिङुण्डरूवत्‌ ॥ -."अत उभयव्यपदे- शदशनादहिकुण्डलवदत्र ad भवितुमर्हति । यथाहि रिलयभेदः ङण्डलाभोगप्राञचुत्वादीनीति च भेद एवमिहापीति” ॥ Dr. Thibaut’s translation is as follows:—“But on account of twofold designation, ( the relation of the highest Self to the individual soul has to be viewed) like that of the snake to its coils......We therefore look on the relation of the highest Self and the soul as analogous to that of the snake and its coils. Viewed as a whole the snake is one, non-different, while an element of difference appears if we view it with regard to its coils, hood, erect posture and so on.” It is akin therefore to the expressions “a forest and its trees,” “a lake and its waters,” so often used by the Vedantists as illustrations of identity. The explanation given by Taranatha in the Vdchaspatyam does not coincide with the above. He says:— “ae: सर्पस्य यथा कुण्डलाकृतिवेष्टनं स्वाभाविकं तथा यस्य स्वाभाव्रिकधर्मो भ्यपदियते तत्रास्य cafe” ॥ 12 :
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 10

gloss_en: The maxim of striking the sky with onés fist.
explanation: The maxim of striking the sky with onés fist. A vain | attempt at an impossibility. It occurs in the Jaimini chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 133 of Bib. Ind. edition, and p. 151 of Jivananda’s ) as 1011098 “^ तस्मादुतपत्तौ हसौ च परतस्त्वे ग्रमाणसंभवात्स्रतःसिद्धं प्रामाण्यमिव्येतस्पूतिकूष्माण्डायत इति चेत्तदेतदाकाशअष्िहननायते" ॥ 1101. Cowell has rendered it thus:—“Therefore, as we can prove that authoritativeness is both produced and recognized by means of something external, the Mimanis4 tenet ‘authoritativeness is self-proved’ is like a gourd over-ripe and rotten. This long harangue of our opponent, however, is but a vain attempt to strike the sky with his fist.” Much older instances of the employment of the nydya are the following :—Tantravirtika, page 170, “'यस्तन्तूननुपादाय तुरीमा- श्रपरि्रहात्‌ | पटं ay समीहेत स हन्याल्चोम fete.” ॥ [ With this compare the words of a great modern preacher-Dr. Maclaren- “Logic without Revelation is like a spinning-machine without cotton, busy drawing out nothing” ]. Pancapddikd page 48, line 19, “qatg नाम ज्ञानं रोके सिद्धं तथापि निरस्तग्रपञ्चात्मविषयमसिद्धमा- काशमुष्टहननवन्न विधातुं शक्यम्‌. Then, in Nydyakandali, page 56, line 6, we find the cognate expression ‘‘qyy efatafra कृपाणawament प्रति व्यापारयन्‌ ”, and again in Nydyakanikd, page 219,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 12

gloss_en: The appearance or disappearance of a quality (or characteristic ) produces a corresponding change in the subject of it, This nydya is the second line of Naiskarmyasiddhi ii.
explanation: The appearance or disappearance of a quality (or characteristic ) produces a corresponding change in the subject of it, This nydya is the second line of Naiskarmyasiddhi ii. 35, the first being ‘ आगमापायिनिष्टत्वादनि्यत्वमिया दृः” Although it is included in Raghundtha’s list, it ought not, strictly speaking, to find a place amongst popular maxims; but I insert it in order to make a necessary correction in the printed text of the Sarvadars'anasangraha where it is quoted. On page 161 13 of the Bibliotheca Indica edition, and on page 182 of that prepared by Jivananda Vidydsigara, we read ‹(ततश्चोपपन्न स्स्वयन्धर्मो विकरोति हि धर्मिणमिति न्यायेन” and this bad reading of course affects the translation as it appears on page 244 of the volume prepared for Triibner’s Oriental Series by Professors Cowell and Gough. The explication given in Vdchaspatyam is as follows—“qar adea रूपरसादिरूपध्मेपराव्रत्तौ रूपरसाद्न्तरोत्पत्तौ च घटदेधौर्भिणो fats यस्य धर्मिणः पूैधर्मस्यापगमेऽन्यधर्मस्योत्पत्तिस्तत्रायं न्यायोऽवतरति? ॥ उषरकण्टकभन्षणन्यायः-- The maxim of a camel’s eating thorns. This is not in Raghu- : natha’s list, but in the Vdeaspatyam it is explained thus :— “seq wileretamagarestt शामीपत्रभक्षणसुखलेशो यथा तथा- भीष्टविषयोपार्जनदुःखकारे तदु पाजितद्रभ्यजसुखलेको यत्रोपदिङ्यते तत्रास प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ 1 regard this, however, as quite beside the mark. In Brahmasdtrabhdshya 2.2.1, S‘ankara, after combating at length the Sankhya theory, that objects are in themselves सुखदुःखमोहात्मक, ६805: -““शब्दाद्यविशेषेऽपि च भावनाविशेषात्सुखादिवि- दोषोपर्ब्धेः,? which Dr, Thibaut renders—* And, further, although the sense-object, such as sound and so on, is one, yet we observe that owing to the difference of the mental impressions (produced by it) differences exist in the effects it produces, one person being affected by it pleasantly, another painfully, and 80 on.” On which Anandagiri says:i—‘qsardifa । भावना तत्तञ्जातियोग्या वासना तद्विशेषादुष्रादीनां कण्टकादौ सुखादिदर्चीनात्‌ cc,” Vacaspatimis’ra, explains the same passage in the Bhdmat?, (pp. 880-1), pointing out that things are not in themselves essentially pleasant or unpleasant, and that what causes pleasure to one may be painful to another, and that even the same thing which at one time is agreeable may at another time be the reverse. Otherwise thorns would be as acceptable to men as they are toacamel. Here are his words:—“‘afs gata एव सुखदुःखस्वभावा 14 भवेययुसततः स्वरूपव्वा्धेमन्तेपि चन्दनः सुखः स्यात्‌ । न हि चन्दनः कदाचिद्‌- चन्दनः। तथा निदाघेष्वपि ऊुंकुमपंकः सुखो भवेत्‌। न ह्यसौ कदाचिंदकुंकुमपंकः। एवं कण्टकः क्रमेरकस्य सुख इति मदुष्यादीनामपि प्राणश्चतां ga: स्यात्‌ | न ह्यसौ कांश्चिसप्रयेवाकण्टक* इति । तस्मादसुखादिस्वभावा अपि चन्दनङुकु- माद्यो जातिकारावस्था्पेश्षया सुखढुःखादिहेतवो न तु स्वयं सुखादिस्वभावा इति रमणीयम्‌ ”. A camel, then, eats thorns because it likes them; a man does not eat them because he does not like them. May not the maxim, therefore, be the equivalent of our “What's one man’s food is another man’s poison?” Mr. M. R. Telang has kindly pointed out Vikramdnkadevacarita i, 29, as illustrating a camel’s love of thorns.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 12

gloss_en: Whalst seeking for one thing he loses another.
explanation: Whalst seeking for one thing he loses another. The saying appears twice, in this form, in the Sarvadars‘anasangraha. The first instance is in the Arhata Section (क. 27 of Bib, Ind. and 83 of Jivananda’s edn. ), translated by Professor Cowell:— ५एतद्ोषपरिजिहीषैया art जडतां agatha qa हन्त तर्हिं तस्या ग्रहणं न स्यादिलेकमनुखन्धिस्सतोऽपरं भ्रच्यवत इति न्यायापातः” । “If in your wish to escape this difficulty, you assert that ‘the perception does not follow the object in being insentient, then there would be no perception that the object is insentient, and so it is a case of the proverb, ‘While he looks for one thing which he has lost, another drops.” ‘The second example, from the Akshapfda section (pp. 118and 134), is as {01108--““नन्वेकमनुखन्धित्सतोऽपरं म्रच्यवत इति न्या- येन दुःखवस्सुखमिच्युच्छिद्यत दयकाम्योऽयं पक्ष इति चेन्मैवं ea” । “Nor may you retort on us that we have fulfilled the proverb of ‘seeking one thing and dropping another in the search, since we have abolished happiness as being ever tainted with some incidental pain &c.” * The printed text wrongly reads कण्टक for अकण्टके, 2 In the Khandanakhandakhédya (page 447) and in Mallinitha on Térkikaraksd, pp. 7 and 25, we meet with the nydya in another form, namely “ag सन्धित्सतोऽपरं प्रच्यवते which means “ Whilst trying to reunite one [ piece of a broken vessel] another falls off.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 14

gloss_en: The maxim of two fruits attached to one stalk.
explanation: The maxim of two fruits attached to one stalk. Used by writers on Alankdra to illustrate a particular kind of -Parono- । masia, namely the coalescence of two meanings under one word. It was first pointed out to me by Mr. F. W. Thomas, Assistant Librarian to the India Office, he having met with it in the commentary on Kdvyddars‘a ii. 810. I have since found it in use in the Alankdrasarvasva, Kdvyapradipa, Sdhityadarpana, Rasagangddhara, Alankérakaustubha, and Sdhityakaumudi, in each case under the figure शेष. The maxim finds a place in Marathi literature also, and is regarded by Molesworth as equivalent to our proverb “Killing two birds with one stone,” ४
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 15

gloss_en: The maxim of the buds of the Kadamba tree.
explanation: The maxim of the buds of the Kadamba tree. They are said to burst forth simultaneously. As, for example, in Hemacandra’s Paris'ishtaparvan i. 241 :— “fra cantata स्ष््यमानोऽवनीपतिः | उत्कोरककदम्बाभो बभूव FIRST” ॥ ` In the Nyéyamanjari, pages 214 and 228, and in the Bhdshdparichchheda (verse 166) this nyfya is given as an illustration of the way in which sound is produced, The lastmentioned reads thus;— 16 वीचीतरङ्गन्यायेन तदुत्पत्तिस्तु कीर्तिता । कद्म्बकोरकन्यायादुत्पत्तिः कस्यचिन्मते ॥ The commentary, Siddhdntamuktévali, however, explains कद्‌- स्बगोरकन्यायात्‌ and this is the form given to the maxim in the Vdchuspatyam which explains it as follows :-—“‘gazaqmieaeq गोखाकारकदस्बस्य सवौवयवेषु यथा YTTINEA एवं सवैश्रदेशेषु युगपय्त्र श्रसरस्तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ 17 Vedantin Mahfdeva’s comment on Sdnkhyasitra Y. 108, we have a third form, namely कद्म्बसुङ्रन्याय. , कफोणिगुडन्यायः ॥ The maxim of treacle on the elbow, Used of something tantalizingly inaccessible. It is found in Udayana’s Almatattvaviveka, page 26 :--अस्तु afe भावस्वरूपातिरिक्ता निवृत्तिनास्तील्यस्य सोपा- ख्येति देषः | नन्वयमपि क्षणभंगस्योद्धारः स च कफोणिगुडायितो ada भवतु वा निच्त्तिरसमर्था तथाप्यहेतुकस्वे तस्याः किमायातम्‌" ॥ Then in the Akshapida chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 116 of Bib. Indica, and 132 of. Jivananda’s edition) we read :— ^नजु॒दुःखात्यन्तोच्छेदोऽपवगै इल्येतदद्यापि कफोणिगुडायितं वर्ते तत्कथं विद्धवल्करय व्यवददियत इति चेन्मैवम्‌ which Prof. Cowell translates as follows :—“But is not your definition of the swmmum bonum, liberation, that is, the absolute abolition of pain, after all as much beyond our reach as treacle on the elbow is to the tongue ; why then is this continually put forth as if it were established beyond all dispute ?’ In a footnote he says, “Compare the English proverb ‘As soon as the cat can lick her ear.’” In the Vdchaspatyam, however, the ny4ya is explained as meaning the absence of a thing, not its inaccessibility. It says:— «कफोणौ गुडाभावेऽपि aaa यथा केहनमेवं यत्र वस्तस्वसद्धावेऽपि तस्प्रलया- इया व्यापारभेदस्तत्रास्य भ्रवृत्तिः” ॥ The St. Petersburg Lexicon (8. v. कफोणिगुडायू ) renders it “like a ball on the elbow.” 17
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 15

gloss_en: The maxim of the crow and the Palmyra fruit.
explanation: The maxim of the crow and the Palmyra fruit. A wow alighted on a Palmyra tree, and at the same moment some of the fruit fell on its head and killed it. The maxim is therefore used to illustrate a startling and purely accidental occurrence. It is well explained in the Kds‘ikdvritti on Pdnini 5. 3. 106 (as quoted by Dr. Eggeling in a footnote to Ganaratnamahodadhi iii, 195):--“"काकतारीयम्‌ । अजाकृपाणीयम्‌ | अन्धकवर्तकीयम्‌ | अतर्कितोपनतं चित्रीकरणञुच्यते | तत्कथम्‌ । काकस्यागमनं याटच्छिकं Tee पतने च । तेन TSA पतता काकस्य वधः कृतः | एवमेव देवदत्तस्य तत्रागमनं दस्यूनां चोपनिपातः । तैश्च तस्य वधः कृतः | तत्र यो देवदत्तस्य दस्यूनां च समागमः स काकतारुसमागमसदशः'ः ॥ We find the saying in Pafichadas’t ix. 12 as 1011078 (“अय्‌ थावस्तुविक्ञानारफलं रभ्यत ईप्सितम्‌ । काकतारीयतः सोऽय संवादिभ्रम उच्यते” ॥ And again in Anandavardhana’s Dhvanydloka ii. 16, and in Nydyavdrtikatdtparyattkd, page 401. There is a capital example of it, too, in the following verses of the Nydyamanjart (page 106):—“ अपि चानागतं ्ञानमस्मदादेरपि कचित्‌ ॥ प्रस्ाणं प्रातिभं इवो मे आातागन्तेति दरयते ॥ नानथेजं न सन्दिग्धं न वाधविधुरीकृतम्‌ । न दु्टकारणं चेति प्रमाणमिद्मिष्यताम्‌ ॥ कचिद्धाधकयोगश्चेदस्तु तस्याप्रमाणता । यत्रापरेदयुरभ्येति sat तत्र किुच्यताम्‌ ॥ काकतारीयमिति चेन्न प्रमाणब्रद्‌- रितम्‌ | वस्तु तस्काकतालीयमिति भवितुमहंति"? ॥ 17 his commentary on S‘antiparva clxxvii. 11, Nilakantha Govind gives another, and less probable, definition of the maxim. He says:—‘‘are: करतलयोः शब्द्‌जनकः संयोगस्तस्मिन्‌ क्रियमाणे उत्पतन्काको देवात्तत्र तारा- भ्यामाक्रान्तोऽभूत्तदेतत्काकतारीयमिन्युच्यते | काकस्पदोसमकारं ASAT ताखब्रक्षसख वा पतनं तदिलन्येः? Molesworth explains it thus:— ५ Said when any occurrence synchronizing with, or immediately following, some other seems, however in truth independent of it, to 1४९७ been occasioned by it;—as the fruit of a Palmyra falling at the alighting upon it of a crow, may appear to fall in consequence,” 3. हालत 18
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 17

gloss_en: The maxim of the examination of a crow’s teeth.
explanation: The maxim of the examination of a crow’s teeth. Used of any useless and manifestly fruitless enquiry. It occurs in the Buddhist treatise Vydyabindutikd, page 1, and again on page 3, line 8. Also in S’ankara’s bhashya on Katha-Upanishad i. 25 [“नचिकेतो मरणं मानुप्राक्षीः" ]---“नचिकेतो मरणं मरणसंबद्धं sat प्ेल्ास्ति नास्तीति काकदन्तपरीश्चारूपं मावुप्राक्षीमेवं प्रष्टुमर्हसि"? ॥ We find it too in Abhinavagupta on Dhvanydloka iii. 19 (page 168 of Pandit Durgaprasida’s edition) as follows:—“‘ayqisdt मवतु Al वाभूत्‌ कस्तत्राभिनिवेशाः | काकदन्तपरीक्षाप्रायमेव तस्स्यादिति भावः" ॥ It appears also in Pancapddikd, pages 58 and 68, and in many works besides. In Nydyamanjaré, page 7, line 5, it takes the form of वायसद्द्नविमरौन्याय. काकाक्षिगोरकन्यायः ॥ । The maxim of the crow’s eyeball. Crows are popularly supposed to have only one eye, which, as occasion requires, moves from the cavity on one side into that on the other. The maxim is used of a word which appears only once in a sentence but which applies to two portions of it; or of persons or things fulfilling a double purpose. I have met with it in the former sense in Svatmaram’s Hathayogapradipikd iv. 10, “'विवैधैरासनैः ङम्मेर्वचित्रैः SUT,” on which the commentator, Brahmananda, says ““विचित्रैरिति काकाक्षिगोरुकन्यायेनो भयत्र संबध्यते.” Also in Abhinavagupta’s comment on Dhvanydloka iii. 1, “‘qarat स्मारकत्वेऽपि पदमात्रावभासिनः") where he remarks “afqqeq: काकाश्चिन्यायेनो - aaala संबध्यते" Of its use in the second sense, we have an interesting example in Kémandaki’s Nitisdra, a work ascribed to the third century before Christ. Chapter xi. 24 reads thus:— ““बकिनोर्विषतोमैष्ये वाचात्मानं समर्पयन्‌ । द्वैधीभावेन वर्तेत काकाक्षिवदलक्षितः.” 19
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 18

gloss_en: The maxim of catching at straws.
explanation: The maxim of catching at straws. he being driven from one argument or position to another equally untenable, Taraप says:—“aengt पतितस्य संतरणानभिक्ञस्य यथा कुशकाशावरम्बनं निरथकमेवं प्रबलयुक्तिषु निराकृतासु दु बेखयुक्त्यवरम्बनं निर्थकमिलयेवमवलम्बनस्य निरथंकस्वविवक्षायामस्य प्रवृत्तिः” ॥ The expression ‘gray. कुशावटम्बनकल्पं" occurs in the Arhata chapter (p. 25 of Bib, Ind. and p. 31 of Jivananda’s edition ) of the Sarvadars'anasangraha, and again in the Panini chapter (pp. 142 and 161 ); and in both places Prof. Cowell has rendered it “like a drowning man’s catching at a straw.” We have the same, though not as a compound, in Naishkarmyasiddhi 1. 76,—‘“‘we निरा- कृतोऽपि कादौ कुरा arawregte.” It appears also 70 Vydyamanjart page 183; and again, as follows, on page 551 :-—‘‘qeqreratotalsशक्ये क्ये वा वस्तुनि्णैये । एवं प्रायमयुक्तं यत्छुशकाश्चावङंबनम्‌'” ॥ 1" Tantravartika 1. 8. 18 (page 213 ) we have the maxim in the following couplet:—“‘srjarg न चेजातं Bette waist: | तस्यानुषङ्गिकेप्वाशा कुशकाशावरंबिनी " ॥ In expounding Brahmasitrabhdshya 3. 3, 29, Anandagiri quotes Amarakos’a 2. 4 166 (weft eat शो दर्भः पवित्रमथ कन्तणम्‌) as follows:—‘‘a@ ware ऊुह्मिलयमरसिहे नानु शिष्टम्‌.” Does this throw any new light on the date of Amara? In his History of Indian Literature ( page 280 ), Professor Weber tells us that the Amarakos’a cannot be widely separated from those dictionaries which we know to belong to the eleventh, twelfth, and following centuries; whilst Professor Macdonell, in his recently issued History of Sanskrit Literature ( page 433 ) assigns that work to about 500 A. D.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 19

gloss_en: The maxim of a frog ina well.
explanation: The maxim of a frog ina well. It is applied to an inexperienced person brought up in the narrow circle of home, and : 20 ignorant of public life and mankind. “Home-keeping youth have ever homely wits” ( Zwo Gentlemen of Verona i. 1). The following passage from Prasannardghava-Ndataka i. (page 18) illustrates the use of the term:—‘‘gq मामपि दरादिग्विलासिनी- कणपूरीकृतकीर्तिपह्छवं च्रिभुवनवीरनामधेयं कूपमंडूक इव सागरमविख्यातमपदिशसि!" ॥ So, too, Upamitibhavaprapaned, page 828:— om न fia fact विरोकयति मेदिनीम्‌ । अनेकाद्धु तदृ्तान्तां स नरः कूपद डरः” ॥ कूपयन््रघटिकान्यायः ॥ The maxim of the pots attached to the water-wheel of a well. As the wheel revolves, some of the pots are going up and others are going down; some are full whilst others are empty; and so it is applied to illustrate the changes and chances of this mortal life. This is well put in Mrichchhakatika x. 60:— ““कांश्ित्तुच्छयति प्रपूरयति वा का्चिन्नय्युन्नतिं कांश्चित्पातविधौ करोति च पुनः कांशचिन्रयलयाङुलान्‌ | अन्योन्यं प्रतिपश्चसंहतिमिमां रोकस्थिति बोधयन्नेष क्रीडति कूपयञ्रघटिकान्यायभ्रसक्तो विधिः" ॥ Tarandtha’s explanation of this maxim is extremely tame, not to say nonsensical! According to him, it is intended to teach that as a pot is raised by the water-wheel from a deep well, so, by means of instruction, the essence of the S’astras is drawn up, deep though they are by reason of their complexity! See the cognate घटीयन्नन्याय in Second and Third Handful. ५
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 19

gloss_en: The maxim of the limbs of the tortoise.
explanation: The maxim of the limbs of the tortoise. Its meaning and application will be apparent from the following passage taken from the Sankhya section of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 150 Bib, Ind, and 170°of Jivananda’s edition )-- व्यथा हि ea 21 स्याङ्गानि sare? निविशमानानि तिरोभवन्ति निःसरन्ति चाविर्भवन्देवं कारणस्य तन्त्वादेः पटादयो विशेषा निःसरन्त आविर्भवन्त उत्पद्यन्त इ्युच्यन्ते निविरामानास्तिरोभवन्तो विन्यन्तीस्युच्यन्ते । न पुनरसतासुत्पत्तिः सतां वा विनाश्यः” Prof. Cowell renders it thus:—* As the limbs of a tortoise, when they retire within its shell, are concealed, and, when they come forth, are revealed, so the particular effects, as cloth &., of a cause, as threads &९., when they come forth and are revealed, are said to be produced; and when they retire and are concealed, they are said to be destroyed; but there is no such thing as the production of the non-existent, or the destruction of the existent.” Very similar language is used by Vachaspati Mis’ra, too, in his Sénkhyatativakawmudi 9 and 15. See also, Kshurikd-Upanishad 8, and Gité ii. 58.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 20

gloss_en: The nyfya stands thus in Tantravdértika 3.
explanation: The nyfya stands thus in Tantravdértika 3. 4, 1 :—‘qeg भाष्यकारेणोपन्यासः कृतः स कत्वाचिन्तान्यायेनेति द्रष्टव्यम्‌.” On applying to my friend Mr. Arthur Venis for an elucidation of the nyaya he replied as follows:—“I have always understood it as the method of granting to your adversary what later you mean to refute, for which another common expression is अभ्युपगमवाद्‌, The word कृत्वा is elliptical for इति कृत्वा, that is, having admitted your adversary’s point, you proceed to the चिन्ता or discussion of it.” This view seems to be confirmed by the fact that in S'dstradipikd, pages 615, 666, 707, 710, and 739, it takes the form of “दति gear चिन्त्यते,” and that in S’abara on Jaimini 11. 3. 16. and 12, 2. 11, we read at the close of each “‘ga@q gat चिन्त्यते । कृत्वाचिन्तेयम्‌.” In his Saddars'anacintanikd, Mr, M. M. Kunte - gives three different renderings of the expression. On page 650 (where it is attached to the title of the adhikarana ) its meaning is said to be “a point already discussed,’ and he claims for it the authority of Madhava; then, on page 1999, it 22 is rendered “ an adjustment not founded on fact;? and, finally, on page 2040, “the examination of a subject after merely granting an opponent's statement.” This third rendering coincides with that of Mr. Venis. I may add that Prof. Ganginatha Jh4 tells me that a pandit would explain the term thus;—“‘geqr (यद्यप्येतद्विषयकतंशयो नोदेति तथापि तद्विषयगतसकरूविचारस्योपन्यासा्थै कल्पनां कृत्वा) चिन्ता (विचारः). Or, as the Professor himself puts it, “the bhasya has introduced certain points of discussion simply for the sake of argument, in order to exhaust all possible alternatives with regard to the subject matter of the adhikarana.” I have not met with the nydya anywhere but in works on Mimémsa. - क्षीरं विहायारोचकग्रस्तस्य सौवीररुचिमनुभवति ॥ “Leaving the milk switable to the dyspeptic, he enjoys the sour gruel.” The nyaya is found in this form in the Akshapada section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (P. 118 of Bib. Ind, edition and p. 134 of Jivananda’s ed.) as follows:—“ सनु सुखाभि- व्यक्ति्क्तिरिति पक्षं vas दुःखनिदृत्तिरेव मुक्तिरिति खीकारः क्षीरं विंहदा- यारोचकग्रस्तस्य सोवीररचिमनु भवतीति चेत्तदेतन्नाटकपक्षपतितं age इल्युेक्षयतेः ॥ Prof, Cowell translates it thus:—“But if you give yp the view that liberation is the manifestation of happiness, and then accept such a view as that which holds it to be only the cessation of pain, does not your conduct resemble that of the dyspeptic patient who refused sweet milk and preferred sour ricegruel ? Your satire, however, falls powerless, as fitter for some speech in a play [rather than for a grave philosophical argument].” There can be little doubt however that Madhava took the ny&ya from Udayana’s Atmatativaviveka where it appears (on page 56, line 5,) as “aft विहाय रंचिररोचकम्रस्तस्य adit.” I know of no other instance of it, and it is not in Raghundtha’s Laukikanyéyasangraha. : 23
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 21

gloss_en: The maxim of the bald (or bare-headed) man, and the woodapple.
explanation: The maxim of the bald (or bare-headed) man, and the woodapple. Vardhaméana (iii. 195 ) explains it thus:—‘‘qyrt खल्वाटः पयटज्नतकिंतं श्रीफरूतरोरधस्तादागतो दैववशाच बिल्वमुपरि पतितं तद्दन्योऽप्युमयवस्तुसंयोग एवसुच्यते” ॥ Bharirihari, in his Nitisataka 90, brings the bald man under a palm tree:— c@eaat दिवसेश्वरस्य किरणैः संतापितो मस्तके वाञ्छन्देशमनातपं विधिवज्ञात्तारुसख मूं गतः | TANT महाफलेन पतता vA सशब्दं शिरः get गच्छति aa भाग्यरहितस्तत्रैव यान्लयापद्‌ः ॥ This maxim belongs to the same class as अज्ञाङृपाणीय and काकतारीय, which see.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 23

gloss_en: The maxim of rwminating on ether.
explanation: The maxim of rwminating on ether. Equivalent to beating the air, It is found twice in the Sarvadars'anasangraha. First, in the Raméanuja section (page 57 of JivAnanda’s edition, and 47 of Bib. 1१.)--“तदेतद्भगनरोमन्थायितम्‌', which Prof Gough renders “All this is about as profitable as it would be for a ruminant animal to ruminate on ether.” Secondly, in the Panini section (pages 162 and 143 respectively ):—“ तदैतद्वगनरोमन्थweqy’, rendered by Prof. Cowell by “All this is only the ruminating of empty ether.” In the Nydyamanjart page 453, it appears in a slightly different form, in the expression "योनि रोमन्थकेखिवत्‌;'” and in S’dlikd p. 154, and Nyéyamakaranda, page 129, we meet with गगनग्रासकस्प. गड्रिकाप्रवाहस्यायः ॥ _ The maxim of @ continuous rush of sheep. It is used to indicate the blind following of others like a flock of sheep, So the Vachaspatygm, which saysi—“agarmradtat सेघादेका 24 चेन्नद्यादौ पतति aqr तस्संवान्तग॑ताः सर्वेऽपि वार्यमाणा अपि aa पतन्तीति लोकप्रसिद्धा यत्र वार्थमाणानामपि अनिष्टमागें धावनं तत्रास प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ The expression occurs in Chap. viii. (page 214) of the Kavya. prakds'a where a very helpful note of Mahes‘achandra’s will be found; and also in Chap. vi, page 188, of the Sdhityadarpana which however, Mr. Pramadadésa Mitra has rendered, “in pursuance of established custom.” It is found, also, on pages 86 and 125 of Abhinavagupta’s commentary on the Dhvanydloka, and in the opening part of Kdvyapradipa viii (p, 327). There seems to be much difference of opinion as to the correct form of the first word of the maxim, since it appears in the four varieties of agit, agfear, गङ्ारिका and agifssr.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 23

gloss_en: The maxim ofthe tongue [smeared] with treacle [in order to disguise an unpalatable draught].
explanation: The maxim ofthe tongue [smeared] with treacle [in order to disguise an unpalatable draught]. The Vdchaspatyam thus explains its use:-—‘ यथा तिक्तताभिया निम्बपानमङुवीणस्य बालस्य जिद्धायां ger द्वा पित्रादिस्तं निम्बं पाययति एवमर्थवादवाक्यानि बह्वायाससाध्ये कर्मण्यभ्रवWard पुरूष स्वगाक्षययादिकं श्रावयित्वा प्रवर्तयन्ति | फलश्चुतिरपि रोचनाथौ॥ An excellent example of this is found in Kévyapradipa, p. 7:- “ये सुकुमारमतयोऽतिसुखिस्वभावा राजकुमारादयो नीरसे नीतिशाखे प्रवर्तयितुमदयक्यास्तान्काव्यं कान्तेव सरसतापादनेनाभिसुखीङृलयोपदेदं महयति गुडजिद्धिकया शिद्यूनिवौपधम्‌ | यथाहुः | स्वादुकाव्यरसोन्मिश्रं वाक्याथंयुपसुञ्जते | ग्रथमारीढमधवः पिबन्ति कटु भेषजम्‌” ॥ It is employed in a similar manner by Abhinavagupta in his comment on Dhvanydloka iii. 80, and by the author of Padamanjart in the early part of his work (the Pandit x. 254); and, somewhat less clearly, in Bhdmoti, pages 342, 534, and Nydyavdrtikatatparyatikd, pages 438, 441. 25
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 24

gloss_en: The maxim of the cattle and the bull.
explanation: The maxim of the cattle and the bull. In the Panaw tor October 1867, Rajarama S‘Astri expounded it thus:—‘‘qy गाः कार्य बरीवर्दे चेत्युच्यते तत्र गोपदेनैव वलीवदैपदसिद्धौ बरीवदैपदं दुद. म्यत्वक्ञापनपरस्वेन सफरमिति कट्प्यते"" ॥ That given in the Vdchaspatyam is somewhat पोल: बलीवदैस्य गोविदेषस्वेऽपि बरीवदेस्य ह्यटिति गोस्वेन बोधनार्थं यथा प्रयोगस्तथान्ययोः सामान्यविशेषरूपयोक्ष॑दिति बोधनार्थं यत्र प्रयोगस्तत्रासख प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ In his exposition of Manu viii. 28, where six classes of women are enumerated as having a claim to the king’s protection, Kullika says:—‘‘arg चानेकmedina गोबरीवदैन्यायेन घुनसक्तिपरीहारः” ॥ The commentators Raghavananda and Govindardja also quote the maxim in the same connection, and we have it in Kdvyapradipa vii. 11 (page 300). Vacaspatimis’ra, too, makes frequent use of it. It occurs in Bhdmatt, pages 518, 536; and in Nyayavartikatdtparyatikd, pages 11, 118, 119 and 404. It belongs to the same class ४5 ब्राह्मणवसिष्टन्याय and ब्राह्मणपरिनाजकन्याय. गोमयपायसीयन्यायः ॥ The maxim of cowdung as a milky preparation. Some stupid person is supposed to argue that cowdung is made of milk, because it comes frorn the cow; hence it is used to denote an utterly absurd argument or statement. It occurs in Vyésa’s bhashya on Yogasitra i. 32. He says:—“‘aufeaanfiaamaaagiaपायसीयन्यायमाक्षिपति' ॥ 0 which the Yogavdrtika remarks:-— “iat पायसं गञ्यत्वादिव्यादिन्यायमतदृषणं समाधीयमानमप्याक्षिपति तिरस्करोति? ॥ 3९9, too, Nydyavdrtikatdtparyatikd, p. 435. It is found also in the Bauddha section of the Sarvadars‘anasangraha (page 18 of Bib, Ind. and 22 of Jivananda’s edition ) in the following sentence:—‘‘g4 चायमभेदसाधको हेतुर्गोमयपायसीयन्यायवदाभासतां WHT”, which Professor Gough renders «Thus क argument which you adduce to prove that there is ई 26 difference between subject and object, turns out a mere absurdity, like milky food made of cowdwng.” The compound maaqradia is included in Ganaratnamahodadhi iii. 196, bat Vardhamana did not explain it.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 25

explanation: . The maxim of day-break in the vicinity of the toll-collector’s hut. A man, anxious to avoid paying toll, takes another road, but losing his way in the dark, finds himself, at day-break, in the vicinity of that very toll-gate! The saying is employed to illustrate ygqrf@fz, as Prof. Cowell puts it; that is, failure to accomplish a desired object. It occurs in the Panini section of Sarvadars'anasangraha as follows:—“aq स्फोटवाचकतापक्चेऽपि प्रागुक्तविकल्पग्रसरेण घटृकटीभ्रभातायितमिति चेत्तदेतन्मनोराञ्यविचम्भणं वैषम्पसं भवात्‌”, which is thus rendered by Prof. Cowell:—“But even on your own hypothesis that there is a certain thing called sphota which expresses the meaning, the same untenable alternative will recur which we discussed before ; and therefore it will only be a case of the proverb that ‘the dawn finds the smuggler with the revenue-officer’s house close by’. This, however, is only the inflation of the world of fancy from the wide difference between the two cases.” S'riharsha, too, used the simile in his Khandanakhandakhadya ( page 35 ):—“afé कारणस्य सत्तामभ्युपगतवानसीतति घटङव्यां ्रभातमिति Aa” ॥ See also Siddhdntales'a, pages 40 and 116; Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 62; Advaitabrahmasiddhi, pages 63, 146, 219, 371; Citswkht, 1. 12; 7. 24 ( Pandit iv. 518; ए. 510 ), and Kusumdnjali iii. 19 ( page 496 ).
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 26

explanation: ‘ The maxim of the letter made by the wood-worm ghuna. This worm bores holes in wood and in books which sometimes : assume the shape of a letter of the alphabet; hence its use to intimate the occurrence of something quite accidental. Here is Vardhamana’s ( ऋ. 195 ) description of it :—gotfewnemita- । जिप्यन्नमक्षरं घुणाक्षरम्‌ । तदिव यदकुशलेन दैवान्निष्पद्यते तद्धुणाक्षरीयम्‌”' ॥ 4 The simile is found in the opening part of Prasannardghava ( page 9 ) as follows:—‘ अहो घुणाक्षरन्यायो यदिदं waza । भ्रति मयोक्तं बन्दिद्वयं प्रति फलितं aa” y Also in वकतवय iv. । 167: त्रीन्वारान्समरे जित्वा जितं मेने स सुम्सुनिम्‌ । सङ्ृजयमरेवीरा मन्यन्ते हि घुणाक्षरम्‌” ॥ 11401 iii. 58 may also be referred to.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 26

gloss_en: The maxim of the moon and its light.
explanation: The maxim of the moon and its light. Used of two insepayable things. It is found in Anandagiri’s S‘ankaravijaya, page 124 :-- “ अतः सर्वैदेवकारणस्य रद्रस्य या दाक्तिश्चनदर चन्द्रिकान्यायेन तदुद्ो- धरूपिणी स्वाधीनवलभेति प्रसिद्धा सेव भवानी? ॥ EN
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 27

explanation: 3 The maxim of the.punishment of Mdndavya for the crime committed by robbers. The story of the Rishi Ant-Mandavya is told at length in Adiparva evii, eviii. Whilst he was practising severe austerities, in conjunction with the mauna-vrata, some robbers concealed themselves and their plunder in his As‘rama, _ The king’s guard found them there, and, believing the sage to be implicated in the affair, carried him off together with them and impaled them all together! Mandavya was eventually removed from the stake, but its point (अणी ) remained in him; hence the name, given him by the people, of Ani-Mandavya. _ The maxim is found in the Ptrnaprajna chapter of Survadars'anasangraha ( page 73 of Jivananda’s edition, and 62 of । आए. Ind. ):—« तस्मान्न मेदय्रस्यक्षं सुप्रसरमिति Ate वस्तुस्वरूपभेदवादिनं प्रति इमानि दृषणान्युदुष्यन्ते किंवा धर्मिभेदवादिनं प्रति । प्रथमे चौरापरा- धान्माण्डव्यनिग्रहन्यायापातः?? ॥ But its earliest occurrence is in Atma__ tattvaviveka, page 70, line 15, where we read “ad हि चोरापराघेन 28 : उवक्तमयं assaf: स्यात्‌ and it was from this source that the Khandanakara, too, derived the nydya together with several pages of context! Compare pages 633-636 of S’riharsa’s work with pages 70 and 71 of Udayana’s.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 27

gloss_en: The maxim of the men with umbrellas, The thought here is of a crowd of men, many of them with umbrellas up, and so all seeming to have them.
explanation: The maxim of the men with umbrellas, The thought here is of a crowd of men, many of them with umbrellas up, and so all seeming to have them. Its application will be apparent from the examples which follow. We have one in S‘ankara’s bhashya on Katha-Upanishad 7. 1 ( “spa पिबन्तौ” &५. ):-- “एकस्तत्र Stas पिबति ys नेतरस्तथापि पातृसंबन्धाप्पिबन्ताविः्युच्यते ofaन्यायेन? ॥ Again, in his bhashya on Veddntasitra 3. 3. 34, where the same text is expounded :-- “तं पिबन्ताविलत्र तु जीवे पिबलयश्नायाद्यतीतः want साह च्याच्छकनिन्यायेन पिबतीव्युपचर्यते' ॥ On the former of these two passages, the commentator Gopila Yatindra says:—«gteqaafa । यथा लोके sao गच्छन्तीति प्रयोगे सपरिवारे uft गच्छति sasfaage छ्तिशब्दो ana एकसमूह वाहिष्वे- नैवे पिबदपिबस्समुदाये पिबतिव्॑तैत gat ॥ ` I have met with the maxim in the Kuvalaydnanda also under the figure gga, and in Anandagiri on Brahmasttrabhdshya 1. 2. 11; 1. 4. 12. But the nydya is found long before S’ankara’s time, and perhaps originated with S’abara, in whose bhashya on Jaimini 1. 4. 28 we read (यथा simon गच्छन्तीव्येकेन छचचिणा सर्वे रक्ष्यन्ते"; and in Yantravdrtika 1. 4. 13 “ तस्मादेकदेशस्थेरपि विश्वेदेवैरपरुक्षितानां Sharam तस्प्र्यतयैव सर्वेषां नामधेयत्वम्‌” ॥ तमोदीपन्यायः ॥ The maxim of darkness and the lamp. This is found in the Veddntasiddhdntamuktdvali ( page 125) where we read :— 4 29 ५ तदयं तमोदीपन्यायः | तथाहि । अक्तानं ातुमिच्छे्यो मानेनात्यन्तमूढधीः | @ 9 7a aa: पदयेदीपेनोत्तमतेजसा?? ॥ ^ Hence the well-known illus५ tration of darkness and the lamp:—Thus that dullest of dullheads who would cognize Nescience by means of a pramana, would forsooth go looking for darkness with a brilliant lamp.” This verse is most probably based on that of Sures’vara in Taittiriyavdrtika 2. 1. 177 — ¢ प्रमाणोत्पन्नया teat atshrat द्रष्टुमिच्छति । \ दीपेनासौ swt पद्ेदहाकुक्षिगते तमः” ॥ दृग्धपटलन्यायः ॥ Tke maxim of the burnt cloth, When a piece of cloth, or a leaf, is thrown into the fire and consumed, its outline is still visible in the charred remains; and this the Vedantists use to illustrate the unreality and unsubstantiality of all phenomena. It occurs twice in Nrisivthasarasvati’s commentary on the Veddntasdra, namely on pages 55 and 66, as follows:—“agr सर्वै afag ब्रहमेतदाम्यभिदं सवैमितयादिश्रुतिवरात्सवैमहमिति गिरिनदी- ससुद्रात्मकै सर्वै जगस्स्वाभिन्नसचिदानन्दब्रह्मतवेनानु भूय. तस्य द्ग्धपटन्यायेन भ्पञ्चभानेऽप्यद्वैतं सच्चिदानन्दरक्षणं वस्तु भासत TAT” ॥ ( Page 55 ). “नन्वे तादृशस्य जीवन्युक्तस्य देहेन्द्रियादिभानमस्ति न targa दग्धपटन्यायेनेन्द्रजालनिमितसोधससुदरादिवच्च बाधितानुवृस्या मिभ्याव्वेन भानेऽपि परमाथेतया भानं नेत्याह अयमित्यादिना न पर्यतीत्यन्तेन? ॥ ( Page 66 ). दण्डापूपिकान्यायः ॥ The maxim of the stick and the cakes. Ifa number of cakes ( chapatis ) are attached to a stick, and the stick is carried off or eaten by mice, the inference is that the cakes have shared the same fate. The application of the maxim is obvious, In the Sdhityakawmudé ( xi. 8), and in the Kuvalaydnanda ( page 244 ), it is used to illustrate the figure अर्थापत्ति and कान्यार्थापान्ति respectively, : : 30 श्द्ण्डापूपिकयान्याथौगमोऽथौपत्तिरिष्यते ॥ ८ ॥ मूषिकेण दण्डो भक्षितश्रे- दिहस्थः पूपोऽपि तेन भक्षित इति न्यायो दण्डापूपिका | तयान्याथौगमोऽ्थान्तर- ॥ १ भरत्ययोऽथौपत्तिरित्यथैः” ॥ ( Sah. ) ““कैमु्येनार्थसंसिद्धिः काञ्याथौपत्तिरिप्यते*। स जितस्स्वन्मुखेनेन्ुः का वार्त सरसीरहाम्‌ ॥ अत्र स इत्यनेन पञ्चानि येन जितानीति विवक्षितं तथा च सोऽपि येन जितस्तेन पद्यानि जितानीति fg वक्तव्यमिति दण्डापूपिकान्यायेन पद्मजयरूपस्यार्थस्य संसिद्धिः काव्याथीपत्तिः | तान्रिकाभिमतार्थापत्तिव्यावसैनाय का- व्येति विशोषणम्‌ '” (Kur. ). See also Brihaddéranyakopanishad-bhdshyavirtika, page 909, verse 135.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 28

gloss_en: The maxim of a lamp on the threshold.
explanation: The maxim of a lamp on the threshold. A lamp so placed gives light both inside and outside the house, and is therefore used as an illustration of anything which fulfills a double purpose. S‘abara refers to such a lamp in his bhashya on Jaimini 12. 1. 3:—“ यथा प्रासादे कृतः प्रदीपः सन्निधानाद्राजमार्गेऽप्यु- पकरोति??. There is a similar expression, too, in Kuvalaydnanda, page 97. Another good example of the nyfya is found in the commentary on Saptapaddrtht, page 52. The text stands thus: —“ geqratuas कायद्रग्यमन्लयावयवि ॥ प्राराभाववत्कार्यम्‌ ॥ भोगायतनमन््यावयावि Aira” ॥ ० which MAdhava Sara8१81 remarks :-- ““हारीरलक्षणेऽन्त्यावयविपदन्ञानाय तछक्चणमाह द्भ्येति | यद्यप्युदेश्ानन्तरं क्षणस्य वक्तव्यत्वाच्छरीरलक्षणानन्तरं भोगादिवदन्ल्यावयवी रक्षयितुञ्ुचितस्तथाप्यत्र द्रव्यपद्ग्यवच्छे्यस्वेन प्राक्तत्वादेहरीप्रदीपन्यायेनो- भयत्रोपकायैतयात्रैव रक्षित इत्यदोषः” ॥ My friend Professor Cowell pointed out another instance of the use of the ny4ya in Anandagiri’s S’ankaravijaya xi, page 82. The maxim is akin to काकाक्षिगोरकन्याय and जामात्र्थं श्रपितस्य सूपदरतिथ्युपकारकस्वम्‌ ॥ * The author of Rasagangddhara finds fault with this definition, See page 487 of Durgaprasada’s edition, Bombay 1888, ; ^ 81
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 30

gloss_en: The maxim of ¢he lost horses and burnt chariot.
explanation: The maxim of ¢he lost horses and burnt chariot. This is based on the story of two men travelling in their respective chariots, and one of them losing his horses and the other having his chariot burnt, through the outbreak of a fire in the village in which they were putting up for the night. The horses that were left, were harnessed to the remaining chariot, and the two men pursued their journey together. Its teaching is—union for mutual advantage. That the story is very old is clear from the fact that the saying is quoted in the 16th vdrtika (^^ संप्रयोगो वा नष्टशवद्ग्धरथवत्‌??) to Pdnini 1. 1. 50, and again in S‘abara’s bhdshya 2.1.1. It appears also in Sures’vara’s Brihaddranyabhdshyavartika 2. 1. 38, which reads thus :—« नष्टाश्चद्ग्धरथवद्यायं चाश्रित्य भूमिपम्‌ । प्राह मानुषवित्ताब्यं देववित्तसमन्वितः? ॥ On which Anandagiri says : “अधिकारिणे frat वक्तु गाम्यशरदुपचक्रमे तदि योग्यं ब्राह्मणं दिध्वा किमिति राजानसुपेल ब्रवीति । cae नष्टेति | यथाहु्यक्तः संयो- गोऽधिकारार्थन हेतुना नष्टाश्वदग्धरथवदितिः ॥ Ramtirtha, too, quotes the maxim in his comment on the Vedintasdra ( page 93, line 8 ) :-- ५ नष्टाश्चद्ग्धरथन्यायेन ‘ कर्मणा पितृरोक › इति श्रुतिरूपपद्यते??) of which the following translation is found in he Pandit for May 1872:—*“The Vedic text ‘The world of progenitors is attained by works,’ can be explained according to the analogy of two men, of whom the horses of the one are lost and the chariot of the other burnt [ for the horses of the latter may be yoked to the car of the former, and they may travel together; and in like manner, constant and occasional works, though no special result has been recorded of them, may supply a cause for the attainment of the world of the progenitors, which requires some special works as a condition }” See also Tantravartika, pp. 15, 709, 832, and Bhdmaté, page 81,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 31

gloss_en: A young fawn cannot stand wp against a full-grown lion.
explanation: A young fawn cannot stand wp against a full-grown lion. न | = | ॐ | s found in the Akshapida chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 136 of Jivananda’s, and 119 of Bib. Ind. );— (नैतत्परीक्षाक्चममीक्ष्यते नहि कठोरकण्ठीरवस्य separa: प्रतिभटो wale”, which Prof. Cowell renders, “This pretented inference will no more stand examination than the young fawn can stand the attack of the full-grown lion.” Compare “'नाल्पीयसा महतोऽभिभवः संभवति, ॥ of S’dlikd, page 94, and 866 the nyéya “ate भवति arg: &.” in Second Handful. नहि खदिरगोचरे परक्ौ Tart दवैधीभावो भवति ॥ The 71480, tree is not cleft when the axe is applied to the Khadira tree. The saying is used to indicate that two objects are essentially distinct. I have met with it in three of VAcaspatimis‘ra’s works. It occurs in Bhdmati 2. 2. 28 ( page 438 ) in the following connection. “स्वरूपं वित्ञानस्यासत्याकारयुक्तं प्रमेयम्‌ । प्रमेयग्रकाशनं भ्रमाणwe, तत्प्रकाहनशक्तिः प्रमाणम्‌ | बाद्यवादिनोरपि वेभाषिकसौत्रान्तिकयोः काट्पनिक एव प्रमाणफलग्यवहारोऽभिमत gare “सत्यपि वाद्येऽथः इति । भिन्नाधिकरणव्वे हि प्रमाणफल्योस्तद्धावो न स्यात्‌ । नहि खदिरगोचरे परलौ पराश दैधीभावो भवति | तस्मादनयोरेकाधिकरण्यं वक्तव्यम्‌” ॥ Then in Yogabhdsyatikd i. 7 we read :—aq पुरूषवतीं बोधः कथं चित्तगताया a: फलम्‌ । नहि खदिरगो चरव्यापारेण परञ्चुना vert छिदा क्रियत इति” ॥ And very similarly in Nydyavdrtikatdtparyatika, page 67:— “ay प्रमाणफर्यो्भिन्नविषयत्वेन विप्रतिपत्या प्रमाणफक्मावायोगात्‌ | नदि पनसविषप्रेण परशुना खदिरे द्रैघीभावो भवति?” ॥ But he is not the only writer who makes use of it, for in Adyaitubrahmasiddhi, page 93, we read :--“'तयोर्भिन्नाधिकरणवृ्तिस्वे खदिरगोचरे परशौ Wet दधीभावो मवेत्‌, ॥ For earlier references to this nyaya, see the Superaddenda to the Third Handful. 33
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 31

gloss_en: The bride is not married for the destruction of the bridegroom.
explanation: The bride is not married for the destruction of the bridegroom. This is found in Brahmasdtrabhdshya 4, 1. 2, and in the Parnaprajna chapter of Sarvadars’anasangraha ( page 63 of Bib. Ind, and 75 of Jivananda’s edn. ), It occurs also in Jivanmuktiviveka, page 101 (line 4 from bottom ). In the Vachaspatyam and in the Laukikanydyasangraha, the maxim is given in the positive form, viz. ‘“‘avatara कन्यावरणम्‌” with the other as a possible variant. Téranétha explains it thus:—“‘fagemat gaat यत्र वरस्य घातः संभाव्यते aa तां नोद्हेदेवं विवक्षायामस्य safe: । तथा चानिष्टान्तरपातादिसतंभावनायामभीषटहेतुरपिं वस्तु न वरणीयमिव्येवं तत््यायतास्पर्यम्‌ | अयमेव न्यायः चिन्न हि वरघाताय कन्यासुद्राहयतीति न्यायतया yeaa” ॥ We have a reference to “poison-damsels” in Kathdsaritsdgara xix, 82, which reads thus:— “विदधे विषकन्याश्च सैन्ये पण्यविरासिनीः । प्रादिणोपुरुषांश्ैव निशासु च्छद्मघातिनः'” In a foot-note to his translation of the passage, Mr. Tawney says, “One of these poison-damsels is represented as having been employed against Chandragupta in the MudraRakshasa. Compare the xith tale in the Gesta Romanorum, where an Indian queen sends one to Alexander the Great, Aristotle frustrates the stratagem.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 33

gloss_en: The maxim of the washing off of mud.
explanation: The maxim of the washing off of mud. It is evidently deduced from Pajichatantra ii. 157, a verse intended to strengthen an argument against the possession of riches under any circumstances, and which reads thus:—“ ya यस्य वित्तेहा तस्यापि न शुभावहा | प्रक्षारनाद्धि पङ्कस्य दृूरादस्पश्चैनं वरम्‌” ॥ “Ifa man desires wealth for charitable purposes, even to him it will bring no good; for, better than the washing off of mud is the keeping away from it altogether.” The ny&ya is therefore the equivalent of our “Prevention is better than cure.” In his bhéshya on Brahmasitra 8. 2. 22. S’ankara twice quotes the second line of the above couplet; and, in commenting thereon, Anandagiri uses the maxim three times, It is again applied twice by him = 9 ‘ 84 । on Brahmasitrabhdshya 4. 1. 16. as follows:—“‘afyetarérarata 4 युण्यान्तरवद्िनायत्वात्पङ्कक्षालनन्यायापातादाररुश्चुणापि तानि नानुष्ठेयानि"?; “gaara कर्मणामचुष्ठानस्य विचयोत्पतत्य्थतया पङ्कमक्षालनन्यायान- ` वकाा्पूयै ज्ञानादनुष्टेयान्यभ्निहोत्रादीनीति सिद्धान्तप्रतिक्ां fan.” ` See also Bhdmati and Bhdshyaratnaprabhd on the same, and = Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 97, line 14. 1 पङ्कन्धन्यायः ॥ | The maxim of the lame man and the blind man. The conception is that of a lame man mounted on the shoulders of one = who is blind, so that the former is furnished with the power of locomotion and the latter with sight. It is intended to illu. strate mutual dependence for mutual advantage, as exemplified = in Sdnkhyakdrikd 21, the text of which, with Colebrooke’s = 1 translation, is as follows —“gegea gata acm तथा प्रधानस्य । agraagaants संयोगस्तत्कृतः सर्गः” “For the soul’s contempla- = tion of Nature, and for its abstraction, the union of both takes place, as of the halt and blind. By that union a creation is 1 framed.” Va&chaspati 2118519 ignores the illustration, but Pandit ; Téranatha has a helpful note onit. He 8958“ “उक्तार्थै दृष्टान्तः प्चन्धवदिति । यथा गतिशक्तिरितस्य पङ्ञोगतिसाधनाय गतिमतोऽन्धस्या- यक्षा, दृष्टिशक्तिरहितस्य चान्धस्य गतिदाक्तिसस्वेऽपि स्वाभीष्टदेशगमनं qe मन्तरेण न संभवति तथा च यथा स्वस्वका्यौय तयोरन्योन्यपेक्षा तथा क्रियारहितस्य पुरुषस्य सक्रियभ्रधानस्यायेश्षा दष्टिशक्तिरहितस्य च प्रधानस्य रय १ ष्टिदाक्तियुक्तपुरषस्यपेक्षेष्यत उभाभ्यामन्योन्यमपेक्ष्य स्वस्वकार्यं निष्पाद्यत इ्‌- zag: The above kdrikd is quoted on the last page of the = 4 Sankhya section of Sarvadars‘anasangraha, and is preceded by a very clear explanation of the maxim.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 33

gloss_en: The maxim of the moving of the bird-cage.
explanation: The maxim of the moving of the bird-cage. An illustration of the power of united effort. Ina discussion on prdna, under Veddntastitra 2, 4. 9, S’ankara introduces this maxim and 35 explains it thus:—“ag पञ्जरचारनन्ययेनेतद्धविष्यति | यथैकपञ्जरविन एकादशपक्षिणः sete प्रतिनियतव्यापाराः सन्तः संभूयेकं पञ्जरं चारयन्ति । एवमेकशरीरवतिन एकाद्दाप्राणाः प्रयेकं प्रतिनियतटृत्तयः सन्तः संभूयैकां प्राणाख्यां वृत्ति प्रतिरुप्स्यन्त इति" ॥ Dr. Thibaut translates the pas. sage as follows :—“ But, an objection may be raised, the thing may take place in the manner of the moving [of the? ] birdcage. Just as eleven birds shut up in one cage, may, although each makes & separate effort, move the cage by the combination of their efforts, so the eleven prinas which abide in one body may, although each has its own special function, by the combination of these functions, produce one common function called prana.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 34

gloss_en: The vigilance of the watchman after the house has been plundered by thieves.
explanation: The vigilance of the watchman after the house has been plundered by thieves. Equivalent to our proverb “ Shutting the stable door after the horse is gone.” It occurs in Khandanakhandakhddya, page 45 :— प्रयोजनानुपयुक्ते कारे तस्य स्वरूपतो- ऽवस्थानं पाटञ्चरलुण्ठिते वेदमनि यामिकजागरणदृत्तान्तमनुहरति"" ॥ पिण्याकयाचनाथ गतस्य खारिकाततैरुदातृत्वाभ्युपगमः ॥ He went to crave the leavings of the oil-seed, and had instead to agree to give 16 measwres of oil. Used of one completely worsted in argument. I render पिण्याक in accordance with its meaning in Pafichatantra iii. 99 (“Rade a पिण्याकात्‌"). The maxim is found in the Pirnaprajna section of the Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 63 of Bib. Ind., and 75 of Jivananda ):—“‘qise पिण्याकयाचनारथं गतस्य खारिकातैरदातृत्वाभ्युपगम इव", which Prof. Gough renders:—*‘ And thus it must be allowed that, in raising the objection, you have begged for a little oilcake, and have had to give us gallons of oil.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 35

gloss_en: The maxim of the grinding of that which is already ground.
explanation: The maxim of the grinding of that which is already ground. Fruitless reiteration, unproductive repetition, The oldest instance, known to me, of the employment of the nydya, is by १ S’abara on Jaimini 9. 2. 3:—“‘a हि लिग्धस्य Ged शक्यं कठ पिष्टस्य वा पेषणम्‌. It occurs again in 12, 2. 16, and in Tantravértika, — pp. 54 ४०१ 477. We find it likewise in S’ankara’s bhashya on = Kena-Upanishad 32:—“afy तावच्छरूतस्याथंस्य wa: कृतस्ततः पिष्टपेषणवत्पुनस्क्तोऽन्थकः wa: स्यात्‌, u, and in Sudars‘anfrya’s com. = । ment on Apastamba-Grihyasitra xiv.9 (“‘geat व्यक्ते at $ तिष्येण??), where he says “ इदमपि सीमन्तवस्रथम एव न तु प्रतिगर्भ १ पिष्टपेषणन्यायात्‌! Compare तुषकण्डनन्याय in Second Handful. : न = mete प्रदीपं प्रञ्चाल्य तमोनाशाय यतमानः ॥ ; Trying to remove the dimness of a lamp by lighting = another. Used of foolishly superfluous and misdirected effort, ¦ It occurs in Khandanakhandakhidya, page 294:—“‘edteqrar- | त्साध्यसिद्धौ भवन्त्या gags प्रवर्तमानस्य aad दोपोद्धावनं प्रदीपे ‘ adiq प्रज्वाल्य तमोनाशाय यतमानस्येव Ga:” ॥ Ina footnote, the editor saysi—“qdty प्रज्वाल्य प्रदीपे तमोनाशाय यतमानस्य Ya इवेत्यन्वयः.'” In Upades'asihasri xvii. 41 ( page 215 ), we read—‘‘a हि Carawtal यद्र दीपप्रकाशने | बोधस्यात्मस्वरूपत्वान्न वोधान्यस्तयेष्यते, and, in Sures'vara’s Brihaddranyakavdrtika 4. 3. 501:—‘taq = दीपः स्वमात्मानं स्वात्मनैवावभाखयन्‌ | cet दीपग्रकाद्चाथ a हि दीपान्तरा- | हतिः?" ॥ See also Nydyamanjart, page 625, on Nydyasd. — tra 5. 1, 10. ४ प्रधानमलनिबरैणन्यायः ॥ | The maxim of the destruction of the chief antagonist. The 4 principle that when the most formidable ललक, has been defeated, the less formidable are already virtully overcome. In the bhashya on Veddntasttra 1. 4. 28 (“ata सर्वे व्याख्याता sqreqran’) we read:—“‘aa: प्रधानमह्छनिबहणन्यायेनातिदिशति । एते- न प्रधानकारणवाद्प्रतिषेधन्यायकरापेन सर्वैऽण्वादिकारणवादा अपि प्रतिषिद्धi तया व्याख्याता वेदितव्याः ?› Here, there is undoubtedly a play on the word प्रधान; the Sankhya theory of the Pradhana being the chief antagonist met and overcome in the foregoing Stitras 4 37 and bhashya. The same expression appears again in the bhashya on Stra 2, 1. 12, and the maxim is found, too, in the Ramanuja chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 54 of Jivananda’s edition, and p. 45 of Bib. Ind. edition ),
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 35

gloss_en: The maxim of an eternal series of seed and shoot.
explanation: The maxim of an eternal series of seed and shoot. As the seed produces the shoot, so the latter in turn reproduces the former. Each therefore is a cause and an effect. The maxim is met with very frequently in the literature. We find it in Brahmasitrabhdshya 2. 1. 36 (on the eternity of the world) as follows: —“‘q च कर्मान्तरेण शरीरं संभवति । न च शरीरमन्तरेण कमै संभवतीतीतरेतराश्रयत्वप्रसङ्गः । अनादित्वे त॒ बीजाङ्करन्यायेनोपपत्तेनै कथिदोषो भवति? which is rendered thus by Dr. Thibaut:—* Without merit and demerit no body can enter into existence, and again, without a body merit and demerit cannot be formed ; so that— on the doctrine of the world having a beginning—we are led into a logical see-saw. The opposite doctrine, on the other hand, explains all matters in a manner analogous to the case of the seed and sprout, so that no difficulty remains.” It occurs again at the end of the bhdshya on 8. 2.9. Also in the Arhata section of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 31 of Bib. Ind., and 87 of Jivananda), in Rématirtha’s commentary on Veddnta8470 ( page 110 ), and in Pancapddikd, page 12, line 12.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 37

gloss_en: The maxim of the Brdhmans and the mendicants.
explanation: The maxim of the Brdhmans and the mendicants. In such a sentence as ब्राह्यणा भोजयितव्याः परिव्राजकाश्च the separate mention of the latter, who are really included in the former term, merely emphasizes their position as a special part of the general body. It is thus the exact parallel of the waérageara and of the aranafageara. It is used by S’ankara three times in his exposition of the Veddntasitras, namely under 1. 4. 16, 2. 8. 15, and 3. 1. 11; but I forbear to quote his words, for without a 2 lengthy portion of context they would be unintelligible It — appears also in Tantravértika, pages 423, 590. 3
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 37

gloss_en: The maxim of the Brahmans and Vasishtha, This is of the same type as ब्राह्यणपरिव्राजकन्याय, which see.
explanation: The maxim of the Brahmans and Vasishtha, This is of the same type as ब्राह्यणपरिव्राजकन्याय, which see. Satyavrata ई gives as a reference ‘ ‘geurd-qaaea,” ९. Patanjalion Pdnini — 1.3.3. But the reference isa pure delusion; and I thinkI ~ may safely say that the maxim is not to be found in any part = of the Mahdbhdshya. It would not be unreasonable, however, = to infer that the author of the Padamanjari had this nyaéyain view when he penned the following:—3Rarmt aearast श्राधान्यख्यापना्थं TAS यथा ब्राह्यणा आगता वसिष्ठोऽप्यागत इति"? ॥ 1 (The Pandit, vol. x, page 282). Compare तक्रकौण्डिन्यन्याय in Second Handful. |
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 38

gloss_en: The maxim of the Brdhman-ascetic.
explanation: The maxim of the Brdhman-ascetic. The श्रमण isa Buddhist = ascetic, and therefore not a Brahman,—but the expression ब्राह्मणश्रमण implies that though now a Buddhist he was formerly | a Brahman. The maxim is used by the authors of the Kdvya- = 1070148. ( page 68 ) and the Sdhityadarpana (article 257 ) 7 = exactly the same connexion. After giving an example of ‘sug- = gested meaning’ in the form of ‘semblance of contradiction’ = (विरोधाभास), the author of the latter work 8848.“ अच्रामित इत्यादावपिशब्दाभावाद्धिरोधाभासो saga: । व्यङ्गवस्याङकार्यत्वेऽपि ब्राह्मण- ` श्रमणन्यायादरुकारत्वसुपचर्यते ”, which Mr. Pramadadasa Mitra = renders thus:—“ Here, from the absence of the particle अपि after the words अमितं &९., the semblance of the ornament‘named = ‘contradiction’ is suggested. The suggested meaning, though । strictly what is ornamented, is figuratively spoken of here 8 = | the ornament, with reference to its being an ornament in ano1 ther condition [ 4. ¢ when it is expressed, not suggested ], just = ^ as we use the word Bréhman-mendicant, which, though it 4 | . 4 59 z etymologically means an absurdity, viz. a mendicant, or one not a Brahman, who is a Brahman, tropieally signifies one who was a Brihman,” 9
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 38

gloss_en: Although the garlic has been eaten the disease is not cured.
explanation: Although the garlic has been eaten the disease is not cured. This proverb is applied as follows by Nrisimhasarasvati in his comment on the opening verse of the Veddéntasdro:—‘ ननु भक्षितेऽपि रखने न शान्तो ज्याधिरिति न्यायेन प्रपञ्चस्याधिष्टानव्यतिरिक्ततया प्रतीयमानस्वात्कथमद्वैतसिद्धिरित्याशङ्कां तृणीकुरवन्नाह अखण्डमिति ” ॥ It is found also in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 87.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 39

gloss_en: The maxim of a beggar’s obtaining a firm footing [in a patron’s house].
explanation: The maxim of a beggar’s obtaining a firm footing [in a patron’s house]. Perhaps approaching our “Give him an inch and he will take an ell.” Taranatha explains it thus:—‘‘ यथा कश्चिद्धि्चुर्ययेष्टभोजनाच्छादनवासगृहादिलाभार्थं कस्यचिद्धनिनो गृहे aaa युगपत्सवौ- भीष्टालाभं मन्यमानः भ्रथमं धनिगृहे मे पादप्रसारणमस्तु पश्चादनेन परिचयसु- त्पाद्य सवंमभीषटं संपादयिष्यामीति धिया स्वल्पामपि भिक्षां बहुमन्यमानः पश्चाक्रमेण स्वाभीष्टं संपादयव्येवं यत्र विवक्षा तत्राख प्रवरत्तिः"" ॥ It occurs in the Bauddha chapter of Sarvadars’anasangraha ( page 14 of Bib. Ind. edn. ) as follows —“qrafieraragaaaat इत्थमचीकथन्भिश्चुपादग्रसारणन्यायेन क्षणभङ्गाद्यभिधानसुखेन स्थायित्वानुकूलवेदनीयत्वाजुगतसवैसत्यत्वश्रमग्यावतैनेन सर्वञ्ून्यतायामेव पर्यवसानम्‌” ॥ Here is Prof. Gough’s rendering:-—‘The Madhyamikas, excellently wise, explain as follows, namely that the doctrine of Buddha terminates in that of a total void ( universal baselessness or nihilism ) by a slow progression Like the intrusive steps छ of a mendicunt, through the position of a momentary flux, and through the ( gradual ) negation of the illusory assurances of pleasurable sensibility, of universality, and of reality.” I have met with one other example only, namely in Venkatanatha’s Tattvamuktdkaldpa, page 254:—“‘aeaqala ta fey40 परादभ्रसारणन्यायेन निरीश्वरवादावतारादतः कोधावक्यम्भावादीश्वरस्यापि दुःखा- „ वङ्यम्भावः'” ॥ 1/9 subject under discussion here is आगमिकेश्वरसिद्धि. For the cognate expression पादप्रस्ारिका see the Second Handful.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 39

gloss_en: The ङ्क्ष of a gem or charm &e.
explanation: The ङ्क्ष of a gem or charm &e. [ as an obstructer or exciter of fire &., by its presence or absence]. This obscure nydya is not in Raghundthavarma’s collection, but is defined as follows in the Vdcaspatyam, page 4166 :—“‘aftiaardtat वहदीहं भ्रति यथा स्वातच्रयेण प्रतिबन्धकत्वं रोकसिद्धे न च तत्र युक्त्यपेक्षा एवं कामिनीजिन्ञासाया अपि saad प्रति प्रतिबन्धकत्वमित्येवं यत्र पथक्‌ ग्रतिबन्धकत्वं तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ Iam much indebted to Mr. Arthur Venis for the following note elucidating the nydya:—* In their analysis of the notion of cause and effect many Indian writers distinguish between those cases in which the processes that intervene (avéntaravydpdra ) between the cause and its final product (kdrya ) are known by ordinary experience (lokasiddha ), and those other cases in which the intermediate stages are not thus known. Of the latter cases, while we know as a fact ( say these writers ) that, for example, a mani ora mantra will produce a certain effect or prevent it from coming into existence, we are quite unable to explain the rationale of the process in terms of ordinary experience. All that can be said in such cases is that the mani or the mantra has the power ( sakti ) to produce this or hinder that result. This postulate of a power transcending ordinary experience is the मणिसच्रादिन्याय, and its ~ proper application ( pravritti ) is to the class of causes thus ध roughly described. If I remember rightly, another, and to us westerns a more interesting, example of this nydya is the double fact of attention to something and attention away from something else. The lover, intent on discovering his mistress ( kémint-jijndsd ), is psychologically dead to all that does not 41 concern her. But how should his attention to her cause attention away from all the world beside? Here, says the Indian psychologist, analysis of the how can proceed no further. The : postulate of the मणिमन्न must be applied here; or, in other words, we can only say that attention to a thing has the power to cause (s‘akti) attention away from something else.” This question is discussed in Kusumdnjali i. 10, and Prof. Cowell’s translation of the karikd and Haridasa’s comment will be found helpful. I have met with the nyAya in the Sanks‘epas‘driraka iii, 87, 88, 91; in Tattvamuktdkaldpa iv. 108, and better still, in ए, 99; and in Vidvanmandana, page 59.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 40

gloss_en: J र The maxim of a frog's leap.
explanation: J र The maxim of a frog's leap. Used by grammarians and others to express the passing from one rule to another over intervening ones. I noted it in the following portions of the Mahdbhdshya :—1.1. 3 (vart. 2); 5.2. 4 (2); 6.1. 17 and 3. 49; and 7, 2.117. I have met with it also in Jayaratha’s commentary on Alankérasarvasva 20 (““विषयस्यापह्ववेऽपहतिः'), where he says :— “किचन मण्डूकड्कतिन्यायेनालुवर्तनस्याचुचितस्वाद्धान्तिमदनन्तरमपहुतिभन्धक्ृता रक्षिता उद्ेखश्चातिशयोक्तयनन्तरमिति ग्रन्थं विप्यासितवन्तः । न चेतत्‌" ॥ See, too, Bhdmati 1, 3. 39, and Ballantyne’s Aphorisms of the Nydya, ii. 80.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 41

gloss_en: The maxim of the central lamp.
explanation: The maxim of the central lamp. The idea is of a lamp in a central position shedding its light on all sides. It occurs in the Mundaka-bhdshyo 1. 1. 8 (ज्लौनको इ वै महाशाखोऽङ्गिरसं विधिवदुपसन्नः पप्रच्छ ) :--““लौनकाङ्गिरसोः संबन्धादवौग्विधिवद्िशेषणादुपसदनविधेः पूरवै- षामनियम इति गम्यते | मयौदाकरणाथे मध्यदीपिकान्याया्थे वा विशोषणम्‌ ॥ Also in R&matirtha on Veddntasdra (page 129 ) :--((मध्यप्रदीपन्वायेनोतरत्ापि जाग्रदासनेव्यत्र कोडात्रयपदं संबध्यते") which is thus ren42 dered by Prof. Gough ( in the Pandit for Feb. 1878, p. 212)-- = “The term triad of sheaths has a double connection [ with both the preceding and following clauses ], after the manner of a lamp placed in the middle of a door [ and throwing light both inwards and outwards.” The following from Vydyamanjart, page 212, further illustrates the nydya :—“‘g@ दधिघटीं द्ष्टुमानीतो गृहमेधिना । अपूपानपि तदेशान्प्रकाश्चयति दीपकः” ॥ Akin to this 8 मभ्यमणिन्याय, which is explained in the Pandit for Dec. 1867 as referring to the central ruby of a nose-ring which casts a lustre on the pearl on each side of it.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 41

gloss_en: To know the thing to be measured you must know the measure.
explanation: To know the thing to be measured you must know the measure.
