This is quoted in the opening part of the Akshapada section of Sarvadars'anasangraha:— मानाधीना मेयसिद्धिरिति न्यायेन प्रमाणस्य प्रथममुदेशञे तदनुसारेण रक्षणस्य कथनीयतया प्रथमोदष्टस्य भरमाणस्य प्रथम ` लक्षणं कथ्यते” ॥ Prof. Cowell's translation is as णठः = accordance with the principle that ‘to know the thing to be = measured you must first know the measure, proof (pramfna)is = first enunciated, and as this must be done by defining it, we = have first a definition of proof.” We find it too, in Tattvapradipikd (or Citsukhé) ii. 18, as follows: “मानाधीना मेयसिद्धिर्मानसिद्धिश्च रक्षणात्‌ । तचाध्यक्षादिमानेषु गीर्वाणिरपि दुर्भेणम्‌” ॥ Compare also the = following from *Sankshepas'drtraka (i. 487):—“ मानेन मेयावगतिश्च ` युक्ता धर्मस्य जाञ्याद्विधिनिष्ठकाण्डे । मेयेन मानावगतिस्तु युक्ता वेदान्तवाक्येष्वजड हि मेयम्‌” ॥ See, too, Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 86, and Sinkhyakdrikd 4 ( ‹‹ प्रमेयसिद्धिः प्रमाणाद्धि").
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 42

gloss_en: The maxim of the extraction of the interior spike of च “Its author, Sarvajndtmamuni, was a pupil of Sures’varAcirya.
explanation: The maxim of the extraction of the interior spike of च “Its author, Sarvajndtmamuni, was a pupil of Sures’varAcirya. See : Mr. K. B, Pathak’s valuable paper Bhartrihari and Kumdrila (1892), page 24. ; 48 Munja grass, The following verse from the 7८00484 (i. 42), with an extract from Ramakrishna’s comment thereon will fully explain the meaning and application of the saying:— “an सुज्ञादिषीकेवमात्मा युत्तया agga: | शरीरत्रितयाद्धीरे : परं ब्रहैव ` जायते ” ॥ “यथा येन प्रकारेण सुञ्ञदेतज्नामकान्तृणविश्ेषादिषीका गर्भस्थं कोमलं तृणे युक्तया बहिरावरकस्वेन स्थितानां स्थूरपनच्राणां विभजनलक्षणेनो- पायेन ayia एवमात्मापि ...शरीरत्रितयात्‌. . धीरैः. .ससुदतः पथक्‌ gaara परं ब्रह्मेव जायते" ॥ The illustration is a very ancient one, since it is employed in S’atapatha Brdhmana 4. 8. 8.16, and in Katha Upanisad vi.17, The latter instance is as follows:— fee स्वाच्छरीरास्रब्रहेन्मुञ्जादिवेषीकां धैर्येण.” For the former, see this nyfya in the Superaddenda to Third Handful. It is found also in Brihaddranyakavértika 4. 4, 1277,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 42

gloss_en: The maxim of borrowed ornaments.
explanation: The maxim of borrowed ornaments. Appearing in borrowed plumes. It is well illustrated by Naisadhacarita vii. 56:— “Ten सुखश्रीग्रतिबिम्बमेव जलाच्च तातान्मुकुराच्च मित्रात्‌ । अभ्यर्थ्य धत्तः ag पद्मचन्द्रौ विभूषणं याचितकं कदाचित्‌” ॥ On which Mallinatha comments thus:—“gifras argnasaagl aga याचितकमित्यमरः । अपमित्ययाचिताभ्यां कक्छनाविति कन्प्रत्ययः ( Pan. 4 4. 21 ) । विभूषणं कदाचिदभ्यथ्य धत्तो दधाते ag । एतदीयमेव सुहृन्धमनयोयौचितं मण्डनं न स्वाभाविकमित्युखेक्षा१. In the above form the nydya is found in Tarkikaraksd, page 46:--“(तेनायथार्थस्यापि यथार्थानुभवजनितत्वेन यथा- dearer इति याचितकमण्डनकमनीयमेव स्तेर्याथाध्यंम्‌.' As याचितमण्डन I have met with it in Khandanoddhdra, page 62, and in Kéwyapradipatikd of Vaidyanitha Tatsat, page 173,
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 43

gloss_en: This occurs in Abhinavagupta’s comment on Dhvanydloka iii, 16 (page 159) in the following sentence:—‘‘aq लोष्टप्रस्तारन्यायेनानन्तवैचिव्युक्तम्‌ ?' and in the first edition T said that it 44 could only mean the maxim of an expanse of clods of earth [ as in a roughly ploughed field].
explanation: This occurs in Abhinavagupta’s comment on Dhvanydloka iii, 16 (page 159) in the following sentence:—‘‘aq लोष्टप्रस्तारन्यायेनानन्तवैचिव्युक्तम्‌ ?' and in the first edition T said that it 44 could only mean the maxim of an expanse of clods of earth [ as in a roughly ploughed field]. Regarding however प्रस्तार as 9 mislection for प्रस्तर it would mean the maxim of a sione and a clod of earth, and would be synonymous with अरमलोष्टन्याय of the dictionaries. Mr. M. R. Telang (of the Bombay High . Court) has however kindly suggested the following, with which I was quite unfamiliar. He says:—“ लोष्ट means a pebble and प्रस्तार a table showing the varieties of metres &९, formed by different arrangements of the short and long syllables in prosody, music &. The Indians make use of this process to find out the number of varieties of any number of given things. So the meaning of the nydya can be better explained by the expression the maxim of the process of permutations and combinations. It may be asked what लोष्ट (a pebble) has to do with permutations and combinations. In finding out the number of varieties of any number of given things, a pebble is used for marking certain figures according to the Indian process with the help of प्रस्तार, @IsHE, नष्टोदिष्ट We. These processes are well known to any one conversant with books on Indian music or prosody. The following references to books on the subject will support my statement. Vide &aftavatet Chap. i verses 61 to 69 (pages 57-60 ); सगीतदर्पण Chap. i ver. 144 to 154.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 43

gloss_en: Better is a certain kdrshdpana than an wneertain nishka.
explanation: Better is a certain kdrshdpana than an wneertain nishka. This and the proverb immediately following are found in the second chapter of Vatsyayana’s Kdmasdtra (page 19 ), and are the equivalents of our saying “<A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush,”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 44

gloss_en: Better is a pigeon to-day than a peacock tomorrow.
explanation: Better is a pigeon to-day than a peacock tomorrow. See above. 45
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 44

gloss_en: Cutting off the tongue while*trying to get a fine plantain.
explanation: Cutting off the tongue while*trying to get a fine plantain. This is found in the Pirnaprajna section of Sarvadars’anasangraha (page 64 of Bib. Ind., and 75 of Jivénanda’s edn.) as follows;—“‘aq च परमेश्वराभेदतृष्णया विष्णोगुणोत्कर्षस्य खगतृष्णिकासमत्वाभिधानं विपुलकदलीफरलिप्सया जिह्ञच्छेदनमनुहरयेतादशविष्णुविद्धेषणा- दन्धतमसप्रवेशप्रसङ्गात्‌ ?› Prof. Gough renders it thus:—* Thus the statement of those (Advaita-vidins) in their thirst to be one with the Supreme Lord, that the supreme excellence of Vishnu is like a mirage, is as if they were to cut off their tongues in trying to get a fine plantain, since it results that through offending this supreme Vishnu they must enter into the hell of blind darkness.”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 45

gloss_en: The maxim of worms bred in poison (or, in manure ).
explanation: The maxim of worms bred in poison (or, in manure ). Apte’s Dictionary alone gives us this nyAya which he describes as follows:—“It is used to denote a state of things which though fatal to others, is not so to those who, being bred in it, are inured or naturalized to it.” In this case it might represent our “What is one man’s food is another man’s poison.” Apte gives no reference to a passage where the maxim is used, and I am indebted for one to that veritable ratndkara, Dr. Bohtlingk’s Indische Spriiche ( 6164 ).. It consists of a quotation from Vriddha-Canakhya, a work which appears to exist in MS. only. It runs thus:— विग्रास्मिन्नगरे महान्कथय कस्तारद्ुमाणां गणः को दाता रजको ददाति वसनं प्रातगरृहीरवा निशि । को दक्षः परदारवित्तदरणे सर्वोऽपि दक्षो जनः कस्माज्ीवसि हे सखे विषङृमिन्यायेन जीवाम्यहम्‌ ॥ The Doctor renders the last line by “Wozu lebst du, ० च 4 46 : Freund? Ich lebe nach Art des Mistkiifers( d. 1. Ich suche das = Beste heraus ).” If this is correct, the ny4ya must be expressive of “living in clover,” or, amidst “marrow and fatness!”
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 45

gloss_en: The maxim of the poisonous tree.
explanation: The maxim of the poisonous tree. This appears to be based on the second half of Kumédrasambhava ii. 55 ( or Paiicatantra 1 245), which runs thus:—faqgetst dacd az छे्तमलाम्प्रतम्‌. ?? “It would be improper to cut down even a poisonous tree, after cultivating it oneself.” This is used as a maxim by the author of Khandanakhandakhddya ( page 727 ) in the following sentence:—‘‘ एते सर्वे तकौः..अस्माभिरेव त्कपद्व्यामभिपिक्तास्ततो न प्रबन्धेन निरस्यन्ते ' विषदरक्षोऽपि संवध्यं स्वयं छेत्तमसाम्प्रतमिति? +» There is another capital instance of it in Upamitibhavaprapancd Kathi, page 715:—“‘er हा मयेदं नो चार्‌ कृतं य्सुतभत्सनम्‌ | विषब्रक्षोऽपि dat स्वयं छेततुमसम्प्रतम्‌ ” ॥ वीचीतरङ्गन्यायः ॥ The maxim of wave-undulation. This is used by the author of the Bhdéshdpariccheda ( verses 165, 166 ) to account for the production of sound. He says—‘‘ सर्वैः शब्दो नमोवृत्तिः ata wag गृह्यते | वीचीतरङ्गन्यायेन तदुत्पत्तिस्तु कीर्तिता | कदम्बकोरकन्यायादु- त्पत्तिः कस्यचिन्मते ? ॥ Almost the same words are used by Vedantin Mahadeva (latter part of 17th century ) in his comment on Sdnkhyasdira V. 103: किंतु शब्द्‌ एव वीचीतरङ्गन्यायेन कदम्बसुकुलन्या- येन वा श्रोत्रदेशं गतः श्रोत्रेण Tea. “But sound comes to the seat of hearing in the same manner as the undulating waves [of water ], or as the anthers of a [globulous] Kadamba-flower and is thus apprehended by the ear.” The translation, is Dr, R. Garbe’s, 47
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 46

gloss_en: The maxim of the request of the aged spinster.
explanation: The maxim of the request of the aged spinster. This 18 recorded in Mahdbhdsya 8. 2. 8 as 1011078 "अथवा बृद्धकुमारी- वाक्यवदिदं द्रष्टव्यम्‌ । तद्यथा । बृद्धकुमारीन्द्ेणोक्ता वरं sonata सा वरमवृणीत पुत्रा मे बडुक्षीरश्रतमोदनं कांस्यपात्रयां खुज्जीरन्निति | न च तावद्स्याः पतिर्भवति ऊतः त्राः कुतो गावः तो धान्यम्‌ | तत्नानयैकेन वाक्येन पतिः पुत्रा गावो धान्यमिति स्वं संगृहीतं मवति?” This would be applied to a sentence having a variety of meanings. In Zantravdrtika 2. 2. 2 (page 452) we meet with it 88 बृद्धकुमारीवरपरार्थन 5; and in the Pandit for December 1867 ( page 156) we find exactly the same kind of thing under the heading agaramnaeara:. This worthy was not only old but blind, and his request was ^“ स्वपौत्रं राजसिहासनस्थितमीक्षितुमिच्छामीति." वृद्धिमिष्टवतो मूलमपि ते नष्टम्‌ ॥ Wishing to grow, you have destroyed your root. This is Prof. Cowell’s rendering of the saying as it appears in the Sarvadars‘anasangraha ( page 27 Bib. Ind, p. 33 Jivananda ):— “aq च बरृद्धिमिष्टवतो qeatt ते नष्टं स्यादिति महत्कष्टमापन्नम्‌'* । In the Vdcaspatyam, however, we have the literal and more usual meaning of the nydya, namely “Whilst seeking to obtain interest, the creditor loses [ that and] the capital too.” “शृद्धिमिष्टवतो मूलमपि विनष्टमिति न्यायः । बृद्धिधनम्रयोगेऽधमणौस्प्राप्यांशAgen: | तामिष्टवत उत्तमर्णस्याधमणदौष्टया्यथा qe aaa यत्राभीष्टान्तरसेपादनाय प्रयतमानस्य |S नयाति तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" y See also Khandanakhandakhddya, p. 31; Pancadas’t, vii. 81; Vedéntakalpataru, page 321; and Syddvddamanjart, page 19. “An amusing illustration of this saying is found in Kuvalaydnanda, under the figure विषम. “ gevightsa किंचित्कमीरन्धवतो न केवरूमिष्टस्यानवािः किन्तु ततोऽनिष्टस्यापि प्रतिरुभश्चेत्तदपि विषमम्‌ । यथा भक्षय्रप्सया सपैपेदिकां दद्रा प्रविष्टस्य मूषकस्य न केवकं भक्ष्यालाभः किन्तु स्वरूप48 हानिरपीति ** ॥ Raghundtha has two other maxims of the same kind, namely, “ पुत्रलिप्सया देवं भजन्त्या भतोपि नष्टः", and “ चमे- तन्तौ महिषीं हन्ति” शरपुरुषीयन्यायः ॥ The maxim of the man and the arrow. Vardhamana explains this, as follows, in his comment on Ganaratnamahodadhi iii. 196:—“ aga faa: प्राकाराच्च पुरुष saa: स तेन हतः । ayer हारयुरुषीयम्‌ ९? ॥ An arrow is discharged from a bow, and at the same moment a man rises up from behind a wall and is killed by it. It illustrates, therefore, a purely accidental and unforeseen occurrence, and must be classed with the अज्ञाकृपाणीय) खस्वाटबिल्वीय, and others of a like nature. is)
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 47

gloss_en: The maxim of the pebble and the [man’s] emerging [from the water }.
explanation: The maxim of the pebble and the [man’s] emerging [from the water }. This, like that immediately preceding, is found in Vardhamana’s work, and on the same page. He explains it thus :-—“ शकरा च fazer पुरुषस्य चोन्मजनं तत्तुल्यं शकंरोन्मजनीयम्‌ '' ॥ At the moment that the pebble is thrown, a man who has been diving or swimming emerges from the water and is struck by it. This, too, therefore, belongs to the अजाङपाणीय and काकतारीय < category. ( ५ of o शिरर॑खेदेऽपि शतं न ददाति विंदातिपञ्चकं तु प्रयच्छतीति शाकटिकन्यायः ॥ The maxim of the carter who would be beheaded rather than pay a hundred, but will at once give five score! It 49 occurs in the Pfrnaprajna section of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 71 of Bib; Ind, and page 83 of Jivaénanda’s) as follows:— “ag प्रपञ्चस्य मिभ्यात्वमभ्युपेयते नासत्वमिति चेत्तदेतत्सोऽयं शिरद्छेदेऽपि ad न ददाति विंश्तिपञ्चकं त॒ प्रयच्छतीति राकटिकनरत्तान्तमनुहरेन्मिथ्यात्वा- स्वयोः पर्यायस्वादित्यरमतिग्रपञ्चेनः' ॥ “If you say that you accept the falsity of the universe, but not its unreality, you are simply acting like the carter who would lose his head rather than pay a hundred pieces of money, but at once gives five score! For falsity and unreality are synonymous. But enough of prolixity.” It is found also in Atmatattvaviveka ( page 81 ), from which Madhava probably took it. In Tattvamuktdkaldpa ii. 71 (page 244) it takes the form of ^^ शतमदित्सतः शातार्धद्वयदानम्‌' », and there is still another variety of it in Khandanoddhdra, page 74, namely «“हिरदछेदेऽपि काकणीं न ददाति पञ्चगण्डकांस्तु ददाति.” Compare with this the following from Nydyamanjari, page 432:—« अथो- च्यते न प्रव्यक्च आत्मा किंत्वपरोक्ष इति नेदमर्थान्तरवचनं शिशव एवं प्रतार्यन्ते न प्रामाणिकाः” ॥ att सर्पो देशान्तरे वैदः ॥ A snake in the head, and the doctor in another country ! This occurs, in Prikrit, in Karpiramanjart iv. ( page 100 ). It is found in somewhat similar form in Mudrdrdkshasa i. 21, also in Prékrit. The chhdyd is as follows. «aq घनं घनरटितं qt दयिता किमेतदापतितम्‌ | हिमवति दिव्यौषधयः रीष सः समाविष्टः” ॥ शकनलिकान्यायः ॥ The maxim of the parrot and the Nalikd-tree. lustrative of causeless fear. In Udyoga-Parva xevi. 42 we read “ काकुदीकं ga नाकमक्षिसंतजनं तथा? । ०१ which the commentator Nilakantha says :— ‘“sledtetengatsemamay: ।...येन छकनछिकान्यायेन अभयेऽपि waghtat हयरथादिपादेषु गाढं ्टिष्यन्ति तच्छुक7 50 मोहनं ara? ॥ Ihave met with it also in a MS. (No. 288 of 1882-83 in Deccan College, Poona ) of Narayana’s commentary on Gopdlottaratépantya-Upanishad 8 ( corresponding with 21 and 22 of Bib. Ind. edition ), where, expounding the words ८ यो हि वै कामेन कामान्कामयते We,” he says :— ^ वास्तवं SPAT बन्धमोक्चादिकमाव्मनो नास्ति किंतु स्वकामपरिकट्पितं छकनकलिकान्यायेन ` ॥ शृङ्खग्राहिकान्यायः ॥ The maxim of seizing oxen by their horns, That is, by way of specification, and not in the sense of our proverb ‘ Taking the bull by the horns! This is very clearly put in Ramakrishna’s commentary on S/ankarfinanda’s Atmapuréna iv. 561-2. The text runs thus:—“ gre चात्र saa ससपरृत्ति ऊुरते द्विधा | विधानेन निषेधेन रोकदष्टिसमाश्रयात्‌ ॥ ५६१ ॥ श्रङ्गम्ाहिकया यद्धि बोधयेत्तद्विधायकम्‌ | यथा रोके करे तेऽस्ति फलमिल्यादिभाषणम्‌ ॥ ६२ Uw” On the latter verse, Ramakrishna says :-—“aq विधायकशाखस्य लक्चणं रोकिकञुदाहरणं चाह wate । खङ्गस्य get यस्यं क्रियायां सा By ाहिका | संज्ञायामिति ण्वुल्‌ । ( Pan. 3. 8. 108-9 ) । यथा गोव्रजे का मदीया मौरिति गोपः पृष्टः खङ्गं गृहीत्वा गां प्रददोयेत्तथाबोधकं शाखं विधायकसुच्यते ” So too, in Sures'vara’s vartika on Brihadaranyakopanishadbhdshya 1. 4. 866:—‘‘ च्ङ्गमराहिकया शरुत्या बरह्यतापोदिता स्फुटम्‌ ?›› on which Anandagiri says :—‘‘ यथा गोमण्डलस्थां गां खङ्गं ग्रहीत्वा विदोषतो दशेयत्येषा agate &e.” The same sense is attached to the maxim in Nilakantha on Udyoga-Parva xiv. 9 (‘ (मदोऽष्टाददादोषः स स्यासपुरा योऽप्रकीर्तितः?) where we 2680: -* अप्रकीर्तित इति दमविरो- धिन एव प्रातिकूल्यादयो मददोषत्वेन सूचिता अपि छ॒ङ्गमाहिकया प्रकषण विधिञुखेन न siter इत्यथैः” A fourth instance of the employment of this maxim in the same sense is
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 48

gloss_en: The maxim of the hawk and the pigeon, Vardhamana, in Ganaratnamahodadhi iii, 195, explains it in the following way -—“ इयेनकपोतयोरिव इयेनकपोतीयो दुर्योगः । यथा कपोतोऽतकिंतमागतेन इयेनेन गृहीतस्तथाकस्मिको यो दुर्योगः स caged” ॥ This must be classed therefore with कारपुरुषीय and others of a similar kind.
explanation: The maxim of the hawk and the pigeon, Vardhamana, in Ganaratnamahodadhi iii, 195, explains it in the following way -—“ इयेनकपोतयोरिव इयेनकपोतीयो दुर्योगः । यथा कपोतोऽतकिंतमागतेन इयेनेन गृहीतस्तथाकस्मिको यो दुर्योगः स caged” ॥ This must be classed therefore with कारपुरुषीय and others of a similar kind. We have a इयेनकपोतीयसुपाख्यानं given in the table of contents which forms the opening part of the Mahdbhdrata, and the story will be found in Vanaparva exevi. There is another in chapter exxxi. :
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 51

gloss_en: The maxim of the mother-in-law who said, ‘Be off’ This quaint illustration appears in Sures’vara’s Naishkarmyasiddhi 52 i.
explanation: The maxim of the mother-in-law who said, ‘Be off’ This quaint illustration appears in Sures’vara’s Naishkarmyasiddhi 52 i. 28, as follows: “sngurargviaa श्वश्निर्गच्छोक्तिवद्धवतो निष्प्रयोजनः arg? ॥ “And since you now express agreement with that which we also acknowledge, your protracted discussion was as unreasonable as was the mother-in-law’s saying [ to the mendicant ] ‘Be off’’ The commentator, Jnanottama, explains this in the following manner :—‘‘fyaraza माणवकाय भिक्षां भरत्याचक्षाणामात्मनः स्युषां मत्स॑चित्वा aa: घुनस्तमाहूय समागते तस्मिन्नासि भिक्षा निर्गच्छेति तथैव प्रयाचे" “After abusing her daughter-inlaw for refusing to give alms to a wandering mendicant, the mother-in-law called him back, and, when he had come, said to him, ‘There are no alms, be off; thus refusing also herself!’
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 51

gloss_en: The maxim of a lion’s glance.
explanation: The maxim of a lion’s glance. This is based on a lion's habit of looking in front and behind, after killing its prey, to see if there is any rival to dispute possession! It is applied, says TArdndtha, where a word in a sentence is connected with what precedes and with that which follows it. Itis not, however, restricted to this. The expression occurs four times in the Taittiriya-Prdtis'ékhya, namely in ii. 51, iv. 4, xiii. 3and 15. Prof. Whitney remarks as follows on the first instance of its occurrence :—“ The ‘and’ of this rule [ वर्मवच्चैषु ], the commentator says, brings forward, on the principle of ‘ the lion’s look’ (a distant glance backward ), the already defined organs of production of the various mute series.” It is found also in Nilakantha’s comment on Vanaparva ccxxi. 1 ( गुरुभिर्नियमेजीतो भरतो नाम पावकः) :--““ सिहावलोकनन्यायेन शंयोः पौत्रमूजंपुत्रं भरतं स्तौति सा- Ga गुरुभिरिति? ॥ It was a favourite maxim of Vacaspatimis'ra’s, and I have met with it eight times in three of his writings. It occurs in Bhémati 2. 3.6 ( page 473 ); in Sankhyatativakaumudt, 7 ( page 36 ); and in Nydyavdrtika-tdtparyatihd, pages 97, 199, 230, 322, 403, 405. An example of a different kind is found in Hemachandra’s Paris ishtaparvan, i. 63:—‘‘fagraaieaन्यायेनारीढः FAIA । प्रलक्चानिव सोऽद्राक्ीत्तानमालान्सुतद्विषः ” ॥ + ४
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 52

gloss_en: The maxim of the needle and the boiler.
explanation: The maxim of the needle and the boiler. It is explained as follows in Molesworth’s Marathi dictionary :—“ A phrase used as an illustration upon the occasion of two matters of which the one is superlatively simple and easy, or altogether insignificant, and the other indefinitely greater, more difficult, or more im- < portant, arising at once to be done ; and of which it is intended to intimate that the trifling one should be despatched first.” It occurs in the opening part of chapter iv of Kdvyapradipa, anid again on page 70. Also in the commentary on Sdhityakawmudt iv. 1, where the same kérika is expounded. The following is from the Saptapaddrthi, page 21. The text runs thus:— “aguatst द्विविधः । प्रमाप्रमा च । अप्रमापि संशयो विपर्ययश्च । प्रमा भ्रयक्षमनुमितिश्च ” ॥ On which the commentator remarks :— gमानिरूप्यत्वात्परस्ताद्वि भक्तामप्यभ्रमां सूचौकटाहन्यायेन प्राग्विभजतेऽप्रमापी- ति?" ॥ There is another good example of the maxim, with a lucid translation by Dr. Ballantyne, in the introduction to Book ii of his Aphorisms of the Nydya.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 53

gloss_en: The maxim of the rice in the cooking-pot.
explanation: The maxim of the rice in the cooking-pot. “ In a cooking-pot all the grains being equally moistened by the heated water, when ` one grain is found to be well cooked the same may be inferred with regard to the other grains. So the maxim is used when the condition of the whole class is inferred from that of a part.” (Apte’s Sanskrit Dictionary). It is therefore equivalent to “Za uno disce omnes.’ Patanjali seems to have laid the foundation of the nydéya in the following words, in Mahdbhdsya 1. 4. 28 (vart. 15 ):—“* qaigt da: gare: स्थाल्या fazarna.’ Then we find it in Jaimini 7, 4 12, the stitra with a portion of S’abara’s bhasya being as follows :— “ लिङ्गस्य पू्वैवत्वाचोदनाशब्दसामान्यादेके- नापि निरूप्येत यथा स्थालीपुखाकेन ॥ ......-एतच्यायपूर्वकं लिङ्गमेकत्रापि दइयमानं तुल्यन्यायानां सर्वेषां धर्मेवत्तां ज्ञापयति । यथा स्थाल्यां तुख्यपाकानां पुराकानामेकञुपश्द्यान्येषामपि सिद्धतां जानाति. ॥ i २4 £ Other instances of its occurrence are Tantravartika 3. 5. 19; Vedintakalpataru, page 446; Kalpataruparimala, pages 115, 468, 667, 685; and Tuttvamuktékaldpa, 293. Of similar import is the following line from Hemachandra’s Parisistaparva vii, 94:--^सिक्थेनपि द्रोणपाकं जानन्ति हि मनीषिणः” ॥ स्थूणानिखननन्यायः ॥ The maxim of the driving in of a post. As a post is driven into the ground by repeated efforts, so a position is strengthened by the bringing forward of a succession of facts or arguments. It occurs three times in S’ankara’s bhashya on the Vedantasdtras, as follows:—“qaa जगजन्मादिहेतुत्वमीश्वरस्याक्षिप्यते स्थूणानिखननन्या- येन प्रति्ातस्या्थस्य etary” 2. 1. 34. ““आशक्षेपपूवंका हि परिहारो- क्तिर्विवक्षितेऽ्थं स्थूणानिखननन्यायेन eet बुद्धिुत्पादयति” 3. 3. 58. “aa प्रसाधितं तस्यैव तु स्थूगानिखननवत्फलद्वारेणाक्षेपसमाधाने क्रियेते sea” 8. 4. 2. But S’abara seems to have originated it in his bhashya on Jaimini 7, 2. 1.
source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 53

gloss_en: One's own body does not hinder one.
explanation: One's own body does not hinder one. It is found at the end of the Akshapada section of the Sarvadars‘anasangraha, as follows :--““न च स्वातच्यभङ्गः शङ्क नीयः स्वाङ्गं स्वव्यवधायकं न भवतीति न्यायेन sega तच्निवीहात्‌ ?' “ Nor need you object that this would interfere with God’s own independence [as He would thus seem to depend on others’ actions ], since there is the well-known saying, ‘One’s own body does not hinder one; nay rather it helps to carry out one’s aims.” This is Professor Cowell's translation. Other instances of its employment are Bhamaté 3. 4. 20 (page 682 ); Tdtparyatikd, pp. 72, 90; Tarkikaraksdtikd, page 50; Nydyamakarandatikd, pp. 201, 215; and Khandanoddhdra, pages 58, 62. 8 अः Dips SE Co op A aE aaa iinet caged ~र - Ps On Attn 5 NS gs San 93 त bigs ध ॐ ध | $ न TH la a 1. नः 8 त Scenes 8 18 द fe 2" | 4 gs oe oe + & ^ 144 ‘fae ध ५६ (द oth Ses. pee 1 ag a Bes sa oe ~ 0 eaten ५ Sipe न im algae Ss al Sag Sa १५8 १ द न : ; < : ea ae oa ४ je a अक ES BS ee ae इ Sa a ae ~ ag fo 1.
