source: Jacob, A Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Tukaram Javaji), 1907 (First Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 54

gloss_en: Better even a doubtful condition of things than a crushing defeat, This occurs in the Nydyavartikatatparyatika 5.
explanation: Better even a doubtful condition of things than a crushing defeat, This occurs in the Nydyavartikatatparyatika 5. 1. 43. (page 491):—“afe त्वस्य कदाचित्सम्यक्साधनवादिनोऽपि प्रतिमाक्षया- स्समाधानं न स्फुरति ततोऽलयन्तपराजयाद्वरं संशयोऽपीति न्यायेन समाधाना- भासेनापि प्रल्यवस्थेयमेवेल्यादायवानाह तेषां साध्वसाधुतायामिति ” ॥ On page 473 of the same, and in Nydyamanjari, page 620, it appears ४5 एकान्तपराजयाद्ररं सन्देहः ॥ 1४ is not in any of the lists of nyayas to which I have had access, but Raghunathavarman has two of the same purport, namely ^ मारणाय गृहीतोऽङ्च्छेदं raf” ( which see below ), and “ मरणाद्वरं व्याधिः ”; and, in Nydyamalavistura 6. 2. 7. Madhava gives us “श्रधानलोपाद्वरमङ्गरोपः' ॥ All of these seem akin to our “ Half a loaf is better than no bread.”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 2

gloss_en: The method of 501 attribution followed by its withdrawal.
explanation: The method of 501 attribution followed by its withdrawal. This nyaya belongs entirely to the Vedantists, but I follow Raghunatha in admitting it here. The two terms are explained as follows in the Veddntaséra:—‘Illusory attribution is the attributing to the real of that which is unreal; as a snake is imagined in a rope which is not a snake.” “The withdrawal is the assertion that the whole of the unreal, beginning with Ignorance, which is an illusory effect of the Real, is nothing but the Real; just as a snake, which is the illusory effect of a rope is nothing whatsoever but the rope.” This rendering is from my Manual of Hindu Pantheism, pages 44 and 83. On page 42, there is the following note which includes a quotation from page 209 of that valuable book A Rational Refutation of Hindu Philosophical Systems:— “12. IUusory attribution &e. ( adhyaropapavada ). In order to describe the pure abstraction Brahma, the teacher ॐ attributes to him, or superimposes on him, certain qualities which in reality do not belong to him, and then afterwards withdrawing them, teaches that the residuum is the undifferenced Absolute. When the Vedantins speak of the origin of the world, they do not believe its origin to be true. This mode of expression they call false imputation ( adhyaropa ). It consists in holding for true that which is false, in accommodation to the intelligence of the uninitiated. At a further stage of instruction, when the time has arrived for propounding the esoteric view, the false imputation is gainsaid, and this gainsaying is termed rescission ( apavdda ).” See also a long note on page 172 of the text of the Vedantasara, The verse in the Vivekaciddmani, there referred to, should be 140 instead of 170.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 2

gloss_en: The maxim of a looking-glass for a blind man.
explanation: The maxim of a looking-glass for a blind man. It is found in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 836, as follows:— ^ केवरं ज्ञातदासखरोऽपि स्वावस्थां यो न बुध्यते । तस्याकिञ्चित्करं ज्ञानमन्धस्येव सुद्षणः ” ॥ 86९ also S'esinantacarya on Nydyasiddhdntadipa, page 22, line 2. The Laukikanydyaratndkara gives the following example-—“ तदुक्तं वासिषटे। यस्य are स्वयं sat शासं तस्य करोति किम्‌. । रोचनाभ्यां विहीनस्य दर्पणः किं करिष्यति ` ॥ I have no doubt that the reference is to the Yogavdsishtha, but the verse is also found in the Hitopades’a (iii, 115). See, too, under अरण्यरोदनन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 3

gloss_en: One who leans on a blind man will fall with him at every step.
explanation: One who leans on a blind man will fall with him at every step. This is akin to the saying “If the blind lead the blind, both will fall into the ditch.” It oceurs in Bhimati ( page 20 ) 4 as follows:—“ योऽयमर्थप्रकाङञाः फं यसिन्र्थश्चात्मा च प्रथेते स किं जडः स्वयंप्रकारो वा । जडश्रेद्टिषयात्मानावपि जडाविति ary करं भ्रकारोताविशो- घादिति प्राप्तमान्ध्यमरोषस्य जगतः । तथा चाभाणकः | अन्धयेवान्धल्नस्य विनिपातः पदे Tz” ॥ Compare the following expression in Venkatanatha’s vritti on his Zattvamuktakalape iii. 50:—* इति चान्धस्य जात्यन्धयष्टिदानोपमं विदुः 7 ॥ Though not exactly parallel with the nyaya, the following verse of Jayanta’s ( page 120) will not be out of place here:— “ हस्तस्प्ौदिनान्धेन विषमे पथि धावता । अनुमानप्रधानेन विनिपातो न goa: ” ॥ अपराद्धेषोरिव WAHT कण्ठाडम्बरः ॥ Noisy boasting like that of an [ unskilful ] archer whose arrows always miss the mark, This simile occurs in the Atmatativavivela (page 49), but was no doubt borrowed from Magha ii, 27:— ‘ aeifeaarrer वाग्जालं वाग्मिनो gar | निमित्तादपराद्धेषोरधानुष्कस्थेव वद्गितम्‌ ` ॥ “The chatter of a talkative man who has no knowledge of affairs, is as useless as the swaggering of an archer whose arrows always miss the mark.”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 3

gloss_en: The simile of crying in the wilderness.
explanation: The simile of crying in the wilderness. Used to imply wasted effort. Molesworth defines it as “A term for unregarded or unavailing complaint or supplication. ” The following verse from Namisadhu’s comment on Rudrata’s Kavyalankara viii. 37 includes not only this nyaya but also Raghunatha’s maga, उषरनव्रषठिन्याय, श्वपुच्छो ज्नामनन्याय, बधिरकणैजपन्याय, and 5 probably his अन्धदर्षणन्यायः for Dr. Bihtlingk, who quotes the verse as from Pancatantra, gives ्रतोऽन्धसुखदपैणः as a variant for कृतान्धमुखमण्डना. अरण्यरुदितं कृतं शवदारीरसुद्र्ितं स्थले कमररोपणं सुचिरमूषरे वर्षितम्‌ | श्वपुच्छमवनामितं बधिरकर्णजापः कृतः कृतान्धमुखमण्डना यदबुधो जनः सेवितः ॥ See also Pancatantra 1. 393; Kirandvali page 5; and Kusumdnjali, vol. ii, page 176,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 4

gloss_en: He has the right who has the want, the power, and the wit.
explanation: He has the right who has the want, the power, and the wit. This nyaya is found in the Jaimini section of Sarvadars‘anasangraha as follows:—“spft समर्थो विद्वानधिक्रियत इति न्यायेन दद पूणैमासादिविषयावबोधमेवक्षमाणास्तत्ववोधे स्वाध्यायं विनियुञ्जते" ॥ Professor Cowell translated it thus:—‘According to the old rule ‘He has the right who has the want, the power, and the wit, those who are aiming to understand certain things, as the new and full-moon sacrifices, use their daily reading to learn the truth about them.” The saying is found in a more complete form in Vaiydsikanydyamalaé 1. 3.9, namely, “अर्थी समर्थो विद्वाव्शाच्चेणापयुदस्तोऽधि- क्रियते, ` which is itself a reproduction of the following passage in S'ankarabhdsya 1. 3. 25:—“are afatargaalt मनुष्यानेवा- भिकरोति शक्तत्वादर्थित्वादपयदस्तत्वादु पनयनादिदाखाचेति वर्णितमेतदधि- कारलक्षणे.› Dr. Thibaut renders it thus:—“The S’astra, although propounded without distinction (४, ५. although not itself specifying what class of beings is to proceed according to its precepts ), does in reality entitle men only ( to act according to its precepts); for men only (of the three higher castes ) are, firstly, capable ( of complying with the precepts of the S‘astra ); 6 are, secondly, desirous (of the results of actions enjoined by the S‘astra ); are, thirdly, not excluded by prohibitions; and are, fourthly, subject to the precepts about the Upanayana ceremony and soon. This point has been explained in the section treating of the definition of adhikara ( Piirva Mimanisa vi. 1).” For the last-mentioned, see under अधिकारन्याय in the third Handful. This question of afeq &९. will be found also in Sinkarabhisya 1. 1. 4 ( page 54 ); 1. 3, 26, 33, 84; and 2, 2. 10,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 5

gloss_en: The simile of the slaying of one half [of a body, whilst the other half is kept alive!].
explanation: The simile of the slaying of one half [of a body, whilst the other half is kept alive!]. Raghunathavarman defines it as follows—“ असंभवविवक्षायामर्धवेशसन्यायः । यथा ङक्रुटीमांसभोजनकामस्तत्सन्ततिकामश्च कश्चिद्यवनस्तद्भीवादिकं चित्वा भुक्त उदरं च सन्तानार्थं स्थापयतीति तस्यार्थः ॥ The nyaya is therefore expressive of absurdity, contradiction, or incongruity; and so, in some respects, resembles the अर्धजरतीयन्याय. The earliest example, known to me, of the use of the term isin Kumdrasambhava iv. 81, where Rati complains that, by destroying Kama, Fate had slain half of herself, The verse stands thus:— विधिना gaadaad ननु at कामवधे विसुञ्चता । अनपायिनि संश्रयद्रुमे aan पतनाय वरी ” ॥ Mallinatha points out that as the slaying of a part involves that of the whole, Rati here announces her own destruction also, as is clearly implied in the second half of the verse. Its employment here by Kalidasa, however, is in a literal sense, whilst the philosophical writers apply it figuratively. In the latter part of S’ankara’s bhasya on Brahmasiitra 8, 8. 18 we find the expression ^ न दयर्धवैदासं संभवति ?, and I have noted it in Tantravartika, pages 84, 89, 97 and 202, The first of the four passages is the 101107६: “अविरोधे aftad न मूरा- न्तरसंभवः | विरोधे त्वन्यमूत्वमिति स्यादरधवेासम्‌ `” ॥ In this passage, as well as in the other three, contradiction or inconsistency 7 is clearly implied. So, too, in a passage in Nydyakandalt, page 6, line 3; and in Khandanakhandakhadya, page 685. One more example will suffice, namely Brihadaranyavartika 1. 4 1276:--“ न चारधवेरासं युक्तं aerate विवक्षिते । संशयो हि तथा श्रोतुः स्यादनिश्ितवाक्यतः ॥ ~Anandagiri explains this in the manner stated above by Raghunatha, viz. “ कुक्कटादेरेको देशः प्रसवाय कल्पते पच्यते taenetaad तदिहायुक्तं नहि वस्तु बह्म चाब्रह्म च aT विवक्षितत्वाद्िरुदवस्यातथात्वादिलयर्थः ” ॥ thy) wort मत्तकारिन्या दृष्टा ag कामिता ॥ Failing to obtain a lovely woman, affection is seen [ to have been lavished ] on animals. This very stupid nyaya is expounded by Raghunatha 28 follows:—* यत्राधिकाथौलाभेऽल्पार्थे natet दोषायेति विवक्षायां amet मत्तकाशिन्या दष्टा fet कामितेति न्यायः | मत्तकारिनी खीविरोषः ? ॥ I have met with it only in the Atmatattvaviveka ( page 130) and in Anandabodhacarya’s Praménamala, page 2.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 6

gloss_en: The simile of a she-mule’s being in foal.
explanation: The simile of a she-mule’s being in foal. Raghunatha explains it thus:—“ नन्वज्ञानकार्यान्तःकरणवृत््यात्मकं A कथं स्वकारणी- wars स्यादुपजीम्यविरोधादिति चेदश्वतरीगभेन्यायादिल्यवेहि । बवायां गदैभादुतपन्नाश्चतरी तस्या गभों यथा तन्नाशाय भवति तथा भवतु ज्ञानमपि स्वहेत्वन्ञाननाद्रायेयनवद्यम्‌ "° ॥ The following verse, bearing on this subject, is found in Hitopades‘a, ii, 185, Pancatantra, ii, 32 and iv. 14:—“ ages तु यन्मित्रं पुनः सन्धातुमिच्छति । aagaa vata गभैमश्वतरी aa” ॥ As Dr. Peterson points out in his Note on the verse from Hitopades’a, the second line is found in Adiparva (Bombay edn.) cxx. 83 (not 75, as wrongly printed ), and in S’dntiparva cx. 30 (not 347 as stated ). In a footnote to Indische Spriiche 58, Dr. Bohtlingk quotes Nilakantha’s comment on the verse from S'dntiparva— 8 ५ अश्वतरी गदैभजाश्वा उद्रभेदेनैव waa इति प्रसिद्धम्‌.” Of like import are two other nyayas quoted by Raghunatha, namely कदली- mera and बृश्चिकीगर्मन्याय. As to the former of these, compare the following, Vanaparva coLxvul. 9 (Bombay edn. ):— “on च वेणुः कदली नलो वा फललयभावाय न भूतयेत्मनः। तथेव मां तैः परिरक्ष्यमाणामादास्यसे ककंटकीव गर्भम्‌ ? ॥ This verse is quoted by Johnson in his Notes on Hitopades'a 7. 147, and he adds, “In the Gulistan, the Persian poet Saadi declares that the young of the scorpion eats its way out through the mother’s entrails”; and in Vedintakalpataru, page 354, line 2, we are told “वृश्चिका दिमौतुरदरं निर्भिद्य gararaa.” Udayana (in Atmatattuaviveka, page 67, line 9) seems to assert the same thing of the crab:— “ कुलीरस्येव सम्रसूतयुक्त्यापलयेनैव प्रतिहतत्वात्‌.” अहिभुकरैवरतन्यायः ॥ The simile of the opiwm-eater and the fisherman, I haye not met with this in actual use in the literature, but include it on the authority of Raghunathavarman, whose interpretation of it, however, seems most improbable. The word अहि is said by him to mean “an intoxicating plant, known in the language of the West as Post” ( “ उन्मादकर ओषधिविदोषः पोस्तेति qrareभाषायाम्‌ ” ). This meaning of अहि is unknown to the lexicographers; but, in Bate’s Hindi dictionary, पोस्त is said to mean “the poppy-plant; an infusion of the poppy formerly much used as a slow poison;” whilst Fallon defines it as “Poppyhead or capsule; an intoxicating drug.” We must take अहि therefore in the sense of अहिफेन which is the original of the modern अफीम, opium. The story on which the maxim is said to be based is as follows:—“ अदहिभुक्रेवर्तन्यायस्तु तादात्म्याध्यास एव ज्ञेयः । श्रूयते हि रोके कश्चिदहिमुञ्चावमाररोह स च तत्र बहुजनससुदायं दष्टा केनचिन्मे विनिमयो न स्यादिति धिया ane रज्जं वद्ा तन्द्रां प्राप । कैवर्तश्नोपहासार्थं तत्पादात्तां मोचयित्वा स्वपादे बबन्ध । नावि पारं गतायामवरोहणसमयेऽदिभुक्सपादे GIA कैवतैपादे च तां दृष्ाहमयमयमहमिति wee निश्चित्यारे tad त्वमहमहं च त्वमिति तेन विवाद कृतवान्‌ ॥ This nonsense is meant to teach the identity of the individual with the one Self! 9 ~
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 7

gloss_en: That which at the beginning and the end has no [real ] existence, has none either during the intervening period.
explanation: That which at the beginning and the end has no [real ] existence, has none either during the intervening period. The Vedantists of S’ankara’s school hold that existence is of three kinds, namely, péramdrthika (true), of which Brahma is the sole representative,—vydvahdrika (practical), to which all phenomena belong,—and pratibhdsika (apparent ), which includes such things as a snake surmised in a rope, or nacre mistaken for silver. The second and third kind, therefore, have no real existence from the beginning to the end of their supposed existence. Raghunatha says regarding it:—“ नन्वादावन्ते च यन्नासि वर्तमानेऽपि तत्तथेति arena तुच्छमेव द्वैतं मन्यन्ते तेषां ब्ह्मबोधेन सविङ्यविद्या- बाधो न स्यात्तस्य ब्रह्मस्वरूपनिलयवोधमदि्ना सदैव बाधितत्वात्‌ ॥ He may have taken the nyaya, like so many others in his book, from the Yogavdsistha where it is found as the first line of 4. 45. 45; but its real source is Gaudapada’s karikas on the Mandikya Upanisad. It occurs twice there, namely in ii. 6 and tv, 31,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 9

gloss_en: Watering a mango-tree, and, at the sume time, satisfying the Manes with a libation.
explanation: Watering a mango-tree, and, at the sume time, satisfying the Manes with a libation. Bringing about two results by one operation. Its earliest occurrence is in the Mahdbhasya, where it appears twice. In 1. 1. 1 (page 14) it stands thus:— ^ कथं पुनरेकेन Tene sey । ङभ्यमिल्याह । कथम्‌ । द्विगता अपि हेतवो भवन्ति | तद्यथा । आच्राश्च सिक्ताः पितरश्च भ्रीणिता इति `? ॥ The second instance is in 8. 2. 3. The nyaya in its consolidated form is found in the following passage of the Nydyamanjari ( 5. 1. 89 ), page 634. “तदेवमनेन चतुविशतिजाल्युदाहरणग्रतिसमाधानोपदेशवत्मैना शब्दानित्यत्वसाधने परकीयसु- पारम्भजातमेवेग्रायमलखिलमपाछृतमाञ्रसेकपितृतपैणन्यायेन भवति भगवता सूत्रara” ॥ Ps is not in any of the dictionaries or lists of nyayas, 10
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 9

gloss_en: Questioned as to mango trees, he speaks of Koviddra trees, This is nyéya 223 of the second part of Raghunathavarman’s large work, the Laukikanydyaratnakara, and is applied by him as follows (page 419a of India office MS.
explanation: Questioned as to mango trees, he speaks of Koviddra trees, This is nyéya 223 of the second part of Raghunathavarman’s large work, the Laukikanydyaratnakara, and is applied by him as follows (page 419a of India office MS. 582 ):—“aar हि रोके भ्रकृष्टप्रकादाश्न्द्र इत्यत्र प्रकृष्टपदेनाप्रङृष्टखद्योतादेः प्रकारापदेनाग्रका- श्ात्मकान्धकारादेश्च व्यवच्छेदेन जिह्धासितश्वन्द्रातिपदिकमाव्रा्थैः अ्रतिपाद्यa) इतरथा ware: कोविदारानाचष्ट॒ इति न्यायेन वक्तुरजिक्तासितम्थ ग्रतिपादयतोऽश्रद्धेयवचनस्वभ्रसङ्गात्‌ ` ॥ 1४ is found in 2101217 1. 1. 22 (page 145 ):--“ यद्यप्याकाडयपदं sara तथापि यत्पृष्टं तदेव भरतिवक्तव्यम्‌ । न खल्वनुन्मत्त आम्रान्प्रष्टः कोविदारानाचष्टे ॥ In Vedantakalpatary 1. 4. 1 (page 201 )-“ जीवे पृष्टे तं gestae तव्यतिरि- क्तपरमात्मप्रतिवचनमाम्रप्रभ्रे कोविदारप्रतिवचनवदसङ्गतम्‌ '' ॥ There is also an excellent example in the Nydyavartikatatparyatika, page 187, line 16, and another on page 545 of the comment on Tativamuktakalapa. Its source, however, is Mahabhagya 1. 2. 45 (vart. 8 ):—“ अन्यद्धवान्परष्टोऽन्यदाचष्टे । ataege: कोविदारानाचष्टे आयुरधृतम्‌ ॥ Butter is life. This scarcely deserves a place amongst maxims, but I follow Raghunatha in admitting it. It is one of the stock illustrations of writers on Alankara, and is found in Namisadhu’s comment on Rudrata’s Kavydlankara vii. 83, as follows:— आयुतं नदी पुण्यं भयं चौरः सुखं प्रिया । वैरं चूतं गुरुक्तीनं श्रेयो बाह्मणपूजनम्‌ ॥ I have traced it, however, as far back as Tait,-Sa™hita 2. 3. 2. 2, and have met with it again in Mahabhasya 1. 1. 59 (vart. 6), and 6. 1. 32 (vart.6). For the last passage see ^ दधित्रुसं sett sag: in the Third Handful. Sures’vara too furnishes an excellent example of it in his large vdrtika 1. 5. 1848:—‘ परीक्ष्य ag यस्माछमते गोधनादिकम्‌ ag: स्यान्मानुषं विततं FUGA” ॥ 11
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 10

gloss_en: The illustration of one who is satisfied with sweetmeats in prospect.
explanation: The illustration of one who is satisfied with sweetmeats in prospect. Itis found in a verse quoted in Nyayakandali, page 130:— “आशामोदकतृक्षा ये ये चोपाजितमोदकाः। रसवीर्यविपाकादि तुल्यं तेषां भ्रसज्यते ” ॥ The same verse is quoted on page 87 of Khandanakhandakhédya, and is translated by Prof. Ganganatha Jha (in the new periodical, Indian Thought) as follows:—“But, says an objector, from your theory it would follow that those who enjoy merely imaginary sweets, and those who eat real sweets, would have exactly the same experiences of flavour, strength, nutritive effects, and so on. He, we reply, who flatters himself with the hope of this objection invalidating our view, truly himself feeds upon imaginary sweets ( geet बाधकत्वमाश्ामोदकायते ). In Nydyadipavali, p. 7, we read “जआद्ञामोदकोपाजितमोदकयोरस्स्येव wast Ragearay,”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 11

gloss_en: The illustration of the arrow-maker, Used of one wholly engrossed in his work, and unconscious of his surroundings It is based on the following verse of S’adntiparva, chapter 178:- ^ इषुकारो नरः कश्चिदिषावासक्तमानसः | समीपेनापि गच्छन्तं राजानं नावबुद्धary” Sankara makes use of it in his exposition of Veddntasitra 8.
explanation: The illustration of the arrow-maker, Used of one wholly engrossed in his work, and unconscious of his surroundings It is based on the following verse of S’adntiparva, chapter 178:- ^ इषुकारो नरः कश्चिदिषावासक्तमानसः | समीपेनापि गच्छन्तं राजानं नावबुद्धary” Sankara makes use of it in his exposition of Veddntasitra 8. 2. 10 [“सुग्धेऽ धैसंपत्तिः परिरेषात्‌.? ‘In the case of one in a swoon ( there is not entrance into either of the states of sleep &c. ), 80, by the only remaining alternative, there is a semientrance (into sound sleep and another state)], He says:— इषुकारन्यायेन सुग्धो भविष्यति । यथेषुकारो जाग्रदपीष्वासक्तमनस्तया नान्या- न्विषयानीक्षत एवं सुग्धो सुसलसंघातादिजनितदुःखानुभवव्यग्रमनस्तया जाम्रदपि नान्यान्विषयानीश्चत इति । न । अचेतयमानत्वात्‌?? ॥ Anandagiri 12 refers to the same nyaya in his comment on Sures'vara’s large Vartika 1. 5. 106 (page 816). See, too, Nydyamakarandaव्र, page 78. Compare with this the picture drawn by John Bunyan of “a man who could look no way but downwards, with a muck-rake in his hand. There stooda 180 one over his head with a celestial crown in his hand, and proffered him that crown for his muck-rake; but the man did neither look up nor regard, but raked to himself the straws, the small sticks, and the dust of the floor”.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 11

gloss_en: The simile of the gradual diminution of the speed of an arrow.
explanation: The simile of the gradual diminution of the speed of an arrow. It is found in Brahmasitrabhdsya 3. 3. 32:— ^ प्रवृत्तफरस्य कर्मारायस्य सुक्तेषोरिव वेगक्षयान्िव्र्तिः? y Then, in Brihadaranyavortika 1, 4. 1529 (page 736) we read as follows:— “ आरन्धफलूरोपैकदेतुत्वादेहसंस्थितेः | रागादिग्रययोद्धूतिरिषुच्रादिवेगवत्‌ "” ॥ “ The experience of passion and other mental conditions, owing to the continuance of the body caused by the remnant of fruetescent works, is like the [ diminishing ] speed of a [ potter's ] wheel or of an arrow.” Upon which Anandagiri remarks:— ^ इषुचक्रेति ॥ यथा भ्रव्रृत्तवेगसेष्वादेर्ेगक्षयादेव क्षयस्तथारब्धक्षयो भोगादेव | “भोगेन त्वितरे क्षपयित्वा संपद्यत › इति न्यायान्न ज्ञानादियर्थः" ॥ 11९ quotation is Veddntasutra 4. 1.19, In S’ankara’s most interesting exposition of sitra 4, 1, 15, we meet with the expression ऊुारुचक्रवत्‌ in the same connection.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 12

gloss_en: The idea of something higher is to be superimposed wpon something lower.
explanation: The idea of something higher is to be superimposed wpon something lower. This is Dr. Thibaut’s rendering of the nyaya as it occurs in Brahmasitrabhdsya 4.1. 5 (the sitra being बह्यदटिर्त्कषौत्‌ ):--“ एवं ma amt ब्रह्मद्टिरेवादिव्यादिषु स्यादिति । 13 कसमात्‌ | उत्कर्षात्‌ | एवसुत्कर्षेणादित्यादयो दष्टा भवन्ति । उत्कृष्टदष्ेतेष्वध्यासात्‌ | तथा च रौक्रिको न्यायोऽनुमतो भवति । उ्कृष्टदटिि ase सितव्येति Steer न्यायः । यथा राजद्टिः क्त्रि °? ॥ “Lo this we make the following reply. The contemplation on Brahman is exclusively to be superimposed on Aditya and so on, Why? ‘On account of exaltation’ For thus Aditya and so on are viewed in an exalted way, the contemplation of something higher than they being superimposed on them. Thereby we also comply with a secular rule, namely the one enjoining that the idea of something higher is to be superimposed upon something lower, as when we view—and speak of—the king’s charioteer as a king.” Vacaspatimis’ra, in his comment on the above in the Bhémati, changes the form of the expression to “ निक्ष्टटषटिनोत्कृष्ट इति रोकरिको न्यायः» and perhaps Ramanuja had this in mind when, in his very short comment on the sutra, he wrote “sad हि राजनि guek: प्रयवायकरी, za तु राजदष्टिरभ्युदयाय.” उत्लातदंष्टोरगन्यायः ॥ The simile of the snake whose fangs have been extracted. The illustration is used by Sures’vara in his vartika on Brihadivanyakopanishadbhashya 1. 4. 1746 (page 776 ):— “उल्ातदंष्रोरगवदविद्या कि करिष्यति | विद्यमानापि विध्वस्तती्रानर्थपरम्परा”॥ उपजीव्यविरोधस्यायुक्तत्वम्‌ ॥ It is wrong to quarrel with that on which one’s livelihood depends, It is found in Paribhashendus'ekhara 85, as follows:— “ संनिपातो द्वयोः संबन्धसतन्निमित्तो Bt संनिपातं यो विहन्ति तस्यानिमि- त्तम्‌ ॥ उपजीव्यविरोधस्यायुक्तत्वमिति amaze” Professor Kielhorn has rendered it thus:— सन्निपात ‘a combination ’ is the junction of two (things; that which is taught in ) a rule (the application of) which is caused by such (a combination ), does not 14 cause (the application of ) another (rule) which would destroy that combination. This ( Paribhasha ) is founded on the maxim that one must not be hostile to that to which one owes one’s existence,” There are references to the nyaya in the following works:— Khandanakhandakhddya, page 128; Vedédntakalpataru, pages 231, and 556 (especially the latter); Parimala, pages 10, 11, 12, 451; Nyayamakarandatika, page 149.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 12

gloss_en: The illustration of the camel and the stick, The equivalent, apparently, of “ Hoist with his own petard” (Hamlet, Act iii, Scene iv).
explanation: The illustration of the camel and the stick, The equivalent, apparently, of “ Hoist with his own petard” (Hamlet, Act iii, Scene iv). The following is Raghunatha’s exposition of it:— ^ स्वमते परेणोद्धाव्यमानानां दूषणानां तन्मते पातने उष्ख्गुडन्यायावतारः | यथेष्रेणोद्यमानेनैव wea are: करियते तथा तार्किकोस्थापितद्‌ षणैसतन्मतमेव वेदान्तिभि्निराक्रियते । तथाहि । अद्रैतवादे यद्धोगसांकरयादिरूपं दूषणं तैरूच्यते तदौपनिषदै्विभ्वनेकाव्मवादिनां तेषामेव मते पाल्यते ॥ 1४ occurs in the following passage of the Atmatattvaviveka (page 54, line 16 ):-^तन्नातीयस्य तु . बाह्यवद्विज्ञानस्यापि विवेचनमेवेति सख्संवेदनबाधि- ast विरुद्वधमौध्यासो a dears इत्युष्टरगुडकं सेवेदनेनैवास्य साधि- तत्वात्‌” ॥ There is another instance of it in Veddntakalpataru, page 118 (where it appears as the उष्टरलकुटन्याय ), and again in Nydyadipavali page 6, line 11.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 14

gloss_en: The simile of rain on a saline barren waste.
explanation: The simile of rain on a saline barren waste. Its application 28 similar to that of अरण्यरोदन, which see above. Hemacandra has a good example of it in his Parisis’ taparvan viii. 417:-— ^ कषायपक्षवरक्षेषु कृतघ्नेषु दुरात्मसु | एतेषु fend दानमूषरेष्वम्बुब्ष्टिवत्‌ *” ॥ LS In Anus‘ dsanaparve xe. line 4314, we read:—“ यथोषरे बीजसुकष न रोहे वा वक्षा ATTA TTT | एवं श्राद्धं gear चेह नासुत्र we carla” ॥
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 14

gloss_en: No one tries to accomplish in ८ round-about way a.
explanation: No one tries to accomplish in ८ round-about way a. thing which can be effected by direct means, This nyaya is the counterpart of अकरै चेन्मधु विन्देत Le, and is used twice by Vacaspatimis'ra in his Nydyavdrtikatatparyatika. On page 195, we read:—“ न च का्येणैव कारणमनुमीयतां जीवच्छररे किं व्यतिरे- किणा चरजुमारगैण सिध्यन्तं को नु वक्रेण साधयेदिति वाच्यम्‌ । कारणमात्रख ततः सिद्धेरिव्युक्तम्‌ "` ॥ Again, on page 203:—“ अन्वयग्यतिरेकिणि हेतौ सत्यपि वैधर्म्ये साधर्म्योदाहरणमेवोचितं तत्र तस्पूर्वकत्वादरधर्म्यप्रतीतेः Raa सिध्यतोऽ्थस्य वक्रेण साधनायोगात्‌ ` ॥ A still older example is found in S’alika, page 86:-- “ ऋलमार्गेणा्थसिद्धौ न वक्रमार्गमाश्रयेत.* See, also, Tarkabhasd, page 48, line 5. nS a 2
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 15

gloss_en: Bare assertion is no proof of the matter asserted, This is Professor Gough’s rendering of the saying as found in the Bauddha chapter of the Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 10 of Jivinanda’s edn.
explanation: Bare assertion is no proof of the matter asserted, This is Professor Gough’s rendering of the saying as found in the Bauddha chapter of the Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 10 of Jivinanda’s edn.):—“afe क्चितप्रामाण्यमनुमानस्य नाङ्गीकुयौत्तं प्रति ब्रूयादनुमानं प्रमाणं न भवतीलयेतावन्मात्रसुच्यते तत्र न किञ्चन साधनसुपन्यस्यत उपन्यस्यते वा। न प्रथमः | एकाकिनी afar हि प्रतिज्ञातं न साध्ये- दिति न्यायात्‌ ° ॥ The following is from the Laukikanydyasangraha:—* नन्वेकाकिनी प्रतिन्ता हि म्रतिक्ञातं न साधयेदिति न्यायेन श्रतिक्ञयार्थसिद्धयभावान्न्रैतं साधयितुं पार्यते । मेदवादिनो हि जीवेशाभेदमपि न क्षमन्तेऽन्यस्य का कथा | तद्धेदेऽपि किं मानमिति चेन्नाहमीश्वर इति ga क्षमिलवेहि ? ॥ The nyaya ^ नहि प्रतिक्ञामात्रेणाथेसिद्धिः"' is given in Raghunatha’s list as one of similar import. Compare Nyayavartika, page 345, line 11:—“q च प्रतिज्ञा प्रतिज्ञां साधयति. 16
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 15

gloss_en: Whilst avoiding one kind of fallacy, another kind appears! This is explained by Raghunatha as follows:—* gq बौद्धाधिकारे उदयनाचार्योक्तिः | यथांुराद्यककैकं शारीर्यजन्यत्वादिलयत्र शरी- रीतिविरेषणेन स्वरूपासिद्ध परिहरतो बौद्धस्य व्याप्यत्वासिद्धिरापद्यते ” ॥ The work here entitled Bauddhadhikara is styled Bauddhadhikkara in Hall's Index ( pp.
explanation: Whilst avoiding one kind of fallacy, another kind appears! This is explained by Raghunatha as follows:—* gq बौद्धाधिकारे उदयनाचार्योक्तिः | यथांुराद्यककैकं शारीर्यजन्यत्वादिलयत्र शरी- रीतिविरेषणेन स्वरूपासिद्ध परिहरतो बौद्धस्य व्याप्यत्वासिद्धिरापद्यते ” ॥ The work here entitled Bauddhadhikara is styled Bauddhadhikkara in Hall's Index ( pp. 81, 82). It is more generally known as Atmatattvaviveka; and the passage in question is found on page 108, as follows:—“ अस्तु ate सत्प्रतिपक्षत्वं शारीराजन्यत्वादिति चेन्न असमर्थविशेषणत्वेनासिद्ध भेदस्यातुल्यवरत्वात्‌ । असिद्धिपरिहा- रेण fart समथैमिति चेन्न एकामसिद्धि परिहरतो द्वितीयापत्तेः 2 ॥ The same passage is clearly referred to in Citsukhi 1. 24 (Pandit, volv. page 110 ):--क्षिल्यादिकमकतैकं शरीयैजन्यत्वादाकाावदित्यादाविव व्याप्यत्वासिद्धिरिति चेन्मैवम्‌ | cares साध्ये तदेकदेशस्याजन्यत्वसेवोपाधि- तया विशिष्टस्य व्याप्यत्वासिद्धेः । उक्तं हि । एकामसिद्धि परिहरतो द्वितीया- सिद्धिरापद्यत fa”? ॥ So, too, Venkatanatha in the comment on his Lattvamuktakalapa iii, 22 (p. 289), and again in his Nydyasiddhanjana, page 100. For a clear and concise definition of the three terms आश्रयासिद्ध, स्वरूपासिद्ध and व्याप्यता- सिद्ध, see Apte’s Practical Sanskrit Dictionary, s. v. असिद्ध.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 16

gloss_en: The illustration of a cow [tied] in an enclosure.
explanation: The illustration of a cow [tied] in an enclosure. This occurs in Khandanakhandakhadya, page 632:—* यत्त awa Te टकगवोदाहरणमनुहरति यतः सत्ताप्यसुना दूषणेनास्ाभिः खण्डनीया. ` The commentator explains as follows:—“ यथा कटके बद्धा गौर्बन्धनरज्जमादाय विद्रवति विद्रवद्धिरश्वैः ae तथासुना खण्डनेन विद्रवता भेदेन सत्तापि विद्रविष्यतीलयर्थः । यद्वा यथा कटके गौरपसार्यमाणापि पुनस्तथेवा- याति तथा aga: खण्डितापि सत्ता पुनरूदाहरणत्वेनायाती्यथैः"” ॥ I may add that the long passage beginning with the words “करं भेदज्ञानं नास्ति, on page 632, down to the words “erat दिक्‌ on page 687, is taken verbatim from Udayana’s Atmatativaviveka, pages 70 to 72, It includes another, and probably the earliest, example of the use of the nyaya “चौरापराधेन माण्डव्यनिग्रहः??, for which, see the first Handful of maxims. 17
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 16

gloss_en: The simile of the golden ornament on the neck.
explanation: The simile of the golden ornament on the neck. A person is supposed to have a golden ornament round the neck and yet to be unaware of it until some one points it out; a kind of illustration greatly in vogue amongst Vedantists, who tell us that although we are already Brahma, and free, we are not aware of the fact until instructed by a competent teacher! For the translation of a passage of the Vedantaparibhdsha bearing on this, see pages 130 and 131 of my Manual of Hindu Pantheism. The above nyaya is found at the top of page 130 of Atmatattvaviveka,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 17

gloss_en: The simile of the fruit of the plantain tree.
explanation: The simile of the fruit of the plantain tree. For explanation of this see अश्वतरीगर्भन्याय. Another instance of it is found in Naiskarmyasiddhi iv. 14:-“बुद्धिमेवापद्द्वाति कदरीं acne यथा. See, also, Bodhicaryavatdra i. 12.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 17

gloss_en: The simile of the woodapple on the [ open palm of the ] hand.
explanation: The simile of the woodapple on the [ open palm of the ] hand. Said of something unmistakably clear—‘“as plain as a pike-staff”! It occurs in Sures'vara’s large Vartika 2. 1. 95:— “‘eaisqiaaa at करबिन्यस्तबिल्ववत्‌ । sa विज्ञापयिष्यामि wart क्र्वविद्धवेत्‌ `? ॥ Again in 2. 5. 136 of the same:—“fa:aratafraearरस्तदेतदिति साम्प्रतम्‌ । उक्लयाविष्करियते साक्षात्करविन्यस्तविल्ववत्‌ ` ॥ A third instance is found in 4. 3. 1334, and there is another in the vartika, on the Zaittiriyopanishadbhdshya, page 200. Of exactly the same import is the करस्थामलकन्यायः, for which see the former Vartika 3. 1, 14, 2 18
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 17

gloss_en: Abundance of labor produces abundance of fruit; from great pains come great gains.
explanation: Abundance of labor produces abundance of fruit; from great pains come great gains. It occurs in the following passage of Vidyaranya’s Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 247:-्रह्मोपासनानां सवैषामपि यदयेकरूपं फरुं तदा गुणोपचयापचयाभ्यासु- पासनोपचयापचयौ व्यर्थो स्याताम्‌ । तथा च कर्मभूयस्त्वात्फरभूयस्त्वमिति न्यायविरोधः'” ॥ Compare S‘abara’s ““अङ्गभूयस्त्वे फल भूयस्त्वम्‌ ` in 10, 6. 62. and 11, 1. 15. It is quoted in Parimala, page 600,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 18

gloss_en: The simile of the man who eats from a brazen vessel.
explanation: The simile of the man who eats from a brazen vessel. Raghunatha explains it thus—‘ मया fret गुरुदरिष्टं भोक्तव्यं कांस्यः पात्रे च भोक्तव्यमिति नियमवतो विनेयस्य नियमाभङ्गाय gett कांस्यपात्रे अक्त इति | यद्यप्ययं श्ास्नीयस्तथाप्येतद्यवहारस्य लोकिकत्वातसुन्दोपसुन्दन्यायवल्लोकरिकेषु परिगणितः 7 ॥ The nyaya is taken from Jaimini’s stitra 12. 2, 34, where S‘abara interprets it as {0110 78:--“कांस्यभोजिवत्‌ | तद्यथा । शिष्यस्य कांस्यपात्रभोजित्वनियम उपाध्यायस्यानियमः | यदि तयोरेकसिन्पात्रे भोजनमा- पदयतेऽमुख्यस्यापि शिष्यस्य धमो नियम्येत । मा भूद्ध्मरोप इति ? ॥ The principle here laid down is that of some one’s doing something which he is not bound to do, in order that he may not hinder another who is required to do it. The converse, that is, of a man’s abstaining from doing something, possibly harmless in his case, lest another should do the same and suffer harm, «Tf meat make my brother to offend, I will eat no flesh while the world standeth, lest I make my brother to offend.” Examples of the nyaya are found in Tantravartika, pages 393, 577, and 907; in Vidhirasdyana, page 50; in Bhimati, page 478; in Veddntakalpataru, pages 314, 425, 502, 517; and in Parimala, pages 462, 572, 666. 19
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 18

gloss_en: The simile of the ९०९४8 and owl’s night-time.
explanation: The simile of the ९०९४8 and owl’s night-time. What is day to the former is night to the latter, and vice versa. This characteristic of the owl is often referred to by the poets, as, for instance, in Bhartrihari’s Nitis‘ataka 93:— “पत्रं नैव यदा करीरविदपे दोपो वसन्तस्य किं नोदकोऽप्यवलोकते यदि दिवः सूर्यस्य किं दूषणम्‌ | The nyaya 18 found is Sures'vara’s large vartika 1. 4. 313:— “काकोल्कनिशेवायं संसारोऽन्ञात्मवेदिनोः । या निया सर्व॑भूतानामिलयवोचत्स्रयं हारिः" ॥ Anandagiri comments on this as {0110 फ8:--“काकेति | at काका- दीनां प्रसिद्धा निद्या तस्यासुलको जागर्तीति तदृष्टया सापरुप्यते | यदा च काका- दयो जाग्रति तदा नक्तंदो निरेति काकादिदृष्टया सापहूयते यथेलय्थः | एवमन्ञस्थायं मात्रादिः संसारो यदा विवर्तेते तदा तदष्टया तच्वस्यासत्कल्पना । यदा विदुषस्तत्वानुभवस्तदा agen मात्रादेरसत्वमिति '” ॥ The quotation in the second line of Sures'vara’s verse is from @itd ii-69 which reads thus:—“qr fat सवभूतानां तस्थां जागर्ति संयमी । यस्यां sate भूतानि सा निशा पर्यतो gat: ॥ 1" Naisharmyasiddhe ॐ. 111 the nyaya is quoted as उल्टूकनिरावत्‌. The passage stands thus:— “अनुदितानस्तमितकूटस्थबोधमात्रस्वाभाव्यादात्मनो दुःसम्भाव्योऽविद्यासद्धाव इति चेन्न | अविद्याप्रसिच्यैव तत्सद्धावसिद्धेरुलटूकनिशावदित्यत इदसुच्यते | अहो धाष्ट्थंमविद्याया न कश्चिदतिवर्तते | श्रमाणं वस्त्वनादत्य परमात्मेव तिष्ठति * ॥ | कारणगुणप्रकरमन्यायः ॥ The principle of the reproduction, in the effect, of certain । qualities, in the proportion in which they exist in the produc. 20 ing cause. In the Veddntasdra, section 12, we read:—“qatat सत्वरजस्तमांसि कारणगुणग्रक्रमेण तेष्वाकाशादिपूत्पद्यन्ते? ॥ 0" which, the commentator Nrisimhasarasvati remarks:—“gerfigeaaerat सत्वादयसखयोऽपि गुणास्तारतम्येन कारणगुणप्रक्रमन्यायेन तेष्वाकादादिषु पञ्चभूतेषूत्तरोत्तराधिक्येन जायन्त gad: ॥ For full notes on कारणयुण, see page 176 of the Vedantasara referred to above.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 19

gloss_en: The illustration of the redness of cotton [produced by smearing the cotton-seeds with red lac].
explanation: The illustration of the redness of cotton [produced by smearing the cotton-seeds with red lac]. One of the stock illustrations of the Buddhist when seeking to establish the doctrine that all existence is momentary ( क्षणभङ्गवाद्‌ ). For the examination and refutation of the tenet, see S’ankara on Brahmasiitras 2. 1. 18, 2. 2. 20 &c.; and the opening part of the Arhata chapter of Sarvadars‘anasangraha. The nyaya is contained in the following verse:— “यसिन्नेव हि सन्तान आहिता कमैवासना | फले तत्रैव Tala कार्पासे रक्तता यथाः” ॥ This is quoted in Syddvadamanjari, pages 155 and 193; in Manibhadra’s comment on karika 5 of Saddars'anasamvuceaya; in a slightly altered form, on page 1501 of Brihadéranyakavartika; in Nyd@yamanjari, page 443; in the vritti on Tattvamuktakalapa i. 29 ; and in the Arhata section of Sarvadars‘anasangraha, where Professor Cowell renders it:—“In whatever series of successive states the original impression of the action was produced, there verily accrues the result, just like the redness produced in cotton”. We find the कार्पासरागसंक्रान्तिदृष्टान्त in Vydyamanjart page 465, in the concluding portion of the author's क्षणभङ्गनिरास, and the following extract from the Atmatattvaviveka (page 102) explains the process:—“qur क्षीरावसेकादम्लत्वं परिहत माधुयैसुपादायानुवतैमानामरकी 21 कालान्तरेऽपि माधुरयसुन्मीखयति, खक्षारसावसेकाद्रा धवलिमानमपहाय THAT सुपादायानुवतंमाने कार्पासबीजं कुसुमेषु रक्तताम्‌.*› In the closing verses of the निरारम्बनवाद्‌ (Slokavartika, page 267) Kumarila deals with this Buddhist illustration in connection with a citron (बीजपूर ) instead of the cotton plant; and we meet with it again in 217014८7 1. 1. 4 ( page 95 ).
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 20

gloss_en: What has a seller of ginger to do with ships? Possibly the equivalent of “No cobbler beyond his last.
explanation: What has a seller of ginger to do with ships? Possibly the equivalent of “No cobbler beyond his last.” It occurs in the following passage of Atmatativaviveka, page 62, line 10:—efada हि तथा तथा ada यथा यथानुभाग्यतया व्यवहियते तत्तन्मायोपनीतोपा- धिभेदाच्चानुभूतिरपि भिन्नैव व्यवहारपथमवतरति गगनमिव स्वमरदष्टवटकटाहकोटरकुटीकोटिभः | तदास्तां तावत्‌ | किमाद्र॑कवणिजो वहित्रचिन्तयेति ॥ HATTA कुयौन्नखच्छेदयं न पण्डितः ॥ A wise man should not imagine that he can remove with a finger-nail that which can only be cut down with an ame A caution against under-rating the strength of an enemy. It occurs in Upanitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 1044:— “नेपेक्षणीयं देवेन तस्मादेतस्प्रयोजनम्‌ | 4 ऊटारच्छे्यतां कुयौन्नखच्छेद्यं न पण्डितः” ॥ Compare Udayana’s saying in Kivandvali, page 74:—“4 खलु waa area छिनत्ति."
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 21

gloss_en: Like a decoration without a wall [ to be decorated; or, like a 22 painting without a canvas].
explanation: Like a decoration without a wall [ to be decorated; or, like a 22 painting without a canvas]. An unreality, like a hare’s horn &c. It is found in the Nyayamanjari, page 103, in a disquisition on योगिप्रयक्चसाधनम्‌. “्रसङ्गसाधनं नाम नास्त्येव THAT: | तद्धि कुड्यं विना तत्र चित्रकर्मव रक्ष्यते ॥ नहि नभःऊसुमस्य सौरभासौरमविचारो युक्तः" ॥ A much older example is contained in Sénkhyakariha 41:— «चित्रं qua स्थाण्वादिभ्यो विना यथा ot तद्वष्टिना विशेषेन तिष्ठति निराश्रयं fear’ ॥ There is a similar thought in Aniruddha’s comment on Sankhyasatra iii, 12. He 8978: ““यद्यात्मना विना देहेऽहमिति भ्रलययस्तदा तदेहेऽहमिति प्रलयः स्यात्‌ । न चैवम्‌ । यथावरकेण विना न छाया भित्ति चिना न चित्रे तथात्रापि" ॥ See also शाता on Tarkikaraksd, page 111 and 176.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 21

gloss_en: The simile of milk and water.
explanation: The simile of milk and water. Used to illustrate the most intimate union of two or more things, The oldest example of it known to me is in Mahabhasya 1. 2. 32:--क्षीरोदके arom आमिश्रीभूतत्वान्न ज्ञायते fait कियदुदकं कस्मिन्नवकाो क्षीरं कस्मि- त्रवकादा उदकमिति.” Writers on Alankara employ it to exemplify the figure called Sankara (Commixture), in which there is a combination of other figures. It differs from Sansrishti (Collocation) which is compared to the union between rice and sesamum, which is less intimate and easily distinguishable. The author of the Alankdrasarvasva (page 192) says:— “अघुनेषां सर्वेषामरुंकाराणां संश्ेषससुत्थापितमलंकारद्रयसुच्यते | तत्र Sea: संयोगन्यायेन समवायन्यायेन च द्विविधः । संयोगन्यायो यत्र भेदस्थोच्कटतया स्थितिः | समवायन्यायो aa तसैवानुत्करत्वेनावस्थानम्‌ | तत्रोत्करत्वेन स्थितौ तिरूतण्डुलन्याय इतरत्र तु क्षीरनीरसादरयम्‌ | कमेगेतटुच्यते | एषां तिरुतण्डुखन्यायेन ford संखृष्टिः । ` `` क्षीरनीरन्यायेन तु संकरः” ॥ 23 Similarly too in Sarasvatikanthablarane ( page 262 ):— Fs “संखष्टिरिति विज्ञेया सवौरुंकारसंकरः | सा तु व्यक्ता तथाव्यक्ता व्यक्ताव्यक्तेति च त्रिधा ॥ तिरुतण्डुवब्यक्ता छायाददीवदेव च । अव्यक्ता क्षीरजल्वत्पांडुपानीयवच सा ॥ व्यक्ताव्यक्ता च संखषट्नरसिहवदिष्यते | चित्रवर्णवदन्यसिन्नानारंकारसंकरे "° ॥ It will be noticed that here there is mention of a third kind of combination which is likened to that of man and lion. The three kinds are noticed in Kuvalaydnanda, also (page 337), as follows:—* अथैतेषामलङ्काराणां यथासंभवं कचिन्मेखने लोकिकालंका- राणां मेलन इव चारत्वातिशयोपरुमान्नरसिहन्यायेन परथगरंकारावस्थितौ तfacia: क्रियते । तत्र तिरतण्डुलन्यायेन स्फुटावगम्यभेदालंकारमेरने eats: | नीरक्षीरन्यायेनास्फुटभेदारुंकारमेलने संकरः "` ॥ BS केपोतन्यायः ॥ The simile of pigeons alighting on a threshing-floor. Used by writers on Alankara to illustrate the production of a certain effect by the simultaneous action of numerous causes. In Sahityadarpana (739) we read—* समुच्चयोऽयमेकस्मिन्सति कार्यस्य साधके । ae कपोतिकान्यायात्तव्करः स्यात्परोऽपि चेत्‌.” ॥ “The conjunction is when notwithstanding the existence of one cause sufficient to bring about an effect, there are represented others producing the same, according to the maxim of the Threshing-floor and the pigeons.” See this, also, very concisely put, im Alankaérasarvasva, page 161, and in Kuvalaydnanda, p. 240. There is further reference to this nyaya in Mallinatha on Magha x. 16, and in Nydyamalavistara 11. 1. 3. 24
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 22

gloss_en: A lamp which has gone out will burn up again if touched with sulphur-powder.
explanation: A lamp which has gone out will burn up again if touched with sulphur-powder. The use of this illustration will be seen from the following passage of the vritti on Tativamuhktakalapa ii. 65:—“‘ag संखूलयवस्थासिद्धं निरयाणां प्रातिकूल्यं स्वाजुभूतं च दुःखं सुक्तः प्यति वा न वा । आचये गन्धाङ्मरजसा eet नष्टो दीपः युनज्वंरेदिति न्यायेन grt दुःखसन्ततिरुदियात्‌ '” ॥ गतेवर्तिगोधामां सविभजनन्यायः ॥ The simile of the partition of the flesh of an 710५५110 whilst it is still in its hole! Used to illustrate an impossibility. Raghunatha says of it—‘aaaget म्रदृत्तिविरूवर्तिगोधाविभजनन्याये- areata ध्वनितम्‌.” It occurs, in the form given above, in Khandanakhandakhadya page 640:—“qeft तथापि क इत्यादि तिर्यक्‌ चे्यन्तं तदपि ग्तैव्तिगोधामांसविभजनन्यायमनुहरति पक्चत्रयस्याप्यु- agen आच्छादितस्य दर्शयितुमशक्यत्वेन तद्विभागव्यवस्थितेरनवसरनिरware” ॥ TS पादुकान्यायः ॥ The simile of the shoes on the neck. This qtiaint nyaya appears to be used when an opponent is compelled to accept certain conclusions or else adopt an utterly absurd alternative. It occurs three times in Citsukht. The first instance is in i, 11 (Pandit, vol. Iv, page 484), as follows:—aaarafy भावानामाश्रयत्वेन संमते । प्रतियोगित्वमलन्ताभावं प्रति रषात्मता ॥ ११ ॥ तथाहि पटघटादीनां भावानां खवाश्रयत्वेनाभिमतास्तन्त्वादयो ये तन्निष्ठालयन्ताभावग्रतियो- गितेव तेषां मिथ्यात्वम्‌ । नहि तेषामन्यत्रसत्ता संभविनी । तत्रापि चेत्वा न स्यात्तदा Tes पाटुकान्यायेन षत्वमेव पर्यवस्येत्‌” ॥ ‘This verse is quoted in the second chapter of the Vedantaparibhasha, and a 25 translation of it, and of the comment on it, by Professor Venis will be found in the Pandit for 1883, page 660. I subjoin that portion which contains the simile. “For the existence of these things cannot be surmised anywhere but in their substrates......, and if the existence of these things, in their substrates, cannot be surmised..., then the unreality of things is the only conclusion (forced upon us), much in the same way that a man must hang his shoes round his neck if he will not wear them on his feet.” The other two examples are in i. 26, and ii, 16 (Pandit, vol v, pages 112 and 435). It is found also in Atmatattvaviveka, page 45, in Khandanoddhara, pages 7 and 124, and in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 284, in the erroneous form “aa पादिका.” The explanation given by Raghundthavarman differs entirely from the above, and is extremely far-fetched and unsatisfactory. He 888: -“ सदसतोरुत्पत्तिनिबृ्यसंभवेन परस्परविरोधे न भ्रकारान्तरस्थितिरिति न्यायसिद्धस्य aa वानुपपततर्गरे पादुकान्याये- नान्ञानकार्यस्य बाधानुपपतत्या चाक्ञानस्यानिर्वचनीयत्वं बलात्खवीकायैम्‌ | यथा कस्यचिद्ृहस्थस्य गृहेऽन्नाद्यार्थैत्वछटेनागतो निषण्णश्च कश्चिद्रिरस्तन तत्वतो ज्ञात्वा गच्छ गच्छेति पुनःषुनरूच्यमानोऽपि यदा dia Gea गमनं न स्वीकरोति तदा पाटुकासदहितं पदं गले निधाय नोदयित्वा च बलाशनिस्सार्येते तथा प्रकृतेऽपि बोध्यम्‌ `° ॥ गृहीतार्थ गताश्चोराः कस्तानाच्छेत्तुमरहति ॥ The robbers have got away with the booty ; who is able to intercept them? This saying is quoted by Vacaspatimis’ra in his comment (on page 59 ) on Nydyavartika 1, 1. 2. “ अपायो- ऽपि तत्वन्तानान्मिथ्यान्ञानस्य स्वरूपतो वा विषयतो वा फख्तो वा स्यात्‌ । न तावत्स्वरूपतः । ...नापि विषयतः । नहि शुक्तिकाज्ञानं रजतत्तानस्य रजतविषयतामपहरैमुत्सहते जातं हि तद्रजतं विषयीक्ृ्य | यथाहुः । गृहीत्वाथ गताश्चौराः कस्तानच्छेनतुमहंतीति It is found also in Khandanoddhira, page 119. 4 26
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 24

gloss_en: The simile of a lighted lamp inside a vessel.
explanation: The simile of a lighted lamp inside a vessel. Raghunatha points out that a lamp so placed illuminates only the interior of the vessel, and he applies it to one whose knowledge of Brahman is of a low order. The maxim is used very differently, however, by Anandavardhana in his Dhvanydloka iii. 33 (page 190), ts the following extract will show:—“q त्वेष वाच्यव्यंग्ययो- न्यायः । नहि apt प्रतीयमाने वाच्यबुद्धिदृरीभवति | वाच्यावभासाविनाभावेन तस्य भ्रकादानात्‌ | तस्माद्ररप्रदीपन्यायस्तयोः | यथैव हि प्रदीपद्वारेण घटग्रतीतावुत्पन्नायां न प्रदीप्रकादयो निवर्तते तद्वबय्यभ्रतीतौ वाच्यावभासः 7 ॥ Abhinavagupta, when explaining Dhvanydloka i. 12, refers to this passage in the following words:—“sq एव तृतीयोद्योते घटप्रदीपदृ्टान्तबला्यग्यप्रतीतिकाटेऽपि वाच्यग्रतीतिनं विघटत इति यद्वक्ष्यति तेन सहास्य अन्थस्य न विरोधः”? According to these great authorities on Alankara, therefore, the nyaya teaches that as the lamp continues to burn after it has lighted up the interior of the vessel, and is indeed essential to the continuance of that illumination, so the expressed meaning of a sentence is absolutely essential as a basis for the figurative meaning which it also conveys.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 26

gloss_en: This has the same meaning and application as the कूपयन्नधरिकान्याय, for which see the first series of maxims.
explanation: This has the same meaning and application as the कूपयन्नधरिकान्याय, for which see the first series of maxims. It occurs in Sures’vara’s large Vartika 4. 4. 248, and 6, 2. 155, as follows:— ^ अनिर्तातात्मतत्त्वः सन्कामबन्धनवन्धनः | घटीयच्नवदश्रान्तो वंश्नमीत्यनिदां ae? ॥ “ घटीयन्रवदश्रान्ता एवमेव ga: पुनः | परिवर्तन्ति संसारे कमवायुसमीरिताः "2 ॥ Similarly, in his vartika on the वद्य bhashya 2. 1. 221 (page 86 }--“तिबीजं भवेजन्म जन्मबीजं तथा तिः | घदीयच्नवदश्रान्तो वंभ्रमीतयनिदां नरः ” ॥ It is found too in a third work of his, namely Naiskarmyasiddhi i. 42. Also in the Jain treatise Prabandhacintimani, page 62, as follows:— 8 27 “aga हससि किं द्रविणान्धमूढ लक्ष्मीः स्थिरा न भवतीति किमत्र चित्रम्‌ । कित्वं न ale घटीर्जखयन्नचक्र रिक्ता भवन्ति भरिता भरिताश्च रिक्ताः? ॥ In Upamitibhavaprapancé Katha, pages 52, and 418, it appears as अरघटघरीयन्रन्याय. In Kirtikawmudz vi. 48, we have the compound ज्रमद्वटीसंघटितारघट्रखाङ्कारशब्दैः. The word अरघट्‌ has become राहाट in Marathi, as in राहाटगाडगे.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 26

gloss_en: The simile of the [continued] revolving of the potter's wheel.
explanation: The simile of the [continued] revolving of the potter's wheel. Followers of both Sankhya and Vedanta have asked why, on attgining to right knowledge, a man is not immediately liberated. Kapila’s answer is contained in Siitra iii, 82. “ चक्रञ्रमणवद्धृतरारीरः ” ॥ On which Aniruddha says:—* यथा दण्डापगमे संस्कारवशाचचक्रं wale तथा विवेकिनामपि देहधारणकर्मणोऽक्षी- णत्वान्न ततक्षणान्सुक्तिः किन्तूपभोगादिना कर्क्षयादिति ॥ तथा च fa: । fata नरो सुच्येत्ति्टन्सक्तोऽपि विग्रहे । ऊुरालचक्रमध्यस्थो विच्छिन्नोऽपि अरमेद्धटः' ॥ Brahmasitrabhashya 4 1. 15 teaches the same thing from the Vedantist’s standpoint, and propounds the very important doctrine that whilst accumulated and current works are destroyed by true knowledge, fructescent works, which brought about the present existence, are not. Therefore the Jivanmukta has to continue here until death—just as the potter’s wheel continues to revolve until the impetus given to it exhausts itself.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 27

gloss_en: The maxim of giving up the fabulous gem Cintémani, and taking instead a mere piece of quartz! Its application is 28 obvious.
explanation: The maxim of giving up the fabulous gem Cintémani, and taking instead a mere piece of quartz! Its application is 28 obvious. Raghunatha applies it to the man who abandons the search for the knowledge of Brahma in order to enjoy the pleasures of this life. S’dntis‘ataka 12, in Haeberlin’s Anthology, bears on (08 जन्मेदं वन्ध्यां नीतं भवभोगोपकिप्सया | काचमूल्येन विक्रीतो हन्त चिन्तामणिमेया ॥ So, too, Hitopades'a ii. 60:— ^ मणिदयैठति पादेषु काचः Arie धार्यते । यथैवास्तु तथैवास्तु काचः काचो मणिमौणिः" ॥ There is an additional example in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 420:--“ निर्वाणसुखसंसारसुखयोश्च परस्परम्‌ । चिन्तारलस्य काचेन यावत्तावद्ुणान्तरम्‌.*” Then, lower down on the same page, this and eight other figures are employed to illustrate the folly of one who, though acquainted with the Jaina creed, still clings to evil. The whole passage is reproduced for the benefit of those who have not the book to refer to. “gt जैनमपि सम्प्राप्य शासनं कर्मनाशनम्‌ | हिंसाक्रोधादिपापेषु रज्यते मूढमानसः ॥ संहारयति ana चिन्तामणिमनुत्तमम्‌ | करोलयङ्गारवाणिञ्यं दग्धा गोशीष॑चन्दनम्‌ ॥ भिनत्ति नावं मूढात्मा Ges स महोदधौ । Gard दारयव्युचेवेयं रलसुत्तमम्‌ ॥ प्रदीपयति fet देवद्रोणी महत्तमम्‌ | रलस्थाल्यां पचलयाम्लखलकं मोहदो- पतः ॥ सौवणैलाङ्गलाग्ेण Rea वसुधां तथा । addi ave तूला मूढमानसः ॥ छित्वा कपूरखण्डानि कोद्र वाणां समन्ततः | वृतिं विधत्ते मूढोऽयमहं सश्चुतिकः किल ?° ॥ On page 170 there is yet another word of Siddharsi’s in regard to the Cintamani, namely “ निरक्षणनरो नैव चिन्तामाणिमवासुते."' चेतनस्य यलहीनस्योध्वगतिश्चेतनान्तराधीना ॥ Movement wpward on the part of a quiescent intelligent being is dependent on [ the action of ] some other being of intelligence. I should call this an axiom rather than a maxim; but as Anandagiri terms it a laukika-nydya I include it here. It occurs in his comment on Brahmasutrabhdshya 4. 3. 5, as 10108 :--“चितनस्य यहीनस्योर्वगतिश्चेतनान्तराधीनेति रोकरिकन्यायेन यलहीनानां गन्दरणां गमयितारोऽचिरादयश्चेतनाः स्युरिति सूत्रयोजनया ब्रूते” ॥ 29
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 27

gloss_en: The simile of particles of the Kataka nut [placed] in water [in order to clear it].
explanation: The simile of particles of the Kataka nut [placed] in water [in order to clear it]. Manu refers to it in vi. 67 thus:— “फलं कतकब्क्षस्य यद्यप्यस्बुप्रसादकम्‌ | A नामग्रहणादेव aT वारि प्रसीदति ° ॥ 1" the Laukikanyadyasangraha the nyaya is explained as follows:—“aqon हि argue निक्षिप्ताः कतकस्योषधिवि- शेषस्य रेणवो रजांसि तस्माज्खात्पद्कं era सख्यमेव विरीयन्ते तथा waar सविलासाक्ञानं fad स्वयमेव fade” ॥ The larger work, the Laukihanydyaratnakara, adds the following quotation ‘in support of the definition.—“agy भगवत्पादैः । अज्ञानकलुषं जीवं ज्ञानाभ्यासाद्िनिमैखम्‌ | कृत्वा ज्ञानं सख्यं नदयेजरं कतकरेणुवत्‌ ॥* The “worshipful feet” are those of S’ankaracharya, and the verse is Atmabodha. 5. Sures'vara has given a capital illustration of the application of this in his large Vartika 4. 3. 975-6 (page 1553);— “अपां कतकसंपकोद्यथालयन्तप्रसन्रता । अपासतादोषसंसारभावनस्यैवमात्मनः ॥ स्वास्थ्यं प्रसन्नतेतस्िन्सुषुसे भवतीत्यतः | सम्प्रसादमिमं प्राहुः gue तद्िदो जनाः"? ॥ There is an interesting example, too, in Hemachandra’s Paris‘istaparvan ii, 4:— “गुरुवाकतकक्षोदसंसक्तमभवत्सदा | AURAT AAAS तन्मनोवारि Paw” ॥ Venkatanatha, however, does not altogether hold with this simile; for in the vritti to his Z'attvamuktakaldpa ii. 50 @page 215), he says:— “a तु चिदपि द्रव्यनाशः | अवस्थान्तरापत्या चाददौनम्‌ | कतकरजोनि- दर्शने च बारगप्ररोभनम्‌। न हि पयसि पकः कतकरजसा शाम्यते विश्ेषमाव्रद्टेः। न च स्वयं तत्र aaa शेषमात्रसिद्धेः' ॥ 30
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 29

gloss_en: Broth cooked for the son-in-law is also useful for the wneapected guest.
explanation: Broth cooked for the son-in-law is also useful for the wneapected guest. This, like the देहीदीपन्याय and many others, resembles our proverb “killing two birds with one stone,” I have met with it only in Kuvalaydénanda (page 98) under the figure दीपक. The passage is as follows:—“afe दीपस्य रथ्याप्रासा- दयोर्युगपदुपकारकत्वेन seat श्रपितस्य सूपस्यातिथिभ्यः प्रथमपरिवेषणेन च प्रासङ्गिकत्वं हीयते &५. This passage also illustrates another of Ragunatha’s nyayas, namely गृहार्थमारोपितस्य दीपस्य रथ्योपकारकत्वम्‌. ज्वरहरतक्षकचूडारलाटंकारोपदेरावत्‌ ul Like instructions for obtaining Takshaka’s crest jewel as a jebrifuge! An illustration of utter impossibility. It occurs in the Nydyabindutika, page 3, line 9, in a passage regarding the anubandhas, It runs thus—“aqag संशायो युक्तः | अनुक्तेषु त॒ प्रतिपत्तभिर्निष्रयोजनमभिधेयं संभाव्येतास्य प्रकरणस्य काकदन्तपरीक्षाया इव | अक्यानुष्टानं वा | ज्वरहरतक्षकचूडारल्ारुंकारोपदेशवत्‌ | अनभिमतं वा । मातृविवाहक्रमोपदेदावत्‌ ॥ ` I am indebted to Professor ५. Bendall for pointing out this passage to me. It is applied by Vacaspatimis’ra, in the same sense, in his 7atparyatika, page 3 and in the Nydyakanika, pages 338 and 417.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 30

gloss_en: The simile of the bird named Tittibha [ Parra Jacana].
explanation: The simile of the bird named Tittibha [ Parra Jacana]. It is based on the story of this bird as given in the Hitopades'a, and is used as an illustration of ridiculous conceit. The verse which paves the way for the story is ii. 137:— “अङ्गाङ्गिभावमन्तात्वा कथं सामर्ध्यनिणैयः | पर्य टिष्टिभमात्रेण समुद्रो व्याकुरीकरृतः' ॥ : 31 नक्रकाणिटन्यन्वाय : ॥ The maxim of buttermilk for Kaundinya. This is one of Raghunatha’s grammatical nyayas, taken from Mahabhasya, and is intended to indicate a special exception to a general rule as in the sentence ब्राह्मणेभ्यो दधि दीयतां तक्रं कौण्डिन्याय, where an exception is made in the case of Kaundinya though included amongst the Brahmans. It occurs in Brihaddranyavartika 1. 6. 71 ( page 881 ):— “तक्रकौण्डिन्यवच्यायो न चेहाप्यवसीयते | उत्सर्गानवकारशस्वाच्छन्यतैवात आपतेत्‌ "” ॥ On which Anandagiri comments as follows:— “अभिन्नशाब्दस्य भेदनिषेधित्वेऽपि न भेदस्य शून्यता ada see कारणे निषेधादार्येभ्यो दधि दीयतामिल्यायौपाधौ प्रा्षस्य दस्त्र कौण्डन्यायेति कौण्डिन्ये निषेधेऽप्यश्चून्यतावदिल्या्ंक्याह तक्रेति"” ॥ T have noted down seven instances of the occurrence of this illustration in the Mahabhdasya, namely, 1. 1. 47; 6. 1. 2 (4); 6. 2+ 1; 6. 4. 163 (2); 7. 1. 72 (8); 7. 2. 117 (ॐ); and 7, 4, 61 (4). 1४ will suffice to quote the first, as the other six are practically the same:—“eieatst दृष्टान्तः । रोके हि सत्यपि संभवे बाधनं भवति 1 तद्यथा | दधि ब्राह्मणेभ्यो दीयतां तक्रं कौण्डिन्यायेति ant सम्भवे दधि- दानस्य तक्रदानं fade भवति. See, also, Nagoji Bhatta’s paribhds& Lv, and Professor Kielhorn’s translation of the same. Other instances of it will be found in Vakyapadiya, ii. 352; S'lokavartika, page 617 (verse 15); Tantravartika, page 262 (last 2 verses); and Bhamati, 3. 3. 26 ( page 628 ), तपनीयमपनीय वाससि ग्रन्थिकतारमुपहससि सयं च कनकमुपादाय गगनाञ्चठे मन्थि करोषि ॥ Thow ridiculest the man who taking his gold ties it wp in 9 32 a corner of his garment, and then thyself taking the gold tiest it wp in the skirt of the sky! It is found in Atmatattvaviveka, (page 58, line 3 from bottom ), as follows:—‘qanefretétat भ्र. काश्यमानत्वं परिपाख्यता आद्यरक्षणे qa: कर्तव्यः परिहर्तव्यं वा प्रकाशमान स्वम्‌ । अन्यथा तपनीयमपनीय वाससि अन्थिकर्तारमुपहससि स्वयञ्च कनकqa गगनाञ्चले ग्रन्थि करोषीति । सेयं सर्वग्रकारमसिद्धिः सवैप्रकारं चानैकान्तिकमिति ” ॥ तस्करकन्दुन्यायः ॥ The simile of a thief [who engaged himself] as a cook. His inability to perform the duties, however, led to his discovery and arrest. This is intended to teach the folly of undertaking to do something quite beyond our powers! Sures’vara is the only author in whose works I have met with it. The following verse, which contains it, appears in his large Vartika ( page 610), and also in that on the Taittiriyopanishadbhashya (page 169), the preceding context, too, being identical in both ०४०७“ aga विनियुक्तोऽपि goer श्रपयेदिति । सर्वात्मनाप्यसौ ऊर्वन्ु्यांत्तस्करकन्दुवत्‌. » The following is an extract from Anandagiri’s comment on the former passage:-—“qy यद्यपि सन्तापमान्नं कृष्णटेष्वपि शक्यं कत तथापि विङ्कित्तिप्रधानः पचतेरथ इति न्यायेन सन्तापजन्यां तामेव तेषु कर्वन्नायासमाच्रभागी स्याद्यथा लोके तस्करः सन्कन्दुरपूपा- Rong स्वयमपि भरविर्य तत्कमं॑ऊर्व्राक्यकासत्वादायासमात्रभारभवये- वमदाक्यत्वाञ््ानेऽपि न वैधी प्रृत्तिरियधैः ॥ The same commenta- {0178 explanation of the nyaya as it appears in the latter work is somewhat different. He षः“ कश्चिच्यर्यं कत्वा स्वकीयचोर्यसंवरणा्थं सन्निहितं aegué प्रविष्टः गृहस्वामिना कन्दुना aged नियु- क्तस्तत्कर्मण्यशक्ये विनियुक्त्वात्त्छुरवन्‌ राजयुरूपैस्तस्करमन्वेषमाणेस्तत्र इटिति समागतैरुक्ते कर्मण्यकुदारतां समालोच्य तस्करोऽयमिति ज्ञात्वा गृहीतो व्यधेङकेशभागी यथा तस्करकन्दुः dave ब्रह्मज्ञाने नियुक्तोऽपि तस्य कलै- TATE भवेदिय्थः' The nyaya occurs again on page 181 of the Taittiriyavartika:—“‘gept परय इयेवं नियु- क्तोऽपि न शक्कयात्‌ ।.क्तयात्स्नियोगाच्े्कुयौत्तस्करकन्दुवत्‌ *” ॥ 33 तस्करस्य पुरस्तात्क्षे सुवर्णमुपेत्य TAMA ॥ A thief’s offer of his limbs for examination when the gold has been found wnder his armpit! This occurs i

gloss_en: The simile of rice and sesamum seeds, Used to illustrate an easily distinguishable union of two or more things, in contradistinction to the more intimate and indistinguishable union exemplified by the commingling of milk and water.
explanation: The simile of rice and sesamum seeds, Used to illustrate an easily distinguishable union of two or more things, in contradistinction to the more intimate and indistinguishable union exemplified by the commingling of milk and water. For examples, see क्षीरनीरन्याय. Also Rudrata’s Kavydlankdra x. 25,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 33

gloss_en: The simile of the raising [with the hand, one scale] of a balance.
explanation: The simile of the raising [with the hand, one scale] of a balance. That, of course, causes the other scale to go down; and so the simile is used to illustrate the bringing about of two or more results by one operation. It occurs in the following passage of Pancapadikd ( page 38 ):—“a¢ रजतमिति aa feria निरस्यते न वस्तुतत्वमवबोध्यते aa तथा भवतु । ge gala 5 84 ज्ञानमेव teat यद्विरोधिनिराकरणमन्तरेण न साधं साधयितुमलं तरो- क्रमनन्यापार इवानमननान्तंरीयकः ॥ तथाद्यन्नमनव्यापारः स्वविषयस्य तुखा- द्रव्यस्योर्ध्वदेशसंबन्धं न साधयितुमरं तत्कालमेव तस्याधोदेशसंबन्धमनापाद्य | न वोन्नमनकारकस्य हस्तप्रयलादेरानमनेऽपि कारकत्वम्‌?” ॥ In commenting on this, Prakas'itman 8958:--“अन्यविषयन्यापारादन्यविषयस्य नान्तरी यकसिद्धि साधयति तथादयन्ञमनव्यापार Zt” ॥ Other good examples of it will be found in Nydyavartika 3. 2, 12 (top of page 412), the substance of which is reproduced in Nydyamanjari, page 456 ; in S'lokavartikatika, page 311 (where it is seen in conjunction with the पद्मपत्रदातव्यतिमेदन्याय); and in Vivaranaprameya, page 99, line 4,
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 33

gloss_en: The simile of the grinding of chaff.
explanation: The simile of the grinding of chaff. Used, like पिष्टपेषणन्याय, of any unnecessary and useless effort. It occurs in Padmapada’s Pancapadika, page 68, as follows:—“aaq gearशरूपताऽनन्यसिद्धता तत्परतिपाद्यता चेति भिद्यन्ते विषयसंबन्धप्रयोजनानि तानि च त्रीण्यपि spay । नापुरुषार्थे काकदन्तपरीक्षायां त॒षकण्डने वा प्रवर्तते ्रेक्षावानू"? ॥ Also in the Hitopades‘a iv. 18:--“अविचारयतो यु- क्तिकथनं तुषकण्डनम्‌ | नीचेषूपकृतं राजन्वालकास्िव मूत्रितम्‌ ॥ Sures’vara too makes very frequent use of it. We find it on pages 676, 1036, 1334, 1505, and 1572 of his large Vartika; and on page 176 of his Tatttiriyavartika. The nyaya is not in Raghunatha’s book, but he has others of the same meaning which I have not met with in the literature; namely जलमन्थनन्याय, and गदभरोमगणनन्याय. The same idea is expressed in the following sentence of the Nydyaman- {५7 ( page 645 ):—* किमयं दग्धो द्यते wat वा मार्यते अनेकान्तिकहेत्‌- पन्यासेनैव खल्वयं तपस्वी निगृही तोऽसाधनाङ्गवचनादिति किं हेत्वन्तराख्यनिमदस्थानान्तरोंदीरणेनेति `” ॥ 35
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 34

gloss_en: This saying is explained by Taranatha as 1011008: “तुष्यतु gaa इति न्यायो यत्र ्रतिवायुक्तपक्षं दुष्टमपि वादिना प्रौडिवादेनाङ्गीकृत्यापि दूषणान्तरस्य दानं तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ It would therefore seem to mean “Let this evil fellow, my opponent, chuckle over his apparent success in this argument, but what about so-and so?” I have met with it in Advaitabrahmasiddhi, page 14, in the following sentence:—“ qurgeq किमधिकरणञुताधेयम्‌ | यद्वा adi तिकालः क्रं वा प्रतीतिरेव । उतावच्छेदकदेशो वेलत्र विनिगमनाविरहात्तुष्यतु दुरजनन्यायेन स्वीकारेऽपि न निवहः” ॥ 1४ occurs again on page 16.
explanation: This saying is explained by Taranatha as 1011008: “तुष्यतु gaa इति न्यायो यत्र ्रतिवायुक्तपक्षं दुष्टमपि वादिना प्रौडिवादेनाङ्गीकृत्यापि दूषणान्तरस्य दानं तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ It would therefore seem to mean “Let this evil fellow, my opponent, chuckle over his apparent success in this argument, but what about so-and so?” I have met with it in Advaitabrahmasiddhi, page 14, in the following sentence:—“ qurgeq किमधिकरणञुताधेयम्‌ | यद्वा adi तिकालः क्रं वा प्रतीतिरेव । उतावच्छेदकदेशो वेलत्र विनिगमनाविरहात्तुष्यतु दुरजनन्यायेन स्वीकारेऽपि न निवहः” ॥ 1४ occurs again on page 16. In the Bhamati, page 243, we have it in the form “तुष्यतु परः? as follows:—“qaq परस्याग्रहो धर्मिण्यगरद्यमाणे तद्धर्मा न शक्या अ्रहीतु- मिति । एवं नामास्तु तथा garg परस्तथाप्यदोष इयर्थः ॥* ‘This is de cidedly the clearest example. In his translation of Haridasa’s comment on Kuswmdnjali i. 3, Prof. Cowell’s rendering of the nyiiya is “ the principle of satisfying an opponent.”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 35

gloss_en: The illustration of the caterpillar.
explanation: The illustration of the caterpillar. This illustration is used and explained in Brihaddranyakopanishad 4. 4 3 as follows :— ^ तद्यथा तृणजलायुका तृणस्यान्तं गत्वान्यमाक्रममाक्रम्यात्मानमुपसंहरलेवमे- amare शरीरं निहत्याविद्यां गमयि्वान्यमाक्रममाक्रम्यात्मानसुपसं- eva’ I include it because it is found in Raghunatha’s list; but it is of no practical value. ठंणारणिमणिन्यायः ॥ . The simile of straw, arant wood, and the burning gem [ as means of producing fire]. The kind of fire produced by each varies ( just as that of a lighted lamp differs from that of burning wood or cowdung); and the method of production, too, is 36 different; that being in one case blowing, in another attrition, and in the third the rays of the sun. The application of the nyaya will be seen from the following passage of Nyiyamanjariséra, page 3, line 5 :--“अच्र नव्याः तृणारणिमणिन्यायेन विन्चध्वंसविदेष एव मङ्गलस्य फलं विश्चध्वसविरेषान्तरं च विनायकस्तवपाठादेः फलम्‌ | समापिस्त॒ विघ्वसंसर्गाभावादिकारणकलापजन्या. For an interesting discussion as to the ‘capacity’ (क्ति) residing in straw &c., see Kusumanjalt pages 58-72, and Prof. Cowell’s translation, pages 6 and 7. The nyaya is not included in Raghunatha’s collection, but is explained in the Vacaspatyam (8. v. न्याय) as follows :—arrate भ्रति तृणस्य, animate प्र्रणेः, aerate प्रति मणेश्च कारणत्वं न तु वदह्धित्वावच्छिन्नं प्रति तृणादेः कारणत्वं परस्परव्यभिचारात्‌ | एवं यत्र कायैकारणभावबाहुल्थं कायंतावच्छेदकं कारणतावच्छेदकं च नाना तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः? ॥ AY
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 35

gloss_en: The simile of a man carrying a vessel full of oil [and who is to be put to death if he spills a drop of it!].
explanation: The simile of a man carrying a vessel full of oil [and who is to be put to death if he spills a drop of it!]. This curious illustration is given in Bodhicarydvatara vii-70, and applied to one who has adopted the ascetic life-—“Aeqraa agefacaरधिष्ठितः | स्खङिति मरणत्रासात्तत्परः स्यात्तथा बरती." त्यजेदेकं FIAT ॥ One should abandon an individual for the sake of a whole family. This is the first pada of Httopades'a i. 115 which reads thus:—“qazs Swear मरामस्यार्थे कुलं जेत्‌ | म्रामं जनपदस्यार्थे आप्मार्थे पृथिवीं aaa’ ॥ 1४ is quoted by Anandagiri, in his comment on Brahmasitrabhashya 1. 1. 22, as follows: ^ल्यजेदेकं geet इति न्यायाद्भूयसीनां ब्रह्मलिङ्गश्चतीनामनुग्रहायाकादाश्चुतेरेकस्या बाध इत्याह? ॥ Raghunatha expounds it thus in the Laukikanyaya8८1/11.01.0;--्यत्रोभयकोटिकसंशये wea बहर्थहानिद्ितीयस्ीकारे ले- काथहानिस्तच्र द्वितीयपक्षः स्वीकर्तव्य इति विवक्षायां uses कुरस्यार्थं इति 37 न्यायः प्रसरति" ॥ Further on he says, “यस्तु ल्यजेदेकमिति न्यायं नानुसरति सोऽल्पस्य देतोवैहु हालुमिच्छन्विचारमूढः प्रतिभासि मे त्वमिति न्यायविषयतां नातिवर्तते ॥ ^ nyaya of similar import to the one under consideration is ^ सर्वनाशे समुत्पन्ने अधं त्यजति पण्डितः,” which see below.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 36

gloss_en: The simile of the fire which has consumed the fucl [and therefore goes out].
explanation: The simile of the fire which has consumed the fucl [and therefore goes out]. This immediately follows the जलकतकरेणुन्याय in Raghunatha’s list, and is meant to teach much the same thing, He 888 :--“ द्ग्धानीन्धनानि येन सोऽभ्चियथा स्वयमेव शाम्यति तथेति gaa” € have an instance of the employment of the figure in S’vetds'vatara Upanisad vi. 19; and again in S'ankara’s bhashya on Brahmasiitra i. i. 4 (page 76), and Sures'’vara’s large Vartika pages 1593 and 1840. The following is ParamérthasGra 77:— “qa कर्मविकलव्पानात्मस्थं मनः केवलं Bal । दग्धेन्धन ga वद्धिः aaa भवेच्छान्तः 77 दण्डिन्यायः ॥ The simile of a man with a stick [ or, men with sticks]. The first instance, which I know of, of the employment of this nyiiya is in a curious passage of Patanjali’s on Panini 8. 2. 83, for reference to which I am indebted to Professor Kielhorn. It occurs also in the Nydyavartika on sitra i. 37. In this, and in the preceding sitra, there is a definition of uddharana, in the course of which the term तद्धर्मभावी occurs. In regard to this the Vartikakara remarks:—“aqy तु तद्धर्मभावीयेतत्पदमन्यथा निराकुर्वन्ति तद्धर्मभावी भवन्चुष्णभोजिन्यायेन वा भवेदण्डिन्यायेन वा भवेत्‌ । तद्यदि तावदुष्णभोजिन्यायेन उष्णं भोक्तुं शीरुमखेव्युष्णभोजी at वा भावयितं शीलमयेति तेद्धमभावी । अत्रापि भावयितं गमयित यावदुक्तं स्यादिति । नायं सूत्रार्थं इति न किञ्चिदेतत्‌ । दण्डिन्यायस्तु दण्डो यस्यास्तीति स दण्डी तद्धमभावो यस्यास्ति स भवति तद्धर्मभावी" ॥ 38 The following from Vachaspatimis‘ra’s Tativabindw closely resembles the explanation given of the छत्रिन्यायः--““दाण्डिनो merdtaa तु दण्ड्यदण्डिषु समूहिषु रक्ष्यमाणेषु तदन्तग॑तस्याविशोषाहण्डिशब्दार्थस्य परिग्रहः ॥ दामनव्याकटन्यायः ॥ The maxim of the Asuras, Déma, Vyala, and Kata. This 28 expounded by Raghunatha in the following manner:— “Comeeaearal न तव स्यात्कदाचन | भीमभासदडन्यायः सर्वदा तेऽस्तु राघवः? gate वासिष्ठ इदं न्यायद्रयं सप्रपञ्नसुपन्यस्तम्‌ | तत्राद्यस्योच्चतरां दरयामापन्नस्याप्यज्तस्यातिनीचददाग्रा्िरवर्यं कारेन भवतीति तत्वबोधो- ऽवदर्यं संपाद्य इति विवक्षायां प्रवृत्तिः | दामव्यारुकटाख्याखयोऽसुराः शबरेण स्वमायया fara च तलग्रहारादिना मेवादिचू्णीकरणे शक्ता अप्यज्ञानप्रभावात्काेन मशकादियोनिं प्रापुरिति ae azn तत्वविन्न कदापि स्वपदास्पततीति विवक्षायां द्वितीयस्यावतारः | तेऽप्यसुरास्तेनैव तथा नि- सिताश्चिरं जीबन्सुक्तिसुखमनुभूय निर्वाणपदं प्राप्ता इति संक्षेपः | प्रपञ्चस्तु तत्रेव द्रष्टव्यः? ॥ “Vasishtha,” means the Yogavdsistha, in Book 4 (chapters xxv—xxxiv ) of which, we have a detailed account of these six Asuras. The verse quoted by Raghunatha is not found in the printed edition exactly in that form, but 4. 34, 36 reads thus :— “दामव्यालकटन्थायस्तस्ान्मा तेऽस्तु TWAT | भीमभासदढन्यायो fea carta” ॥ There is one of similar import in the opening part of their history, and Mr. M. R. Telang has pointed out a third in the closing part of chapter xxiv.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 37

gloss_en: The simile of Arjuna.
explanation: The simile of Arjuna. Used to show that something, though once done, may 06 done again, as in the case of Arjuna who defeated the Kuru race after Krishna had already defeated 39 them. Raghunitha says:—“ निल्यबोधमदहिश्ना बाधितेऽपि 23 aver wire धर्नेजयन्यायेन वाधकत्वोपपत्तेः । wag: । “नित्यबोधपरिपीडितं जगद्धि्रमं जुदत्ि वाक्यजा मतिः | वासुदेवनिहतं धनञ्जयो हन्ति कौरवकुलं यथा ga’ “Knowledge effected through Vedic sentences destroys that error termed the world, which had already been destroyed by eternal knowledge (Self, Brahman): just as Arjuna slays again the Kuru race already slain by Vasudeva.” The verse is Sankshepas'ariraka ii. 88, and the translation is that of Mr. Arthur Venis in the Veddntasiddhantamuktivali (page 174) where the verse is quoted.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 38

gloss_en: The simile of grain and its husk.
explanation: The simile of grain and its husk. The earliest example of this figure is in the Brahmabindu Upanishad, verse 18:— ‘camara मेधावी ज्ञानविक्तानतत्वतः । पलालमिव धान्यार्थी दजेद्रerrata: ॥ 118 verse, with others of similar import, is quoted in Pancadas't 1v, The following, from Bhdmati, page 54, appears also, without any acknowledgement, in the first chapter of Sarvadars’anasangruha :-—* अवर्जनीयतया दुःखमागतमपि परिहलय सुखमात्रं भोक्ष्यते | तद्यथा । मल्सयार्थी सशव्कानू सकण्टकान्मwget स॒ यावदादेयं तावदादाय विनिवर्तते । यथा वा धान्यार्थी सपलालानि धान्यान्याहरति स यावदादेयं तावुपादाय निवर्तते” ॥ Vacaspatimis’ra, however, was not the originator of the illustration. It occurs four times in the Mahabhdsya, namely, 1, 2. 39; 3. 3. 18; 3. 4. 21 (vart. 2); and 4. 1. 92. The following is the passage, the substance of which is reproduced in the Bhamati and Sarvadars'anasangraha :—“ कश्चिदन्नाथीं रालिकलापं सपलारुं सतुषमाहरति नान्तरीयकत्वात्‌ | स यावदादेयं तावृदादाय तुषपलाला- न्युस्सजति । तथा कश्चिन्मां सार्थं मत्स्यान्सकण्टकान्सदाकलानाहरति नान्तरी- यकस्वात्‌ । स॒ यावदादेयं तावदादाय शकर्कण्टकानुस्सजति.*' See, also, Nagoji Bhatta’s paribhdsaé 738. The nyaya seems to have a different application in Marathi literature. Molesworth’s defini40 tion is as follows:—“The law of the corn and its straw. Conquer the king and you conquer his subjects; accomplish or acquire a matter‘and you attain all it sustains or involves.” न खलु शालग्रामे किरातशतसङ्कीर्णे प्रतिवसन्नपि जा- ह्मणः किरातो भवति ॥ A Brahman does not become a Kirdta by living on the S'dlagrama mountain filled with hundreds of those barbarians! This is equivalent to our saying, “A horse does not become an ass by being born in the stable of the latter.” Compare, too, Sankara’s ५ न ह्यश्वस्थाने गां पदयन्नश्वोऽयमिलयमूढोऽध्यवस्यति ” in Brahmasitrabhisya 1. 4. 1. The saying as given above is found in Vacaspatimis'ra’s comment on Yogabhdshya i. 5; and he makes use of it again in his Bhdmati 2. 1. 5 (page 126) in the sentence “ अन्यथा किरातरातसंकीणदेशनिवासिनो ब्राह्मणायनस्यापि कि TAT”? ॥ कै यद्विरि > न NFA गृह्यते तदप्रत्यक्षम्‌ ll A thing does not become imperceptible because perceived by one who has ascended a mountain peak—tThis saying, quoted from Tantravartika 1. 2. 2. (page 6), appears in the Nydyamanjari (page 422) in the course of a discussion on the sddhutva and asadhutva of words. The passage is as follows :—“ag यदि श्रोत्रकरणकेनैव प्र्ययेन साधुत्वासाधुसवे प्रतिपत्तारः रतिपद्यन्ते व्याकरणाध्ययनवsages प्रतिपद्येरन्‌ । न च प्रतिपद्यन्ते तस्मान्न ते इन्दरियविषये इति । नैष दोषः | वैयाकरणोपदेयसाहायकोपकृतश्रत्रेन्दरियमराद्यत्वाम्युपगमात्‌ । यथा AAMAS AVI saa AAA A UTA न प्रयक्चगम्यतामपोज्छति । यथाह ‘a यद्विरिश्ङ्गमारद्य gaa तदप्रयश्चमिति › uw’? Jayanta quotes it on pages 96 and 222, also. 41 [4
