gloss_en: The simile of the union of man and lion.
explanation: The simile of the union of man and lion. Used to illustrate a particular kind of Alankara consisting of a combination of figures, See the quotations from Sarasvatikanthabharana and Kuvadlayénanda, under क्षीरनीरन्याय. न हि निन्दा निन्द्यं निन्दितुं प्रयुज्यते किं तर्हिं निन्दिता- दितरत्‌ प्रशंसितुम्‌ ॥ Blame is not employed in order to blame something that is blameworthy, but rather to praise something other than that. This is the form taken by the nyaya in S’abara on Jaimini 2, 4. 20. In Tantravartika, page 16, it appears as “a हि निन्दा fei निन्दितुं sada अपि तु विधेयं aga,” and Anandagiri quotes this reading of it in his comment on Brihaddranyakopanisadbhisya 2. 5. 16. The following passage from Agamapramdanya, page 51, admirably illustrates the meaning of the nyaya:—“ag चेदं वेदमूरत्वं पञ्चरात्रतन्राणामनुपपन्नं वेदनिन्दाददौनात्‌ । उक्तं हि चतुषु वेदेषु पुरुषार्थमलभमानः शाण्डिल्य इदं शाखमधीतवानिति | अनवगतवचनव्यक्तेरयं पर्यनुयोगः । न हि निन्दा fed निन्दितुं प्रवर्तते अपि तु निन्दितादितरत्प्ररंसितुम्‌ । यथेतरे- यकव्राह्यणे ‘sa: maga ते वदान्त" इत्यजुदितदोमनिन्दा उदितहोमप्रदंसा- ति गम्यते | यथा मानवे Civ. 124) । ^ ऋग्वेदो देवदैवत्यो यजर्वेदस्तु मानुषः | : सामवेदस्तु पित्यः स्यात्तस्मात्तस्याखचिर्ध्वनिः' ॥ इति सामवेदनिन्दा इतरवेदग्रशंसार्था | यथा वा भारते । ^ चस्वार एकतो वेदा भारतं चैकमेकतः। समागतैस्तु अरषिभिस्तुलयारोपितं पुरा ॥ महस्वे च गुरुत्वे च ध्रियमाणं यतोऽधिकम्‌ | ASA THATS महाभारतमुच्यते" ॥ इति महाभारतग्रशंसार्थेति' गृह्यते a वेदनिन्देति । एव पञ्चरात्नप्रशंसेति गस्यते* ॥ 6 42 Another reference to the nyaya will be found in Vyayamanjari page 278. नहि भवति aca: प्रतिपक्षो eftormaner ॥ A hyena does not find a suitable opponent in a young _ fawn. This may be contrasted with the saying “a हि कठोरकण्ठीरवस्य कुरङ्गद्यावः प्रतिभटो भवति.” It is found in the Nyayavartikatatparyatika, page 33:—“geyaet हि मिथः म्रतिपश्चौ भवतो ag दुवलोत्तमबलौ । न दि भवति तरुः प्रतिपक्षो हरिणशावकस्य किन्तु समरकण्डूनिश्चविषाणकोटिससुदिखितगण्डदौलस्य rarer” ॥ न हि भिश्वुकाः सन्तीति स्थाल्यो नाधिश्रीयन्ते न च मृगाः सन्तीति यवा नोप्यन्ते ॥ Men do not refrain from setting the cooking-pots on the Jire because there are beggars [ who may come to ask for some of the contents ], nor do they abstain from sowing barley because there are wild animals [ which may devour it}. This oft-quoted saying appears three times in the Mahabhdsya, namely in 1. 1. 39 ( vart. 16), 4. 1. 1 ( vart. 15), and 6. 1. 13 (vart. 13), and this is probably the original source of it. I have met with it in two of Vacaspatimis’ra’s works, as follows. In the Nydyavartika tatparyatika, page 62:—‘at खल्वयं परेश्चावतां समाचारो यदुःखभिया सुखपरित्याग इति अपि तु सुखं दुःखाद्विभिद्योपाददते दुःखं च वजेयन्ति | न हि गाः सन्तीति seat नोप्यन्ते भिश्चुकाः सन्तीति स्थाल्यो नाधिश्रीयन्त इतिः? Similarly, on page 441 of 76 8870९. In Bhamati, page 54, we 2९90: -^तस्मादःखभयान्नानुदरलवेदनीयमै दिकं वासु- fre वा सुखं परिलक्तेसुचितम्‌ । न हि खगाः सन्तीति शालयो नोप्यन्ते भि- gat: सन्तीति स्थाल्यो नाधिश्रीयन्ते ? ॥ The same passage, with a good deal of the preceding context, reappears, without acknowledgment, in the Charvaka chapter of the Sarvadars’anasangraka, We find the saying in a modified form in the Panca43 0416, page 68:--“अतोऽजी्णभयान्नाहारपर्तयागो भिश्चुकभयान्न स्थाल्या अनधिश्रयणं दोषेषु प्रतिविधातव्यमिति न्यायः ॥ It appears in this form in Jivanmuktiviveka, (page 8) also, and is there ascribed to Anandabodhacarya. See 18 प्रमाणमालखा page 21. Then we have the well-known verse, Hitopades'a ii. 50:— “दोषभीतेरनारम्भः कापुरुषस्य लक्षणम्‌ । कैरजीर्णभयाद्धातर्भोजनं परिहीयते ॥” बीजं परिकर्मसहस्रेणापि न दि स्यामाकवीजं परिकर्मसदसेणापि कलमाङ्कराय कल्पते ॥ Not even by the employment of a thousand different proceases can S'yamaka grain be made to germinate as rice, ‘Vicaspatimis’ra was fond of this kind of saying. That above 18 from his Nydyavartikatatparyatika, page 55, and another of the same class occurs twice in the Bhdmati. On page 180 (1. 2. 18 ) ^ नहि जातु वटाङ्करः कुटजवीजाजायतेः” and on page 704 (4. 1. 1. ) “न
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 41

gloss_en: The edge of a sword, even though very keen, is not employed to cut itself.
explanation: The edge of a sword, even though very keen, is not employed to cut itself. The nyaya is found in this form in Syadvddamanjart, page 89, in combination with that which immediately follows ; and Mr. Thomas, the Librarian at the India Office, tells me that he has met with the two together in Nagarjuna’s इश्वरकतत्वनिराकरण, but there the sword-nyaya takes the form of “q हि खरतरकरवालधारा स्वमात्मानं छेत्त समथ भवति.” In Madhyamakavritti, page 62, it again occurs in conjunction with another simile—“qerfy नाम तयैवासिधारया सैवासिधारा न Watt SA न तेनैवा- yeaa तदेवाङ्खल्यग्रं शक्यते स्प्रष्टुं &९. For the latter, see Third Handful. Further instances will be found in Tatparyatika, page 255; Nydyamakaranda, page 131; and others of a like nature in Venkatanatha’s Sarvdrthasiddhi, page 391, न हि सुशिक्षितोऽपि acag: खस्कन्धमधिरोदुं पटुः ॥ No young actor, however well-trained, is clever enough to get on his own shoulder. This is Mallisena’s version of the nyaya, as cited in conjunction with the cognate one above. In Brahmasitrabhisya 8. 8. 54, S'ankara quotes it as “a हि az: शि- क्षितः सन्स्वस्कन्धमधिरोक्षयति.° In the vartika on Taittiriyabhasya, page 108, Sures'vara puts it thus:-—“‘are स्वस्कन्धमारोदुं निपुणोऽषीह साधकः” Other varieties are the following. “a हि सुशिक्षितोऽपि वि- ज्ञानी स्वेन स्कन्धेनात्मानं वोदुसुत्सहते, Bhamati 1. 8. 41 ( page 277 ); “a हि सुशिक्षितोऽपि acag: स्वस्कन्धमारद्य gure,” Khandanakhandakhadya, page 592; and, finally, “a हि पटुतरोऽपि azag: स्वस्कन्धमारद्य atrata’, Vidydsagart on Khandana, page 57. It will thus be seen that no two authors agree as to the form of the maxim ! प 45 ५ at
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 44

gloss_en: A lamp does not illuminate until it [ i.
explanation: A lamp does not illuminate until it [ i. ९. its light ] reaches the object to be illwminated. It therefore comes under the head of भ्राप्यकारी, for which, and its opposite, see Nydyakandali, page 23. It occurs in the Vydyamanjari on 5. 1. 7 (page 624): “सोऽयं हेतुः प्राप्य वा साध्यं साधयेदभ्राप्य वा । प्राप्य चेद्‌ द्रयोैन्धस्वरूपयोरप्रा्तिभैवतीति किं कस्य साध्यं साधनं वेतयाविदोषः। अप्राप्य तु साधकल्वमयु- पपन्नमतिप्रसङ्गात्‌ | न द्यप्राप्य प्रदीपः प्रका्यं प्रकादायतीति"? Also in 10८7०186 page 271:—“ हि दाह्यमम्रा्तो दहनो दहति भरकार्यमग्राप्य प्रदीपः प्रकादायति.'° Then in Sarvarthasiddhi (on Tattvanuktakalapa i. 32 ) we 268त:-“अग्रासोत्पादने सर्वसमात्सर्वसुत्पयेत प्रकारयमप्राप्य वा दीपः प्रकारयेदाद्यमम्राप्य वा दहनो दहेत्‌.” Compare Nagarjuna's karika vij. 11:--अभ्रप्यैव प्रदीपेन यदि वा निहतं तमः । इहस्थः स्ंोकस्थं स तमो निहनिष्यति ॥ नान्यदृषटं स्मरत्यन्यः ॥ One person does not remember what another has seen, This is the first pada of Kuswmdnjali i. 15, the whole verse being as follows:— “नान्यदृ्टं स्मरलयन्यो नैकं भूतमपक्रमात्‌ | वासनासंक्रमो नासि न च गल्यन्तरं स्थिरे" ॥ Professor Cowell translates thus:—“ One does not remember what another has seen; the body remains not one and the same from decay; there cannot be transference of impressions, and if you accept a non-momentary existence there is no other means.” The karika, however, is hardly intelligible apart from the preceding context of which it is a sort of summing up. The nyaya did not, however, orginate with Udayana, since it is quoted in Vyasa’s Yogabhdasya iii. 14, and in Nydyabhasya 1. 1. 10. It is found, too, in Syddvadamanjari, pages 61 and 154; also in Nydyamanjari, page 437, line 10. 46 :
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 45

gloss_en: Not even a thousand blind travellers can discover the road [to be taken].
explanation: Not even a thousand blind travellers can discover the road [to be taken]. This is contained in Bh@mati 1. 1. 5 (page 124), in the following passage:-—“q हि प्राधानिकान्यन्तर्वहिष्करणानि त्रयोदशा सच्वम्रधानान्यपि सखयमेवाचेतनानि तदत्तयश्च स्वं वा परं वा वेदितसु्सहन्ते । नो खल्वन्धाः सहखमपि पान्थाः पन्थानं विदन्ति । aera चैकेन Baa स एव तहि मार्गदर्शी AAS: कतौ नेता तेषाम्‌" ॥ पण्डकमुद्धाह्य मुग्धायाः पुत्रपरा्थनम्‌ ॥ It is better to leave this untranslated. The पण्डकोपाख्यान is found in Vyasa’s Yogabhashya ii. 24, as follows:—“‘axy कश्चि- त्पण्डकोपाख्यानेनोद्धारयति | सुगधया भा्यायाभिधीयते । पण्डक आथपुत्र अपत्यवती मे भगिनी किमर्थं नाम नाहमिति । स तामाह तस्तेऽहमपल्यसु- स्पादयिष्यामीति ? ॥ On this Vacaspatimis'ra remarks:—* aq कश्चिन्नास्तिकः कैवल्यं पण्डकोपाख्यानेनोपहसति ” ॥ The ग, as given above, is found in the Nydyavartikatatparyatika, page 29:— % यदि हि पक्षं विहाय बहिरेव सपक्षासपश्षयोरविनाभावो गम्येत तदा बहिर्व्या- सिमात्रबटेन पश्चधर्मोऽपि faa पक्ष साध्यं साधयेत्‌ | असिद्धा हि तत्र स्रसाध्येन ama: । तदेतत्पण्डकमुद्राद्य सुधायाः युत्रप्रार्थनमिव? ॥ See, too, Citsu. 117 ii. 26 ( Pandit, vol. v.page 514) where reference is made to Vacaspati’s use of the nyaya; and the same objection is taken to it by S'riharsha in the Khandanakhandakhadya, page 354.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 46

gloss_en: Professor Venis tells me that the Benares pandits regard this as a shortened form of the भिश्चुपादम्रसारणन्याय (for which, see the First Handful of maxims ), -and that it means “unduly extending one’s claim or one’s position generally.
explanation: Professor Venis tells me that the Benares pandits regard this as a shortened form of the भिश्चुपादम्रसारणन्याय (for which, see the First Handful of maxims ), -and that it means “unduly extending one’s claim or one’s position generally.” Its equivalent in Marathi is पाय पसरणें, which, Molesworth tells us, means “to establish one’s self freely and fully: to extend one’s power far and wide.” He gives, as an example of its use, the Marathi 9. proverb “yzia दिली ओसरी wz पाय qa,” which is the equivalent of our “Give him an inch and he'll take an ell.” Inthe passages, however, in which I have met with the expression, it seems to imply a dogged adherence to a position in spite of previous failure, and when there is little prospect of future success. Two passages in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha pages 798 and 907, seem to confirm this:—“ugedaa@ea कृत्वा पादप्रसारिकाम्‌ | विवेकचश्चुः सम्मील्य aoa ननु जन्तवः ॥ “ ततो विषादमापन्नः सर्वकर्म॑- पराद्धखः | स्थितोऽहं मौनमारुम्न्य कत्वा पादप्रसारिकाम्‌ ?' ॥ There are two other instances of it on pages 656, 657 of the same, and it occurs three times in the Vydyamanjar?, as follows. On page 113:—“ug हि द्विविधं प्रतिबन्धमनुमेयाव्यभिचारनिबन्धनमनुक्त्वा केवरुसा- इचर्यनियममात्रवर्णनं यत््रसारिका सैवेति । उच्यते । पादभ्रसाःिकैव साधीयसी स्थूखदृष्टिभिरवरंबिता वरं न सूक्ष्मृष्टिभिस्त्मेक्षितास्तादार््यादि्रतिबन्धाः `” ॥ On page 121:—“¢ कंचिदर्थमारोक्य यः कश्चिन्नावगम्यते | कंचिदेवाक्षिपल्यdaa: कश्चिदिति स्थितिः ॥ तत्र वस्तुस्वभावोऽयमिति पादप्रसारिका | दयते ह्यविनाभूतादथौद्थान्तरे मतिः? ॥ On page 504:—“q च न कदाचिदनी- र जगदिति पादग्रसारिकामात्रं कतुसुचितं सर्गम्रबन्धम्रखयग्रबन्धस्य समधथि- तत्वादिति ॥ अतश्च पक्ान्तरदुर्वरत्वाद्यथोदितः सिध्यति भूतवर्गः। तं यस्तु पश्यन्नपि निहववीत ae नमः पण्डितरोखरायः” ॥ There is one instance of it in “Khandanakhandakhadya (page 81) also:—‘a च सनत्ताभेदानन्त्यमस्येवेल्यपि पादप्रसारिका निस्तारायः? which is rendered by Prof. Ganganatha Jha:—*Nor will you escape from this predicament by taking the long step of assuming an infinity of different kinds of real existence.” Indian Thought, page 17.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 46

gloss_en: Leaving the sweet morsel he licks lis hand! It is found ia Pajcapadika, page 49, as follows -- “अथ वेदाधिकरणे वेदांशचके सन्निकर्षमिति विोषाभिधानाद्रदिकसत्वसिद्धिरिति । सोऽयमाभाणको रोके पिण्डaaa करं BAe सूत्रकारस्याप्यकौडारं प्रदतं स्यात्‌" ॥ In Raghnnatha’s list it appears as पिण्डं हित्वा करं छेडि.
explanation: Leaving the sweet morsel he licks lis hand! It is found ia Pajcapadika, page 49, as follows -- “अथ वेदाधिकरणे वेदांशचके सन्निकर्षमिति विोषाभिधानाद्रदिकसत्वसिद्धिरिति । सोऽयमाभाणको रोके पिण्डaaa करं BAe सूत्रकारस्याप्यकौडारं प्रदतं स्यात्‌" ॥ In Raghnnatha’s list it appears as पिण्डं हित्वा करं छेडि. We may compare it with the saying “ett विहायारोचकम्रस्तः सौवीररुचिमनुभवतिः. 48
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 47

gloss_en: The simile of a father’s conforming to [the ways of] his little child, This is set forth as a model for the knower of Brahma, that, by a lowly and humble demeanour, he may attract the ignorant.
explanation: The simile of a father’s conforming to [the ways of] his little child, This is set forth as a model for the knower of Brahma, that, by a lowly and humble demeanour, he may attract the ignorant. It is thus explained in the Laukikanydyasangraha:—wagee तत्त्वविदोऽतत्वविदुद्धारातिरिक्तकरतैव्याभावाद्यथा तदुद्धारः स्यात्तथैव कर्तव्यम्‌ | सुरनदरादिपूज्येनापि विदुषा पित्रनुसृतस्तनंधयन्या- येनान्लोऽनुसतंव्यः । तेर्निन््यमानोऽपि देहस्य निन््यत्वमात्मनोऽवाङ्मनसगम्यत्वं च जानन्नोधिजेत्‌ | किन्तु प्रत्युत aera स्वयमप्याचरेत्‌' ॥ In the larger work the following passage is quoted by way of illustration—*‘ तदुक्तं ae)... अविद्भद नुसारेण तिदस्य युज्यते | स्तनंधया- नुसारेण aaa तत्पिता यतः ॥ अधिक्षिक्षस्ताडितो वा बारेन स्वपिता तदा । न छ्िश्नाति न कुप्ये बां प्रत्युत wea ॥ निन्दितः स्तूयमानो वा gaat निन्दति । न स्तौति किन्तु तेषां स्याद्यथा बोधस्तथाचरेत्‌ " ॥ "11५ “elder” is Vidyaranya, and the verses are Pancadas’t ए. 286-288. पिशाचानां पिशाचभाषयैवोत्तरं देयम्‌ ॥ 12185405 should be answered in the Pis'ica language, This nyaya is found on pages 214 and 410 of Sarvarthasiddhi (on Tattvamuktakalapa ii, 49 and iv. 13 ), in the first instance in conjunction with “यक्षानुरूपो बलिः °? with which it is clearly synonymous. See “यादो यक्षस्तादद्रो ate: ””.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 48

gloss_en: The simile of a stout cudgel.
explanation: The simile of a stout cudgel. Such a stick, hurled at a yelping cur, may at the same time strike and silence other dogs near it; and so the nyaya seems to be used somewhat in the sense of “Killing two birds with one stone.” It is thus defined by Raghunatha:—‘ एकतान्रिकमतनिरासाय प्रयुक्तया युक्तया तत्सइरमतान्तरनिराकरणं थदा विवक्ष्यते तदा GESTS: । यथा बहूनां नां मध्य एकस्य Ba: wens alae: युष्टगुडस्तं प्रह्लयान्यानपि प्रहरति तथा ब्रह्मकारणवादिभिः सांख्याभिमतप्रधानकारणवादनिरासाय प्रयुक्ता ` दक्ष तेनांशब्दं" ‘ रचनानुपपत्तेश्च नानुमानं ` इद्यादिसमन्वयाविरोधाध्यायगतसूत्रस्था 49 युक्तयस्तं निरस्य योगाभिमतभ्रधानकारणवादमपि साम्याननिराकर्वन्तीति दिक्‌" ॥ The simile is employed in this sense in Advaitabrahmasiddhi, page 100:--“न चेद दोषत्रयं वैदोषिकेषूक्तं ata विल्ञानवादिषु किमर्थमापद्यत इति वाच्यम्‌ । पुषटलगुडन्यायेन वैशेषिकमत इव “अन्तः सत्‌" इतिवादिनो ‘aft: aa’ इतिवादिनश्च बौद्धस्य मतेऽपि प्रसरतीतयभि प्रायात्‌ः॥ It is akin 0 प्रघानमह्धनिबहेणन्याय, which see in First Handful.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 48

gloss_en: The simile of a lamp.
explanation: The simile of a lamp. We have here another of the many lamp-illustrations. In Mahdbhdsya 1. 1. 49 ( vart. 4) an adhikara is said to be of three kinds, and in the first it is likened to a lamp in the following words:—“gfigze2erea: समै शाखमभि- wert यथा प्रदीपः सुप्रज्वङ्ति एकदेशस्थः स्वै वेरमाभिज्वल्यति.” In the opening part of 2, 1. 1, where the question is asked «कः पुन रथिकारपरिभाषयोर्विरोषः; the paribhdsd, and not the adhikara, is likened to a lamp, in the same words as above. Nages’a (in vol. iii. page 8 of the Uddyotw) quotes the following verse:-— ^ एकदेशस्थिता शाखभवने याति दीपताम्‌ । परितो व्याष्रतां माषां परिभाषां प्रचक्षते ” ॥ We find the same figure in Jaimini’s siitra 11, 1. 60, which S‘abara explains thus:—“sdiq एकस्मिन्देशे भुञ्जानानां बाद्यणानामे- कस्य arn प्रञ्वरितः सर्वेषासुपकारं करोति.” In dealing with this adhikarana Madhava substitutes the figure of a single dancer amusing a number of spectators. See the न्वकन्याय in Third Handful.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 49

gloss_en: This nyiya, which is quoted by Amaradisa in his tika on Vedantas'ikhamani, page 262, is apparently another form of the more concise प्रसज्यभ्रतिषेध which, as it occurs in the Yoga section of Sarvadars'anasangraha, is rendered by Prof.
explanation: This nyiya, which is quoted by Amaradisa in his tika on Vedantas'ikhamani, page 262, is apparently another form of the more concise प्रसज्यभ्रतिषेध which, as it occurs in the Yoga section of Sarvadars'anasangraha, is rendered by Prof. Cowell 7 50 । “Express negation.” In a footnote (on page 250 ) he explains it thus :—* Where the negation is prominent it is called prasajyapratishedha, ; but where it is not prominent we have the paryuddsa negation. In the former, the negative is connected with the verb: in the latter, it is generally compounded with some other word; as, for example, (a) ‘Not a drum was heard, not a funeral note’. (b) ‘Unwatched the garden bough shall sway’. The former corresponds to the logicians’ atyantdbhdva, the latter to anyonyabhdva or bheda”. In the Vacaspatyam the nyaya is quoted under प्रसज्यप्रतिषेध as follows :--श्रसज्य प्रसक्ति सम्पाद्यारोप्येति याव्प्रतिषेधः | अ्यन्ताभावे । “प्रसक्तं हि प्रतिषिध्यत › इति न्यायेन आरोपितप्रसंगस्यैव निषेधः । तेन वायौ रूपं नासतीत्यादावपि वायौ रूपारोपं कृत्वैव निषेधो नना बोध्यत इति विवेकः 0” According to this, then, the meaning of the पङ is “that which has been applied or asserted is subsequently withdrawn or denied.” Both the forms of negation are contained in Mahabhasya 1. 4. 50 &९., in Vakyapadiya ii. 86, and in Sures’vara’s large vartika 3. 9. 73. Verses defining the two are quoted on page 214 (Chap. vii.) of the Sahityadarpana, and renderings will be found on page 254 of Mr. Pramadadasa’s translation. Compare Molesworth’s definitions of the terms.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 49

gloss_en: The principle that whatever has no result of its own, but is mentioned in connection with something else which has such a result, is subordinate to the latter.
explanation: The principle that whatever has no result of its own, but is mentioned in connection with something else which has such a result, is subordinate to the latter. This is Dr. Thibaut’s rendering of the nyaya as it occurs in Brahmasitrabhashya 2. 1. 14 (page 443), and he explains it thus in a footnote:-— “A Mimamsa principle. A sacrificial act, for instance, is independent when a special result is assigned to it by the sacred texts; an act which is enjoined without such a specification is merely auxiliary to another act.” The source of the nyaya is 51 S'abara 4.4.19, and Madhava applies it in Nydyamdlavistara 4. 3. 16 (stitra 37). I have met with it also in Nydyavartikatatparyatikad, page 178, line 2; and in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 117, line 11; and page 147, line 9 from bottom.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 50

gloss_en: The simile of the capture of a crane.
explanation: The simile of the capture of a crane. Raghunath explains it thus:—A man wishing to secure a crane puts butter on its head, which, when melted by the sun, goes into its eyes and blinds it, so that he can then take hold of it! He clearly took this explanation from the Tattvadipana, a commentary on the Pancapadikivivarana (itself a commentary ), and I subjoin a portion of each. Vivarana, page 283, line 4:— “ag स्वगैकामिनो यागकतंब्यता स्वर्मसाधनमन्तरेणानुपपन्ना । तच ANTAL क्षणभंगिनः कमणो मध्यवर्तिकाथेमन्तरेणानुपपन्नमिति श्चुतार्थापत्याऽपूर्वं गम्यते तत्र शब्दस्य सामर्थ्य गद्यत sta सोऽयं बकवन्धः°॥ On this the Dipana, page 779, bottom line :-—“ बकबन्ध इति | बकबन्धसमानन्याय gua: ॥ बकम्रहणे क उपाय इति केनचिष््ष्टे खरतरदिनकरसंपकौत्तन्मस्तकानिहितनवनीतविन्दुभिर्नयनयोः wari ase सुकरमिति कश्चिततच्छमतिः dare न च तदुपपद्यते | बकम्रहणमन्तरेण तन्मस्तके नवनीतपरक्षेपानुपपत्तेः | तसिश्च परि- गृहीते तस्प्क्षेपोऽपि सुधा.” Then follows his application of the nyaya. Both writers evidently regard it as an illustration of something ridiculous; and to me it recalls the nursery tradition that the way to catch a sparrow is to put salt on its tail! Raghunatha, however, classes it with nyayas deprecating a roundabout way of doing a thing. Amongst these he gives the दण्डसर्पमारणन्याय, where a man whilst looking for a stick with which to kill a snake, comes upon an axe; but, instead of using that against the enemy, he goes out to cut a stick with it. In Vivaranaprameyasungraha, page 262, line 9, we again find the बकबन्धप्रयास. 52
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 51

gloss_en: The illustration of whispering in the ear of a deaf ११2१, A good example is found in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 1062:— “बधिरे कर्णजापोऽयमन्पे वृत्तप्रददीनम्‌ । ऊषरे degree या ध्मदेराना ” ॥ Compare the following from Nydyamanjari, page 405:— “azaghre रामायणे वणितमस्माभियं एवमपि gear वेदार्थपरिगमाभ्युपायं waa.
explanation: The illustration of whispering in the ear of a deaf ११2१, A good example is found in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 1062:— “बधिरे कर्णजापोऽयमन्पे वृत्तप्रददीनम्‌ । ऊषरे degree या ध्मदेराना ” ॥ Compare the following from Nydyamanjari, page 405:— “azaghre रामायणे वणितमस्माभियं एवमपि gear वेदार्थपरिगमाभ्युपायं waa.” Also the expression “बधिरेष्विव गायनम्‌?” in Naiskarmyasiddhi iv. 21. For similes of a like kind, see अरण्यरोदनन्याय. aghequendiaeara: ॥ The simile of a lamp in a vessel with many holes. Raghunatha explains it as follows—“agufegrr afetataa जीवोपा- धिभूता धीबाद्यविषयान्व्यासोति तद्योगाच्च चिदाभासोऽपि faa ga प्रती- यत इति विवक्षायां बडुच्छिद्रघटभ्रदीषन्यायोऽवतरति | अयं भगवत्पादैः aa पेण भाष्यतात्पयंप्रकादाके श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तिस्तोत्रे सोदाहरणसुक्तः । “नानाछिद्रघटोद्रस्थितमहादीपग्रभाभास्वरं ज्ञानं यस्य तु चश्चुरादिकरणद्रारा बहिः स्यन्दते | ` जानामीति तमेव भान्तमनुभाेतत्समस्तं जगत्तस्य ame नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूतंये' इति? ॥ The above is verse 4 of S'ankara’s poem, to the exposition of which Sures’vara devotes 37 verses in his Manasollisa.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 52

gloss_en: The illustration of the lowering of one part of a load [ and so easing one’s burden].
explanation: The illustration of the lowering of one part of a load [ and so easing one’s burden]. This is found under Tantravdrtika 1.3. 22 (page 222 ):--“टढविपर्ययन्ञानानन्तरं सहसैव च सम्यग्न्ञानो- स्पादातिभाराद्धरिकदेक्ञावतरणा्थं संदायोत्थापनामात्रमेव तावदुक्तम्‌.” Prof. Ganganatha Jha renders the passage thus:—‘When a certain conclusion to the contrary has been laid out in an exceptionally 53 strong manner, if one proceeds to immediately point out the true theory, it involves a very hard work; and hence with a view to lighten this burden, the present siitra proceeds only to weaken the contrary view by throwing it open to doubt.” Jayanta Bhatta reproduced this on page 419 of the Wydyamanjari as 10110 ,8.--ूर्वपक्षिकोक्तयुक्तिसमुत्थापितस्थिरतरविपरययक्ञानसमनन्तरं सहसैव सम्यग््ानोत्पादनातिभाराद्धारेकदेश्ावतरणन्यायेन संद्ायस्तावदुपपद्यते.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 52

gloss_en: The illustration of the three Asuras, Bhima, 21650, and Dridha.
explanation: The illustration of the three Asuras, Bhima, 21650, and Dridha. See this explained under दामनग्यालकटन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 53

gloss_en: The simile of the bird Bhiilinga.
explanation: The simile of the bird Bhiilinga. It is supposed to say “ma । sahasam,’ “don’t do anything desperate”, and then does desperate deeds itself! The purport of the nyaya would therefore seem to be, “Practise what you preach.” There are two references to this bird in Sabhdparva. The first is in xt 18 (Bombay edition )—‘q गाथागाथिनं शास्ति ag चेदपि गायति । प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि भूटिङ्गशकुनिर्यैथा?' ॥ This is explained by the second passage ( XLV. 27-32.):—“ory चेषां नते बुद्धिः safe याति भारत। मयैव कथितं a भूरिङ्गशकुनियंथा ॥ २७॥ भूलिङ्गराऊुनिर्नाम ` पार्थ हिमवतः परे । भीष्म तस्याः सदा वाचः श्रूयन्तेऽ्थविगर्हिताः ॥ २८ ॥ मा साहसभितीदं सा सततं aad किल । साहसं चात्मनातीव चरन्ती नावबुध्यते ॥२९॥ सा हि मांसा्मैरं भीष्म सुखात्सिहस्य खादतः । दन्तान्तरविल्नं यत्तदादन्तेऽल्पचेतना ॥ ३० ॥ इच्छतः सा हि सिंहस्य भीष्म जीवलयसंद्ायम्‌ | ARATE सदा वाचः प्रभाषसे ॥ ३१॥ इच्छतां भूमिपाानां भीष्म जीवस्यसंशयम्‌ | रोकविद्विष्टक्मा हि नान्योऽस्ति मवता समः wary mn the Calcutta edition of 1834, the chapters are XL and XLIII respectively. Raghunatha’s remark on the simile is as follows ~~ 54 ५ यो भूलिङ्गन्यायेन परोपदेशमात्रकुश्षलः स्वयं च यथेष्टाचरणक्ीरः सोऽपि दांभिकत्वाच्यक्तव्यः । भूलिङ्गाख्यः पक्षी मा साहसं कुर्विति पुनः पुनस्चर्वदन्सि- इरदष्रान्तलम्नं मांसर्वं Radia असिद्धम्‌ ॥? For the following interesting example of the application of this nyaya (though the bird is not mentioned by name) I am indebted to my friend Mr. C. H. Tawney, ©. 1. E. It is found on pages 138-9 of the Parisistaparvan:—“ ततः कमलरवत्यूचे हे नाथ FASTA! मासाहसदङुनिवन्मा त्वं साहसिको भव ॥ १४१ ॥ तथा Be पुमान्देशान्तरे दुर्भिक्चपीडितः | चचार स्वजनं हित्वा सार्थेन महता सह ॥ ५४२ ॥ एकस्यां च महाटव्यां साथे आवासिते सति । आहरत वृणकाष्टादि स॒ एकोऽपि विनिर्ययौ ॥ १४३ ॥ तदा च सुक्षवयाघ्रास्यात्क्ष्येको वनगब्हरे | दन्तरस्नामिषखण्डान्यादा- यारोहदंहिषम्‌ ॥१४४॥ मा साहसमिति BE: स भणन्मांसखादकः | शकुनिस्तेन जगदे पुरुषेण सविस्मयम्‌ ॥ १४५ ॥ रोषि मा साहसमिति व्याघ्राखान्मांसमत्सि च । मुग्धस्त्वं दरयसे वाचोऽनुरूपं Set न च ॥ १४६ ॥ हित्वा साक्षाद्धवसुखं तददषटसुखेच्छया | तपश्चिकीषुसत्वमसि मासाहसखगोपमः' ॥ १४७ ॥ An interesting conversation on the inconsistency of not practising what one preaches (though not in connection with this nyaya ) is found also in the Bhagavata Purdna x. 83. 27-40. भौतविचारन्य यः ॥ The simile of the reflections of a madman. The story connected with this is told in the following passage of Atmatattvaviveka, page 64—“ तचेद्िचारासहं किं तेन भोतविचारकल्पेन । तथाहि केनचिद्धौतेन राजद्वारि द्िरदमारोक्य विकल्पितं क्रिमयमन्धकारो मूख्कमच्या- होखिजनरुवाहो बराकान्वषैति गर्जति च । यद्वा बान्धवोऽयं राजद्वारे स्मान च यस्तिष्ठति स बान्धवः" इति परमाचार्यवचनात्‌* | अथवा योऽयं भूमौ द्यते तस्य छायेति दूषितं च । तत्र नाद्स्तस्य सूरपयुगलग्रस्फोटनामावात्‌ | न द्वितीयस्तस्य स्तंमचतुष्टयाभावात्‌ । न तृतीयस्तस्य खगुडश्रामणाभावात्‌ । न चतुरथस्तस नरदिरःशतोद्धिरणाभावात्‌ | ततो न किञ्चिदिदमिति । किमेतावता reer radar” ॥ * Pancatantra V. 41 ( Indische Spriiche 1221 ). 55
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 53

gloss_en: The supposition that the light of a gem is itself the gem.
explanation: The supposition that the light of a gem is itself the gem. This follows Mr, A. E. Gough’s explanation of a slightly varied form of the nyaya which is found inthe Bauddha section of Sarvadars'anasangraha. He adds that, in this case, “we may yet handle the gem, because it underlies the light, while, if we were to take nacre for silver, we could not lay hold of any silver.” The correctness of this view is established by an important passage at the beginning of Pancadas’t Ix, which treats of ध्यान as ameans of arriving at a right knowledge of Brahman. Such meditation, being directed towards Brahman with qualities, is of course erroneous, inasmuch as that Impersonality has no qualities; but it nevertheless leads to the underlying nirguna Brahman, just as the mistaken notion regarding the sparkle of the gem leads to the discovery of the gem itself. This is styled संवादिभ्रम; an error which hasa corresponding reality underlying it. To mistake the distant shining of a lamp through the keyhole of a door for a gem, is an illustration of विसवादिश्चरम) an error entirely devoid of an underlying reality. The passage is as 1011078 “मणिप्रदीपप्रभयो- ्मणिबुच्याभिधावतोः । मिथ्यान्ञानाविशेषेऽपि विरोषोऽथैक्रियां प्रति ॥ २ u दी- पोऽपवरकस्यान्तरवत्तते त्भा बहिः । द्यते द्ा्यथान्यत्र तद दष्टा मणेः प्रभा ॥ ३ ॥ दूरे प्रभाद्वयं दृष्टा मणिबुद्वाभिधावतोः । प्रभायां मणिबुद्धिस्तु मिथ्या ज्ञानं द्वयोरपि ॥४॥ न saat मणिर्दपिप्रभां प्रयभिधावता । प्रभायां धावतावर्यं seta मणिमैणेः ॥ ५॥ दीपग्रभामणिभ्रान्तिर्विसंवादिभ्रमः eae: | मणिग्रभामणिश्रान्तिः संवादिभ्रम उच्यते ॥ ६॥ The commentator, Ramakrishna, ascribes verses 2-5 to a vdrtika; whilst Citsukha Muni, in his comment on verse 2 which is quoted in the Nydyamakaranda (page 148), names Dharmakirti as its author. This is not improbable; for Dharmakirti is known to have composed véartikas on the works of Dignaga, a famous Buddhist writer of the sixth century (See Mr. K. B. Pathak’s paper “On the authorship of the Nyayabindu”). In Nyayamanjari, pages 24 (line 1), 88 (line 4 from hottom ), and 158 56 (line 10), the nyaya is found! as सणिप्रभामणिबुद्धिवत्‌. Then on page 308 (line 9 from bottom ) there is the following passage which corresponds with the extract from Pancadas't, namely:— अर्थ हि मूलवर्तिनसुपलम्य प्रवतैमानस्तमामोति अपवरकनिहितमणिप्रसतायां ऊुच्चिकाविवरनिर्मतायामिव प्रभायां मणिबुच्या प्रवर्तमानः | यत्र तु मूलेऽप्यर्थो नास्ति तत्र व्यामोहास्मवतैमानो विप्रलभ्यते दीपग्रभायामिव तथैव agen प्रवर्तमानः ॥ The nyaya occurs again on page 317. Other references to it are S’alika, page 22, line 4; Nydyakandali, page 190; Atmatattvaviveka, page 45; and Tarkika१286, page 16.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 55

gloss_en: The illustration afforded by the sale of gems.
explanation: The illustration afforded by the sale of gems. It is intended to teach that, in disposing of precious stones, one who understands their value will derive greater advantage than one who is without that knowledge. This would undoubtedly be the case if the seller were a S’abara and the buyer a dealer in gems! The illustration is S’ankara’s, and is used by him in his exposition of Chhdndogya 1. 1. 10, which sets forth the value of an intelligent use of the syllable Om. The passage is as follows :—* तेनोभौ get यश्चैतदेवं वेद यश्च न वेद । नाना तु विद्या चाविद्या च । यदेव विद्यया करोति..-तदेव वीर्यवत्तरं भवति. An objector here urges that the result of an action does not depend upon the intelligence of the performer of it, but on the due perforance of the act itself, and he supports his view with the following homely illustration “es हि लोके हरीतकीं भक्षयतोस्तद्रसाभिनज्ञेतरयोर्विरेचनम्‌.*› The Sidhantin disallows this, and gives another illustration :—“ इष्टं हि रोके वणिक्डाबरयोः पद्मरागादिमणिविक्रये वणिजो विक्ञानाधिक्यात्फलाधिक्यम्‌ | तस्माद्यदेव विद्यया विक्तानेन युक्तः सन्करोति aa. dea कम वीर्यवत्तरमविदठत्कर्मेणोऽधिकफलं भवति." ॥ The nyaya is quoted, in a slightly different form, by Anandagiri in his comment on Brahmasitrabhasya 3. 3. 42; and again, by Amalananda, in the same connection, in company with the drug-illustration, 57 मण्डूकवसाक्ताक्षाणां FITTS: ॥ Mistaking bamboos for snakes on the part of those whose eyes have been smeared with the fat of frogs. This curious illustration, taken from Slokavartika, page 520, is found in the following passage of Tatparyatiha, page 314:—“ न च मण्डूकवarmanntaramaadiannaty प्रथमाक्षसन्निपाताद्ंशेषूरगारोप इति साम्प्रतम्‌ । सवौसामेव आान्तीनां प्रमाणगृदीतारोप्यारोपविषयग्रहणपुरःसरत्वनियमात्‌ | तदनुसारेण मण्डूकवसाक्ताक्चाणामपि वंदोषूरगञ्रमो व्याख्येयः । वंशानां तावदस्ति भूयः सारूप्यञुरगेण तेन चेते तन्मात्रेण रूपेण रशाक्तवन्ति गृहीताः स्ारयितुसुरगमस्‌ | एवमपि यदन्येषां मो न भवति तत्र सर्पाकारव्या- दृत्तवंशग्रहो हेतः मण्डूकवसा्जनं च वंशाकारपिधानमात्रहेतुः'” ॥ The S'lokavartika passage containing the nyaya forms the second quotation in the following excerpt from Sarvirthasiddhi on Tattvamuktakalapa ii. 64:—* संको चकानां Resraettoeara चात्र Cama जनयेदीपो निम्बदृन्दाकरेणुमान्‌ ` इतिवक्किञ्चिन्निमित्तमन्तरेण स्वतः- भरा्तप्रकारोकदेशाभङ्गः स्यात्‌ | “मण्डूकवसयाक्ताक्षा वंशाजुरगडुद्धिभिः' इतिवद्रा केनचिद्धेतुना प्रागनुक्टेषु प्रतिकूलबुद्धियक्ता ॥ Another instance of the nyaya is to be found in Parimala, page 48, line 9.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 56

gloss_en: The simile drawn from fish.
explanation: The simile drawn from fish. It is used to illustrate the oppression of the weak by the strong. In Raghunithavarman’s list, it follows the सुन्दोपसुन्दन्याय, and he explains it thus:— “अयं [ सुन्दोपसुन्दन्यायः | तुल्यबख्योर्विरोधे प्रसरति । प्रबलनिवैरविरोधे स~ बलेन निवंलबाधविवक्षायां त मास्सन्यायावतारः | अयं प्राय इतिहासपुराणा- दिषु द्यते । तथाहि वासिष्ठे म्रह्वादाख्याने तत्समा प्रस्तुलयोक्तम्‌ | ‹ एताव ताथ eet तद्रसातरुमण्डलम्‌ | बभूवाराजकं तीक्ष्णं मास्सखन्यायकदर्थि- तम्‌ › ॥ यथा प्रबला ae निवैलांस्तान्नाशायन्ति तथाराजकेऽमुकदेशे प्रवा जना ene न्यायार्थः ? ॥ ‘The verse quoted here is 58 Yogavasistha 5. 87. 7. There is a good example of the usage of this nyaya in Kamandakiya-Nitiséra ii. 40 which reads 8: “परस्परामिषतया जगतो भिन्नवत्मनः | दण्डाभावे परिध्वंसी मात्स्यो न्यायः प्रवर्तते" My friend Mr. Tawney has given me a reference to the commentary on i. 13 of the same work, and also to Kathdsaritsagara cii. 63 which I here subjoin together with his translation ( vol. ii, page 390):—“ नास्तेवाराजकं faSee कोऽपि भ्रजास्वहो | राजचाब्दः सुरैः खृष्टो मात्स्यन्यायभयादयम्‌ `` “There is no race in the world without a king; I do believe the gods introduced the magical name among men in their alarm, fearing that otherwise the strong would devour the weak, as great fishes eat the little.” Kullika gives “जले areatfraneeg:” as a various reading in the second line of Manw vii. 20, and adds “aq qeardt दुवैकान्डिस्युरिति मत्स्यन्याय एव स्यादिव्युक्तम्‌ ` ॥ For this, also, I am indebted to Mr. Tawney.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 57

gloss_en: One who has been seized im order to be put to death, [ gladly] agrees to the amputation of a limb [as an alternative}.
explanation: One who has been seized im order to be put to death, [ gladly] agrees to the amputation of a limb [as an alternative}. The nearest approach to this nyaya of Raghunatha’s is found in the following verse of Bodhicarydvatdra ( vi. 72 ):— “arta: करं fer सुक्तशेक्किमभद्रकम्‌ । मनुष्यदुःखैर्नरकान्युक्तशेत्किमभदरकम्‌ * ॥ On the former part of this, the commentator 888 :-- श्यो हि - मारणमदैति a यदि हस्तमात्रं छित्वा मुच्यते तदा न काचिरक्षतिरस्ति ्रवयुत रब्धलाभमात्मानं मन्यते | अल्यल्पमिदं मरणटुःखात्करच्छेदनदुःखमिति ” | 59 मारतीगन्धगुणविहर्भ न रमते Ale: ॥ The bee that knows the excellence of the perfume of jasmine cares not for darbha grass. This is found in the following passage of Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 1081 :— ^ अत एवागमक्ञस्य या क्रिया सा क्रियोच्यते । आगमक्ञोऽपि यस्तस्यां यथाशक्त्या भ्रवतेते ॥ चिन्तामणिखरूपह्ो दौगत्योपहतो नहि | तद्मा्युपायवैचिन्ये TIAA TAT ॥ न चासौ तत्सवरूपक्लो योऽन्यत्रापि प्रवतेते । मातीगन्धगुणविदभ न रमते ate: ॥ माषरारिप्रविष्टमषीन्यायः ॥ The simile of @ grain of soot in a heap of spotted beans. Perhaps akin to a needle in a haystack. It seems to have originated in that very ancient drama the Mricchakatika where it is found (on page 40) in the following Prakrit passage :— “ger: ॥ भावे भावे बिए क्खु अन्धे ameter विअ मशीगुडिआ दीशान्ती दीदान्ती जेव wer वदान्तशेणिआ ” ॥ ( भाव बरुवल्यन्धकारे माषरारिप्रविष्टेव मज्ञीगुडिका दर्यमानैव TACT वसन्तसेना ). In vol. ix of the Harvard Oriental Series, Dr. A. W. Ryder (in imitation of the इकार ) renders it thus:—“But mashter, it’s pitch dark and it’s like hunting for a grain of soot in a pile of shpotted beans. Now you shee Vasantasené and now you don’t.” The nyaya is quoted in Udayana’s Kirandvali, page 79 :— “a तु माषराशिप्रविष्टमश्ीवन्महाप्रकाशसमाहाराजनक्षयते", and again on pages 208 and 451 of Venkatanatha’s Sarvarthasiddhi, the latter being as follows भयथा माषरादौ मषी यथा वा नीरोत्पखवने काद्म्बसतद्धेदामहात्तद्‌ष्रथगभावेनाभिमन्यते व्यवद्ियते च. ° 60

gloss_en: If Mithila should be in flames nothing of mine would be burnt up.
explanation: If Mithila should be in flames nothing of mine would be burnt up. This is the second line of a verse in S@ntiparva, chapter 178, the first line being “अनन्तं बत मे वित्तं यख मे नासि किञ्चन. It is used to indicate the freedom from anxiety of one who has nothing to lose; like Juvenal’s “Cantabit vacwus coram latrone viator.” S‘ankara quotes the phrase in his exposition of the words “a हास्य कर्म क्षीयते \” in Brihaddranyakopanishad 1. 4. 15:—“a हाख कर्मं क्षीयते । कर्माभावादेवेति निल्यानुवादः । यथावि- gu कर्मश्चयरक्षणं संसारदुःखं सन्ततमेव न तथा तदस्य विद्यत इत्यर्थः । मिधिरायां ade न मे cafe किञ्चनेति यद्वत्‌ ` ॥ It appears also in the following verse of the Khandanakhandakhadya, page 278 :-- « तथाहि मिथिलानाथो quia: पुरा । wed मिथिलादाहे न मे किञ्चन gat” ॥ मुण्डितशिरोनक्षत्रान्वेषणम्‌ ॥ Enquiring as to a suitable date for the shaving of one's head when one has already performed that ceremony! It occurs in the following passage of the Nydyamanjari, page 171 :—“aga: gerd त्िश्चयकरणे दूषणमितरेतराश्रयत्वं वा सुण्डितशि- रोनश्चत्रान्वेषणवद्वैयथ्य वेति वर्णितं amet निषये प्रामाण्यनिश्चयपूर्विकायाः म्रृत्तरभ्युपगमान्नेतरेतराश्चयं चक्रकं वा? ॥ This saying was explained to me by my learned friend the Principal of the Government Sanskrit College at Benares. It is similar to two given by _ Raghunathavarman, namely, “gq कार्ये कं gedaata,” and “a हि विवाहानन्तरं वरपरीक्षा करियते.” 9९० also कृतक्षौरस्य नक्षत्रपरीक्षा in the Third Handful. 61
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 60

gloss_en: The simile of [molten] copper powred into a mould [ and assuming its shape ].
explanation: The simile of [molten] copper powred into a mould [ and assuming its shape ]. Raghunathavarman expounds it thus:— “agra बहिनिःखतस्यान्तःकरणस्य waiters विषयाकारता भवति । तदुक्तं भगवत्पादैः । “मूषासिक्तं यथा at तन्निभं जायते तथा । रूपादीन्ब्यामवचित्तं तन्निभं दस्यते ध्रुवमिति? This verse is Sankaracarya’s Upades‘asdhasri xiv, 3, on which Ramatirtha comments as follows --“भूषान्तःसुषिरा wentat | यथाश्चिसंपकाद्रिवीभूतं at मूषायां निषिक्त Rie सत्तन्निभं जायते तत्समानाङृति भवति तथा चित्तमपि रूपादीन्विषयान्व्याञुवत्तन्निभं दङ्यते तदाकारं जायत इल्य्थः' ॥ I may add that the nyaya which immediately follows this in Raghunatha’s list, namely व्यञ्जकव्य॑ग्यन्याय, is based on the very next verse of the Upades‘asdhasri [“argqat वा यथा रोकः &c.”], and his explanatory remarks are taken verbatim from Ramatirtha’s comment. The nyaya we are now examining appears also in Brahmasitrabhashya 1, 1. 12 in the expression “quilifregaaraientarag’, and in Taittiriyavdrtika (7. 94):—* विद्यादन्नमयेनैव मूषायां द्ुतता्रवत्‌ । सवौन्प्राणमयादींसान्रचितान्पुरुषाङृतीन्‌." मूषिकभक्षितबीजादावङ्करादिजननप्रारथना ॥ Looking for the production of germs when the seed has been eaten by a mouse! This seems to belong to the same category as the काकदृन्तपरीक्षा. It occurs in the Bauddha chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 14 of Jivananda’s edn. ). The whole passage is too long for quotation, but the nyaya-portion is as follows:—“@diat स्थायित्वद्रच्याशा मूषिकभक्षितबीजादावङ्करादिजननप्ा्थनामनुहरेत्‌”' ॥ 62 मृतं दुण्डुभमासाद्य काकोऽपि गरुडायते ॥ ` Even a [cowardly ] crow can assume the bearing of an eagle, when it comes upon a dead lizard! This is the first line of Bodhicarydvatara vii. 72, the second being “ आपदाबाधतेऽल्पापि मनो मे यदि दुर्बलम्‌.” ॥ How true to nature this is!
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 61

gloss_en: He who causes a thing to be done by another is himself the real doer of it.
explanation: He who causes a thing to be done by another is himself the real doer of it. “Facit per alium facit per se”. This nyaya is of common occurrence. There is a good instance of it in Anandagiri’s comment on Brahmasitrabhashya 1. 2. 11, Explaining Mundaka Upanishad 3. 1.1, S'ankara says:—“aqaकैनापि पिबता दधौ पिबन्ताबुच्येते । यद्वा जीवस्तावप्िबतीश्वरस्तु पाययति aerate पिवतीव्युच्यते । पाचयितयैपि पक्तुत्वप्रसिद्धिददनात्‌”” ॥ ५0४ which Anandagiri remarks:—‘qrag@fa | प्रधानकर्तैरि अयोगो गुणकतैरि कथमिलयारशक्याह पाचयितरीति | यः कारयति स करोलेवेति न्याया- Rak: ॥ 866 also Latparyatika, page 187, line 1.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 62

explanation: Anything that has been made is :non-eternal. In other words, that which has a beginning has also an end; except of course, the Naiyayika’s ya4eqrara, which has a beginning but no end! The nyaya is found in the Vydyabindu, page 108, and its converse, यदनिलयं amas, on page 116. The following are additional examples of its use. Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 240, line 3:—“a amas aeufare-arargenontraत्वादिकं मोश्चसख sata”? ॥ Nydyavartikatatparyatika, page 187 line 8 from bottom:—“‘aqafie aed उुञुत्समानायानिलयः weg 63 इ्यनुक्सवा यदेव किंचिदुच्यते कृतकत्वादिति a यकृत तदनित्यमिति वा कृतकश्च शब्द इति वा तस्सर्वमस्यानपेक्षितमापाततोऽसंबद्धाभिधानं तथा चानवहितो न बोद्धमहैतीति | यच्छृतकं तत्सवैमनिलयं यथा घटः ृतकश्च शब्द्‌ इति वचनमर्थसामध्यैनेवपेक्षितदाब्दानित्यत्वनिश्वायकमिलयवधानमत्रेति चेत्न परस्पराश्रयत्वभ्रसंगात्‌` ॥ Part of this latter passage is. quoted in Citsukhi 2. 23 ( Pandit, vol. V. page 27). यदश्वेन हृतं पुरा तत्पश्चाद्रदभः प्रासं केनोपायेन AAT ॥ By what means can a donkey overtake [so as to bring: back] that which has been carried off long before by [one mounted on] a horse? This phrase, borrowed from Tantravartika ( page 780 ), is introduced into the Nydyamanjari ( page 262 ) in the course of a discussion on the relative value and authority of S‘ruti and Smriti, in the following verse :— “सोऽयमाभाणको रोके AAT हतं पुरा । तत्यश्चाद्रदैमः प्रापु केनोपायेन HAT” ॥ According to Kumarila, a man who has accepted the teaching of s‘ruti will not allow it to be upset by a contradictory smriti, and vice versd. This is expressed, as follows, in two passages of Tantravartika 1. 8. 8. (as pointed out by the editor of Nydyamanjart. )--“न च श्रुतिजनितप्र्ययस्य csfraltat बाधकत्वं श्रतिपद्यते ॥ स्मात्तस्य बाधकः श्रौतो बरुवस्वाद्रतीयते | प्रयक्षे चानुमाने च भ्रागेतद्यवधारितम्‌ ॥ ...... न च शीघ्रहृतेऽर्थ॑ऽसि चिरादागच्छतो गतिः । अश्रैरपहतं को हि गर्दभः arate”? ॥ Page 92. Again on page 94:—“at हि श्रुतिं प्रथममश्चुतवा स्तिमेवेकां wes तस्याप्रतिहतश्चुलनुमाने वृत्ते ॥ न पश्चाच्छरयमाणापि तिः स्यात्मतिबन्धिका । गरदभेनापनीतं हि eta श्रश्चिराद्रतः' ॥ 64 ५ यद्विशेषयोः कार्यकारणभावोऽसति वाधके तत्सामान्ययोरपि ॥ This nydya is found in Raghunatha’s larger work, the Laukikanydyaratnakar (India Office MS. 582, page 185 a), and on page 6 of S‘ikhamanitika. Prof. Cowell, however: quoted and explained it in a footnote to his translation of Haridasa’s comment on Kuswmanjali ए, 4. I quote a portion of the comment to elucidate the note. “You may not say that ‘the volition of the conscious agent is the cause in effort only, and not in all action generally, because even though a particular kind of volition may be the cause in the case of effort, this does not preclude volition generally; otherwise, because a particular seed is the cause of a particular shoot, it would follow that seeds in general [ 7. ९. the class, seed] could not be the causes of shoots in general.” The following is the footnote.. “This argument depends on two principlesa. The same relation of cause and effect which exists between particulars, exists likewise between their respective classes, यद्विशेषयोः कार्यकारणभावस्तत्सामान्ययोरपि and b. the general causes only produce their effects when conjoined with the particular causes, (सामान्य सामग्री विदेषसामग्रीसहितैव कायै जनयति. Thus Archbishop Whately has made a book on Logic,—man can therefore make logical books; only in each particular case we require the concurrents, education, leisure &c.” Test यक्षस्तादशो as: ॥ As is the Yaksha so should be the offering, This is included in Raghunatha’s list, but without any definition of its meaning. It is embedded, however, in the philosophical part of his 65 treatise, as follows:—* यस्त्वनेकजन्मा्जितपापपुञ्जजन्यदुर प्रहादेकभक्ति- च्छकेनान्यं निन्दति असकृदोध्यमानोऽपि चजैमारौणाभेदं नोपैति saa च मन्दान्स यादृशो यक्षस्तादशो बलिरिति न्यायात्तत्मरतिपादितोत्कर्षापकर्षविपरीतो- त्कर्षापकर्षोपपादनेन विजित्य पश्चा्पूर्वोक्तरीलयालन्तामेदोपपादनेन बोधनीयः | एवे हि स मारणाय गृदीतोऽङ्गच्छेदं स्वीकरोतीति न्यायेनाभेदं खीकरिष्यति"'
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 62

gloss_en: Conveying the meaning actually expressed [ and therefore needing nothing to supplement it].
explanation: Conveying the meaning actually expressed [ and therefore needing nothing to supplement it]. Kumarila puts it thus in Tontravartika 3. 5. 19:—“qara विदोषः । स यदि वाचनिकस्ततो यावद्वचनमेव कर्तव्यः”? ॥ Compare, too, the latter part of S’abara on 2. 3.2. It occurs twice in Bhamati. On 4. 1. 4 ( page 710 ) we २९४१ :--“यस्मायस्य यन्मात्रात्मतयोपासनं विहितं तस्य तन्मात्रात्मतयैव प्रतिपत्तव्यं यावद्वचनं वाचनिकमिति न्यायान्नाधिकमध्याहरेव्यमतिग्रसङ्गात्‌” ॥ Again on 4. 3. 4 (page 742) as 1011078 :-- “न चामानवस्य पुरुषस्य विद्युदादिषु वोदत्वदरशनादचिरादीनामपि वोदृत्वसुननेय यावद्वचनं हि वाचनिकं न तदवाच्ये सञ्चारयितुसुचितम्‌” ॥ Anandagiri, too, quotes the nyaya in his comment on 4. 8. 4, He says:—“aaraagera विद्युदादावातिवा- हिकत्वदष्टेरचिरादीनामपि तदुन्ेयमिलय्थः । यावद्वचनं वाचनिकमिति न्यायान्न तेषामातिवाहिकल्वसाधकमेतदिति sea तदिति? ॥ It is found also in the philosophical portion of Laukikanydyasangraha (1. O. MS. 1081, page 45 b.):—“ar तु पिद्ाचमोचनाख्याने पिशाचस्यापि TAM: सा निषादस्थपतीष्टिवयावद्रचनं तावद्वाचनिकमिति न्यायात्तन्मात्रविषयैव । न च छिङ्गस्य कादीप्रवेदात्पापनारो वचोऽस्ति तसरा- व्काञ्युसपन्नविषयाणि तत्तलिङ्गदरीनादिनेकब्यादिजन्मपापश्चयबोधकानि वचां- सील्याहुः" ॥ 8००, too, 2१३०७४8 Uddyota, vol. i. p. 574.
