gloss_en: The illustration of the king's son [ who was brought up Jas a hunter.
explanation: The illustration of the king's son [ who was brought up Jas a hunter. The story is that a young prince, abandoned by his parents at his birth, was adopted by a hunter and brought up as his own son. The boy remained in ignorance of his real origin until he was discovered by a kindly person and restored to his rightful position. S’ankaracarya seems to have been the first to utilize the tale, and he gives it as follows in his bhasya on Brihaddranyakopanisad 2. 1. 20:— 67 “aq च संप्रदायविद्‌ आख्यायिकां संप्रचक्षते | कश्चिक्किर राजयुत्रो जातara एव मातापितृभ्यामपविद्धो व्याधगृहे संवर्धितः | सोऽसुष्य वंडतामजानन्ग्याधजातिग्रययो व्याधजातिकमौण्येवानुवतेते न राजास्मीति राजजातिकमौ- ण्यनुवतेते | यदा पुनः कश्चित्परमकारुणिको राजपुत्रस्य राजश्री्राक्षियोग्यतां जानन्नसुष्य पुत्रतां बोधयति न त्वं व्याधोऽसुष्य Ua: पुत्रः कथंचिब्याधगृहमनु- gfe इति स एवं बोधितस्त्यक्त्वा व्याधजातिग्रययकमौणि पितृपेतामहीमात्मनः पदवीमनुवरेते राजाहमस्मीति. Sure’svara refers to this several times in his large Vartika. On page 71 we read:—“‘qarfenferda व्याधभावनयाञ्जितः । राजसूनोः स््तिप्राप्तौ व्याधभावो faa.” Then, on pages 970-2, he devotes ten verses to the nyaya, and returns to it once more on page 1845. The author of the Siddhdntalesa (on page 20) cites it as the “न्याधकुलसंवधितराजकुमारदष्टान्त, and it is reproduced, in a slightly different form, in the comments of Aniruddha and Vedantin Mahadeo on Sdénkhyasttra iv. 1, See also Bhdmati 1. 4. 22. Raghunathavarman links with the above the सिंहमेषन्याय which tells of a lion’s cub being brought up as a ram; but T have not yet met with it elsewhere.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 66

gloss_en: The simile of the manner of entering a royal city.
explanation: The simile of the manner of entering a royal city. It is found in both of Raghunathavarma’s treatises, but the following explanation of it is taken from the Vacaspatyam:—“fazpaecat राजपुरम्वेरो राजपुररक्षकैस्ताडनादिकं क्रियेतेति भिया श्रेणीभूततया यथा तद्पुरभरवेश एवं॒सुखंखरुतया यत्र कायैकरणस्य विवक्षा तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः" ॥ Raghunatha points out that we do not grasp the meaning of a long sentence as a whole, but that the sense of each word enters the mind singly, on the principle of राजपुरप्रवेद्ा,- “तत्र हि waa बहूनां पुरुषाणां प्रवेशो भवति न युगपत्‌.” 68 च I have met with the nyaya in 15९७8 Bhatta’s comment’ on Kaiyata. In Mahabhasya 1. 1. 58 ( under vartika 1) we read corgi संनिविष्टानां यथेष्टमभिसंबन्धो भवति । तयथा । अनड़ाटgeek या त्वं हरसि शिरसा gat भगिनि साचीनमभिधावन्तमद्राक्षीरिति । तस्य यथेष्टमभिसंबन्धो भवति । उदहारि भगिनि या त्वं कुम्भं हरसि शिरसा. agré साचीनमभिधावन्तमद्वाक्षीरिति" Kaiyata remarks on this :— “^पाठक्रमादार्थक्रमो बरीयानिति यथेष्टमच्राभिसंबन्धः? and on these words Nages'a says “आर्थक्रमो am राजपुरभ्रवेशन्यायेन स्वस्याकांक्षितार्थान्वयकमः । एवं च कल्पितासन्नवाक्याद्वोधविषये aa नियामकमिति भावः ॥ The passages here quoted will be found on pages 389 and 390 of vol. i of the edition of Mahabhasya with the Pradipa and Uddyota, published at the Nirnayasagar Press in 1908.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 67

gloss_en: This is the second line of a verse on page 872 of the Tantravartika.
explanation: This is the second line of a verse on page 872 of the Tantravartika. To make it intelligible I quote a portion of the context as interpreted by Professor Ganganatha Jha in his translation ( page 511, last line ):~ “It has been urged above that, if the Aparva inhere in the Soul, then it becomes only an end in itself desirable by men, But this does not affect our position; because one thing becomes subservient to another only when its sole use lies in the serving of some purpose of this latter, and not merely when it rests in this ; for instance, though the Red Dye is carried by the camel ( and as such rests upon its back ), yet it serves the purposes of the king ( for whom it is carried ).”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 68

gloss_en: The circulation within the body of poison which has entered the blood.
explanation: The circulation within the body of poison which has entered the blood. Used as a warning against the beginnings of evil in however small a degree. The figure is found in Bodhicarydvatara vii. 69 — ? 69 “fd रुधिरमासा् प्रसपैति यथा तनौ । तथेव चिद्रमासाद्य दोपश्चित्ते प्रसपैति " ॥ The commentary runs (05 :--“ अणुमातन्रस्यापि दोषस्यावकाशो न दातव्यः | अन्यथा तन्मात्रस्याप्यनुप्रवेरो चित्ते तस्प्रसरावरोधस्य कर्तुमदाक्यत्वात्‌ | यथा हि स्वल्पव्रणेऽपि रधिरसम्पकंवतो विषस्य शारीरे | तस्मादणुमात्र्ेदाप्रहारनिवारणेऽपि तात्पर्य कुर्यात्‌.” Compare “The beginning of strife is as when one letteth out water; therefore leave off contention before there be quarrelling.”
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 68

gloss_en: The illustration of wood thrown into the salt-lake [ or mine] Ruma.
explanation: The illustration of wood thrown into the salt-lake [ or mine] Ruma. The Medini 1088 explains Ruma as “ विद्िष्टलवणाकरे ”’, and it is said to be situated near Ajmere. The tradition is that anything thrown in there becomes saline itself. The earliest mention of Ruma, with which I am acquainted, is in the following verse of Tantravirtika ( page 182 ):— “an रुमायां लवणाकरेषु मेरौ यथा वोज्वलरक्मभूमौ । यज़ायते तन्मयमेव तस्सयात्तथा भवेद्वेद॒विदात्मतुष्टिः ” ॥ Kumiarila seems here to regard Ruma as the region in which the salt mines are situated, rather than as the mine itself; and this may give some ground for the footnote by the editor of the Medini, ( Calcutta, 1869 ) where he defines विद्िष्टकवणाकरः as ५लवणखनिभूयिष्देदाविरोषः.* In his translation of the above verse, Prof. Ganginatha Jha omits Ruma altogether. He says :— “Just as in the case of salt mines, and in that of Meru the land of bright gold, whatever is produced in them, becomes salt and gold (respectively),—so also in the case of the inner satisfaction of one who knows the Veda (which imparts Vedic authority to all that it touches).” 70 We have an example of the 02 in Vacaspati Mis‘ra’s comment on Yogasitrabhasya iv. 14. The siitra is ^प्रिणासैकलत्वा- gegawaa” on which he says:—“qgaracte: परिणामो दृष्टः | तद्यथा । गवाश्वमहिषमातङ्गानां र्मानिक्षिक्षानामेको ख्वणत्वजातीयलक्षणः परिणामो वसितेलानलानां च भ्रदीप इति.” Then Venkatanatha uses the illustration in Tattvamuktakalapa ए, 28, and in his vritti thereon, as follows :— ^ स्यादुष्णः gua afeeatt तथा शीतमस्तु प्रकृत्या स्पर्योऽन्योऽप्यत्र eee तु भवतु स्माक्षिक्तखावण्यवच्ेत्‌ ” | “so: कृष्णवत्मौ तथैव जलमपि शीतमिव्युपरभ्यते प्रकृतया | अत्र कश्चिदाह अन्योऽपि स्प दहने awe च कदाचिुपलभ्यते स त॒ स्माक्षि्षकाष्टादिलवणन्यायेन तस्यैव परिणतिविरोष इति. In the vritti on ii. 1 of the same, the author says:—“ गुणसंक्रमो न efaety । न चात्र स्माक्षिसकराष्टन्यायः' ॥
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 69

gloss_en: Popular usage overpowers etymological meaning.
explanation: Popular usage overpowers etymological meaning. There is a capital illustration of this in the Vivaranaprameyasangraha .3 (pages 134, 135 ) where Badaréyana’s first siitra is under discussion :-—“aq जिक्ञासाशब्दो विचारे रूढः । भाष्यकारादिभिस्तत्र विचारविवक्षया ्रयुक्त्वात्‌ । अतो रूढिर्योगमपहरतीति न्यायेनावयवार्थस्ी- कारो न युक्तसतोऽ्थशब्दोऽप्यधिकारार्थो भविष्यतीति विचारस्य omg शक्यत्वादिति चेन्मैवम्‌ | रूढिर्योगमपहरतीति न्यायसात्राप्रसरात्‌ |... तत्र यः शब्द CET रूढोऽपरत्र यौगिको यथा च्छागे रूढोऽजराब्द आत्मनि यौगिकसतत्राजं पदयेवयक्ते रूढिर्योगमपहरतीति न्यायः प्रसरति । इह तु जि- ज्ञासादाब्दो न विचारे रूढः" ॥ The following verse is quoted in the Nyayapradipa, a commentary on Tarkabhdsa, page 5:— ““छब्धात्मिका सती रूढिरभवेद्योगापहारिणी । कल्पनीया तु रभते नात्मानं योगबाधतः? ॥ The editor cites a very modern author who ascribes the verse to Kumarila. See also Pancapadikavivarana, pp. 132-8; Vedantakalpataru, p. 207; and Anandagiri on Brahmasitrabhashya, 1. 3. 42. 71
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 70

gloss_en: The illustration of the sketch of the Bos Gavaeus ( Gayal ).
explanation: The illustration of the sketch of the Bos Gavaeus ( Gayal ). Raghunathavarma, explains and applies it as follows:—afzsit गवय इति भ्रामीणेन get वन्यो लिखित्वा sama a चजंबुद्धित्वाद्रेखागवयमेव गवयं मेने । gage गवयं दद्रा रेखायां dale व्याजेति ोकिकी गाथा | तथैष पुरुष इयादिश्चतेः पूर्वोक्ततात्प्यानभिक्तोऽनात्मानमेवात्मतया जानीते | गुरुशाखोपदेरोनात्मनि at तदात्मबुद्धिमपवदति.” It is found on page 457 of Vacaspatimis’ra’s Tatparyatikd, and again on page 363 of Veddntakalpataruparimala, The latter passage reads thus:—“goy ताचिकारन्धतीप्रतिपच्युपायतया नानापुरूपैः Beet मानायां तत्प्ाच्योदीच्यादिनक्षत्ररूपायां स्थूलारन्धत्या यथा वा रेखागवयन्यायेन निलयशब्दग्रतिपद्युपायतया नानाव्याकरणेः परस्परभिन्नप्रकृतिप्रलययविभागेन कत्रिमशाब्द्‌ इति भावः" ॥ A third example is found in the following extract from Kaiyata on Mahabhasya 1. 1. 46 :--^असलयप्रकृतिग्रययोपदेरोन Fae पदस्य ed क्रियते रेखागवयेनेव aerate.” See also S’ribhdsya page 322, and page 77 of Dr. Thibaut’s translation.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 71

gloss_en: [The existence, or nature, of ] an object is established by means of some distinguishing characteristic, and by a recognized form of proof [ such as sense-perception, scripture &e.
explanation: [The existence, or nature, of ] an object is established by means of some distinguishing characteristic, and by a recognized form of proof [ such as sense-perception, scripture &e. |. “यथा गन्धव्वादिरक्षणेन reat च प्रथिव्यादिसिद्धिः"?॥ 0, just as the wonders of creation establish the “eternal power and Godhead” of the invisible Deity, to which Scripture also bears testimony. Raghunathavarma quotes the nyaya in the following passage on page 28 of the Benares edition of his फणः :--““एवमश्नीन्द्रा- दिवयेश्वरवादा अपि तन्माहात्म्योपपादकश्तीतिहासपुराणवचनान्याश्निलय तत्रैव 72 भ्रपञ्चिता ज्ञेयाः । तत्तदधक्ता अपि सगौदिहेतुत्वरूपेश्वरलश्चणं श्रुत्ादिप्रमाणं च aa तत्र दद्रीयन्तो रक्षणग्रमाणाभ्यां वस्तुसिद्धिरिति न्यायेनेश्वरत्वं साधयन्ति." Commenting on the opening verse of the Veddntaparibhasa, the author of the S'’tkhamani says—“‘ag ब्रह्मणि लक्षणग्रमाणाभावेन त्यैवासिद्धेः कथं जीवव्रह्माभेदः शाखा इति चेन्न तावत््रमाणाभावो भूतभौति- कोत्पत्तेरेव प्रमाणत्वात्‌.” Amaradasa’s tika on this begins as follows :—“saurammat वस्तुसिद्धिरिति न्यायमाश्रित्यांकते नन्विति." sige जीवनम्‌ ॥ A plough, is existence. That is, it is a means of existence; cause and effect being here identified as in आयुर्घृतम्‌. The nyaya is found in Sures’vara’s Sambandhavartika, page 9, as fol1078: -“यथोक्तविद्याबोधित्वाद्भन्थोऽपि तदभेदतः | भवेदुपनिषन्नामा was जीवनं यथा. On which Anandagiri गणपः“ साध्यसाधनयोरमे- दोपचारेण ANT साधने प्रयोगे दृष्टान्तमाह लाङ्गरमिति.' We may compare with this the phrase “The plough supports the bullocks”, which occurs in Brahmasitrabhdshya 8. 2. 4 :-- “यथा wigs गवा- dagedfe निमित्तमाच्रत्वादेवसुच्यते न तु प्रयक्षमेव was गवादीनु- दहति 1 2
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 71

gloss_en: The simile of the measuring out [ or distribution] of beans by the daughter-in-law.
explanation: The simile of the measuring out [ or distribution] of beans by the daughter-in-law. I am much indebted to Mr. Govind Das, Honorary Magistrate of Benares, for giving me what seems to be the real meaning of this hitherto-puzzling nyaya. He believes it to be the adaptation of a Maithila proverb with which the following story is connected. “A very miserly old = Brahman used to have a fistful of grain given daily by his wife = to every beggar who came to the door. The old man having | z 78 married his son, the idea struck him that if he got his daughterin-law to do the distribution instead of his old and ugly wife, the smaller fist would measure out a smaller quantity of grain! But, unluckily for him, the girl was very beautiful, so even persons who were not in need began to drop in, disguised as beggars, in order to admire her! The result. was that, while each measure was less, the total amount given away was very much more.” It occurs in the Atmatattvaviveka, page 87, line 12, as follows:~ ey चानवस्था अवरयवेद्यत्वानभ्युपगमान्निश्चयवदन्यथा स्वनिश्चितनिश्चयस्य नाद्यनिश्चयोऽपि सिध्येत न चासावात्मन्यनिश्चय इति तदिदं वधूमाषमापनब्र्ता- न्तमनुहरति"? ॥
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 72

gloss_en: The maxim of the destroyer and its prey.
explanation: The maxim of the destroyer and its prey. Used of two things which cannot exist together. It occurs in Taittiriyavartika 2. 1. 66 ( page 58 ):—* प्रतिपद्य पदार्थं हि विरोधात्तद्िरोधिनः। पश्चादभावं जानाति वध्यघातकवत्पदात्‌.' Anandagiri explains it णः :--“यथावच्छयेनमूषकादिना दूषितां भूमिसुपलभ्य तद्विरोधिनो घातकस्य मार्जारादेरभावोऽथीदवगम्यते तथा सल्यादिपदात्पदार्थं warned ada भ्रतीतपरमार्थत्वादिविरोधिनोऽसल्यत्वादेरभावोऽरथापत्या ज्ञायते न हि सलदेरसल्यादेश्चैकाधिकरणत्वं घटते. See also Pras/astapada’s Vais’eshikabhashya, pages 112, 113; and the latter part of Citsukht iv. 4, (Pandit vi, 390). Compare the बाध्यबाधकभाव of Naisharmyasiddhi i. 55 (qareiori:), and 7. 85 (आखुनङुख्योः).
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 73

gloss_en: The illustration of a lion in a forest.
explanation: The illustration of a lion in a forest. Used of things which mutually aid or protect each other, This, and the दनक्रन्याय which is of similar import, occur together in the following 10 74 passage of the Vedantakalpataruparimala (page 100 ) “asd वनसिंदद्ृदनकन्यायः । किरतिंन्तुं शक्योऽपि सिंहो महद्वनं शरणं भविङय दुराधषैस्तेभ्यो न विभेति वनं च तत्िहाधिष्टानानुगृहीतं aot भवति ?2 ॥ Similarly, the lake shelters the alligator, and the alligator protects the lake. There’ is another reference to the वनसिहन्याय on page 627 of the same work :--““तथा च वनरसिंहन्या- येन प्रमाणतरकन्यायेन वोभयोरप्यंशभेदेन Teas न परस्पराश्रय दोषः? ॥ 116 source of the nyaya is doubtless Udyogaparva xxxvii. 46, for the reference to which I am indebted to Professor Dr. R. Pischel. The verse is as follows :— ५न स्याद्वनश्धते AAA न PRT वनम्‌ । वनं हि रक्ष्यते व्यात्र्व्याघ्रान्‌ रक्षति काननम्‌” ॥ Compare also verse 64 of the same.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 73

gloss_en: The maxim of the discussion of matters with a view to obtaining a husband [ for one’s daughter].
explanation: The maxim of the discussion of matters with a view to obtaining a husband [ for one’s daughter]. It is thus explained by Raghunathavarman ---“गोष्ठिरन्योन्यवातां वरलाभाय गोष्ठिव॑रman यथा वरवधूवन्धूनाभकमवये सति विवाहरूपमेकं at Aes ayaa: ॥ Ihave met with the expression twice in the Pancapadika ( pages 72, 73 ), in a description of the erroneous views of common people ( such as the Laukayatikas &c. ) in regard to the dtman. The passages are as 01108:--“एवमिन्द्रियाण्येव चेतनानि आत्मे्यपरे । इन्द्रियाणां चष्षुरादिमनःपयैन्तानामेकैकस्मिन्नसत्येव शरीरे रूपा- दिह्ञानानामभावात्तेषामेव व्यस्तानां चेतनत्वमहंग्रययविषयत्वं च मन्यन्ते करमे- ण च वरगोष्ठीवदितरेतरगुणभावं च ॥ ^€" :--“यदि ताव्यस्तानां युगपस्परिकल्प्येत ततः स्वा्थ्रयुक्तत्वास्पवरत्तेरङ्गङ्गिभावो नावकव्पेत | न चाङ्गा- क्गिभावमन्तरेण संघात उपपद्यते | तस्मान्न व्यस्तेषु युगपद्धोगः | अस्तु Te कमेण विरोधाद्वरगोष्ठीवदिति । नैतदेवं युक्तम्‌” ॥ It needs a more intimate acquaintance with वरगोष्ठी than we Westerns possess in order to grasp the full significance of the nyaya, and I must confess to a certain amount of haziness as to 75 its exact sense in the passages here cited. In a later part of his treatise Raghunatha gives us the maxim ^यद्विवाहस्द्रीतगानम्‌” appended to which is the remark “केचित्त प्रागुदाहतं वरगोष्टिन्यायमेतद्र्थकत्वेन व्याचक्षते. The way in which he applies the latter will be apparent from the following excerpt from the philosophical portion of his work :—“uq हि वादिनो यद्विवाहस्तद्रीतगानमि- तिन्यायालुसारीणि खखेष्टदेवमाहात्म्यवाक्यानि पर्यन्तोऽन्यानि तु पदयन्तोऽपि दुरा्रहपिनद्धदष्टित्वादपर्यन्त इव तदेकवाक्यतादिकं चाजानन्तोऽन्धगजन्याये- नान्योन्यं बिवदतेऽबुद्धैव च मतान्तरं कूपमण्डूकन्यायान्निराङुर्वन्तो बद्धैरुपहसनीयतां यान्ति ॥ Compare Kumarila’s 4कन्यावरणा्थागतमूखंवरगोच्रwaa । यदेव भवतां गोत्रं तदस्माकमपीतिवत्‌”? ॥ Tantravartika, page 169-70. Prof. Ganganatha Jha points out that if the would-be bridegroom was really of the same gotra, it would make the marriage impossible!
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 74

gloss_en: Retaining possession of a cow after it has been sold to some one else.
explanation: Retaining possession of a cow after it has been sold to some one else. This illegality is dealt with by Narada and Yajnavalkya in the “ f@aitqrameranavng”, “The non-delivery of a sold chattel.” In chapter viii, 1, the former defines it thus :— “ Rater पण्यं मूल्येन Rota प्रदीयते । विक्रीयासम्पदानं तद्िवादपदसुच्यते ” ॥ The latter lays down the law on the subject in chapter ii, 254-8, Udayana’s application of the above in Atmatattvaviveka, page 58, is as follows :— “ यदनात्मान एवैताश्चतखः कोटयो भासन्ते न वा प्रतिभान्तीति । तत्राप्रतिभानमुत्तरम्‌ । भरतिभाने तु आद्यलक्षणायोगेऽपि आाद्यभाव इति चेदेवमेतत्‌ |. -प्कारा- मानत्वं तु नीरादीनामदाक्यापह्नवम्‌ | तावन्मात्रं चास्माकमभिमतमिति चेत्तदेतद्विक्रीतगवीरक्षणम्‌ "° ॥ The drift of this is not very clear. 76
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 75

gloss_en: The illustration of the shaking of a tree.
explanation: The illustration of the shaking of a tree. A man is supposed to be up a tree whilst others are standing below it. One of the latter points to a particular branch which he wishes to be shaken, and the others point out other branches for the same ` purpose; so the man shakes the whole tree at once and thus satisfies every body by the one effort! Raghunatha applies this in the following way --“ यत्रैकस्य वस्तुनो मतभेदेन बहूनि रूपाणि are प्रतिपाद्यमानान्युपरभ्य क्रिया हि विकल्प्यते न वस्त्विति न्याया- ge विकव्पासंभवं मत्वा विरुद्धानां ससुच्चयस्याप्यसंभवेन तेप्वेकमतप्रति- पादितं सवीविरुदधं वस्तु स्वीक्रियते तत्र वृक्षप्रकंपनन्यायः sada’ ॥ 1" his larger work, Raghunatha says that the simile is also found as बृक्षप्रचलनन्याय, and in this form I have met with it in Mahabhagya, vol. i. page 28 (bottom ), “ge: प्रचलन्सहावयवैः प्रचरति." Also in 6. 1. 1 ( vart. 13 ),
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 76

gloss_en: Running away through fear of a scorpion, he falls into the jaws of a poisonous snake! Avoiding Scylla, he falls into Charybdis! The nyaya occurs in the following passage of the Nydyavartikatitparyatika, page 53:—“ यद्यपि रागादिनिवृत्तिहितु- नैरात्म्यदर्शानं तथापि नासि कर्म नासि कर्मफलमिति इष्टेः परमं निदानम्‌ । एवं भ्रेयभावाभावक्तानस्य च । तथा च दुःखदेतोर्हेयवग॑स्याभावान्न तद्धानाया- नेन घटितव्यम्‌ । न चाघटमानो at हातुमहंति सोऽयं इृश्चिकभिया पलायमान आश्रीविषसुखे निपतितः? ॥ It is found also in Kuswmnanjalt ii.
explanation: Running away through fear of a scorpion, he falls into the jaws of a poisonous snake! Avoiding Scylla, he falls into Charybdis! The nyaya occurs in the following passage of the Nydyavartikatitparyatika, page 53:—“ यद्यपि रागादिनिवृत्तिहितु- नैरात्म्यदर्शानं तथापि नासि कर्म नासि कर्मफलमिति इष्टेः परमं निदानम्‌ । एवं भ्रेयभावाभावक्तानस्य च । तथा च दुःखदेतोर्हेयवग॑स्याभावान्न तद्धानाया- नेन घटितव्यम्‌ । न चाघटमानो at हातुमहंति सोऽयं इृश्चिकभिया पलायमान आश्रीविषसुखे निपतितः? ॥ It is found also in Kuswmnanjalt ii. 8 ( page 328 ), in Vidvanmandana, page 4, and in Nydyamakaranda, page 223. Of somewhat similar import is the nyaya “एकामसिद्ध परिहरतो द्वितीयापद्यते*”, which see. वृश्चिकीगभेन्यायः ॥ For this see the अश्वतरीगर्भन्याय. 4 . 77 SIISTRSATT? ॥ The maxim of the snake and the mungoose. The wellknown innate antipathy of these two for one another ( Panini 2. 4. 9,) is a commonly-used illustration of inherent opposition between two things. Mr. Tawney has reminded me of the story in Panchatantra V. 2. which speaks of the enmity between them in the following words :--“अग्रान्तरे दैववद्ाटष्णसर्पो बिलान्निष्करान्तः । नऊुरोऽपि तं स्वभाववैरिणं मत्वा आत्‌ रक्चणाथं ain सह युद्धा सपै खण्डशः कृतवान्‌! ॥ 716 nyaya is employed by Udayana in Atmatattvaviveka, page 58, as follows :—* स्वसंविदि तद्रूपत्वादि- ति चेत्तक्किमङ्गपरिणतज्ान्तेराश्रमपदमिव area ष्यारनकुकादेरिव नीरुधवरादेः शाश्वतिकविरोधल्यागो निश्तवैराणां तत्फलल्यागो वा । न तावam: परस्परनिषेधविधिनान्तरीयकविधिनिषेधयोरविरोधे जगति विरोधोष्छेद्‌- saga’ ॥ 10 is more commonly known as अहिनकुलन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 76

gloss_en: The simile of the [ apparently simultaneous ] piercing [ with a needle] of one hundred lotus leaves.
explanation: The simile of the [ apparently simultaneous ] piercing [ with a needle] of one hundred lotus leaves. It is found under the figure agea in Kuvalayémanda, in connection with the following example :— ^ बिञ्नाणा हृदये त्वया विनिहितं प्रेमाभिधानं नवं शल्यं यद्विदधाति सा विधुरिता साधो तदाकण्यैताम्‌ | शेते ष्यति ताम्यति प्ररुपति भ्रम्कायति shake भ्नाम्य्युद्यूठति प्रणस्यति गरत्युन्मूच्छंति sens ॥ अत्र कासांचिक्ियाणां किञ्चित्कारभेदसंभवेऽपि शतपत्रपत्रद्ातभेदन्यायेन यौगपद्यं विरहातिशयद्योतनाय विवक्षितमिति लक्षणाजुगतिः” ॥ In the Sahityadarpana, also, we have the same idea somewhat differently expressed in the description of असंलक्षयक्रमन्यंग्य The following is the passage (on page 102) with Mr. Pramadadasa Mitra’s translation :— 78 “ अत्र व्यंग्यग्रतीतेर्विभावादिग्रतीतिकारणकत्वाच्रमोऽवद्यम स्ति किन्तूर्पलपत्रशतव्यतिभेदवल्याघवान्न संरक्ष्यते ” ॥ “ Now, the perception of the suggested, caused as it is by, and hence succeeding, the perception of the Accessories &., has necessarily a process, but from its quickness it is not perceived, like the process of the apparently simultaneous piercing through of a hundred lotus leaves ` placed one upon another.” The expression ^ sqearaqaerfaaद्वत्‌? is used by Aniruddha in his comment on Sdnkhyasttra ii. 82; and Dr. Garbe thinks that he took it from the Sahityadarpana, See his Preface to the Sankhyasitravritti, There are two examples of this nyaya in S’ridhara’s Nydyakandali. On page 28 :--“यद्रच्छति तस्सन्निहितव्यवहिताथौँ करमेण sate तत्कथं ब्राखाचन्द्रमसोस्तुल्यकारोपरुढ्धिरिति चेदिन्द्रियदृ्तेरा्सञ्रारित्वात्परारा- तव्यतिभेदवत््रमाग्रहणनिमित्तोऽयं श्रमो न तु वास्तवं यौगपद्यम्‌." The other is on page 158. : A much older example is found in S'lokavartika, page 311 (verse 157 ), to which I append Mr. Ganganatha Jha’s transla४०० :--यस्मदीपम्रमादुक्तं सृष्ष्मकालोऽसि aa नः । दक्षस्तु यथा वेधः पद्मपश्रदाते तथा.” “You have brought forward the case of the lamp and the light emitted by it, as an instance of the simultaneity of the cause and the effect. But in this case also, there is a minute point of time (intervening between the appearance of the lamp and that of the light), though this is imperceptible ; just as is the case with the piercing ( with a needle) of the hundred petals of the lotus.” Professor Jacobi has kindly pointed out an instance of it in Nydyavartika, page 37, in the fom उत्पल्दलदातव्यतिभेदवत्‌, from which, perhaps, Aniruddha took the nyaya rather than from the very modern Sahityadarpana. The same expression उत्परपत्रदातव्यतिभेदवत्‌ is found in the Jaina work Syadvadamanjari ( page 92 ). Besides these, Thave met with the nyaya in Tatparyatika, page 334, line 2 (in the form ज्ञीघ्रतरबाणेतुकडातपत्रदातव्यतिभेदवत्‌ ); in Nydyamanjari, page 498 (४5 सूच्यञरभिद्यमानकोकनददरकदम्बकवत्‌ ); in Tarkabhasatika, page 24; in Tarkikaraksatika, page 126 (as ्तपत्र्ातनवत्‌ ); and in Citsukht ४. 9 (कतपत्रपत्रदातव्यतिभेदानु- भववत्‌). ` 79
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 77

gloss_en: Fifty [is contained] in a hundred.
explanation: Fifty [is contained] in a hundred. The greater includes the less. In the Vacaspatyam the nyaya is thus defined :— “ब्यापकातसंख्यायां यथा व्याप्यपञ्चारात्सं्या निविष्टा एवं यत्र व्यापके उ्याप्यस्य निवेशस्तत्रास्य प्रवृत्तिः” ॥ I have met with it only in the Vedantakalpataru, page 121, line 12, where a highly technical passage from S‘abara 6. 1. 43 is discussed, regarding the pronouncement of the names of Pravaras at new and full moon sacrifices, A reference to Kunte’s पड़ दर्शनचिन्तनिका, page 1776, would throw light on this dark passage. WAIT: ॥ The simile of perfuming a dead body. For the application and illustration see अरण्यरोदनन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 79

gloss_en: The simile of the moon upon a bough.
explanation: The simile of the moon upon a bough. Molesworth defines it thus:—*A Sanskrit phrase adduced as a simile or an illustration when an object seen or a matter debated has its position or relation assigned to it as at, on, in consistency with &. a particular object or matter, simply from the appearance of contiguity or connection which, under one line of view or one train of reasoning, it ordinarily presents; whilst actually and truly it is remote from it so widely as to preclude altogether affirmation of connection. We say the sun sinks in the ocean by the same law as we say the moon is upon a bough of a tree, speaking in both cases from the appearance presented.” It is thus akin to the अर्न्धतीप्रदर्हनन्याय. The following example is found in Taitiriyabhadshyavartika 2. 1. 232 (page 88):— wearameeta सोमं यद्रत्मदशयेत्‌ । निष्कोशं कोरादृ्टयैव प्रतीचि अद्म asia’? And in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 202, we 80 1680 :-“नन्वन्र सूत्रे ब्रह्मस्वरूपरक्षणं नोक्तं न च तदन्तरेण स्वरूपमवगम्यते श्रकृष्ट्रकाशात्मत्वमनुक्तवा WAT चन्द्र इव्येवोक्ते चन्द्रस्वरूपानवगमात्‌"” ॥ , सकृत्कृते कृतः शाखार्थः ॥ To doa thing once is sufficient to satisfy the demands of the S’astra. The nyaya is found in Mahdbhdsya 6. 1. 84 (vart. 4 ), 108 ( vart. 3), and in 6, 4. 104 (vart. 3). Also in S‘abara 11. 1. 28, 35; and 12. 8, 10. It seems to resemble the Marathi phrase शाखापुरता, Which Molesworth thus defines :—“To be enough indeed for the supplying, serving, or fulfilling of any matter or point required by the S’astra, but without excess beyond; to exist in just sufficient quantity, or to be performed with just sufficient definiteness of action, as to warrant the name or designation borne, and to preclude disallowal of its existence or its performance; to be enough to swear by.” The Sanskrit phrase occurs also in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 154 (line 2 from bottom ):—* ननु सङृत्कृते कृतः rend हति म्यायेन सकृदध्ययनादेव Rarer रुभ्येतेति Fe”? ॥ See too 21211041 4. 1. 12, and compare कपिञ्जरन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 79

gloss_en: A woman who has fallen once need veil her face no more.
explanation: A woman who has fallen once need veil her face no more. This occurs in Tantravartika, pages 708, 704, in the course of the discussion (under 8. 1. 12) of the meaning of the expression “ geo पिङ्गक्षयैकहायन्या सोमं sof’. On page 708 we read :— ५ अरुणाद्राब्दस्तावदवर्यमेव केनचिद्धणिना सम्बन्धनीयः | एकद्ायनीदाब्दस्यापि क्रियासम्बन्धात्स्वातच्यमपनीतम्‌ | तत्र पदान्तरसम्बन्धेऽपि सङ्ृस्मवृत्तायाः कि- मवगुण्ठनेनेतिवत्तस्य dada श्ुतिपीडेति.' The nyaya is quoted by Parthasarathi in S'astradipika 1. 4. 4 (page 177, line 6 from bottom ), while discussing the subject of words like Agnihotra &c., as the names of sacrifices, 81
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 80

gloss_en: Like produces like.
explanation: Like produces like. Jayanta Bhatta denies that this is a fixed principle, on the ground that scorpions are produced from cowdung. He puts it thus (page 466 ):— “aq चेष नियमो रोके सदशात्सदशोद्धवः | बृश्विकादेः ससुत्पादो गोमयादपि द्यते" ॥ This “old wives’ fable” regarding the scorpion was deeply rooted in the Indian mind! It is found in Mahabhdsya 1. 4. 30, and is used as an illustration by S’ankaracarya in his bhasya on Brahmasitra 2. 1. 6. Ramanuja followed suit. Udayana, too, has it in his vritti on Kuswmédnjali ii. 2, and the commentator Haridisa remarks that a scorpion can be produced from cowdung as well as from a scorpion. Thanks, however, to the now well-established Law of Biogenesis, we are better informed at the present time. To quote Henry Drummond:—‘It is now recognized on every hand that Life can only come from the touch of Life. Huxley categorically announces that the doctrine of Biogenesis, or life only from life, is ‘victorious along the whole line at the present day. And even whilst confessing that he wishes the evidence were the other way, Tyndall is compelled to say, ‘I affirm that no shred of trustworthy experimental testimony exists to prove that life in our day has ever appeared independently of antecedent life’.” ©
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 81

gloss_en: When there is doubt reason comes into play.
explanation: When there is doubt reason comes into play. This is found in Jnanottama’s comment on Naiskarmyasiddhi iv. 3. He says:— “Rat न्यायः sata इति न्यायात्सन्दिग्धस्यैव विचार्य्॑वात्तत्परिदोधयित॒मit 82 विप्रति पत्नमै तावदर्शयति.* Akin to this is the nyaya “सन्दिग्धं सप्रयोजनं च विचारमर्हति, ` which is found in the earlier part of the same work (namely in the comment on i. 29), and which Raghunatha expounds thus in his smaller work :—“‘fearqeaa यावद्यावद्िवेकदाद्यै भवति तावत्तावद्धमद्रौथिल्यं जायते तरतमभावापन्नसाधना- यत्तं फलं तरतमभावापन्नमिति न्यायात्‌ । बिचारबिषयत्वं च नाक्तातस्य नापि निश्चितस्य किंतु सन्दिग्धस्य सन्दिग्धं सप्रयोजनं च विचारमहैतीति न्यायात्‌.” aaa समुत्पन्ने अर्धं त्यजति पण्डितः ॥ When the loss of all is impending, a wise man will ge up half [if by so doing he can save the other half}. It occurs twice in the Pancatantra, namely in iv. 27, and v. 42, as follows :— “स्वेना aga अधं यजति पण्डितः । अर्धेन कुरूते कारय सर्वनाशो हि दुस्तरः ॥' In the second passage, the final word is दुःसहः. See Dr. Biihler’s note on ससुत्यन्ने aq. The first half of this couplet is quoted in Kumarila’s Tantravartika, page 91, but there the reading is a.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 81

gloss_en: No cognition is erroneous in respect of a thing as possessed of certain properties ; but there may be error in regard to the exact form of the thing.
explanation: No cognition is erroneous in respect of a thing as possessed of certain properties ; but there may be error in regard to the exact form of the thing. For example, a man sees a glittering object on the ground, and supposes it to be silver; but it turns out to be nacre and not silver. There is no mistake in his cognition of the shining object, but his conception of the nature of the object is erroneous, The nyaya is found in Cétswkhi 88 ii, 18 (The Pandit, vol. v. page 496 ) - “स्व ज्ञानं aired प्रकारे तु व्यत्यय इति वदद्धिरिदं रजतमिति विश्नमक्ञानमिदमंदो प्रमाणमभ्रमाणं रजतांरोऽभ्युपगम्यते परीक्षकैः” ॥ Underlying the words “सविकव्पकनिर्विकल्पकयोस्तु प्रमायामप्रमायां चान्तर्भावः?? ॥ 0 page 25 of the Suptapaddarthi, we find the following comment :—quq एवेदं रजतामिति सविकल्पकं प्रमा । अरजते रजतज्ञानं wa इयथः | निर्विकल्पकं तु प्रमायामेवान्त्भवति । तस्य प्रथमाक्षसन्निपातजस्य वस्तुसवरूपमात्रविषयस्य क्राप्यवाधात्‌ | सर्वे ज्ञानं धर्मिण्यश्नान्तं vat तु ञ्यत्यय इति न्यायात्‌ | निर्विकट्पकस्य च प्रकाराभावात्‌” ॥ Another interesting example is to be found in Tattvamuktakalapa iv. 104, I subjoin the second half of the verse and a portion of the author’s own vritti on it :— ““आत्मस्वात्मांशयोश्च aft न भवेद्धान्तिरंशान्तरेऽपि स्यादेषा न स्वरूपे क्वचन परमसौ द्विभ्रकारे प्रकारे” ॥ ““अंयान्तरेऽपि विषयांदोऽप्येषा आन्तिः । स्वरूपे क्रचन न स्यात्सर्व art धर्मिण्यश्नान्तमिति वचनात्‌ | तथा च विषयेऽपि eet सर्वज्ञानसाधारण्या्प्रामाण्यस्य स्वामाविकत्वमेव gait: । तर्हि aa आ्रान्तिरिलत आह परमिति | असौ आन्िरदवि्रकारे प्रकारे । द्विभकारे स्वरूपनिरूपकधम निरूपितस्वरूपविदोषकधर्मे Teast: | इदं रजतमिलयत्र wer निरूपकधमिवेपरीलयम्‌ । पीतः शंख gaa निरूपितस्वरूपबिदोषकधर्मवैपरी- लमिति विभागः" ॥ On page 403 of Vidyasaigara’s tika on Khandanakhandakhadya the nyaya is ascribed to eteraetere. I would commend to students a helpful note ( No 34) at the end of Professor M. N. Dvivedi’s edition of the Turkakawmudi, as bearing on the principle enunciated in this nyaya.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 82

gloss_en: In this contracted form the nyaya is quoted by the author of the Veddntaparibhisa (chapter vii, page 411 ); in its entirety 84 it reads ४08 :-“सविदोपणे हि विधिनिषेधौ विदरोष्ये बाघे सति विरेषणसुपसंक्रामतः'” ॥ ‘The following is Mr.
explanation: In this contracted form the nyaya is quoted by the author of the Veddntaparibhisa (chapter vii, page 411 ); in its entirety 84 it reads ४08 :-“सविदोपणे हि विधिनिषेधौ विदरोष्ये बाघे सति विरेषणसुपसंक्रामतः'” ॥ ‘The following is Mr. Arthur Venis’ rendering of it (in The Pandit, vol. vii. page 460):—“An affirmation or a negation, when made of a subject together with its predicate, applies to the predicate if a bar exists to the affirmation or negation being attached to the subject’. An extract from Rational Refutation of Hindw philosophical Systems ( page 232 ) may tend to elucidate the above. “When the Vedintins give to intelligence appropriated to the internal organ the name of subject of right notion, we are to understand, that the character which they ascribe to intelligence associated with the internal organ, really belongs to that organ. They have a maxim,-which all the other Systems subscribe to-that ‘An affirmation, or a negation, when predicated of anything together with its associate, if debarred from the object substantive, is to be referred to the object adjective’. In their opinion, the quality of being a cognizer cannot be assigned to the soul, and, consequently, is debarred from it.” The nyaya is found in Tatparyatikd, page 31, line 5, and in Atmatattvaviveka, page 72, line 3 from bottom; but, in both cases, without the words “f@zrey बाघे सति.” It is quoted, too, in Laukikanydyasangraha, page 69, line 15. सहैव दशभिः gaat वहति गर्दभी ॥ Though ‘possessing ten sons the mother-donkey curries thé load! This evidently well-known saying, taken from Tantravartika, page 807, is found in 21601417 3. 4. 38 ( page 691) in the following connection “सहकारित्वं च कर्मणां न कार्य विद्यायाः क्रं art कोऽर्थो विद्यासहकारीणि कमौणी्ययमर्थः | सत्सु कर्मसु विधैव स्वकाय च्प्ाग्रियते । यथा सहेव दशभिः gait वहति गरद॑भीति सद्स्वेव 85 qagag सैव भारस्य वाहिकेतिः' ॥ 1116 saying is quoted by Anandagiri also, in his comment on the same portion of the bhishya. See, too, Veddntas ikhamani, p. 168.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 83

gloss_en: The simile of Sunda and Upasunda.
explanation: The simile of Sunda and Upasunda. Used of conflicting and mutually destructive things. It is thus explained by Raghunithavarma in his Lawkikanydyasangraha :-५अन्योन्यनास्यनाशकमावविवक्षायां सुन्दोपसुन्दन्यायः | यथा हि सुन्दोपसुन्दसन्कौ सहोदरावसुरौ Reward वध्यघातकभावेनोभावपि नष्टाविति भारते प्रसिद्धम्‌ । तथा वीचीतरङ्गन्यायेनोत्पन्नानां कार्यशब्दनाइयानामन्तयोपान्लकाब्दौ परस्परेण नार्या wa उपान््येनोपान्तश्वन्त्येनेति केचित्ताकिंकाः”? ॥ The story of Sunda and Upasunda is told at great length in Adiparva ecix-cexii, but is condensed into six verses in Kathasaritsigara xv., of which the following is Mr. Tawney’s translation :—“There were two brothers, Asuras by race, Sunda and Upasunda, hard to overcome, in as much as they surpassed the three worlds in valour. And Brahma, wishing to destroy them, gave an order to Vis‘vakarman, and had constructed a heavenly woman named Tilottama, in order to behold whose beauty even S‘iva truly became four-faced, so as to look four ways at once, while she was devoutly circumambulating him. She, by the order of Brahma, went to Sunda and Upasunda, while they were in the garden of Kailiisa, in order to seduce them. And both those two Asuras distracted with love, seized the fair one at the same time by both her arms, the moment they saw her near them. And as they were dragging her off in mutual opposition, they soon came to blows, and both of them were destroyed.” The simile is met with in Sénkhyatattvakawmudt 13, as follows:—“agq qeafattaaiiet गुणाः सुन्दोपसुन्दवत्परस्परं ध्वंसन्त gaa युक्तं प्रागेव तेषपामेकक्रियाकर्तृतायाः' ॥ See also Kamanda~ 86 kiya Nitisara, ix.61. In Sarvarthasiddhi ( on Tattvamuktahalapa ii, 53) we have the expression ^सुन्दोपसुन्दविषनाडाकविषन्यायेन. This nyiiya is used, says Raghunatha, when the things in opposition are of equal strength; but when they are of unequal strength, and the weaker go to the wall, the Mdatsyanydya is employed, प 61 खुमगाभिक्षकन्यायः ॥ “^^ “1 ˆ ~ ` ‡ ५.८; ~/“^- “य The simile of Subhaga and the mendicant. The following 18 Raghunatha’s explanation of it:—“ngq विरुद्धानेकधर्मसमवेशासं- भवविवक्षायां तु सुभगाभिष्चुकन्यायः sade | यथा सुभगष्वं fread खीत्वं Get च तथाविधपदद्रयसामानाधिकरण्याद््तीयमानमपि विरुद्रत्वादेकस्मिन्युगपन्न संभवति तथेकस्मिन्नीशरूपाक्तवचने प्रामाण्याभ्रामाण्यलक्षणविरुदं धर्मद्वयं न संभवतीति केचित्‌ । अन्ये तु यथा सुभगाभिष्ुकौ ्रबरुघातकभयात्कंचि- च्छरणं प्रपन्नो स च हरणागतल्यागदोषश्रवणात्सवभ्रयन्नेनोभयो रक्षणे प्रबरा- रिकृतस्वघातभयेन ant ar mast सुभगां wate fags त्यजतीति यत्तत्र तस्येच्छैव नियामिका न तु किंचिद्धिनिगमकमस्ि तथा प्रकृतेऽपीडवचनत्वा- gaat: प्रामाण्ये पौरूषेयस्वान्मूरप्रमाणसपिश्चतया धर्मादौ तदभावादप्रामाण्ये वा प्रसेऽपयुक्तविभागे ता्िकेच्छैव नियामिकेयर्थमाडुः'? ॥ This seems to me very unsatisfactory, but I can suggest nothing better. The only work in which I have met with the nyaya is the Atmatattvaviveka (page 54), where it is wrongly printed as सुभगाभिकन्याय. It would need a long extract to make it intelligible, so I must refer the reader to the work itself.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 85

gloss_en: The simile of the ascent of a staircase.
explanation: The simile of the ascent of a staircase. Used of knowledge arrived at gradually, by easy steps. “Line upon line, precept 87 upon precept, here a little and there a little” There is an instance of its use in Bhamati 1. 8. 8 ( page 201 ):—“ag चानात्मविद्‌ आत्मानं विविदिषोनौरदस्य प्रश्ने परमात्मानमेवासमे व्याख्यास्यामीलभि- सन्धिमान्सनत्कृमारः सोपानारोहणन्यायेन स्थूलादारभ्य तत्तद्ूमब्युलपादनक्रमेण मूमानमतिदुक्लनतया waged व्युत्पादयामासः" ॥ सोभरिन्यायः ॥ The illustration afforded by Saubhari. The story of this sage is told in Book 4, chapter 2, of the Vishnu Purdna, and, with less detail, in Book 9, chapter 6, of the Bhagavata Purana, We there learn that, after remaining immersed in a piece of water for twelve years, the Muni was so much impressed by the happiness of the little fish which disported themselves around their great progenitor named Sammada, that he determined to marry and raise up progeny himself! He accordingly went to king Mandhata, the father of fifty charming daughters, and asked for one of them in marriage, Taken somewhat aback by the appearance of this old and emaciated suitor, but fearing to displease him, the king replied that it was the custom for princesses to select their own husband, but that if any one of them chose him as such, he could take her to wife. He was accordingly conducted to the ladies’ apartments; but, on the way there, he transformed his repelling person into one of handsome and youthful appearance, and the consequence was that each of the fifty maidens fell violently in love with him and demanded him as a husband, and so he married them all! Each of them lived in a beautiful mansion by herself, surrounded by every luxury. After a time, the king went on a visit to them to see how they fared. The first one pointed to her lovely surroundings and told of her husband’s goodness to her, but added that there was one thing 88 which troubled her very much, namely, that her husband was always with her, and therefore her sisters could never enjoy his society at all. The king then visited each of the others in turn, and heard exactly the same thing from each; and so the necessary inference is that the sage entered into fifty bodies at one and the same time, and this is the sole point of the nyaya! It occurs in Bhémati 4. 4. 11 as follows:—“dhaatatiitaविविधदेदस्यापयीयेण मान्धातृकन्याभिः पञ्चादाता विहारः पौराणिकैः waa.” Venkatanatha is the only other writer in whose works I have met with it. On page 65 of the Nydyasiddhdnjana we read:— “orama तु निलयसर्वज्ञस्योपाधियोग एव ॒परिहास्यः । उपाधिभिर्छेदनाययोगेन ब्रह्मण एव संसारित्वानपायः | उपाधिसञ्चारे प्रतिक्षणं बन्धमोक्षप्रसङ्गः सौभर्यादिवदुपाधिभेदेऽपि प्रतिसन्धानस्य दुस््यज्वात्‌ | ठेदाभ्युपगमे चाच्छेदयत्ववादविरोधः?? ॥ In his vritti on Tattvamuktakalapa iti. 22, where the same subject is discussed, we find the following:-— «न च स्वेनान्यदेहादेरधिष्टानादिसम्भवः | सोभरिन्यायतस्तत्ततप्रतिवन्दिग्रसङ्गतः ॥ It occurs again in the text and comment of verse 31.
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 86

gloss_en: The simile of the redness of the crystal.
explanation: The simile of the redness of the crystal. Such redness is owing to the proximity of a red object, suchas a rose &. The illustration is much used by writers on Vedanta &, For example, we read in Paraméarthasira, verses 16 and 61:— “ नानाविधवस्तूनां वर्णान्धत्ते यथामलः स्फटिकः । तद्वदुपाधेयणभावितस्य भावं बिभुरधतते ॥ १६ ॥ विगतोपाधिः स्फटिकः खप्रभया भाति निर्मखो यद्वत्‌ । चिद्दीपः खप्रभया तथा विभातीह निरुपाधिः ॥ aan” So, too, Aniruddha on Sénkhyasitra ii, 35:-- “यथा जपाकुसुमसं- सर्गात्स्फटिके ate तदपगमास्स्फटिकः aeaarataga’'n See also Atmabodha, 14; and a verse, by some unknown author, quoted in 89 the Panini section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 144 Bib, Ind.,and 168 in Jivananda’s edn.). In the Kuvalayananda (page 289) under the figure aqgu, we 1680:--“अन्यदीययुणग्रइणाग्रहणे च रक्तस्फटिकवसख्रमाङिन्यादिन्यायेनान्यदीयगुणेनेवानुरजजनाननुरजजने विवक्षिते" ॥ See also Vivaranaprameya, page 214. a
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 88

gloss_en: Nature is hard to overcome.
explanation: Nature is hard to overcome. This is no doubt based on Hitopades'a iii. 56:— “यः स्वभावो हि यस्य स्यात्तस्यासौ दुरतिक्रमः । श्रा यदि क्रियते राजा ale नाश्नाद्युपानहम्‌ Ww” Raghunatha applies it in the following manner:—“agq सविरासाक्ञानवाधकस्वभावत्वं Agra तदा स्वमावो दुरतिक्रम इति न्याया- WaT Walesa सविलासाद्वानबाधनादेहपातस्तात्कालिकः स्यात्तथा चोच्छिन्नसंप्रदायकत्वादुपनिषदामबोधत्वरक्षणाप्रामाण्यग्रसङ्ग इति ae साय यदाद्वानस्य नादोऽप्यारन्धकर्मणा प्रतिबन्धान्न देहादिक्चय आरन्धकर्मणश्च भोगलक्षणकायैक्षयादेव क्षय इति समाधीयते तदेषुवेगक्चयन्यायग्रवरत्तिः | धनुषः सकाशान्सुक्तस्येषोर्बाणस्य कर्मणः भ्रारञ्धवेगक्षयादेव क्षय इति प्रसिद्धम्‌ ॥ The expression occurs also in the following verse of the Kusumanjali (i. 7 ):— “opel न कमः wife afd च समस्य न । शक्तिभेदो न चाभिन्नः स्वभावो दुरतिक्रमः ॥ ५. S

explanation: The snake stupified by its own poison bites its own body! This saying is found in Udayana’s Atmatattvavireka, page 67, line 6:—“af हि न ज्ञातं किञ्चिदस्तील्यादिम्रतिन्तार्थः ्रतित्तां स्प्ररोत्कथमयमैः प्रत्येतव्यः | नचेत्कथं सानुपपन्ना । तदुपपन्नत्वे च कथं पुनः प्रतिज्ञा उपपद्यते | तदिदमायातं स्रविषमूच्छितो भुजङ्ग आत्मानमेव दशतीति ॥ 12 90 ;

gloss_en: The simile of the relation as master and servant.
explanation: The simile of the relation as master and servant. “It is used to mark the relation of the feeder and the fed, or the supporter and the supported, subsisting between any two objects.” Apte’s Sanskrit Dictionary, It is of very common occurrence. For instance, in S‘ankara’s bhashya on Brahmasitra 2. 1. 4 in a discussion as to the relation between Brahma and the world, he says:—“afe साम्ये सत्युपकार्योपकारकभावो भवति । नहि प्रतीपौ परस्परस्योपकुरुतः | aq चेतनमपि ata स्वामिश्लन्यायेन मोक्तुरुपकरिष्यति । न । स्वामि्ध्योरप्यचेतनां शस्यैव चेतनं प्रतयुपकारकत्वात्‌” ॥ Also in 2. 8. 48, we read:—“qaa जीवेश्वरयोरप्युपकार्योपकारकभा- वाभ्युपगमाक्कि स्वामिश्ुतयवत्संबन्ध आहोख्विदभिविस्फुशिङ्गवदित्यस्यां विचि- कित्सायामनियमो वा प्रा्नोति'? ॥ See, too, Ramatirtha on Veddnta877 19 ( page 141, last line ).
source: Jacob, A Second Handful of Popular Maxims, Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1909 (Second Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 90

gloss_en: The simile of an alligator in a lake.
explanation: The simile of an alligator in a lake. Used of things which mutually aid or protect each other. See qaf@erara. « SOME OPINIONS OF THE PRESS ON THE FIRST HANDFUL. SS RE “There are few books which give the results 0६80 much reading in ˆ 80 small a compass as this little pamphlet of some fifty pages. As its name indicates, it is a collection of those popular maxims, or, as Dr. Bihler calls them, “inferences from familiar instances,” which one hears so frequently in conversation with Pandits.........Similiar collections have been frequently put together...but we very rarely find in these any reference to the use of nyayas in actual literature. The great value of Colonel Jacob’s work is that at least one such reference is given for every maxim quoted. He has drawn principally from works on philosophy and on rhetoric, branches of Sanskrit literature which he has made peculiarly his own, and the modestly styled ‘Handful’ is only one more example of the labourious care and love of accuracy for which its author is distinguished. The book is useful to other than Sanskrit scholars.........--..«Lhe student of Tulasi Dasa, or of Malik Muhmmad, will find many an obscure passage illumined by this true déhali-dipaka, throwing light, as it does, both upon the past and on the present.” Journal of Royal Asiatic Sosiety ( July 1901). “Under the title ‘Laukikanyayafjali? or ‘A handful of Popular Maxims’, Colonel G. A. Jacob has published and explained a number of those allusions to popular and, at the time, no doubt, well-known proverbs or stories which abound in Sanskrit literature. These , nyayas find their parallels from our own language in such common sayings as ‘like the pot and the kettle’, ‘like the hare and the tortoise’ ete. ‘The proverbs or stories to which they allude are perfectly wellknown and need no explanation. In the case of their Sanskrit 2 counterparts, the memory of their origin has not always been preseryed or has become obscured. The list now published consists of those examples which Colonel Jacob has been able either to trace to their source or to partly explain. Let us hope that this useful little work, the result of many years of reading may, in his own words, ‘become the nucleus of a very much larger collection’,” Luzac’s Oriental List ( March-April 1901 ). “From what we have written above, we think our readers will see what a useful little book Colonel Jacob’s is, especially for those whowish to address the people of this land in forms of speech and with thoughts that are familiar to them. 4 Prakds'ak ( Kolhapur, March 1900 ). ॥ Printed by B.
