gloss_en: A thing done at a wrong time [might as well be left undone, for it] would be regarded as not done.
explanation: A thing done at a wrong time [might as well be left undone, for it] would be regarded as not done. It oceurs in the following passage of Madhava’s Nydyamalavistara 10. 1. 1:-- “किं चतुर्धा- करणादध्वमावाद्यते किंवा प्रयाजेभ्यः पुरा ATT । अकाले EHS स्यादिति न्यायेनावाहनस्य निरर्थकत्वात्‌." Again, in S‘abara on Jaimini 6. 2. 25, with reference to the times prescribed for the Agnihotra, and New and Full Moon sacrifices, we read “ तसादन्येषु कारेषु अविहितत्वात्छृतमप्यङरृतं स्यात्‌. Compare the following which is quoted on page 284 of Nydyakandali :— “स्वकाले यदङुर्वस्त्करोयन्यदचेतनः | प्र्वायोऽस्य तेनैव नाभावेन स जन्यते"? ॥ And somewhat similarly we have Patanjali 1. 2. 64 ( vart. 48 )--“अद्राखेोक्ते क्रियमाणे विगुणं at walt विगुणे च कर्मणि फलानवापिः" ॥
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 1

gloss_en: The simile of the eyeball.
explanation: The simile of the eyeball. An illustration of extreme sensitiveness—in persons or things. The following from Yogabhasya ii, 15 (page 78) is an example of its application to a per. 80:--एवमिदमनादिदुःखसखरोतो rad योगिनमेव अ्रतिकूलात्मकत्वादु- दवेजयति | कस्मात्‌ । अक्षिपात्रकल्पो हि विद्धानिति । यथोर्णातन्तुरक्षिपात्रे न्यस्तः स्पेन दुःखयति न चान्येषु गातव्रावयवेष्वेवमेतानि दुःखान्यक्षिपात्रकल्पं योगिन मेव Bala नेतरं प्रतिपत्तारम्‌." - This is very well put, also, in the Maniprabha on the same siitra. See, too, Tatparyatika, page 442, line 8, It looks as if this nyiya, like many others found 9 : in orthodox works, came from a Buddhist source. Prof, L. de la Vallée Poussin has kindly pointed out the following verses on page 476 of the Madhyamakavritti:—“sutqen यथैव हि करतलसंस्थं न विद्यते पुंभिः । अक्षिगतं तु तदेव हि जनयत्यरतिं च पीडां च ॥ करतल. सदृशो बालो न वेत्ति संस्कारदुःखतापक््म | अक्षिसदरास्तु विद्धान्‌ तेनैवोद्रेजते गाढम्‌.” ॥ The word अक्षिपाच्र is not in any of our dictionaries, In the Yogavartika it is defined thus—“‘sem: पात्रेणाधारेण Test तुल्यो विद्वानिति." ॥ अच्चिहोत्रन्यायः ॥ The rule as to the Agnihotia sacrifice [consisting of morning and evening libations]. It forms the subject of Jaimini 6. 2. 23-26, where the injunction “qrasttaaftela ज॒होति' is discussed. The interpretation put upon these words by the purvapaksin is that the householder is to do nothing else but offer the Agnihotra during his whole life! Kunte thus summarizes his argument:— “From the time of the establishment of a sacred fire to the time of death the Agnihotra is to be performed continuously, without the remission of a moment. This is the duty of an Arya. He cannot rest fora moment. The Agnihotra is not a constituent part of any other sacrifice. It is an independent sacrifice by itself. It therefore accomplishes the purpose of a person; and must therefore be unremittingly adhered to; and it does not matter if, in performing a principal act, minor acts are neglected. It is therefore reasonable to perform the Agnihotra-sacrifice alone continuously for life.” The reply to all this is that the meaning of the vidhi is simply that it is to be offered every morning at daybreak, and every evening, according to the injunction “gdtanfela होतव्यं व्युष्टायां प्रातः.” For a description of the Agnihotra, see S'autapatha Brahmana, Kanda 2, Adhyiiyas 3 and 4; also a very useful excursus of Kunte’s on pages 410-420 of his Suddars'anacintanika. Brahmasitrabhdsya 8. 4. 82 shows how Vedantists apply the injunction relating to the Agnihotra. 8
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 1

gloss_en: Attempting to dispel the darkness with alanyp no bigger than your finger! Endeayouring to bring about a great result by the use of manifestly inadequate means.
explanation: Attempting to dispel the darkness with alanyp no bigger than your finger! Endeayouring to bring about a great result by the use of manifestly inadequate means. It occurs in the following passage of Atmatattvaviveha, page 52:—“q चास्ाकामिव वाप्यत्र मूकतैव शरणं सवधा वचनविरोध द्यदासीनस्य सा शोभते । न चात्र विधौ विरोधः कश्चित्‌ । न च त्वमुदासीनः प्रयोजने Tawar । तस्मादलमङ्गकिदीपिकया ध्वान्तध्वसविधिमनुष्टाय. Iam indebted to Mr. Arthur Venis for an explanation of this nyaya.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 3

gloss_en: The tip of a finger cannot be touched by itself.
explanation: The tip of a finger cannot be touched by itself. Akin to the sayings ^ A man cannot mount on his own shoulder,” and “The edge of a sword cannot cut itself.” It occurs in Nydyavartikatatparyatika page 466, line 10 from bottom:—“qurgeat न तेनैवाङ्क्यभ्रेण स्णर्यत एवं ्ानं न तेनेव ज्ञानेन ग्रहीतुं शक्यते. Then in Madhyamakavritti, page 62, we have the double simile:—‘“qथापि नाम तसयैवासिधारया सैवासिधारा न शक्यते Se न तेनेवांगुल्यम्रेण तदे- वांगुख्यग्रं शाक्यते स्पष्टुमेवमेव न तेनैव चित्तेन तदेव चित्तं शक्यं द्रष्टुम्‌.” ॥ We meet with it again in Parthasarathi’s comment on the S‘inyavada section of the S'lokavartika (page 288 ):—“a हि पाकः पच्यते छिद्‌ वा Baa नापि acre कर्तकर्मत्वं वा एकस्य संभवति । ने ह्यङ्कल्यम्रेेवाङ्गल्यम्र wat नाप्यङ्गल्यग्रमात्मानं erate | तेनासां विधानां werd ्विदप्यदर्शनाञ्ानेऽपि नासि संभवः. I do not understand the double statement here about the finger-tip. Parthasarathi could not mean that the tip of one finger cannot be touched by the tip of another finger! The second part of the statement looks like a marginal gloss which has got into the text. The following verse is found in Prakaranapancika, page 63, and in Nydyakanikd, p. 268:— 4 ARS यथात्मानं नात्मना स्प्रष्टुमर्हति | स्वांशेन ज्ञानमप्येवं नात्मानं ज्ञातुमर्हति ॥ See also Nydyamakaranda, pages 181, 188; S‘ribhdsya page 169; and Sarvarthasiddhi, page 391. 4
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 3

gloss_en: There are a hundred herds of elephants on the tip of my finger! This illustration of an absurdity occurs frequently.
explanation: There are a hundred herds of elephants on the tip of my finger! This illustration of an absurdity occurs frequently. In Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 232 g, we read:—“‘ary केचिच्चो- qafa । aise ब्युत्पत्तिनिरूपणप्रयासः । शब्दस्याथौसंस्परिीव्वात्‌ । न हयङ्कल्यम्रे हसतियूथशातमास्त इत्यादिश्दैः कश्चिदर्थः प्रमीयते । यत्राप्तवाक्ये प्रमीयते तत्रापि मानान्तरनिबन्धना सा प्रमिति शब्दनिबन्धनेति.› Then in (15४7 ii. 32:--“जआाप्तोदीरितवाक्येषु मारुतीमाधवादिषु | व्यभिचारान्न तद्युक्तमासषस्वस्यानिरुक्तितः ॥ ३२ ॥ सखरकपोरुकल्पितमार्तीमाधवादिवाक्येषु प्रामाण्याभावादतिभ्याक्चिः । न हि gua एव सन्नाटकनारिकादिभ्रबन्धविरचनमात्रेणानाषो भवति भवभूतिः | उक्तं चैतदुम्बकेन दासोऽपि कसरचिदुपदिशति न त्वयानजुभूताथैविषयं वाक्यं प्रयोक्तव्यं यथाङ्कल्यमे हस्ियूथरतमासत इतिः | तत्रार्थव्यभिचारः स्फुटः.” Compare 8811108, p. 13, verse 4. In the commentary on Khandanakhandakhadya, page 104, the saying is modified to “अङ्गुल्यग्रे करिशतं विहरति?” and another of a like kind is added, namely “ay कणैकुहरं प्रविरय fae: stata,” and in Atmatattvaviveka, page 65, Udayana gives us “ga aut nfasa गजो गर्जति भेषजसुच्यताम्‌.'* The Umbaka quoted above is perhaps the Umbeka* referred to by Hall (on page 166 of his * Hall found this name ( together with those of Prabhakara, Vamana, and Revana ) in a verse of the vritti by Charitra Sinha Gani on the Saddars'anasamuccaya. What is manifestly the same verse is found also in the vritti ascribed to Manibhadra; but there the name Utpala is substituted for Umbeka, The verse is as follows:—® उत्पलः कारिकां वेत्ति तत्रं वेत्ति प्रभाकरः | वामनस्तूभयं वेत्ति न किचिदपि रेवणः ” ॥ From the fact that all the other authors and works mentioned in Gani’s vritti are found in the latter also, I am inclined to think that the two are identical. १ 5 Index) as an authority on Mimamsa. In the Catalogus Catalogorum, the latter is identified with Mandanamis'ra, which is one of the names by which Sures’varicarya is known. अलयन्तबटवन्तोऽपि पौरजानपदा जनाः । दुबैेरपि रूपै zy
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 4

gloss_en: Even very powerful men from town and country are held in check by weaker men who have the king’s swpport.
explanation: Even very powerful men from town and country are held in check by weaker men who have the king’s swpport. This verse from the Zuntravartika ( page 863), found also, as a quotation, in Mimamsainydyaprakds'a, page 35, is thus applied by Raghunatha:—“qg निवैलेनापि प्रबलसहायेन Tat बाध्यते तत्र अत्यन्तबलवन्तोऽपि पौरजानपदा जनाः | THVT बाध्यन्ते पुरुषैः पार्थिवाश्रितेः' इति न्यायोऽवतरति | स्पष्टार्थोऽयम्‌ | उदाहरणं त॒ श्रु्यपेक्षया gder अपि स्मृतेराचमनरूपप्रवरुपद्ाथौश्रितत्वेन प्राबल्यम्‌ | अतः श्रोतक्रमल्यागेन वेद्‌- करणानन्तरं Fa आचमनमेव कामिति दिक्‌. We may compare with this the following from Sures’vara’s large vartika, page 753 :— “antad बरीयांसमबरीयानपि खयम्‌ | धर्म AS TAA जेतुं लोके तथा यथा ॥ राज्ञा बखेनाल्पबरो बलीयांसं कुटु म्बिनम्‌ । ॥ जेतुमा्ंसते तस्माद्धमैः स्याद्रल्वत्तमः' ॥ अदित्सोरव॑णिजः प्रतिदिनं पत्रकिखितश्वस्तनदिनभणनन्यायः ॥ The simile of the merchant who was unwilling to give, and who wrote every day saying that he would give on the morrow ! It occurs in the following passage of Mallisena’s Syidvddaman107 (page 128) :--“सौगताः किलेत्थं प्रमाणयन्ति सै सलक्षणिकं यतः सर्वै तावद्भटादिकं वस्तु सुद्भरसन्निधौ नाशं गच्छदृङ्यते | तत्र येन स्वरूपेणान्त्यावस्थायां घटादिकं विनश्यति तचचैतस्स्ररूपमुस्पन्नमात्रस्य विद्यते । तदानीसुस्पादानन्तरमेव तेन नष्टव्यमिति व्यक्तमस्य क्षणिकत्वम्‌ | wien एव स्रभावस्तस्य हेतुतो जातो यत्कियन्तमपि ae स्थित्वा fase | एवं तर्हिं सुद्भरादिसक्निधानेऽपि एष 6 एव तस्य स्वभाव इति पुनरप्यनेन तावन्तमेव कारं स्थातव्यमिति नेवं विनस्ये- दिति सोऽयमदित्सो्वणिजः प्रतिदिनं पत्रलिखितश्वस्तनदिनभणनन्यायः'? -॥ Those who, in an Indian cantonment, have ever undertaken the thankless task of the collection of promised subscriptions to a fund, are very familiar with the “kal Go,” or “parson ke din Go,” with which their messenger is often greeted, with perhaps stronger language superadded! Human nature is much the same everywhere. Compare Proverbs iii, 28,
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 5

gloss_en: A truth or conclusion which implies another truth or conclusion.
explanation: A truth or conclusion which implies another truth or conclusion. This is the third of four kinds of सिद्धान्त defined in Nydyasiitras 1, 1. 28-31, the others being (1) सर्वैतन्रसिद्धान्तः (2) भ्रतितन्रसिद्धान्त, and (4) अश्युपगमसिद्धान्त. Ballantyne’s rendering of the four is (1) a dogma of all the schools, (2) a dogma peculiar to some school, (3) a hypothetical dogma, and (4) a dogmatic corollary. In Tarkikaraksa i. 29 (page 126) we have the following description of १10108.--शयुगपदूज््ानानुत्पा्िरमनसो लिङ्गमिति | एवं चाणुतयैव मनसः सिद्धिः | अन्यथा युगपदनेकेन्दरियाधि- छानाद्युगपदृज््ञानोदयमप्रसंगात्‌” । On this, Mallinatha comments thus:— “aq चेति aed: सर्वज्ञत्वादिवन्मनसोऽणुत्वमधिकरणसिद्धान्तन्यायाद्धा्भमराहकादेव सिद्धमिल्थः?' There is another example in Atmatattvaviveka, page 83, line 9; and a third in Yamunacarya’s Agamapraméanya, page 17, line 1. For an example of the three other kinds of siddhanta, see Nydyavartikatatparyatiha, page 36, lines 16-27.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 6

explanation: the rule regarding the qualifications [required of a sacrificer]. Jaimini 6. 1. 1-8 deals with part of this: The decision is that he must be desirous of heaven, according to the vidhis “द्ञपूर्णमासाभ्यां enter यजेत" “sahtettd स्वगकामो aaa.” The 7 principal thing here is the desire for heaven, whilst the sacrificial act is subordinate. The remainder of the pada deals with the physical and social fitness demanded. See under आख्यातानामर्थं saat &९, below. For a full description of the four kinds of injunction, of which adhikaravidhi is the third, see Laugaksibhaskara’s Arthasangraha, page 4, with Dr, Thibaut’s translation, page 7 &. अनधीते महाभाष्ये व्यथा स्यात्पदमञ्जरी | अधीतेऽपि महाभाष्ये व्यथौ सा पदमञ्जरी ॥ The Padamanjart would be of no use to one who had not read the Mahabhasya, and would be equally useless [ because unnecessary ] if the latter had been read! This saying is used by Raghunatha to illustrate the position of the Ganapatas who regard the worship of Ganapati as essential and all-inclusive, A portion of the argument is as follows:—“sq: Saad: स्वैरपि स एवाराध्यः | agai विनोन्यपूजाया वैयर््यस्रणेन फलजनकत्वायोगात्‌ । अवश्यपेक्षितानपेक्षितयोरपेश्षितं स्मरणीयमिति न्यायेन कृताकृत्रसङ्गी यो विधिः स fa इति न्यायेन च तदाराधनस्यावश्यकत्वात | कते च तस्मिन्विद्यार्थी लभते विद्यां धनार्थी wad धनम्‌ । पुत्रार्थीं खमते पुत्रान्मोक्षार्थी परमं पदमिलया- दिवचनेभ्यः सवें्टाभसंभवेनानधीते महाभाष्ये व्यर्था स्यात्पदमञ्जरी । अधी- तेऽपि महाभाष्ये erat सा पदमञज्जरीति न्यायेनान्याराधने प्रयोजनाभावात्‌." The second nyaya quoted here is a slight modification of Nagojis paribhasi उत, “gagarale fa तद्विपरीतमनिलयम्‌. In the Preface to vol, 2 of his edition of the Mahabhasya Dr, Kielhorn, referring to the above dictum of the Pandits, says,— “ Whatever truth there be in this remark, I can say for myself that I have been much assisted by Haradatta’s learned work, even though it is based to a great extent on the Mahabhisya itself and on Kaiyata’s commentary ”; and he points out that, though the Padamanjari is primarily a commentary on the Kasiika, yet its author discusses, often at great length, most of 8 ; the arguments advanced in the Mahabhisya. Jayanta Bhatta has several verses of the same type as that regarding the Padamanjari. They will be found on pages 29, 39, 55, 61, 182, 447, and 448 of his Vy@yamanjari, I quote that on page 182 as a sample:— कार्य चेदवगम्येत किं कारणपरीक्षया | कर्यै चेन्नावगम्येत किं कारणपरीक्षया ॥ अनन्तरस्य विधिर्वा भवति प्रतिषेधो वा ॥ [A rule containing ] an 4771011, or a prohibition [ enjoins or forbids only ] that which is nearest [to it in some other rule]. - Here is one of Raghunatha’s grammatical nyayas, included in both of his works. My translation is based on that of Dr. Kielhorn in his well-known edition and translation of the Paribhasendus ekhara, where it appears as Paribhasa Lx1. NagojiBhatta took it, of course, from the Mahabhdsya, and I have noted the following ten instances of its occurrence:—1, 1. 43 ( vart. 3); 1, 2. 48 (vart. 7); 1. 3. 12( vart. 7); 1. 3. 14 (vart. 3); 1.3. 58 (vart.3); 1. 4. 17 ; 3. 1. 67 (vart. 5); 7, 1. 21 (vart. 1); 7, 2.3 (vart. 2); and 7. 3. 85 (vart. 4). HAAS: शब्दार्थः ॥ The meaning of a word is that which cannot be known from any other sowrce [ such as implication &. ]. This is Mr. Venis’ rendering (in the Pandit, vol. vi, page 97) of the maxim in the Vedantaparibhdsa (chap. iv. page 280). It occurs in Tantravartila (page 340) in a more extended form, namely भयावानेव हि अनन्यरभ्योऽर्थः शब्दाद्रम्यते स aa: शब्दाः” ॥ Prof, Ganganatha Jha (on page 474, line 10 of his translation) translates thus:—“In the case of any word, all that is not cognizable by means of any other word is held to be the meaning of that word.” This, howeyer, seems, to overlook the words 9 ^क्ाब्दाद्रम्यते. The nyaya is found in its usual form in Agamapramanya, page 35, line 10. In the Pirnaprajna section of ` Sarvadars'anasangraha ( page 85 of Jivananda’s edition) we have the cognate nyaya “अनन्यरभ्यः शचाखार
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 6

gloss_en: From seeing smoke rising from one house we do not infer that there is w fire im another house.
explanation: From seeing smoke rising from one house we do not infer that there is w fire im another house. This is from Tantravartika (page 180, line 9) on Jaimini’s siitra “अनुमानव्यवस्था- नात्तत्संयक्तं प्रमाणं स्यात्‌" (1. 8. 15 ). अन्यार्थमपि प्रकृतमन्यार्थं भवति ॥ A thing, though made for one purpose, may also serve for another, This is found in Mahabhasya 1. 1. 23 (vart. 4), 1. 3. 12 ( vart. 5), and 6. 1. 50, as follows—“qaragera a चान्यार्थ भ्रकरतमन्यार्थं भवतीयन्यार्थमपि प्रकृतमन्यार्थं भवति । तद्यथा | शाल्यर्थं कुल्याः भ्रणीयन्ते ताभ्यश्च पानीयं पीयत उपस्प्श्यते च शाख्यश्च भाव्यन्ते.” 1४ is quoted by S’abara on Jaimini 8. 1. 12 (page 220), and is referred to by Kumarila in his long and interesting discussion of दोष (an accessory—that which serves the purpose of something else) in the opening part of the third chapter of the Tantravartike. On page 668, line 13, we read:— “a हि कश्चिदपि amsकल्यास्थसुदकं पिबन्मदथमेताः sir इल्यध्यवस्यति । तस्मादन्यत्तादर्थ्यमन्यश्चोपकार इति विज्ञायते. Patanjali’s illustration is found in Pancapadika, page 45, and is employed by Vidyaranya in Vivaranaprameyasangraha (page 118, line 9), where it is styled कुल्याप्रणयनन्याय. Compare the nyaya “ara aftaet सूपादेरतिथ्युपकारकल्वम्‌?” in the Second Handful. 12 ४ |
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 11

gloss_en: The law regarding the interruption [of a procession of priests], It is thus explained by Goldstiicker:—« Used in the liturgical writings of the interruption of a procession of priests, caused by the inadvertence of one or several amongst them; thus, it being the rule that at the first Savana of the Jyotishtoma the priests must proceed one after the other ‘in the black-ant fashion, the one that comes after holding his preceder by the hem of his garment, an interruption caused by the dropping of the hem, on the part of one priest would be एककतृकोऽपच्छेदः Ye” This curious ceremony is discussed in Jaimini 6.
explanation: The law regarding the interruption [of a procession of priests], It is thus explained by Goldstiicker:—« Used in the liturgical writings of the interruption of a procession of priests, caused by the inadvertence of one or several amongst them; thus, it being the rule that at the first Savana of the Jyotishtoma the priests must proceed one after the other ‘in the black-ant fashion, the one that comes after holding his preceder by the hem of his garment, an interruption caused by the dropping of the hem, on the part of one priest would be एककतृकोऽपच्छेदः Ye” This curious ceremony is discussed in Jaimini 6. 5. 49-56, where certain penalties are prescribed for letting go the garment (कच्छविमोचन). The matter is well and concisely put in the Nyayamalavistara on the above portion of Jaimini, and much information is contained in Kunte’s notes on the same sfitras, The nyaya is employed by writers on Vedanta. It is found, for example, in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 10, line 8:— “उये्टस्यापीति । अपच्छेदन्यायेन पूर्वस्य परेण बाधमादंक्य तदपेक्षसयेति विशेषितं तेनोत्तरस्य पूर्वापिक्षायासुपक्रमाधिकरणन्याय एव प्रवर्तत इति सूचितमिलय्थः" ॥ The passage of the Veddntakalpataru here explained is found on page 6, line 8:--“जयेष्टस्यापि पौ्वापर्यन्यायेन बाधमाशंक्याह तदपेश्चस्येति.” The पौवीपथन्याय is a part of the अपच्छेदन्याय, and derives its name from sitra 54, namely ^पौवौपर्ये पूवैदौ्बल्यं प्रकृतिवत्‌; ' the subject of the adhikarana being that when the priests, walking in procession, let go their hold one after another, the one who does 80 last is liable to a penalty. This same siitra is quoted in full in Bhamati, page 5, last line, and is immediately followed by a verse from Kumarila’s Tantravdrtika, page 819; where, however, the reading of the first line is पौकौपर्यबरीयस्त्वं instead of the garqzadtaed of the Bhimati. The same verse is quoted by Vacaspatimis’ra at the bottom of page 59 of his Nydyavirtikatatparyatika, where the reading agrees with that of the Bhamati, The अपच्छेदन्याय is found in Venkatanatha’s Sarvarthasiddhi, pages 210, 359, and874 ; and also in S’ribha13 syd, page 143, where Dr. Thibaut (on page 26 of his translation) renders it “ As in the case of the Udgatri and Pratihartri breaking the chain in succession.” The whole ceremony is very clearly explained on page 156 of the Tattvadipana, and the passage will repay perusal.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 12

gloss_en: General rules are set aside by special ones, This well-known grammatical rule, found thus in Mahabhasya 2.
explanation: General rules are set aside by special ones, This well-known grammatical rule, found thus in Mahabhasya 2. 1. 24 ( vart. 5) and in a variety of forms in paribhasas, is admitted to these pages chiefly because, in two of his poems, Kalidasa has adopted it asa sort of मात्खन्याय to illustrate a phase of human affairs, namely the subordination of the weak to the strong, One instance is in Kumarasambhava ii. 27:— ^ लब्धगरतिष्ठः प्रथमं ot किं बलवत्तरैः । अपवादैरिवोत्सर्गाः कृतव्यावृत्तयः we? ॥ The other is in Raghwamsa xv. 7:— ^ यः कश्चन रघूणां हि परमेकः परंतपः | अपवाद इवोत्सर्गं व्यावर्तयितुमीश्वरः'” ॥ In a note on this latter passage Mr. Shankar P, Pandit says “Whatever may be the value of the simile as regards the similitude, it certainly cannot be said to be very poetical, being derived altogether from a pedant’s life.” At the end of his comment on the former verse, Mallinatha adds “'इल्यर्मतिगहनावगाहनेन', १111011 may possibly indicate some feeling of disapproval on his part also, It is on the principle enunciated in this nydya that the law forbidding the taking of life is superseded by the Vedic ritual which demands animal sacrifices; and it is interesting to note the famous Jaina Hemacandra’s denunciation of the whole argument in the eleventh verse of his Vitaraga~ stuti, the first half of which stands thus:— “ न धर्महेतुषिहितापि हिंसा नोत्खष्टमन्यार्थमपोद्यते चः” | 14 In his vritti on the verse Mallisena states the case from the Mimamsaka’s standpoint as follows—“sq न हिंस्यात्सर्वभूतानी- त्यादिना हिंसानिषेधः स ओत्सािको मार्गः सामान्यतो विधिरित्यर्थः । ततश्चापवादेनोत्सगंस्य बाधितत्वान्न श्रौतो हिंसाविधिदौषायोत्सगौपवादयोरपवादो बि- धिर्बङीयानिति न्यायात्‌.” ( Syadvadamanjari, page 84). In connection with the above quotation from Hemacandra, see the डमर्कमणिन्याय. AMA शाखरमर्थवत्‌ ॥ Scripture attaches a meaning [to an act &९.] when such [a meaning ] has not been established [and could not be established in any other way]. I take this to be the drift of this somewhat difficult nyaya which forms part of Jaimini’s sitra 6.2.18. In Brahmasitrabhdsya 3. 8, 18 there is a discussion as to the aim of certain S'rutis which prescribe the rinsing of the mouth, before and after eating, in connection with the prinavidyd. Were they intended to enforce आचमन as an act of cleanliness, and also as an act of ritual directed to prana? The decision is that the former was already provided for by smriti, and that s‘ruti merely attached to it its significance as a religious ceremonial. Bharatitirtha sums up the case in Adhikaranamala 3. 3. 9, as follows:—“gft sma qa: अप्राते शाखमर्थवत्‌" इति न्यायेन मानान्तराप्राप्तमनञ्नताचिन्तनमेव विधेयम्‌. .-आचमनं तु gata wires प्राप्तमिति न विधीयते...तस्मादाचमनस्य प्रप्तत्वाद्‌- नम्नताबुद्धिरेव प्राणोपासकं भरति विधेया.” The nyaya is found also in Tantravartika, page 145, line 3, and again on page 282; in S'ribhasya, page 554 ( where it is rendered by Dr. Thibaut, on page 138 of his translation, “Scripture has a purport with regard to what is not established by other means”); and in Nydyakandali, page 5 (where Prof. Ganganatha Jha’s interpretation of it is “An injunction has its use only in a case where its object has not been accomplished by other means”), Other references to it are Citsukht i. 7 ( Pandit, vol. iv. page 15 475 ); the Ramanuja section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 69, line 12, of Jivananda’s edition); and Sarvarthasiddhi pages 93,263. In Tattvadipana, page 544, the nyaya is quoted as “अनधिगते शाखमर्थवत्‌.*” अभ्यर्हितं पूर्वम्‌ ॥ The more worthy should come first. These words form part of Patanjali’s comment on a vartika on Panini’s rule 2. 2. 34 in regard to the position of words ina dvandva compound, The whole sentence is as follows:—“aaqied va निपततीति वक्तव्यम्‌ | मातापितरो श्रद्धामेधे"? ॥ Its use is not restricted to grammar, however, as the following extract from the first paragraph of Sayana’s introduction to his commentary on the Rigveda shows:- “aig marta सर्व्॑रा्नातत्वादभ्य्ितं पूर्वमिति न
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 13

gloss_en: The principle of an implied axiom [ 0, dogmatic corollary }, This is taken from Nydyasiitra 1.
explanation: The principle of an implied axiom [ 0, dogmatic corollary }, This is taken from Nydyasiitra 1.1.31 which Dr. Ballantyne rendered as follows:—“A ‘dogmatic corollary’ is the mention of a particular fact in regard to anything, not expressly declared in an aphorism, [our knowlege of the fact coming so immediately ] from what is recognized [by the maker of the aphorisms, as to render a demonstration superfluous—the fact being thus entitled to rank not as a deduction but as a dogma}.” The = ` nyaya is applied by Udayana in ,Kirandvali, page 20, line 4 from bottom, See also under जधिकरणसिद्धान्तन्याय16 अम्बुनि मजन्त्यलावृनि aa: वन्ते ॥ Gourds sink in water, but stones float! This is often quoted as an illustration of an absurdity. It is as old as the Mahabharata and appears at the end of chapter Lx1v of the Sabhaparvan:— “मजन्त्यलाबूनि शिखाः gaa सुद्यन्ति नावोऽम्भसि शश्वदेव. 1 have met with it twice in S’abara’s bhashya. In 1. 1. 5 ( page 11 )-““एवे- जातीयकं प्रमाणविरुद्धं वचनमप्रमाणम्‌ । अम्बुनि मजन्त्यलावृनि ्रावाणः वन्त इति यथा.” In 4 8 10:--“न चैवंजातीयकं seated वचनं प्रमाणं भवति | यथाम्बुनि मजन्तयलखाबूनि शिखाः वन्ते पावकः शीत इति." Other references are Sanksepas'Grivraka ii. 2 (Pandit, vol, vii, page 169); and, in Prakrita, just after verse viii, 31 of Balardmayana.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 15

gloss_en: Here is another boil on the top of a previous one! An illustration of difficulty upon difficulty; trouble upon trouble.
explanation: Here is another boil on the top of a previous one! An illustration of difficulty upon difficulty; trouble upon trouble. It occurs in 2171077 2. 2. 37 as follows:—“q हीश्वराधीना जनाः स्वातच्र्येण कपूयं कमं कतमहंन्ति । तदनधिष्टितं वा कपूयं कर्म॑ we प्रसोतुसुत्सहते | तस्मास्स्वतच्नोऽपीश्वरः कर्मभिः Tada इति दष्टविपरीतं कल्पनीयम्‌ । तथा चायमपरो गण्डस्योपरि स्फोट इतरेतराश्रयः भ्रसज्येत कर्मणेश्वरः भ्रव्तनीय ईश्वरेण च कर्मेति. In the same form it is put into the mouth of Raksasa in Mudrdrakshasa ए (page 220). The oldest examples, however, are in Prakrit. In the opening part of S’akuntala ii. we find it as “तदो गण्डस्स उवरि पिण्डि संबुत्ता” (or, in Dr. Pischel’s edition “जदो गण्डस्स sate विप्फोडओ संबुत्तो”); and in Viddhas'dlabhanjiké i. (page 12), as “अवरो गण्डस्स उवरि पिण्ठओ संबुत्तो.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 16

gloss_en: The maxim of a red [cow] one year old.
explanation: The maxim of a red [cow] one year old. This nyaya, found in Tantravartika 1. 2. 41, in Nyagyamanjari, page 294 (line 2 from bottom), and in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 619 17 (line 4), is the आरुणिन्याय of Jaimini 3, 1. 12, and is based on the following words connected with the ritual of the Jyotistoma sacrifice—“srenar पिङ्गा्षयैकहायन्या सोमं क्रीणाति, “he buys Soma with a red-coloured, yellow-eyed [cow ] of a year old.” The Mimamsaka delights in hair-splitting, and in trifling with language; and we have a typical instance of this idiosyncrasy in the way in which this simple sentence is dealt with. Because the cow is not actually mentioned, and the word अरुणा denotes a quality (redness ), an objector says “how can one buy Soma with a mere quality?” S’abara’s reply to this occupies ten octavo pages, whilst that of Kumarila fills twenty-nine ! The objection is concisely put in the Vydyamdldvistara, part of which is as follows:— “अरूणाशब्दोऽरणिमानं गुणमाचष्टे | गुणिविषयतया प्रयुज्यमानखापि ना- गृहीतविदेषणा विशिष्टे बुद्धिः" इति न्यायेन गुणबोधकत्वात्‌ | अन्वयव्यतिरेका- भ्यां गुणमात्रे aaa । तस्य चारणिमगुणस्य ठृतीयाश्रुला सोमक्रयसाधनत्वं म्रतीयते तच्चाजुपपन्नम्‌ | अमूर्तस्य गुणस्य वासोहिरण्यादिवत्कयसाधनत्वासंभवात्‌"? The reply to this 28:-“यद्यप्यमूरतौ गुणस्तथापि हायनवदशक्चिवचचच गोद्रव्यमवच्छिनत्ति | तच्च द्रव्यं साधनमिति agi गुणस्य क्रयेणान्वयो भवति | एवै सति वाक्यभेदो न भविष्यति" ॥ There is a long discussion on the nyaya in Ramanuja’s S’ribhagya 1. 1. 18, commencing on page 813 of the Benares edition. See Dr. Thibaut’s translation, page 222.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 16

gloss_en: A mungoose's standing on hot grownd.
explanation: A mungoose's standing on hot grownd. Used of a fickle, changeable person who never sticks to a thing. It is found in Mahabhasya 2. 1. 47, as follows:—“qaraaa aget न चिरं स्थातारो भवन्येवं कार्याण्यारभ्य यो न चिरं तिष्ठति a उच्यतेऽवतसेनङुरस्थितं त एतदिति.” It occurs a second time in 6, 2. 49 (vart. 6), in company with the expression उदकेविद्यीणै. The compound तीर्थकाक which is found in 2. 1. 42, has much the same meaning. Patanjali sth तीर्थे काका न चिरं स्थातारो भवन्येवं यो गुरुकुकानि गत्वा न 18 चिरं तिष्ठति स उच्यते तीथैकाक इति. In Marathi, however, the name is applied to “a person ever watchful after some booty or spoil,” a meaning which seems more in accord with the character of the crow than that assigned to it by Patanjali! अवयवप्रसिद्धेः समुदायप्रसिद्धिर्बरीयसी ॥ For this paribhas& sce under रथकारन्याय, . It is quoted by Kumarila in Tgntravartika 1. 4. 11, more than once, but one example will suffice-—“aogrtaar हि समुदायप्रसिद्धिरवयवप्रसिद्द बाधते तस्यास्त्वात्मराभो यत्र प्रमाणान्तरेण पूर्वालुभूतावयवार्थरदितेऽ्थं शब्दभ्रयोगो इयते | यथाश्वस्वकर्णत्वरहि ते बरक्षेऽश्वकर्णशब्दस्य.? As a parallel to this, we might take our word cockroach, which is neither a cock nor a roach! For other examples of the paribhasa see Tantravartika pages 538, 1002, 1048, and 1149. I have met with another reference in Tdtparyatika, page 150, line 12:--“अन्र च घ्राणादिदाब्दाः पंकजादिपदवदवयवा्थं निमित्तीकृत्य क्रचित्रचित्सामान्यविेषे वतन्ते अवयवार्थ॑स्य प्रतीयमानस्यासति बाधके परि- ल्यागायोगात्‌ | अश्वकणौदौ दृक्षविरोषवाचके वाजिकणौयोगेन वाधकेनावयवाथै- परित्यागात्‌. See also Vydyamanjari, page 385, line 10; and page 534, line 15.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 17

gloss_en: The principle of the words avi and avika.
explanation: The principle of the words avi and avika. Though both mean ‘a sheep, yet a derivative in the sense of the flesh of a sheep (arf) can be formed only from the latter. It occurs in Mahabhasya 4.1. 88 (vart. 2) as follows:—“qy gat: शब्दयोः समानार्थयोरेकेन विथ्रहोऽपरस्मादुत्य्तिर्भविष्यतयविरविकन्यायेन । vara | अवेमौसमिति विगृह्य अविकश्ब्दादुत्पत्तिभवति आविकसिति? ॥ Similarly in 8. 1. 89 (vart. 6); 4. 2. 60; 4. 38. 181; 5. 1. 7 ; 5. 1. 28; and 6 2. 11 (vart. 2). This inaccurate compound is one of the instances brought forward by Kumarila of the way in which grammar &. are set aside by learned writers. On this, see the nyaya “stsareer: &e.” below. 19 : In Nydyamanjari, pages 413 and 414, Jayanta Bhatta joins in the attack on this irregular compound, and on grammatical deformities found in the works of such writers as Manu, As‘valiyana, Valmiki, and Dvaipaiyana. The following is a portion of his comment:—‘aneqanisht अविरविकन्यायेनेति gat ages प्रयुयुक्षिते ‘qn धातुश्रातिपदिकयोः* इति saath लोपं न कृतवान्‌ । ‘gage terrae परिहारः gaa च 'अन्यथेवंकथमित्थंसु सिद्धाप्रama इति प्राप्तमपि णञ्चटसुपेक्ष्य क््वाप्रययं aR” ॥ The quotation “अन्यथाक्रत्वा We.” is from Patanjali on Panini 4. 1. 7 (vart. 3), and the siitra quoted is 3. 4 27. See Kumarila’s remarks on this expression in Tantravdrtika page 201. They would apply equally to “कथंङ्त्वा चोदितं We.” in 7. 1.3 ( vart. 5 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 18

gloss_en: How could men of intelligence be mounted on horses and yet forget their horses! Yet grammarians and others sometimes ignore their own rules! In Tantravartika 1.
explanation: How could men of intelligence be mounted on horses and yet forget their horses! Yet grammarians and others sometimes ignore their own rules! In Tantravartika 1. 3. 18 (according to the numbering of the Benares edition), Kumarila comments at great length on the corrupt forms of words employed by even learned writers. On page 200, he says “अन्तो नास्त्यपडाब्दानामितिहासपुराणयोः'? and then instances the curious word उमाम्य which is made to mean “a blow given by an elephant with both tusks” (qragareat दन्ताभ्यां प्रहारः). On the following page he says—“Ssfq व्याकरणस्यैव परे पारे प्रतिष्ठिताः । सुतरां तेऽपि गाव्यादितुल्यानेव प्रयुञ्जते ॥ सूत्रवार्तिकभाष्येषु cera चापदाब्द्‌- नम्‌ | अश्वारूढाः कथं चाश्वान्विस्मरेयुः सचेतनः ॥ A variant of this is found in S’alika, page 16, verse 41;— “ नन्वेवं तुरगारूढस्तुरङ्ग esa भवान्‌ । वेदभ्रामाण्यसिद्धयर्थसुस्थितस्तस्प्रहमीणवान्‌' ॥ । Mallinatha probably had this in mind when, in his comment on Varadaraja’s Taérkikaraksi (page 20), he wrote:—qz20 वत्तरगाधिरूढस्य guitar यदवेदप्रामाण्यसाधने श्दृत्तस्य॒मीमांसागुरो- स्तत्प्रमाद इति सोपहासं परिहरति." It is very clear, however, that the nyaiya came from a Buddhist source, since it is found in the following harika of Nagarjuna’s, on page 502 of the Madhyamakavritti ( for the reference to which I am indebted to Prof. Poussin );:— “ स त्वं दोषानात्मनीयानस्मासु परिपातयन्‌ । अश्वमेवाधिरूढः सन्नश्वमेवासि विस्तः" ॥ In a footnote, the Professor gives a variant from another Buddhist work, namely, “घोटामेवाभिरूढः aq &९.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 19

gloss_en: Names are given in consideration of some speciality.
explanation: Names are given in consideration of some speciality. This was perhaps taken from Sénkhyasiitra V. 112—“@qq प्रथि- व्युपादानमसाधारण्यात्तव्यपदेशः Taq.” “In all [bodies] earth is the material: in consideration [however] of some speciality, there is designation as this [or that other element than earth, as entering into the constitution of some given body], as in the preceding case.” This is Dr. Ballantyne’s rendering, The nyaya is found in the early part of the Aksapada section of Sarvadars‘anasangraha, and I append Prof. Cowell's translation:—“qq भ्रमाणादिपदाथैषोडशके stamens aad न्यायल्ञाखमिति व्यपदिर्यते । aay) तथाप्यसाधारण्येन व्यपदेशा भवन्तीति न्यायेन न्यायस्य परार्था- जुमानापरपयौयस्य सकलविद्यानुभ्राहकतया सवैक्मानुष्ठानसाधनतया प्रधानत्वेन तथा व्यपदेशो युज्यते" “But here an objector may say, ‘If these sixteen topics, proof &९., are all thus fully discussed, how is it that it has received the name of the Nyaya S‘astra [as reasoning, i. ९. Nydya, or logic, properly forms only a small part of the topics which it treats of ?’]. We allow the force of the objection; still, as names are proverbially said to be given for some special reason, we maintain that the name Nyaya was rightly applied to Gotama’s 21 System, since ‘reasoning’, or inference for the sake of another, is justly held to be a predominant feature from its usefulness in all kinds of knowledge, and from its being a necessary means for every kind of pursuit.” See also S’alikd, page 98, line 8; and 2111417 1-3-14 ( page 208 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 20

gloss_en: The simile of licking honey from the edge of a sword! This is found in the Jaina chapter of Sarvadars‘anasangraha ( page 45 of Jivananda’s edn, ), as 10110 78:--“सदसद्रेदनीयस्य सुखटुःखो- स्पादकत्वमसिधारामधुखेहनवत्‌; '? which Prof.
explanation: The simile of licking honey from the edge of a sword! This is found in the Jaina chapter of Sarvadars‘anasangraha ( page 45 of Jivananda’s edn, ), as 10110 78:--“सदसद्रेदनीयस्य सुखटुःखो- स्पादकत्वमसिधारामधुखेहनवत्‌; '? which Prof. Cowell renders, “An object recognized as simultaneously existing or non-existing produces mingled pleasure and pain, as licking honey from a sword’s edge—this is vedanitya.” Compare Bodhicarydvatira ए-64:--“कामैनं gfe: संसारे grawrergTa:’
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 21

gloss_en: Heartless words get heartless answer.
explanation: Heartless words get heartless answer. Like receives like. This occurs in Vedantatattvaviveka, (The Pandit for May 1908), page 14, line 4 from bottom:—‘q@y चाहदयवचसामहृदयसुत्तरमिति न्यायेन सदन्यत्वं प्रपञ्चस्योक्तं न तु तत्वाभिभ्रायेण.** In the Tatparyatika it takes the form of “अहृदयवाचामहदया एव प्रतिवाचो भवन्तिः and is combined with “great यक्चस्ताद्दो बलिः? The passage will be found under that nyaya in the second Handful. In his Nyayadipavali, page 2, Anandabodhacarya quotes the nyaya in accordance with Vacaspatimis'ra, आख्यातानामर्थं aaai शक्तिः सहकारिणी ॥ Power [of understanding on the part of the hearer] co-operates with the verbs expressing a certain sense. This 18 Dr. Thibaut’s rendering of the nyaya as it appears in Laugakshibhaskara’s 22 Arthasangraha (page 16) ina passage explanatory of adhikaravidhi. The portion connected with the nyaya, and the translation, are as follows:—“ug arretatt | आख्यातानामर्थं gaat शक्तिः सहकारिणीति न्यायात्समरथं seta विधिप्रवत्तःः॥ “In the same manner, capability ( to perform the duty is an understood qualification ); for the injunction applies only to those who are capable (by bodily strength and health, mental power Wc. ) to carry it out, according to the principle expressed in the words ‘power (of understanding on the part of the hearer) co-operates with the verbs expressing a certain sense’ (the verbs although possessing a certain sense have no effect on a person not able to understand it,” The commentator, Rames'vara S‘ivayogibhikshu, explains that blind, deaf, and lame persons are excluded as being incapable of performing various parts of the sacrificial ritual. A much earlier instance of the employment of the nyaya is met with in Sures’vara’s Sumbandhavartika, verse 75, which I here subjoin, together with Anandagiri’s comment. Cqeadt भवेच्छक्तिरिति न्यायाद्धवे्यदि । मनुष्यगोचरोऽपीति नाख्यातासंभवात्तथा ॥ ७५ ॥ आख्यातानामर्थं बोधयतामधिकारिदाक्तिः सहकारिणीति न्यायाद्विषेया्था- जुष्टानशक्तमधिकारिणं विना विधेर्विधित्वायोगात्काम्यादि सुसुश्चरवेजेयेदि- arenas मनुष्यं अति स्वार्थं बोधयतो मनुप्यशक्तिसापक्षत्वादेवमात्रगोचरत्वमस्यासिद्धमिति wea । सहकर्त्रीति | यत्राख्यातमस्ति aa तत्सहकत्री कर्वृशक्ति- रिष्टान च मोक्षकामी काम्यादि वजयेदिलाख्यातं ख्याल्तो न मनुप्यगो- चरतोक्तदेतोरिति समाधत्ते नाल्यातेति' ॥ The verse immediately preceding is the following:—“q g यादच्छिकी सिद्धिर्वक्तभ्येह विपश्चिता । दैवगोचर एवैष न तु माजुषगोचरः `” ॥ The two are rendered as follows in a translation ( by Mr. 8. V. Aiyar) which appeared in The Pandit:—«A wise man ought not to speak of success as depending on mere accident. Such a thing would be within the scope, not of human effort, but of destiny. It cannot be said that it is also within the scope 23 of human effort, on the principle that injunction implies endeavour; for there is no injunction ( akhydta ) to that effect.” The nyaya probably originated with S’abara who makes use of it in his bhasya on Jaimini 1. 4, 30, where the reading is आख्यातशब्दानामर्थं &c. The expression “सवौख्यातसहकारिदक्तयनुसा- ga”? in Tantravartika 2. 2. 27, page 558, line 9, incorporates the same nyaya, aa Gert निमिते छाया गस्ध इत्यनूत्पद्येते ॥ Though ¢ mamgo-tree is planted for the sake of its fruit, yet shade and fragrance are also incidentally produced. This illustration is employed by Apastamba in his Dharmasiitra 1.7.20. 3 which I quote together with Dr. Biihler’s translation (in Sacred Books of the East, vol. ii):—“ तद्यथाग्रे फलार्थे निमिते छाया गन्ध दलयनूत्पद्ेते । एवै धरम चर्य॑माणमथौ aaa.” “Worldly benefits are produced as accessories to the fulfilment of the law, just as in the case of a mango-tree, which is planted in order to obtain fruit, shade and fragrance are accessory advantages.” Plain and intelligible as this is, it is invariably misquoted, and instead of निमिते ( from the root मि प्रक्षेपणे ) we find निर्मिते, or the clearly impossible निमित्ते. The nyaya is found in S’ankara’s bhasya on Brahmasitra 4. 3. 14, and in every edition known to me the text and comment stand
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 21

gloss_en: When the wind is blowing in the month Ashadhe [ १.
explanation: When the wind is blowing in the month Ashadhe [ १... in the rainy season, when strong winds prevail] and the lordly elephant is being driven about, nothing but the sea can be the final resting place of the donkey. That is to say, if the mighty elephant can with difficulty withstand the force of the wind, the puny donkey must inevitably be blown into the sea! I am greatly indebted to Mr. D. Sundara Rajas‘arma of Madras for giving me a reference to this nyaya in the Veddntas'ikhamani (a work which I had not then read). It appears in a passage on page 393, and I append an extract from Amaradasa’s comment on it:— ५ वामदेवादीनासुत्पन्नब्रह्मसाक्षात्काराणामप्यसुक्ताववौचीनानामाषाढवाते चरति fre चक्रीवतो afta कष्टेति न्यायेन सुक्तिदैरोत्सारिता खात्‌.” . “area इति आषाढवायुसम्बद्धे tex गजेन्द्रे चरुति इतस्ततो दोखायमाने सति चक्रीवतो रासभस्य वारिधिरेव agg एव काष्ठा विश्रामावधि- रियः” ॥ Raghunatha’s application of the simile is somewhat different, and makes it the equivalent of the qaraagmacnera. He says:—“ याभियक्तिभिरतिप्रबरुछुष्कतर्ककर्कशत्वेन प्रसिद्धोऽपि द्ैतसल्यत्ववादी वैशेषिकादिजैय्यस्ताभिरितरे श्चुद्ाश्वावौकादयस्तु दूरतो निरस्ता भवन्तीति विवक्षायामाषाढवाते चरति fat चक्रीवतो वारिधिरेव का्टेति न्यायsafe: | चरतीति सम्तम्यन्तं पदं देहकीदीपन्यायेनोभयन्र सम्बध्यते | चक्री- वान्‌ गर्दभः | स्पष्टमन्यत्‌" ॥ In a manuscript of the 80010009 copied for me in Poona the nyaya stands thus:—“arqeara वरुद्धिपेन्दरे चक्रिवतोऽरे भवति दयुकाष्टावधिरेव काष्टा.” Though partly inaccurate this clearly furnishes a variant of the simile. The reading of the India Office manuscript (No. 568, page 73 b) differs materially from above and seems hopelessly corrupt. It puts it as follows:— “आषाढमासे चरुति rex चक्रिवतो धावतो धावति काष्ठवाहौरिति न्यायेन !*” Does this, in spite of its inaccuracy, indicate the existence of another variant ? 4 “ 26
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 25

gloss_en: On one side a tiger, on the other a precipice! A serious dilemma! There is a good example in Hemacandra’s Parisistaparvan iii.
explanation: On one side a tiger, on the other a precipice! A serious dilemma! There is a good example in Hemacandra’s Parisistaparvan iii. 166:—“geraatt मे da: प्रेयांसत्वमपि हे सखे । किं करोमि द्विधाचित्त इतो व्याघ्र इतस्तरी.” Another is found in Syddvidamanjari, page 151:--“अवास्तवत्वम्राहकं प्रमाणं साब्रतमसां इतं वा स्यात्‌। यदि ata कथं तस्माद्वास्तवाद्वास्तवस्य शून्यवादस्य सिद्धिः ser । तथा च वास्तव एव समस्तोऽपि प्रमात्रादिव्यवहारः | अथ तद्भाहकं प्रमाणं स्वयम aiad afé क्षीणाः प्रमात्रादिग्यवहारा वास्तवत्वभ्रतिन्ञातेनैव ग्यभिचारात्‌ । तदेवं पक्षद्वयेऽपीतो sa इतस्तरीति न्यायेन व्यक्त एव परमार्थतः स्वाभिमतसिद्धिविरोधः' ॥ In the following verse on page 896 of Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha we have the nyaya as a compound:— “gat हि मेथुनस्या्ञा इतः सागरवारणम्‌ | स व्याघ्रदुस्तगीन्यायः संजातो मे सुदुस्तरः” ॥ इष्यमाणस्यैव प्राधान्यं न विच्छायाः ॥ The thing wished for is of more importance than the wish. This occurs in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 56, as follows—“ aq विध्यन्वयित्वेनाविवक्चितमपि प्रेषाजुवचनमनुवाद्यतया यथा- विधेयदण्डान्वयि एवमिच्छापि प्रारभ्यमाणन्रह्ज्ञानान्वयिनी सती ब्रह्मत्तानस् प्रयोजनत्वं ब्रह्मणः सन्दग्धत्वं च गमयेत्‌ | इच्छेष्यमाणसमभिग्याहारे चेष्यमा- णप्राधान्यं यजेत स्वर्गकाम इत्यादौ छम्‌ | अत इहापीच्छाप्राधान्यं विहाये- भ्यमाणम्राधान्यमभ्युपगन्तं युक्तम्‌ ? ॥ Again, on page 62 of the same-—“ प्रलयाः प्रधानमिति सामान्यन्यायादिच्छेष्यमाणसमभिव्याहता- विष्यमाणे प्रधानमिति स्वगकामपदादिषु ङसो विशेषन्यायो बरूवानिति भावः ॥ So too, in Ramanuja'’s Sribhdsya, page 31:--(ज्ञातुमिच्छा जिज्ञासा । इच्छाया इष्यमाणग्रधानत्वादिष्यमाणं ज्ञानमिह विधीयते" ॥ This is repeated verbatim in the Ramanuja section of Sarva~ dars'anasangraha, page 69 ( Jivananda’s edn). 27 7 > उदरे Tt कोशो भरतः ॥ When his stomach is full his coffers are full. Used of a lazy fellow who has no ambition beyond his daily food. “Whose god is his belly.” It occurs in Hemacandra’s Paris'istaparvan iii, 118:-^ arta मदीयेन बिभ्युद्रमण्यदः | उदरे च wa air wa gaa मन्यसे.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 26

gloss_en: It is better to beg than to starve.
explanation: It is better to beg than to starve. This is one of Raghunitha’s nyayas and he applies it thus:— “येषां त॒ धीमान्द्ाद्ूयो ya: श्रूयमाणोऽप्यभेदो न बुद्धिमारोहति तैस्तूपवा- सादरं भिक्षेति न्यायेन degenft खप्रेमास्पदविम्रहावच्छिन्नेशाराधनं कार्यं तत्प्रसादान्मधोदये Misael कोद्रवाशनल्यागन्यायेन त्याज्या भेदधीः” ॥ It is found in 71८04287 ix. 119, 120:— ५अस्त्येवोपासकस्यापि वास्तवी ब्रह्मतेति चेत्‌ । पामराणां तिरश्चां च वास्तवी ब्रह्मता न किम्‌ ॥ अक्ञानादपुमर्थेत्वसुभयन्नापि तत्समम्‌ | उपवासाद्यथा भिक्षा वरं ध्यानं तथान्यतः” ॥ उपसंजनिष्यमाणनिमित्तोऽप्यपवाद्‌ उपसंजातनिमि- तमप्युत्सरग बाधते ॥ This is another of Raghunatha’s grammatical nyayas. It is not met with in the Mahabhasya, but forms one of Nagojibhatta’s paribhasas. The following is Prof. Kielhorn’s rendering of it:—* An apavada, even though the causes of its (application) are still to present themselves, supersedes a general rule the causes (of the application) of which are already present.” In connection with this paribhasi we have the देवदत्तहन्तृहतन्याय which see below. 28 उभयतपारा Tye ॥ : A rope which binds at both ends. An embarrassing position ; a dilemma. ‘The following is from the Jaimini section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 133, Bib. Ind., or 150 of Jivananda’s edition), with Professor Cowell’s translation (page 198):— “अभावः कारणमेव न भवतीति चेत्तदा वक्तव्यमभावस्य कायैत्वमस्ति न वा । यदि नासि तदा परग्र्वंसानुपपस्या नि्यताप्रसङ्गः | अथास्ति किमपराद्ध कारणत्वेनेति | सेथञ्ुभयतःपाशा रजुः” ॥ “If you object that non-existence ( or absence ) cannot be a cause, we reply by asking you whether non-existence can be an effect or not? If it cannot, then we should have to allow that cloth is eternal, as its ‘emergent non-existence’ or destruction would be impossible. If it can be an effect, then why should it not be a cause also? So this rope binds you at both ends.” The earliest occurrence of the nyaya isin Patanjali 6. 1. 68 (vart. 2 and 5); and it is found in Tantravartika 3. 6. 42 (page 1113) as follows:—“gen@ न बाधस्तथापि विकल्पस्ताव्परामोति न हि तुल्यार्थानां कचित्समुच्चयो दष्टः सेयसुभयतःपाशा wa: ॥ Mandanamis‘ra used it in Vidhiviveka, page 88; and we find instances of it in Nydyamanjari, page 436, line 16; in Kusumdnjali ऋ. 6 (page 374); in the same author's Laksandvali, page 56; in Khandana, page 530; and in the opening part of Citsukhi ( Pandit, vol iv. page 466 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 27

gloss_en: A thing that is changed in one part does not thereby become something else (literally, like something else).
explanation: A thing that is changed in one part does not thereby become something else (literally, like something else). For example, as Patanjali says under 1. 1. 56 (vart. 10), the cutting of a dog’s ears or tail does not turn it into a horse or a donkey, but it is still a dog! Other passages of the Mahabhasya where this paribhasa occurs are as follows:—S'ivasiitra 2, vartika 4 (५९. ष्ण]. + page 21); 2. 4 85 (vart. 10); 4, 1. 83; 6. 1, 69 29 (vart. 3); 6, 4. 149 ( vart. 2); and 8. 3. 85. The paribhasa is No 37 in Dr. Kielhorn’s edition of the Paribhadsendus'ekhara, and my rendering of it is based upon his. It is included in Raghunatha’s list of nyayas, and is quoted as such by Jayanta Bhatta in Nydyamanjari, page 589, line 6. For further illustrations of the paribhasa, see under श्रा aut वा Wc.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 28

gloss_en: On seeing one thing we are reminded of others connected with it.
explanation: On seeing one thing we are reminded of others connected with it. The nyaya is found in this form in the Vydyapradipa on Tarkabhasé, page 44, where the presence of smoke is said to remind the spectator of the invariably connected fire. In Amaradisa’s tika on S’ikhdmani, page 93, it is quoted as “एकसम्बन्धिक्ञानमपरसम्बन्धिस्मारकम्‌ `. In both of Raghunathavarman’s books it appears as एकसम्बन्धिदशनमन्यसम्बन्धिस्मारकम्‌, and, in the larger of the two, he illustrates it thus:—“qyr हस्तिपकददौनं हस्तिस्मारकं तथा anita ऊदाकाशजलतुंबिकाजद्कानां तत्सम्बन्धिनां स्मरणहेतुत्वम्‌.** Taranitha (8. ४. न्याय ) quotes the saying in the form एकसम्बन्धिक्ञानम्‌ &e. He reverses the above illustration by saying that the elephant reminds one of the driver; and adds that a word reminds one of its meaning, a statement which, in these degenerate times, is not universally true! See, too, Tatparyatika, page 167, line 18.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 29

gloss_en: The rule as to the Kapinjalas [9 kind of partridge].
explanation: The rule as to the Kapinjalas [9 kind of partridge]. In Vajasaneyi-Samhita xxiv. 20, we read वसन्ताय “afteerarwaa,’ and the question arises, how many of the birds are to be sacrificed? Jaimini devotes eight siitras[ 11. 1. 38-45] to the discussion of this point, and finally decides that three, the lowest figure representing plurality (two being merely duality), will satisfy the requirements of the s'ruti. S’abara’s lengthy argument is very concisely summed up in the Nydyamdldvistara, as follows;— : 30 «८ कपिञ्जलेषु frara रेच्छिकाखित्वमेव वा । आद्यो बहुत्वसाम्यान्न Betta कृतस्वतः ॥ अश्वमेधे “वसन्ताय कपिज्जरानारुमतः इति त्रित्वचतुष्कादिसं ख्यानां बहुत्वसाम्यादिच्छया या काचिद्रहुष्वसंख्या स्वीकार्येति चेत्‌ । न । त्रित्वेनैव शाखस्य कृतत्वात्‌ | यो हि चतुष्ादिसंल्यासुपादत्ते तेन न तदन्त भूतं Bes वजयितं शक्यं त्रिस्वसुपाददानेन स्वनन्त भूतं sages asd शक्यते अतोऽवर्यं भावित्वेन श्रथमभाविस्वेन wala च fea उपात्ते शाखाथसिद्धौ ततोऽधिकपक्षिहिसायां श्रत्यवायात्‌ | तस्मान्रिस्वमेवोपादेयम्‌ ॥ There is an admirable example in Nydyakandali, page 50, where, in commenting on Pras’‘astapida’s “इहेदानीं चतुणी महाभूतानां खष्टिसंहारविधिरूच्यते* 31101478 588 “महाभूतानामिव्युक्ते त्रयाणामेव परिग्रहः कपिञ्जरानालभेतेतिवद्हुत्वसंख्यायास्तावत्येव चरितार्थ त्वात्‌ | अतश्वतुणौमिव्युक्तम्‌.” It is found also in Tantravartika 2.1. 12 ( page 894), and 3. 5. 26 ( page 1049); in Parimala, page 550, line 3; and in Sikhamani, page 303. Commenting on Manu viii. 105, where certain offerings are directed to be made to Sarasvati, Kullika decides that, in accordance with the kapinjala-nyaya three are sufficient. The nyaya is clearly of the type of qgra& ga: ares: and in accord with the Marathi phrase grergzar.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 29

gloss_en: The simile of the cleansing of a course blanket [by beating it on the feet, and so dusting them at the same time]! One of the numerous examples of the accomplishment of two objects by one operation:—“ killing two birds with one stone.
explanation: The simile of the cleansing of a course blanket [by beating it on the feet, and so dusting them at the same time]! One of the numerous examples of the accomplishment of two objects by one operation:—“ killing two birds with one stone.” It is found in S‘abara 2. 2. 25:—“aft च दधि उभयमसमर्थं कठ फलं साधयित होमं च । ननु कंबरूनिर्गेजनवदेतद्धविष्यति । निर्णेजनं हि उभयं करोति । कबल्छुद्धि पादयोश्च rea’ The कंबल (the कांबढ of the Marathas) is made of extremely coarse and rough material, and 31 generally black or black and grey. In the case of the very poor, it is often their sole garment by day as well as their only covering at night.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 30

gloss_en: The nyaya of the trumpeting of an elephant.
explanation: The nyaya of the trumpeting of an elephant. The word करि is really redundant, since the whole meaning is conveyed by बृंहित alone—afea करिगजितमित्यमरः-0प॥ the addition, in thig and similiar cases, is made for some special purpose. Raghunatha defines it {10 :--^विशिष्टवाचकानामपि पदानां सति gaftasranवाचकपदसमवधाने विश्ेष्यमाच्रपरतायां करि्रंहितन्यायः sada.” This principle may be exemplified by the following verse from Kdvyaprakis'a vii. 10:— ; ५५ सोन्द््यसम्पत्तारुण्यं यस्यास्ते ते च विभ्रमाः । पट्पदान्पुष्पमाटेव BAHIA सा सखे"? ॥ Here the author of the Kévyapradtpa remarks (page 295):— “qemet यद्यपि gaa at रशक्तस्तथापि न पुष्पपदमयपुषटार्थम्‌ । लक्षणयोत्कृष्त्वभ्रतिपादकस्वात्‌ । अयमेव करिनंहितन्यायः.? So too, the Uddharanacandrika:—“ aq निरूपपदान्मालाडाब्दादेव पुष्पखक्प्रतीतेः पुष्पपदसुच्ृष्टपुष्पत्वे संक्रमितवाच्यम्‌ | एवमेव करिच्ुहितादिष्वपि बोध्यम्‌.” The following additional nyayas are cited by Raghunatha as belonging to the same class:—aqgzrara, नीलेन्दीवरन्यायः, पर्वताधिल्यकान्यायः पवेतोपत्यकान्याय, वाजिमन्दुरान्याय, Baers. In each case the first word might be omitted without affecting the meaning. FTAA: ॥ The law of [abstention from] the flesh of an animal killed with a poisoned arrow. “Some hold the Kalanja to be the flesh of a deer killed by a poisoned arrow; others, hemp or bhang; 32 others, a kind of garlic.” (Cowell’s note on page 81 of his translation of Kuswmdnjali). But this can hardly be correct as the garlic and bhang are mentioned in addition. It may be noticed, however, that qarage is given as an equivalent of करज, the meaning of which is the tobacco plant. It is deduced from Jaimini 6. 2. 19, 20,—an adhikarana designed to teach the evil result of doing forbidden things. The words “a करज्ञं भक्षयितव्यं न wy न गृञ्जनं च' are discussed, and the question arises as to whether this is an instance of पयुदास or प्रतिषेधः - whether it means अभक्षणे करव्यं or भक्षणं न कन्यम्‌. The subtle intellect of the Mimanisaka sees a great difference between these two, the former being something to be done, and the latter being something to be avoided! The siddhantin decides in favour of the latter. I would advise my readers to study pages 39-41 of Dr. Thibaut’s excellent translation of Arthasangraha, in order to get, if possible, a clear understanding of this peculiar tenct. Many years ago, when in India, I heard a statement made by a Brahman, to the effect that the words “put no oil in the lamp,” did not mean “don’t put oil in the lamp,” but rather “put in the lamp” some “no-oil”; in other words, it was not a prohibition of an act but an injunction to do something! This 28 just the position of the Mimatisaka, who, in regard to the rule that a Snataka must not look at the rising or setting sun, says that “not-looking is something to be done.” The above nyaya is quoted by Anandagiri on Brahmasitrabhasya 3, 4. 28, and 4. 1. 18, and is found in Sanksepas¢driraka, i. 417-420, and in S’ribhdsya 1. 1. 4 (page 687).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 31

gloss_en: Like the erection of a temple including the pot-shaped finial [which is placed on the summit at its completion ] Used ironically of one who considers that he is doing something as meritorious as the building of a complete temple.
explanation: Like the erection of a temple including the pot-shaped finial [which is placed on the summit at its completion ] Used ironically of one who considers that he is doing something as meritorious as the building of a complete temple. 38 In a footnote to page 73 of his translation of Prabandhacintémani, Mr. Tawney says:—“Dr. Burgess informs me that kalas‘a is really the finial of the spire, which is shaped like a vase or urn.” Then, on page 135, there is the following foot_ note:—‘“Mr. Cousens writes in a letter, which Dr. Burgess has kindly shown me, ‘I understand that the term kalas'adandapratisthaé refers solely to the setting up of the kalas‘a or pot-finial, the danda being the pole or stick which supports the finial and upon which it is set up. With a small kalas‘a made solid, if would not be required, the neck of the same taking its place, but it is always required with the larger and more complex kalas‘as, especially those made of hollow metal.” Frequent mention is made by Merutunga of the erection of a qeat and ध्वज्ञ as the completion of a temple. The two are mentioned on pages 119 and 211, whilst on pages 219 and 222 we have the wearavenfaer referred to above. On pages 120 and 224, the कल्या is mentioned alone. In Hemacandra’s Paris'istaparvan i. 14, we find ga for gea—‘aa Fag सोव्णध्वजकुम्भमरीचयः'?. The illustration occurs in S’arngadhara’s tika on Udayana’s definition of dravya in his Laksandvali (page 4). The definition is— ^ तत्र गुणात्यन्ताभावानधिकरणं व्यम्‌ ,”? and, whilst explaining it, the commentator attacks the views of Citsukha Muni as follows:—“arqa तत््वप्रदीपिकाकारेणोत्पत्यनन्तरक्षणे अग्या्ति- सद्धाव्य गुणवच्वात्यन्ताभावानधिकरणत्वे च परिहारमाशंक्योत्पत्यनन्तरक्षणRe कदाचिदपि गुणानुदयात्पुनरव्यासस्तदवस्थत्वाक्नैवमिति परिहृतम्‌ | तत्करुदापुरःसरप्रासादनिमौणतुल्यम्‌.' It is necessary here to bear in mind the tenet of the logicians—“ उत्पन्नं दरव्यं क्षणमगुणं तिष्ठति. ” The Tattvapradipika, better known as Citsukht, is described by Hall as “a confutation of the Nyaya philosophy, on the basis of the Vedanta.” The wrath of the logicians would of course, therefore, be kindled against it. The passage complained of above is the 81018 ii. 4 with the vritti' The former stands thus:—“‘seqrazaftenased नैव गुणाश्रयः । आचये क्षणे गुणाभावाद्णा- arate वीक्षणात्‌.” 5 34
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 32

gloss_en: The simile of a crow as a destroyer of curds.
explanation: The simile of a crow as a destroyer of curds. An example of wpalaksana, where one represents many, or a part stands for the whole. So if any one were warned to keep the crows off the curds, it would imply that all other possible raiders were also to be warded off. It is thus put by Bhartrihari in Vakyapadiya 7. 314 :— “ काकेभ्यो रक्षतां सर्पिरिति aretst atta: | उपघातपरे वाक्ये न श्वादिभ्यो न रक्षति `” ॥ This is reproduced, with slight variations, in Tantravartika, page 781, and is quoted in the same form in Bhaémati 1. 4. 3 (page 287). Raghunatha’s application of it is as follows:— ^ तदुक्तमीश्वरगीतायाम्‌ । ^ये fat विष्णुमव्यक्तं मां च देवं महेश्वरम्‌ । एकी- भावेन परयन्ति न तेषां पुनरूदढवः ` इति। अत्र हरिहरयोग्रहणं काकदधिघातकन्यायेन विधेरप्युपलक्षणम्‌ 9, The १101 forms part of the uttarabhaga of the Kirma Purana, and the verse here quoted is xi. 8. In the fourth chapter of Vedaéntaparibhasa ( page 285 ) this is given as an example of ‘Jahadajahallaksand. ^“ जहदजहदक्षणोदाहरणं तु काकेभ्यो दधि रक्ष्यतामिल्यादिकमेव | तत्र शक्यकाकपरित्यागेनाशक्यदध्युपघातकल्वपुरस्कारेणाकाके काके च काकशाब्दस्य प्रवृत्तेः ”॥ 1178 kind of 14150096 is termed bhagalaksana in Vedintasara 23; and bhagatydgalaksané in Vivaranaprameya, page 229. In the अ्रहैकत्वन्याय, too, a part represents the whole.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 34

gloss_en: The being something on which a crow is perched.
explanation: The being something on which a crow is perched. This illustration seems to have originated with Patanjali, and is found in Mahabhasya 1. 1. 26 ( vartikas 3 and 4 ):—* अनुबन्धो- उन्यत्वकर इति चेत्तन्न । कं कारणम्‌ । लोपात्‌ । लप्यतेऽतराचुबन्धः । खते- Sagara नान्यत्वं भविष्यति | तद्यथा | कतरदेवदत्तसय गृहम्‌ । अदो यत्रासौ 35 काक इति | उत्पतिते काके ad age भवति । एवमिहापि छतेऽयुबन्धे नष्टः neat भवति । यद्यपि लप्यते जानाति त्वसौ सानुबन्धकसेयं dat कृतेति । तद्यथा | इतरत्रापि कतरदेवदत्तस्य गृहम्‌ | अदो यत्रासौ काक इति । उत्पतिते काके यद्यपि नष्टं age भवयन्ततस्तसुदेशं जानाति. It occurs in Vivaranaprameyasangraha (page 195 ) in the course of a discussion on Badarayana’s second siitra:—“qa: कारणसंबन्धिनो जन्मादेरलक्षणत्वमिति चेन्मैवम्‌ | काकाधिकरणत्ववदु पपत्तेः । काकाधिकरणत्वं हि न गृहेऽन्तभैवति । तथा च सति काकबिगमे गृहेकदेदा- भङ्गबुद्धिग्रसङ्गात्‌ | अतो गृहस्याधिकरणत्वं नामौपाधिको धर्मैः स a परि- शेषाक्षणे एवान्तर्भवति | तन्निरूपकस्य काकस्य यथा रक्षणत्वं तथा ब्रह्मणोऽपि कारणत्वमौपाधिको धर्मो लश्चणान्तःपाती. 7 Similarly in Sanksepasdrivaka i. 206 :-- यत्रैष काक इदमेव तु देवदत्तवेदमेति लाक्चषणिकलर्ति- रिहाभ्युपेता | काकास्पद्स्वमवधी्य तथापि वेरममात्राकृति्मैवति लाक्षणिकी तु बुद्धिः" ॥ 70 slight modifications it is found also in Tantravartika, page 277, line 8 :--“यथानमिधीयमानमपि काकनिर्यनं देवद्तग्रहशब्दस्य wardatiqaaayad प्रतिपद्यते तद्रदाकृतिश्चिह्वं व्यक्त्य भिधाने भविष्यति. See, also, Khandana,, page 502.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 34

explanation: A Nisddi gives birth to a son, and a Nisaida drinks the decoction of herbs [ prepared for her]! For the context of this आभाणक्र see under ^ yfatga मूर्खो सुच्यते."”

gloss_en: The law relating to “the performance of all prescribed acts of ritual in orderly succession for a particular object before performing the same acts in the same order for a second object.
explanation: The law relating to “the performance of all prescribed acts of ritual in orderly succession for a particular object before performing the same acts in the same order for a second object.” This is Sir Monier-Williams’ definition based on the commentary on As'valayana’s Grihyasiitra 1. 24. 7 which prescribes certain gifts commencing with विष्टर and ending with गो. The 36 commentator says “विजा मधुपरकदाने दे गती संभवतः। पदार्थाचुसमयः काण्डानुसमय इति | तत्र पदा्थाजुसमयो नाम सर्वेषां वरणक्रमेण विष्टरं दत्वा ततः पाद्य ततोऽध्यैमिति | काण्डानुसमयो नाम एकस्यैव विष्टरादिगोनिवेदनान्तं समाप्य ततोऽन्यस्य aa ततोऽन्यखेति. ? Ina case of this kind, where there is merely a bestowal of gifts, and not the performance of sacrificial ritual, the definition given by Molesworth is more suitable:—“The order, when a suit or set of things is to be given to each individual of a multitude, of giving at once all the articles composing the suit or set, as distinguished from पदार्थानुसमय. The nyaya represents the purport of Jaimini 5. 2.3. Kunte says that the word अभ्याचृत्ति used in the siitra means kindanusamaya. His notes on siitras 1-3 contain some very interesting items of information, of especial value to us of the West. See पदवा्थानुसमयन्यायः, below. कुस्याप्रणयनन्यायः ॥ The figure of the laying down of a water-course for irrigation. An example of a thing made for one purpose subserving other purposes also. It is found in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 118, line 9:--जतो न विधेयग्रलयये तात्पर्यमिति ऊल्याप्रणयनन्यायेनो- भयार्थत्वातिधेयतवात्‌ | यथा werd कल्याः प्रणीयन्ते ताभ्य एव पानीयं च पीयते तदत्‌ ॥” For the source of this nyaya, see अन्या प्रङृतमन्याथै भवति. ¢
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 35

gloss_en: The simile of [the unwitting employment of] base money [in one’s business].
explanation: The simile of [the unwitting employment of] base money [in one’s business]. It is used by Kumarila (in Tantravartika 1. 3. 8, page 95) in the course of an argument on the relative value of S'ruti and Smriti, He maintains that if any teaching of the latter is found to be in opposition to that of the former, it must be given up; just as a man who finds that he has been . 37 using counterfeit coins must at once abstain from so doing. The portion containing the nyaya is as follows—“qy हि qearatपणेन कंचिस्कारमन्ञो रोकमध्ये व्यवहरति न तेन विवेकल्ञानजनितव्युत्पत्ति- नापि तथैव व्यवहर्तव्यम्‌.” In Tantravartika 1. 3. 8 (page 149, line 3 from bottom), Kumirila compares words to coins which can be tested by intelligent people. He 8978 :--“शाब्दापश्रंदावदेव गौणश्रान्त्यादिप्रयोगनिमित्ता अरयापञनंरा भवन्ति ते शाखस्थेरेवावि्ुताथक्रियानिमित्पुण्यार्थिभिः शक्यन्ते साध्वसाघुकाषीपणमभ्यादिव तत्परीक्षिभिर्विवक्तुम्‌.” The nyéya is found in Nydyamanjari, page 162, as follows :-—* नापि बाधकाभावपरिच्छेदासप्ामाण्यनिश्चयः स हि तात्कालिको वा स्यात्काखान्तरभावी वा । तात्काछिको न पर्याप्तः प्रामाण्यपरिनिश्चये । कूटकाषी- पणादौ किंचित्कारमनुत्यन्नवाधकेऽपि कालान्तरे तदुत्पाददरशनात्‌.*' It occurs again on page 169, line 3, on page 187, line 4 from bottom, and on page 531, line 1.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 36

gloss_en: The simile of the well-digger.
explanation: The simile of the well-digger. It is applied by Raghunatha as follows:—“ यथा कूपखानके पतितं पंकादि कूपान्निःसतेनाम्भसा भक्षाल्यते तथा तत्तद्विम्रहावच्छिननेशभेदबुद्धिजो दोषस्तदुपासनाजन्यसुकृतमदिश्नोत्पन्नेना- वैतबोधेन समूलं निवलयैत इति क्तेयम्‌॥ 2 The illustration is as old as Patanjali who made use of it in the introductory chapter of the Mahabhdsya (vol. + page 11) when discoursing on the importance of the study of grammar, and on the merit which accrues to the user of correct’ words. He says:— ५“अथवाभ्युपाय एवापशाव्दक्ञानं शब्दज्ञाने । योऽपशब्दाज्ञानाति शाब्दानप्यसौ जानाति | तदेवं art धम इति व्रुवतोऽथदापन्नं भवल्यपशब्दक्ञानपूवैके शब्द्‌- ज्ञाने धर्मं इति | अथवा कूपखानकवदेतद्धविष्यति | तद्यथा कूपखानकः कूपं खनन्यद्यपि aa पांसुभिश्चावकीर्णो भवति सोऽप्सु संजातासु तत एव तं गुणमा- सादयति येन स च दोषो निर्हण्यते भूयसा चाभ्युदयेन योगो भवयेवमिहापि यद्यप्यपदाब्दज्ञानेऽधरमैस्तथापि यसू्वसौ शब्दज्ञाने धर्मस्तेन स च दोषो Fratनिष्यते भूयसा चाभ्युदयेन योगो भविष्यति." 88 ` कृतक्षौरस्य नक्षत्रपरीक्षा ॥ This is identical in meaning with सुण्डितरिरोनक्षत्रान्वेषणम्‌ for which see the Second Handful. It occurs in Nydyamanjovi, page 164:—“ अनिश्चितप्रामाण्यसख तु प्रवृत्तौ पश्चात्तन्निर्णयो भवन्नपि gaa नक्षत्रपरीश्चावदफर एवेत्युक्तम्‌.” Lower down on the same page, there is another nyaya of similar import, in the following verse :— ४ “समर्थकारणक्ञानाद्योऽपि प्रामाण्यनिश्चयम्‌ | बूते सोऽपि कृतोद्रादस्तत्र wa परीक्षते.” ॥ कृत्रिमाकृत्रिमयोः कृत्रिमे कार्यसम्प्र्ययः ॥ The rule that “ whenever it may appear doubtful whether an operation has reference to that which is expressed by the technical or to that which is expressed by the ordinary mean- - ing of a particular term, the operation refers (only) to that which is expressed by the technical meaning of the term in question.” This is Professor Kielhorn’s rendering of the nyaya as quoted in Nagojibhatta’s vritti to Paribhasé ix:—“agq संख्या्रहणे बह्वादीनामेव अहणं स्यासप्रकरणस्याभिधानियामकत्वसिद्धत्छृत्रिमाङ़्- त्रिमयोः sha arta इति न्यायात्‌ 2 ॥ 1४ is taken from the Mahabhasya where it appears several times. In 1. 1. 28 (vart. 3), it is followed by the example « लोके गोपालकमानय कटजकमानयेति यस्यैषा संज्ञा भवति स आनीयते न यो गाः पालयति यो वा कटे जातः 22. Siradeva includes it in his collection of paribhasas.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 37

gloss_en: Like an impecunious debtor deceiving the money-lenders with empty promises.
explanation: Like an impecunious debtor deceiving the money-lenders with empty promises. This occurs in Atmatattvaviveka, page 20, as follows:—* अवस्तुत्वादिति चेन्नन्वेतदपि ऊतः eq सर्वैसामथ्यै- विरहादिति चेत्सोऽयमितस्ततः केवलेर्वचनेर्निभनाधमर्णिक इव साधून्भ्ामयन्प- ` रस्पराश्रयदोषमपि न पद्यतिः?. 39
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 38

gloss_en: Action may vary, but substance cannot, This is plainly set forth in S'’énkarabhdsya 1.
explanation: Action may vary, but substance cannot, This is plainly set forth in S'’énkarabhdsya 1. 1. 2 (page 37) as follows:— ““कतमकठैमन्यथा वा ey शक्यं Sikhs वेदिकं च कर्म । यथाश्चेन गच्छति पद्यामन्यथा वा न वा गच्छतीति | ,.....न तु वस्त्वेवं नेवमस्ि नास्तीति वा विकल्प्यते ’’. Similarly in 2. 1. 27 (page 471):-“क्रियाविषये हि... Rarer ......g¢ तु विकल्पाश्रयणेनापि न विरोधपरिहारः संभवत्यपुरुषतच््रत्वाद्स्तुनः”. Then, in Anandagiri’s comment on 1. 4,1 (page 325), we find the expression क्रियायामिव वस्तुनि-विकल्पाभावात्‌,' and on page 359, “= हि वस्तुनि विकल्पोऽस्ति.' It occurs also on the first page of the Ramanuja chapter of Sarvadars‘anasangroha—“a च aera: परस्परविरुद्वयोः ससुच्यासंभवे विकल्पः किं न स्यादिति ata क्रिया हि विकट्प्यते न वस्त्विति न्यायात्‌. “Nor should any one say: Granting the impossibility of the coexistence of existence and non-existence, which are reciprocally contradictory, why should there not be an alternation between existence and non-existence, there being the rule that it is action, not Zns, that alternates?” This rendering is Professor Gough’s, The nyaya is found, too, in S'astradipika 1. 3. 8 (page 154, line 6 ), and Naiskarmyasiddhi iii. 82, are: क च नीराजना ॥ What connection has a camel with the lustration of arms? None at all; and the phrase is used to indicate that certain things are not connected. The नीराजनाविधि (as described in chapter 267 of the Agni Purana) was a ceremony performed by kings or generals before going forth to battle, and consisted, of the purification of the component parts of the army, including that of the horses, the elephants, and the weapons The बाजिनीराजनाविधि is mentioned in Raghuvaris’a iv. 25, on 40 which Mallinitha remarks:—“arfeeot गजादीनामप्युपलक्षणं तेषा- मपि नीराजनाविधानात्‌.› The illustration appears in Upamitithavaprapanca Katha, page 522:--“नद्यादिवस्तुमेदा्थं कथितं मे कथानकम्‌ | त्वयेदं तत्र मे माति कष्टो नीराजना wa” ॥ क्षते क्षारमिव ॥ Like salt on a wound. “sare क्षते क्षिप्‌ has become proverbial, and means ‘to aggravate the pain which is already unbearable, ‘to make bad worse,’ ‘to add insult to injury’.” (Apte’s Dictionary). He cites Uttararamacarita iv. 7:—‘q एव मे जनपर्वमासीन्मूर्तो महोत्सवः । क्षते क्षारमिवासद्यं जातं तस्यैव दीनम्‌. Also Mricchakatika ए. 18. Ihave met with it again in Upamitibhavaprapancad Katha, page 5, verse 42 -- “कथां कामार्थयोसस्मान्न कुवीत कदाचन | कः क्षते क्षारनिक्षेपं विदधीत विचक्षणः” ॥ There is another example on page 993 of the same.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 39

gloss_en: The figure of the infliction of a fine of one hundred on the Gargas.
explanation: The figure of the infliction of a fine of one hundred on the Gargas. There are two paribhasais which are frequently found together in the Mahabhasya, viz. “nae वाक्यपरिसमासिः and “agar वाक्यपरिसमा्िः.* Professor Kielhorn (in his translation of Nagojibhatta) renders them (the latter slightly modified) thus:—“What is stated (in grammar of several things) must be understood (to have been stated) of each of them separately.” And “sometimes (it) also (happens that what is stated in grammar of several things) must be understood (to have been stated) of all of them collectively.” Patanjali’s illustration of the former is “देवदत्तयक्दत्तविष्णुमित्रा dara,” where, of course, each separate person is to be fed; and his illustration of the latter is “ani: शतं दण्ड्यन्ताम्‌? where the Gargas collectively are to be fined a hundred. The passages where these occur are 1. 1. 1 (vart. 12); 1. 1. 7 (vart. 1); 2.1. 4; 2. 8. 46; 6. 1. 5 (vart. 2); 8.3. 58; and 8 4. 2 (vart. 3). 41 The two paribhasas and examples are quoted by S’abara on Jaimini 3, 1. 12 ( page 223), and the second one is referred to ४8 follows by Kumarila in his vartika on the same passage (page 712):—“ ay: gare सल्यमेषं न दोष इति। क्रियाप्राधान्ये तु तद्रशीकृतविरोषणसमुदाये वाक्यपरिसमािः | गगौरातदण्डनहोमाभिषवभक्षवदिति परिहारः | न त॒ दण्डस्य दण्ड्यसंस्कारत्वाद्गोजनादिवस््रतिगगीमावृत्तिः sate । नैष दोषः। शारीरो निग्रहो यत्न तत्र प्रयेकभिन्नता । हिरण्यादानदण्डस्तु समुदाये समाप्यते ॥ This paribhas occurs also in Tantravartika 1. 4, 8 (page 294, line 15), and in 1. 4 8 (page 300); whilst the former one is quoted in 1. 4.18 (page 314),
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 40

gloss_en: The simile of a snake lying dead in a hole.
explanation: The simile of a snake lying dead in a hole. It is employed by Vijnana Bhiksu (in his Brahmasiitrabhasya 1, 1, 2, page 36) when explaining the line ^ प्राक्स; प्रर्यादृरध्वै नासीक्किचि- द्विजोत्तम `° from the Narasimha -7 ५70१0, He says ^ नासीदिति विरतव्यापारतया कारणरूपेण गर्ैस्थद्तसपैवद्विरीनमासीदिल्थः wu’? Again, on page 96 of the same:-—“uq qaqa तां च प्रकृतिं गुणत्रयरूपिणीं निल्यामपि निव्यौपारतया गतैस्थ्धतसर्षवत्करणकारणविविक्ते चिन्मात्रे विलाप यन्ति.” On the next page we have it again, in a similar connection, as aaadad.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 41

gloss_en: The rule as to the [application to the] Garhapatya-fire [of a mantra in which reference is made to Indra].
explanation: The rule as to the [application to the] Garhapatya-fire [of a mantra in which reference is made to Indra]. This represents Mimamsasiitras 8. 2. 8, 4 where the Vedic mantra ‘ निवेशनः सङ्गमनो वसूनामिलैन्द्र॑या गाईैपलयसुपतिष्ठते"” is discussed. The question is raised whether, since the mantra makes mention of Indra the adoration is addressed to him or to the Garhapatya (one 6 42 of the three sacred fires perpetually maintained by a householder, and from which fires for sacrificial purposes are lighted). Jaimini’s decision is in favour of the latter on the ground that direct enunciation (s'ruti) is stronger than suggestive power (linga). The following extract from Arthasangraha page 6, with Dr. Thibaut’s translation ( page 11 ), will explain this—* सेयं शरुतिरिङ्गादिभ्यः प्रबला ।..-अत एव teen शिङ्गननेनद्रोपस्थानाथै- त्वम्‌ । किंतु ten गाहंपल्यसुपतिष्ठत gaa गाहैपल्मिति दवितीयाश्चुला गाहैपत्योपस्थाना्थत्वम्‌.* “Direct enuniciation is stronger than suggestive power and the other proofs ( by which one thing is shown to be subsidiary to another ); ...for this reason we do not conclude at once on the ground of suggestive power that a verse (apparently) addressed to Indra (that is, a verse containing a mention of Indra’s name, or one of his attributes &.) is really to be used for addressing Indra; but rather decide if we find at the same time a direct enunciation as, for instance, ; ‘with a verse bearing Indra’s mark he addresses the Garhapatya’, that in consequence of the direct enunciation of the second case termination (Garhapatyam ) the verse is subsidiary to the act of addressing the Garhapatya-fire.” The words “Raat: सङ्गमनो agar”, which, amongst other places, are found in the Atharvasamhita x. 8, 42, are rendered by Professor Whitney “The reposer, the assembler of good things”; and by Mr. Griffith, “Luller to rest, and gatherer up of treasures,” As quoted in S'atapatha Brahmana 7. 2. 1. 20, Prof. Eggeling translates it “The harbourer and gatherer of riches”! Who shall decide between these learned doctors ? The Garhapatyanyaya is explained and applied in Bhamati 8. 8, 25 (page 613), and in other works on Vedanta, Owing to the word tet in sutra 3, the adhikarana is sometimes styled देनीन्याय. 48
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 41

gloss_en: The simile of the milk-pail.
explanation: The simile of the milk-pail. It is laid down that a sacrificer who wishes for cattle must bring the water for his sacrifice in a milk-pail. But that is not done in other cases. The godohana is therefore used as an illustration of something which is occasionally, and not universally, connected with an act or performance as an essential part of it, It occurs in S‘ankarabhasya 3. 8. 42, as follows-—“aa कर्माङ्गव्यपाश्रयाणि विज्ञानानि “ओमियतदक्षरसुद्रीथसुपासीतः इयवमादीनि । किं तानि नि- ल्यान्येव स्युः कर्मसु पणैमयीत्वादिवहुतानि्यानि गोदोहनादिवदिति विचारयामः. Then, at the close of the same:-—“qaqremn ऋत्वाश्रयाण्यपि गोदोहना- दीनि फरसंयोगादनिव्यान्येवसुद्रीथादयुपासनानीतयपि द्रष्टव्यम्‌.” It appears again in 3, 3. 65; 3. 4, 45, and in 4. 1. 6. Sures‘vara quotes it also in his vartika on the Brihaddranyakopanisad 8. 8. 51:— “qq गोदोहनन्यायः काम्यकर्मसु युज्यते । तेषां सुक्तिफल्त्वे हि न मानं विद्यते यतः” ॥ For the mantra regarding the use of this pail, see S’abara and Nydyamalavistara on Jaimini 4, 1.2. Other references to the nyaya are Tantravartika 3. 6. 43 (page 1118); and Nydyamanjari, page 166, line 4, in connection with which a verse is quoted from S'lokavartika (page 63) where mention is made of the godohana. Kunte’s long note on the श्र of Jaimini referred to above will be found useful, Woy कार्यसम्परत्ययः ॥ When a word has both a primary and a secondary meaning, an operation takes effect (only) when the word conveys its primary meaning. This is an abridgment of Professor Kielhorn’s rendering of Nagojibhatta’s fifteenth paribhasa; and the following extracts from the vritti may help to explain it:—“A word which is employed in a secondary sense is so 44 employed (only ) because (the character of that which it denotes in) its primary sense is ascribed (to that which it denotes in a secondary sense).” “For example, the word गो ‘ox’ conveys the secondary meaning ‘one who is only fit for bearing burdens, an unintelligent person, because (such a person has) certain qualities such as stupidity &. (in common with : anox)”, The nyaya appears in the above form in Mahabhdsya 1. 1. 15, and 6. 3. 46; but in 1. 4. 108, and in 8. 3. 82, the word कार्यं is omitted. In this shortened form it is quoted by Anandagiri + on Brahmasiitrabhadsya 1. 2. 18 (page 185), and 1, 3. 14 (page 246). Then in 4, 3. 12, S’ankara says “gy हि ब्रह्म बह्यशब्दस्य सुख्यमारंबनं गौणमपरम्‌ | खख्यगोणयोश्च सुख्ये संम्र्ययो भवति. In Vedamtakalpataru, page 346, line 8, we read:—“ag सन्दिग्धं वस्तु प्रायद्रीनान्नर्णीयते | गोणसुख्य्हणविषये च ger सम्परल्ययः. In S‘abara’s bhasya on Jamini 3. 2. 1 (which deals with the Barhirnydya) we have a very interesting discussion on गौण and सख्य, in the course of which he says “agafrart yet गौणमभिवदति शब्दः".
