The conclusion arrived at is thus expressed: — ‘Targenioge कायंसम्मलयय इति सिद्धम्‌. भ्रहेकत्वन्यायः ॥ The illustration furnished by [the mention of the washing of] one cup only. This is the title of the adhikarana comprising Mimamsasiitras 3. 1. 13-15 in which the passage relating to the cleansing of the soma-cups is discussed. The direction given is “दशापवित्रेण अहं date’, “he cleanses the cup witha fringed filtering-cloth.” But there are many such grahas in use; is only one of them to be washed? The decision is that all may be cleansed; and this, according to Madhava (who is -closely followed by Krishna Yajvan in the Mimdmsdparibhasa), is 45 because one represents the whole, and also on the ground of the rule that “a subordinate act is to be repeated in the case of each principal thing.” The cup is here the ‘principal, and the act of cleansing is ‘subordinate, and is therefore to be repeated until each one has been cleansed. The passage stands thus in the Vydyamdlavistara:—* अहमिति द्वितीयया अ्रहस्योदेश्यतया भ्रयोजनवत्तया च प्राधान्यं गम्यते । महं प्रति गुणः संमार्गः | रति प्रधानं च गुण आव्तेनीय इति# न्यायेन यावन्तो अहाः सन्ति ते संमाजैनीयाः” ॥ Kumirila explains this in the following karikaé of Tantravartika 8. 1. 14:— “व्यक्तीरुदि
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 43

gloss_en: Like a delineation on stone.
explanation: Like a delineation on stone. Used of something unalterably fixed. “तन्मां वच्रकुमाराय सम्प्रदत्तान्यथा तु मे । मरणं शरणं तात ary रेखेव गीरियम्‌.' Paris'istaparvan xii. 275. Compare Job’s words (xix. 28 ):—“Oh that my words were now written ...-.that with an iron pen and lead they were graven in the rock for ever.” : * For this, see below. 46 :
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 45

gloss_en: The simile of Sandal-oil.
explanation: The simile of Sandal-oil. Badarayana uses this as an illustration in siitras 2. 3. 23, 24, As the application of a drop of the oil to one part of the body produces a pleasant sensation in the whole of it, so soul, abiding in one part, namely in the heart, is yet perceived as present in the entire frame S‘ankara’s exposition of the former siitra is as follows:— “यथा इरिचन्दनविन्दुः शरीरेकेदेशसंबद्धोऽपि सन्सकरुदेहव्यापिनमाहादं करोलयवमात्मापि देहैकदेशस्थः सकरुदेहव्यापिनीमुपलन्थि करिष्यति 1 त्वक्संबन्धाच्चास्य सकलशरीरगता वेदना न विरुध्यते । त्वगात्मनो्िं संबन्धः grat स्वचि वतेते | त्वक्च कत्लशरीरव्यापिनीति"?. चित्राङ्गनान्यायः ॥ The simile of a woman in a picture. An illustration of that which has only an appearance of reality. Raghunatha connects with this 6 चित्रानलन्याय and चिच्राख्धतन्याय, and, in his larger work, भगवान्वसिष्ठः is quoted as follows:—“Rarga नाखतमेव विद्धि Rad नानरूमेव विद्धि । चित्राङ्गना नूनमनङ्गनेति वाचाविवेकस्त्वविवेक एवेति. Like his other quotations from Vasistha, this, too, is from the Yogavdsistha, where it stands as verse 4. 18. 69, Others of a like nature are 4. 1. 11 and 12:--“चित्रोद्यानमिवोत्फुलमरसं सरसाङ्ृति । म्रकाशमपि निस्तेजश्चित्राकीनलवस्स्थितम्‌ ॥ अनुभूतं मनोरा- उ्यमिवासल्यमवास्तवम्‌ | चित्रपद्माकर इव सारसोगन्ध्यवर्जितम्‌* Also 6. 57, 28:--“चित्राभ्चिदाहो विज्ञातो यथा दा्येषु निष्फलः | तथाहंमावसगौदि ज्ञातं निष्फलतामियात्‌" ॥ छायापिशाचीन्यायः ॥ The illustration of a shadow mistaken for a she-goblin! It is found in Nrisimhasarasvati’s commentary on the last section of the Vedantasdra, as follows:—“arqmree. करियासाध्यस्य 47 वस्तुनो बि्यमानानथनिनरत्तेशच पुरुषार्थत्वं इष्टमत्र तदभावात्कथं पुरूपाथेत्वमिति चेन्न तयोरेव पुरुषार्थत्वमिति नियमाभावात्सरच्छायायामारोपितरक्षसो Presa कण्टगतचामीकरस्य रान्तपुरुषस्याप्तवाक्येन तयोरनिदृ्याक्योरपि TET AAT | अत्र संग्रहः | (आत्माक्ञानमलं निरस्तममरं प्राप च तत्वं परं कण्ठस्थाभरणादिवछदमवशाच्छायापिराची यथा | आोक्तया्िनिदृत्तिवच्छरुतिरिरोवाक्याद्रोरत्थि- ताद्धस्तध्वान्तनिरासतः परसुखं Te तयोरुच्यत' इति ॥ जरतुंबिकान्यायः ॥ The simile of a gourd in water. The idea is that of a gourd, thickly covered with mud, and therefore sinking in the water, but gradually resuming its buoyancy as the mud is washed off, The Digambara Jains use the figure to illustrate the release of the soul from the encumbrance of the body. Raghunatha puts it thus:—“qa gefeer तंबिका नदीससुद्रादौ मजन्ती पंके aftitsaara आगलय तिष्ठति तथा जीवो देहादिपंके क्षीणेऽखोका- कारो mat तिष्ठति | अयमेव तस्य ata इति दिगम्बराः । इति जरतुंबिका- न्यायः ॥ This description, without mention of the nyaya, is found in very similar language in the Jaina chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 48 of Jivananda’s edition), and also in Vedantakalpataruparimala, for which see the पज्जरसुक्तपक्षिन्याय below. The former passage is as follows:—“qar वा शृत्तिकाटेपकृतमखाबुद्रव्यं जकेऽधःपतति पुनरपेतण्धत्तिकाबन्धमूर्वै गच्छति तथा कमेरहित आत्मा असङ्गत्वादूरध्व गच्छति बन्धच्छेदादरण्डबीजवच्चोध्वेगतिस्वभावाचाभ्जि- शिखावत्‌.” The figure of the castor-oil seed is found in the Parimala passage also. The term अलोकाकाद्च which appears in Raghunatha’s definition, is the name of a subdivision of one of the five categories (astikdya) of the Jaina system. It is explained as follows by Anandagiri on Brahmasitrabhdsya 2. 2. 88 (page 568 ):-—“‘orearnfcart द्वेधा लोकिकाकाशोऽरौकिकाकादाश्च । खोकानामन्तवैतीं Gere | तदुपरि मोक्षस्थानमलोकाकान्ञः'. This is 48 = referred to in a verse quoted by Madhava in the immediate context of the passage already cited from the Swrvadars‘anasangraha, namely :—“aeqr गस्वा निवतैन्ते चन्द्रसूयौदयो अहाः | अद्यापि a निवर्वन्ते व्वारोकाकादामागताः”? ॥ Professor Cowell’s rendering is:—“However often they go away, the planets return, the sun, mon, and the rest; but never to this day have returned any who have gone to Alokakas'a.” This should of course be Alokakas’a, as the long vowel includes the preposition at ‘up to, ‘as far as, “those who have reached Alokakas'a” (जा अरोकाकाशम्‌ ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 46

gloss_en: The law regarding the oblation on the birth of a son.
explanation: The law regarding the oblation on the birth of a son. This is the subject of Jaimini 4. 3. 38, 39. The question arises whether the offering is for the benefit of the father or the sot (aa सन्देहः किमात्मनिःश्रेयसाय उत पुत्रनिःश्रेयसाय); and the answer is that it benefits the latter. In the commentary on Laksandvali this is applied as follows—“afe चैत्रानुष्टिताभ्निहोत्रजनितमपूरव चचैत्रसमवेतं Fa स्वगैफकभागिनं कलैसुत्सहते । पु्रेटिपितृयक्ञवदेतस्स्यात्‌ । न । तत्राप्यतिश्यस्य पुत्रादिसमवेतस्वेनेवाभ्युपगमात्‌.” जामातञ्चद्धिन्यायः ॥ The story of the son-in-law's revision [of a book}. This is the original of the Marathi जांवदशोध which Molesworth defines as “ A phrase, founded on a popular story, to express the examination of a piece of a composition by 8 shallow-witted fellow incapable of discerning its merit.” The popular story, as given on pages 6-9 of Merutunga’s Prabandhacintémami, is to the effect that Vararuci, having been instructed by Vikramaditya to find a suitable husband for his daughter, the princess Priyangumanjari, he in revenge for a supposed insult offered 49 him by her when his pupil, palmed off upon her an ignorant cowherd as a man of learning! The king accepted him, and he became his son-in-law. “In accordance with the advice of the pandit, the herdsman preserved unbroken silence; but the princess, wishing to test his cleverness, entreated him to revise a newly-written book. He placed the book in the palm of his hand, and with a nail-parer proceeded to remove from the letters in it the dots and the oblique lines at the top indicating vowels, and thus to isolate them, and then the princess discovered that he was a eowherd. After that the ‘son-in-law’s revision’ became a proverb everywhere.” This quotation is from Mr. ©. H. Tawney’s admirable translation of Merutunga’s work; the original being as follows:—“ पण्डितो- gee सवैथा मोनमेवारम्बमानो [ महिषीपालः | राजकन्यकया ager aren नवछिखितपुस्तकस्य शो धनायोपरुद्धः | करतले पुस्तकं बिन्यस्य तदृक्षराणि बिन्दुमान्नारदितानि नखच्छेदिन्या केवान्येव कुच्‌ राजयुज्या महिषीपारु एव निर्णीतः । ततः्रश्ति जामातृशुद्धिरिति aaa: प्रसिद्धिर भूत्‌.” In the subsequent part of the story we are informed that the cowherd eventually became the famous poet Kalidasa! For a similar account from a Buddhist source, see Indian Antiquary, vol iv. page 103; also vol vii. page 116.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 48

gloss_en: Knowledge {of Brahma] abolishes nothing but the ignorance [which obscures that Impersonality].
explanation: Knowledge {of Brahma] abolishes nothing but the ignorance [which obscures that Impersonality]. This is a bit of Vedantism pure and simple, and has scant claims to a place amongst popular maxims. It is admitted solely because Raghunatha includes it in his list. His explanation is as follows: —“stfaqaqध्यासेऽज्ञानं कारणम्‌ | तच्वन्ञानेन वातदीपन्यायेन तन्निबत्तावज्ञेहदीपन्यायेन तदृध्यासोऽपि निवतेते । न च क्तानेनैवोभयनिदृत्तिः कुतो न स्यात्‌ । ज्ञानमज्ञानस्यैव निवतैकमिति न्यायविरोधात्‌ । द्विविधा हि निटृत्तिः । विरोधिना सामग्रीनिदृ्या च । यथा वातादिना deat यथा च तैरुवत्यीदि निद्या दीपनिदृत्तिः । तत्राद्या निद्रत्तिरज्ञानस्य द्वितीया का्यवगखेति बोध्यम्‌” ॥ 7 50 The so-called nyaya is found in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 8, line 9, and in Pancapadika, page 1.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 49

gloss_en: The डमरुक Or डमर, is a sort of small drum, shaped like an hour-glass, and held in one hand: The मणि is, I suppose, the small piece of wood attached to the string tied round the middle of the drum, which strikes each end alternately as it is shaken in the hand, and thus fulfils a double purpose.
explanation: The डमरुक Or डमर, is a sort of small drum, shaped like an hour-glass, and held in one hand: The मणि is, I suppose, the small piece of wood attached to the string tied round the middle of the drum, which strikes each end alternately as it is shaken in the hand, and thus fulfils a double purpose. The garudis, or conjurors, use a drum of this kind; and Apte’s dictionary tells us that the Kapalikas carry one. (For a description of the latter, see Wilson’s Religion of the Hindus Vol. + pages 21 and 264.) The nyaya is found in Syadvddamamjari, page 84, where in explaining the words “dteqearatdaataa a’? in Hemacandra’s eleventh karika, Mallisena says, “अन्यार्थमिति मध्यवति पदं डमरुकमणिन्यायेनोभयत्रापि सम्बन्धनीयम्‌.” See the nyaya “अपवादैर्त्सर्गा बाध्यन्ते? and compare also, the घण्टारोलान्याय on page 35 of Syadvadamanjari.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 50

gloss_en: The simile of a young bird which [ has got out to sea on a floating log and ] 48 wnable to discover the shore.
explanation: The simile of a young bird which [ has got out to sea on a floating log and ] 48 wnable to discover the shore. Its application is obvious, It occurs in the second line of verse 19 of _ Hemacandra’s in Syddvddamanjari, as follows :—“ ततस्तटाददिी- शङ्न्तपोतन्यायात्वदुक्तानि परे श्रयन्तु.” Mallisena explains it thus:— “ad न पद्यतीति aziaait यः शङुन्तपोतः पक्षिशावकस्तस्य न्याय उदाहरणम्‌ । तस्माद्यथा fee कथमप्यपारपारावारान्तःपतितः काकादि शङुनिरावको बहि- ्िरजिगमिषया प्रवहणकूपस्तभादेस्तयग्राक्षये सुग्धतयोडीनः समन्ताजलेकाणैवमे- वावलोकयखटमद्दैव Mage , तदेव कूपस्तमादिस्थानमाश्रयते गय न्तराभावादेवं तेऽपि ऊुतीथ्यौः प्रागुक्तपक्षत्रयेऽपि वस्तुसिद्धिमनासादयन्तस्त्वदु- क्तमेव चतु मेदामेद्पक्षमनिच्छयापि कक्षीर्वाणास्त्वच्छासनमेव ATTA,” 51
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 50

gloss_en: The principle of meditation on that [viz.
explanation: The principle of meditation on that [viz. Brahman], Or, one who meditates on that. This is taken from Brahmasitra 4.3. 15, ^ अग्रतीकारंबनान्नयतीति बादरायण उभयथाऽदोषात्तत्करतुश्च ””. The bhasya on the last term is as follows:—“ तक्करतुश्चोभयथाभावसख समर्थको हेतु्द्टव्यः । यो हि ब्रह्करतुः स ब्राह्यमैश्वयैमासीदेदिति Pad (तं यथा यथोपासते तदेव भवति इति श्चुतः । न त प्रतीकेषु ब्रह्मक्रतुत्वमस्ति भ्रतीकम्रधानत्वाुपासनस्य | नन्वब्रह्क्रतुरपि aa गच्छतीति श्रूयते यथा पञ्चाभिविद्यायां “स एनान्ब्रह्म गमयति [ छा* ५, १०. २ ] इति । मवतु यत्र वमाहत्यवाद्‌ उपरभ्यते तद्भावे त्वौस्सर्मिकेण तत्कतुन्यायेन ब्रह्मक्रतूनामेव तत्प्राधिर्नेतरेषामिति गम्यते”. I subjoin Dr. Thibaut’s rendering, with a few additions of my own in parentheses. “The words, ‘and the meditation on that, state the reason for this twofold relation [viz. of those who meditate ‘on Brahman with qualities, and those who worship by means of symbols or images}. For he whose meditation is fixed on Brahman reaches lordship like that of Brahman, according to the scriptural relation, ‘In whatever form they meditate on him, that they become themselves.’ In the case of symbols, on the other hand, the meditation is not fixed on Brahman, the symbol being the chief element in the meditation —But scripture says also that persons whose mind is not fixed on Brahman go to it; so in the knowledge [ rather, the doctrine (vidya )] of the five fires, ‘He leads them to Brahman.’—This may be so where we observe a direct scriptural declaration. We only mean to say that, where there is no such declaration, the general rule is that those only whose purpose is Brahman [४ ९. whose mind is fixed on B,] go to it, not any others.” This isa noteworthy passage. It practically condemns the use of symbols, or images, in worship, by declaring that the mind of the upasaka is directed to the pratika, and not to Brahman (or Ts'vara), The sruti, ^ तं यथा यथोपासते &९.,* which is quoted also in 3. 4. 52, may mean much more than S’ankara sees in it; for it is an undoubted fact that, in 52 the present life, the worshipper becomes assimilated to the object of his worship; that the characteristics of that object, as conceived by him, become his own characteristics; and that a man never rises above the moral or spiritual level of that which he worships, This was clearly set before the Jewish nation in the teaching of Psalms cxv and cxxxv, where, with reference to the making and worshipping of images, we read “They that make them shall be like them, every one that trusteth in them” ( Revised version ). The तच्करतुन्याय, which, in its full form seems to be तत्करतुस्तद्धवति (as in Anandagiri on the closing part of 4. 8. 15), is constantly quoted in the Vedadntakalpataruparimala. It will be found on pages 229 ( three times), 230 (line 9), 235 (line 11), 246 (line 6), 478 (line 1), 591 (line 4 from bottom ), 669 (line 10), and 730 (lines 9and 15). Also Pancadas’t ix. 145.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 51

gloss_en: The principle that there is another scriptural ‘passage declaratory of it (namely, of the secondary matter connected with a sacrifice, such as the deity to whom it is offered, the materials to be used, &c).
explanation: The principle that there is another scriptural ‘passage declaratory of it (namely, of the secondary matter connected with a sacrifice, such as the deity to whom it is offered, the materials to be used, &c). The nyaya represents Jaimini’s sutra 1. 4. 4 ( तत्प्रख्यं चान्यशासखम्‌ ); and is one of the four means by which Mimamsakas prove that an injunction comes under the head of नामधेय (‘name’), and is not a गुणविधि (‘an injunction relating to the accessories of the sacrifice, provision being made for them in other scriptural passages ). The three other tests are styled मत्वर्थलक्षणा (‘possessive indication’) वाक्यभेद (‘split of the sentence’ caused by the inclusion of two or more distinct topics); and तद्यपदेशान्याय ( which see below). The following extracts from the Arthasangraha, with Dr Thibaut’s renderings, illustrate the nyaya. ““क्मैस्वरूपमात्रबोधको विधिर्त्पत्तिविधिः। यथाभिहोत् जहोतीति । ...ननु 53 यागस्य द्वे रूपे द्रव्यं देवतां च । तथा च रूपाश्रवणेऽभ्निहोतरं जहोतीति कथसुत्पत्ति- विधिः। अभ्निहोत्रराब्दस्य तु त्रख्यन्यायेन नामधेयत्वादिति चेन्न । रूपाश्रवणेऽप्यस्योत्पत्तिविधित्वात्‌ । अन्यथा रूपश्रवणादधा जहोतीत्ययमेवोत्पत्तिविधिः स्यात्‌ । तथा चाभ्निहोत्रं जहोतीति वाक्यमनर्थकं स्यात्‌ ॥ ( Page 4 ) “That injunction which merely indicates the general nature of some action is called ‘ originative injunction,’ as, for instance, the passage ‘He is to offer the Agnihotra oblation.”’ But it may be objected, the two forms (aspects) of a sacrifice are the material ( offered ) and the divinity (to whom the material is offered). How then can the passage ‘ He is to offer the Agnihotra-oblation,’ in which neither of these two forms is mentioned, be considered an originative injunction, the word Agnihotra (which seemingly indicates the divinity of the sacrifice, viz. Agni) being merely a name (and not indicating the divinity ) according to the principle of (the existence of another scriptural passage ) declaratory of it. To this objection we reply:—The passage is an originative injunction although neither of the two forms of the sacrifice is mentioned in it. Otherwise the passage ‘ He is to perform the oblation by means of sour milk’ would have to be considered as the originative injunction ( of the Agnihotra ) as it contains a statement of one of the two forms ( viz. the material ), and then the passage ‘He is to offer the Agnihotra’ would be purposeless.” ( Page 7 ). Again on page 20 of the same:— “og च प्रयाजेषु समिदादिदेवतानां समिधः (समिधो aa आज्यस्य व्यन्तु? इत्यादिमन्नवर्णेभ्यः प्रा्त्वात्‌ । समिधो यजतीलयादिषु समिदादिशब्दास्तत्मस्यश्ाखरात्क्मनामधेयाः” ॥ “Jn the same manner the pieces of. firewood called samidh and other things are established as the divinities of the Prayaja oblations by means of the Mantras ‘the samidhs may partake of the butter &., and therefore the words ‘samidh’ &c. which occur in the injunctions “He sacrifices the samidh &c,’ are to 54 be taken as names of certain sacrifices (not as denoting secondary matters) on account of another scriptural passage declaratory of it.” The nyaya is explained in almost identical language in Apadeva’s work Mimamsdnydyaprakas'a, and is applied in Vedantakalpataruparimala, page 150, line 7. We find it, also, in Nages’a’s Uddyota (vol. 3. page 623), under आ 2. 3. 3.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 52

gloss_en: One who takes the place of somebody else takes wpon himself also the functions of the latter.
explanation: One who takes the place of somebody else takes wpon himself also the functions of the latter. This is Professor Kielhorn’s rendering of the maxim as found in Nagojibhatta’s comment on paribhasi oxvi It may have originated with Kaiyata. On Panini’ssiitra 1.1. 56 (स्थानिवदादेशः &e.) Katyayana says ^“ स्थान्यादेशष्रथक्त्वादादेशो स्थानिवदनुदेशो Geagega इति यथा. ` Patanjali follows with the remark ^ लोकत एतसिद्धम्‌। तद्यथा। कोके यो यस्य प्रसंगे भवति रभतेऽखौ तत्कायांणि। तद्यथा । उपाध्यायस्य शिष्यो याज्यकुलानि गत्वा अग्रासनादीनि रभते. Then Kaiyata says «लोकत इति। रोके हि वचनमन्तरेणापि तर्स्थानापच्या तद्धर्मलाभो दष्टः" ॥ Under siitra 1. 1. 4, Nages’a Bhatta prepares us for this with the remark (on page 209 of his Pradipoddyota) “अ्ञाख्रीयस्यापि तस्य ‹ तस्स्थानापन्ने तद्ध्मलाभ ` इति रोकन्ययेनातिदेश इति स्थानिवत्सूत्र वक्ष्यते 0
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 54

gloss_en: This nyaya, which is found in Vedantakalpataruparimala, page 581, line 9, and again in line 8 of the next page, is taken from the तदादितदन्तन्याय of Jaimini 5.
explanation: This nyaya, which is found in Vedantakalpataruparimala, page 581, line 9, and again in line 8 of the next page, is taken from the तदादितदन्तन्याय of Jaimini 5. 1. 23, 24, the अनुयाजाद्युत्करषभ्रयाजान्तापकषाधिकरण, which Kunte renders “The subject of the forward transference of acts which follow the Anuyaja-offerings . and the backward transference of acts which precede Prayaja55 offerings together with the offerings themselves.” Regarding the तदादितदन्तन्याय he says:—“Tadadi signifies the first part of that; the Tadanta signifies the last part of that. These are Bahuvrihi compounds, and therefore signify that of which the first part is taken, and that of which the last part is taken. When forward transference is to be made, the Tadadi is taken and when backward transference is to be made, the Tadanta is taken. The following illustration will explain this. When the Anuyaja-offerings are to be transferred in point of time, the Suktavaika and the Shanyuvaka which follow it, are to be transferred along with it, because the Anuyaja is the first part. Again, when Prayaja-offerings are to be transferred in point of time, the Aghara and the Samidheni which precede it, are to be transferred along with it, because the Prayaja is the last part.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 54

gloss_en: The law that a certain thing is seen to appear when a certain other thing appears [thus establishing a connection between them].
explanation: The law that a certain thing is seen to appear when a certain other thing appears [thus establishing a connection between them]. Raghunathavarman expounds it thus:— “जाग्रदादौ बुद्धौ सत्यां दुःखादयो ददयन्ते सुपुश्यादौ afeawt Terra इति तद्धमौ एव नात्मधमौः सुषुसावप्युपरुब्धिग्रसंगादियत्र तदागमे हि तदुर्यत इति न्यायोऽवतरति | cera हि तेलादजुद्धूतस्पदतेजोऽवयवागमे सन्तापोपलBate तदधम एव न तैलादिधरमं इति. 1 have met with it in the following passage of the Tantravartika (page 348), and quote Prof. Ganganath Jha’s translation of it:— “qa कमवतोर्निलं भ्रकृतिभ्रययांशयोः | भरत्ययश्चुतिवेखायां भावनात्मावगम्यते ॥ म केवलमेतावेवान्वयव्यतिरेको यो परस्परपरित्यागेन रक्ष्येते ahaa हि पदे तदागमे हि तदह्यत इनेन न्यायेन विवेकोऽवधायैते”. “The Root and the Affix are always found to appear in a definite order of sequence; and, as a matter of fact, we find 56 that it is only when the Affix is heard to be pronounced, that we have an idea of the Bhévand; and this, too, leads us to the conclusion that the Bhavana& is denoted by the affix. Because the invariable concomitance of any two objects is not only such that one cannot exist in the absence of the other, but also of a kind which we find in the present case, where we find that it is only where a particular word—the Affix for instance—is pronounced that there is a denotation of the Bhavana ; and as such, in accordance with the rule that when one object is always seen to appear when another appears, there is always an invariable concomitance between the two, we must admit that the Bha@vand is denoted by the Affix.” (p. 483). I may add that the passage here cited from the Tantravartika is closely connected with that quoted under the nyaya ५ प्रकृतिप्रत्ययौ प्र्ययार्थं सह ब्रूतः?” which see below. तद्रमपदेशन्यायः ॥ The principle of a name indicating resemblance to something. This is the title of Jaimini’s sitra 1. 4. 5, where the sentences “ अभ्चैष दइयेनेनाभिचरन्यजेत ”, “se सन्दंशेनाभिचरन्यजेत ” and “ aq गवाभिचरन्यजेत्‌ ” are discussed, and it is decided that इयेन, सन्दंद, and गो are not the materials of the various sacrifices, but their names. This is well put in Colebrooke’s Essay on the Mimamsa :— “It is a question whether the hawk-sacrifice ( s'yenaydga ) which is attended with imprecations on a hated foe, be performed by the actual immolation of a bird of that kind. The case is determined by a maxim that ‘a term intimating resemblance is denominative. Hawk, then, is the name of that incantation; ‘it pounces on the foe as a falcon on his prey.” So tongs is a name for a similar incantation ; ‘ which seizes the enemy from afar as with a pair of tongs’; and cow, for a sacrifice to avert such imprecations,” 57 This nyaya, as pointed out under तस्प्रख्यन्याय, is one of the proofs applied in the Mimamsa in support of the arqaa division of the Veda, and Laugaksibhaskara (on page 20 ) explains it thus:— ““इयेनेनाभिचरन्यजेतेत्र ware कर्मनामधेयत्वं तद्वपदेशात्‌ । तेन व्यपदेशादुपमानात्तदन्यथानुपपत्तेरिति यावत्‌ ।...यदा त॒ श्येनसंक्ञको यागो विधीयते तदा्थवादेन श्येनोपमानेन तस्य स्त॒तिः करतु शक्यत इति रयेनशब्दः कर्मनामधेयं तव्यपदेशादितिः? ॥ In this passage, and in a similarly worded one in Apadevi, उपमान is given as the equivalent of eqqzar, whilst Kumarila employs सादृश्य, The dictionaries, however, are silent as to any such sense of the word. In his translation Dr. Thibaut renders it “ comparison.” ad aaa संबध्यते ॥ Hot goes with hot. Like loves like. This is found in the following passage of Bhdmatt 3. 3. 25 (page 620):—“aq सन्निधानमपि संबन्धकारणम्‌ | अयमेति gat राज्ञः पुरूषोऽपसा्यतामिलत्र राज्ञ इत्यस्य पुत्रपुरुषपदसन्निधानाविरोषान्मा भूदविनिगमना | तसादाकांक्षा निश्चवयहेतुर्वक्तव्या |... wate सन्निधाने आकांक्षाभावादसंबन्धः | तथा चाभाणकः। तप्तं ततेन संबध्यत इति । तथा चाकांक्षितमपि न यावत्सक्निधाप्यते तावन्न संबध्यते. ॥ Mr. M. R. Telang has given me another excellent example in Vikramorvast ii, 16:—“ साधारणोऽयञ्ुभयोः प्रणयः स्मरस्य aaa तप्तमयसा घटनाय योग्यम्‌. ` Compare, too, “za रत्नेन सङ्गच्छते" of Mricchakatika;
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 55

gloss_en: The simile of [a drop of] water conswmed by hot oron [when thrown upon it].
explanation: The simile of [a drop of] water conswmed by hot oron [when thrown upon it]. Ramtirtha employs it when expounding the words “a तस्य प्राणा gaRiqiza’’ in the last section of the Vedantasira. He says:— 8 58 “Rumaraararad: प्राणा नोत्तामन्ति किन्तु प्रलग्ब्रह्मण्येव तप्तायः- पीताम्बुवह्टीयन्त इत्यत्र प्रमाणमाह a तस्येति. In Nrisimhasarasvati’s comment on the same portion the nyaya is expanded and made clearer :— “Te जीवन्मुक्तस्योपाधिविगमसमयें प्राणाख्यं लिङ्गदारीरमतितप्तरोदक्षि्- नीरविन्दुवस्प्र्यगभिन्नपरमानन्दे छीनत्वास्स्थूलशरीरं नोत्तिष्टतीति। अत्र श्ुतिमाह न तस्येति.” Neither of them, however, was the originator of the simile; for it is clearly an adaptation of the line “ सन्तप्तायसि संस्थितस्य पयसो नामापि न ज्ञायते ? which forms the commencement of Pancatantra i. 250, and also of Bhartrihari’s Nitis'ataka 67.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 57

gloss_en: The illustration [ of the co-existence ] of light and darkness.
explanation: The illustration [ of the co-existence ] of light and darkness. It is found in Pancapadika, page 8, ४8 follows:—“ कोऽयं विरोधः कीददयो वा इतरेतरभावोऽभिप्रेतो यस्यानुपपत्तेस्तमःप्रकाशवदिति निदर्लनम्‌ । यदि तावत्सहानवस्थानलक्षणो बिरोधस्ततः भ्रकाशभावे तमसो भावानुपपत्तिः | तदसत्‌ | इयते हि मन्दप्रदीपे वेरमन्यस्पष्टं रूपददौनमितरत्र च स्पष्टम्‌ । तेन ज्ञायते wearer Aza तमसोऽपीषदनुड्त्तिरितिःः. The substance of this is reproduced in Viwaranaprameyasangraha, page 10.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 58

gloss_en: The custom of taking grass in the mouth [ lit.
explanation: The custom of taking grass in the mouth [ lit. of eating grass ], as a token of submission. This interesting illustration is found in Prabandhacintémani, page 93 :-वैरिणोपि हि सुच्यन्ते भ्राणान्ते तृणभश्चषणात्‌ | तृणाहाराः asad हन्यन्ते परावः way.” Mr. Tawney renders it thus:—“Since even enemies are let off, when near death, if they take grass in their mouths, how can you slay these harmless beasts [deer &c.] which always feed on grass ?” In a note on page 210, it is stated that we have here an allusion to a most ancient custom. ‘There is a reference to it in Harsa39 carita (Bombay edn. 1892 ) page 132, line 11, on which, the translators, Cowell and Thomas, remark, “'To carry a straw in the mouth was a sign of surrender; compare Acworth’s Maratha Ballads, page 43 :— ‘And’ twixt the teeth a straw is fit For curs who arm but to submit.’ ” These two lines are deduced from the three words “ sq} तोंडांत am ”’ of the original. Merutunga refers to this custom again on page 300 :—“arar नः परमदनेन वदनन्यस्तेन संरक्षितः । पथ्वीराजनराधिपादिति तृणं तत्पत्तने पूज्यते. “Grass is now worshipped in Paramardin’s city, because, when taken in the mouth, it preserved our lord Paramardin from Prithviraja, the king of men.” (Tawney’s translation, page 189 ). The late Colonel Meadows Taylor, who was so thoroughly acquainted with Indian life, put the following into the mouth of one of his characters in Seeté ( chapter xLvii ):—“* We have a good many prisoners, for I could not kill the wretches who had put grass in their mouths and were crying for quarter.” तेकट्पितशालिबीजादङ्करानुदयनियमः ॥ # The certainty of the non-appearance of shoots from grains of rice spoilt by [ contact with ] oi. It occurs in Veddntakalpataru, page 545, line, 17:—* क्रियाभोगद्यत्त्योः सल्योरपि तत्मरति- बन्धात्कायानुदयः संभवति तैलकल्षितशालिवीजादह्कराजदयनियमवत्‌. In S‘ankarabhagya 2. 3. 81, we read :—“qay लोके पुंस्त्वादीनि बीजा- त्मना बिद्यमानान्येव बाल्यादिष्वनुपरभ्यमानान्यविद्यमानवदभ्प्रियमाणानि यौ- वनादिष्वाविर्भवन्ति नाबिद्यमानान्युत्प्न्ते षण्डादीनामपि तदुत्पत्तिप्रसङ्गात्‌.” Of the आदि in षण्डादीनाम्‌ -Anandagiri ७878 :-^तैलकलुपितवीजादि- ग्रहा्मादिपदम्‌.* See also Nyayamakaranda, page 60. Compare the मूषिकभक्षितबीजादावङ्करादिजननग्रार्थनाः, in Second Handful. 60
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 58

gloss_en: The simile of the burnt seed.
explanation: The simile of the burnt seed. An illustration of that which has for ever ceased to be an operative cause. It appears in a verse of Syddvidamanjari, page 208 :— ava बीजे यथालन्तं प्रादुर्भवति sige: । कर्मबीजे तथा ara न रोहति भवांकुरः ॥ The following is from the Prabandhacintémani, page 206 :— राजग्रतिग्रहदग्धानां ब्राह्मणानां युधिष्ठिर । दग्धानामिव बीजानां पुनजन्म न विद्यते ॥ Merutunga ascribes it to a Purana. Vijnana Bhiksu quotes from some Smriti another of a like kind, under Yogavartika 7. 3:— बीजान्यश्युपदग्धानि न रोहन्ति यथा ga: । क्ानदग्धैस्तथा BMA सम्पद्यते पुनः ॥ See also a verse ascribed to a Charvaka in Prabodhacandrodaya, page 35.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 60

gloss_en: That which is given once is received back a thousand times.
explanation: That which is given once is received back a thousand times. This is found in Merutunga’s work, page 266, and I append Mr. Tawney’s rendering—“aq वीरधवलस्यायुःपर्यन्ते प्रतितीर्थं भ्रस्थि- तेन दनत्तमेकधा सहतरगुणसुपलभ्यत इति ee: श्रीतेजःपाटेन aged ददे.” “Then, when Viradhavala’s life was approaching its termination, Tejahpala, who was on his way to a holy place, in accordance with the proverb that what is given once is received back a hundred (?) times, gave him the merits of his last birth.” Though the illustration is fable, the principle is true. “ Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom.” Among the Turks there is the saying “Who gives alms sows one and reaps one thousand.” (Rev. T. Long's Eastern proverbs and emblems, page 187). 61
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 60

gloss_en: To sleep like a debtor whose debt has been paid.
explanation: To sleep like a debtor whose debt has been paid. To sleep like a top! It occurs in Hemacandra’s Paris'istaparvam, ii. 563:— ५ इति खुषाया दौशील्यामषैचिन्तां विहाय सः । सुष्वाप दत्तणै इवाधमणैस्तत्र॒ ` Raa.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 61

gloss_en: Curds and cucumber are fever personified! That is, they cause fever.
explanation: Curds and cucumber are fever personified! That is, they cause fever. This is found in Mahdbhdsya 1, 1. 59 ( vartika 6), and 6. 1. 32 (vart 6), as follows:—* अन्तरेणापि निमित्तराब्दं निमित्तार्थो गम्यते । तद्यथा । दधित्रपुसं प्रस्यश्चो अवरः । उवरनिमित्तमिति गम्यते | नडुखोदकं पादरोगः | पाद्रोगनिमित्तमिति गम्यते । आयुतम्‌ । आयुषो निमित्तमिति ava’ This closely resembles Raghunatha’s दधिपयसी प्रत्यक्षो ज्वरः which [ have not yet met with anywhere. See आयुतं and लाङ्गलं जीवनम्‌ in Second Handful. वे्रवीजविनादाकत्वं 2 2 दवदाहस्य वेत्रबीजविनाशकत्वं रूपान्तरजनकत्वं च ॥ The forest-fire which destroys the seeds of the Ratan is also [ by that means] the promoter of the growth of another form [ namely, the plantain]. This is found in Cétsukhi i. 15, as follows:—“gat च दवदाहस्य वेत्रबीजविनाशकत्वं रूपान्तरजनकत्वं च तथा दोषाणामपि यथार्थक्ञानग्रतिबन्धकत्वमयथार्थक्ञानजनकत्वं च किं न स्यात्‌.” The same idea, with the express mention of the plantain tree, is found under i. 14, and also in the following passage of the Nydyavartikatatparyatika page 57:—“es च दुष्टानामपि कारणानामोत्सिककार्यम्रतिबन्धेन कार्यान्तरोर्पादकस्वम्‌ | तद्यथा वेत्रबीजानां qaqa कदलीप्रकाण्डजनकस्वं wages चोद्यस्य तेजसो agaरान्नपानपात्रकत्वम्‌.*› The same passage is found in Bhémati page 18, line 7; and, one of the same nature, in Sikhdmani, page 134, line 8 ; whilst, in Sarvdrthasiddhs ii. 46 (page 207), Venkata says “द्गधवेत्रबीजस्य रम्भां रारम्भकत्वं CTS.” See also Maniprabha 7. 14 (page 55), and Nydyamakaranda, page 75, line 6. 62
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 61

gloss_en: The illustration of the eating of an elongate [ confection called] S‘askuli [ said, by the commentator on Yajnavalkyasmriti 1.
explanation: The illustration of the eating of an elongate [ confection called] S‘askuli [ said, by the commentator on Yajnavalkyasmriti 1. 173, to be सेहपक्रगोधूमविकारः ]. The nyaya is well defined in the following extract from the Vis'istadvaitin (vol. i. p. 102), for which I am indebted to my friend Dr. Grierson:— “ S‘ashkult is a stick-like edible, which a person begins to eat from one end. While he so eats there are several sensations present, such as the hardness or the softness of the thing; its surface, round, square &c., uniform or indented; its taste, smell, color &c. All the sensations do not affect the person simultaneously, but one after another. When he isintent on the taste, he is non-intent on the color; when he is intent on the smell he is non-intent on the surface ; though all the sensations seem to come to him simultaneously, by reason of the infinitesimal interval of time dividing one sensation from another.” Nydyasitra 1. 16 defines Mind as that in which knowledge [ of more things than one ] does not arise simultaneously ; and ‘we find the following in Mallinatha’s comment on Tarkikarahsa 29, where that siitra is referred to:—“a च daracttaaarat ज्ञानयोगपद्यसंभवादसंभवि लक्षणमिति वाच्यम्‌ | शतपत्रद्यातनवदाञ्भावनि- बन्धनो यौगपद्याभिमानो यत इति सर्वमवदातम्‌” Again, in the Vyayapradipa on the definition of मनस्‌ 1 Tarkabhasa(p.126)we read:-— “'अण्विति । विभुस्वे ddaradt मक्षयतः पुंसो रूपरसाच्यनेकविषयाणि ज्ञानानि युगपज्ायेरन्‌ | आत्मेन्द्रियविषयसंबन्धास्मिकायाः सामभ्याः सस्वादणुत्वे स्विन्दि- याप्रसेन युगपज्ज्ञानानि भविष्यन्तीति भावः ॥ In a discussion on the same subject in Nyayamanjart page 497, Jayanta Bhatta brings forward three examples of apparent simultaneity, the first of which is quaintly versified as follows ;— “सुगन्धि शीतलां दीधमश्न्तः पूपराष्कुरीम्‌ | कपिलब्राह्मणास्सन्ति युगपत्पञ्चजुद्धयः”* ॥ 63
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 62

gloss_en: The illustration of distant trees [ 7.
explanation: The illustration of distant trees [ 7. ९. of two trees standing apart, but which, owing to their distance from the spectator, seem to be one]. It is contained in the Lawkikanydyaratnakara (10 MS. 582, page 219 a ), but not in the smaller work. Its author most probably derived it from Citsukht, where the following passage is found at the beginning of the second chapter :-—“ ननु क्षीरनीरयो्वि्यमानमेदयोरपि स्वरूपग्रहणे भेदग्रहो न इद्यत इति चेन्मैवं तत्र समानाभिहारलक्षणदोषप्रतिबन्धादेव senate: | एतेन दूरस्थवनस्पत्योरपि भेद्हप्रसंगः म्र्युक्तः ॥ The Muni then quotes Sankhyakarika vii. “अतिदूरात्‌ &e.”; and the same karika, with a somewhat similar context, is cited in the Pirnaprajna section of sarvadars'anasangraha. The simile of the two trees is met with in the much older work Sanksepas'driraka (i. 44), namely:—“‘qrerittg वनस्पतिवस्तुनोस्तद्धेदो न दष्टिविषयोऽवगते च ते 770 We have it again in the Pancapadika (page 7, line 18). It is interesting to compare with Is'varakrisna’s causes of anupalabdhi those assigned by Patanjali in Mahabhdsya 4. 1.3 (vol. ii. page 197), He says: —“qgit: salt: सतां भावानामनुपलन्धि- मैवल्यतिसंनिकषादैतिविग्रकषौन्मूत्यन्तरम्यवधानात्तमसावरृतत्वादिन्द्ियदोरबल्याद्‌- तिप्रमादादिति,ः? देवदत्तशौयन्यायः ॥ The figure of Devadatta’s bravery. Equivalent to Horace’s “Celum non animum mutant, qui trans mare currunt.” The illustration is used by Sankara in his bhasya on Brahmasiitra 8. 3. 10:- “एकस्यामपि शाखायां श्रुता गुणाः श्चुता एव aaa भवन्ति गुणवतो भेदाभावात्‌ | न हि देवदत्तः दौयीदिगुणत्वेन VS प्रसिद्धो देशान्तरं गतस्तदेदयैर बिभावितद्नौयदिगुणोऽप्यतद्कणो मवति | यथा च aa परिचयविशे- षादेशान्तरेऽपि देवदत्तगुणा विभाव्यन्ते ॥ The देवदत्तशौर्यनिदरीन is mentioned again in the bhasya on the next siitra. Compare with this the following from Tantravartika, 3. 6. 41 ( page 1108) --“यथैव देवदत्तस्य स्थानोपवेशनगमनरायनाद्यवस्थाभेदेऽपि देवदत्तत्वं 64 नापैति &० See, also, Slokavartika, page 780; and compare the following from Mahabhasya 1. 1. 1 ( vart. 13):—* ननु च भो अभेदका अपि गुणा eet । तद्यथा । देवदत्तो सुण्ड्यपि जव्यपि शिख्यपि स्वामाख्यां न जहाति.” Commenting on this, Kaiyata says :—“quaq हि कृते चौर्ये ऊुंतङित्वावस्थायामपि चौरोऽयमिति व्यपदिश्यते. देवदत्तहन्तृहतन्यायः ॥ The illustration of the slaying of the murderer of Devadatta. The point is that the death of the murderer does not bring his : victim to life again. It is applied by Raghunatha as follows:— ^“नन्वक्ञानबाधके Ait विनष्टे पुनरक्तानोदयेन बन्धप्रसङ्ग इति शंकायां देवदत्तइन्तृहतन्यायावतारः | यथा देवदत्तहन्तरि हतेऽपि न देवदत्तस्य जीवनं तथा म्रङृतेऽपि.” He probably took it from Nagojibhatta’s comment on 0010756 Lxiv [ उपसंजनिष्यमाणनिमित्तः Xe, which see above] where it reads thus:—‘ aq देवदत्तस्य हन्तरि इते देवदत्तस्योन्मजनं नेति न्यायस्य विषय एव aria । हते देवदत्त उन्मजनं न । देवदत्तहननो्- तस्य तु हनने भवययवोन्मजनम्‌? Nagoji, on the other hand, derived it from the Mahabhdsya, where (in 1. 1. 57, vartika 4) it appears as “न ह्न्यस्यासिद्धत्वादन्यस्य प्रादु मावो भवति । न हि देवदत्तस्य इन्तरि हते देवदत्तस्य रादु मौवो wala.” So, too, in 6. 1. 86 (vart. 3), It is quoted in the consolidated form in Pradipoddyota 1, 1, 5 (page 213). धारावाहिकबुद्धिः | A persistent state of cognition. This is Mr. Arthur Venis’ rendering of the expression as it occurs in Vedantaparibhasa (The Pandit, vol. iv., page 105), and he elucidates it in a foot- . note as follows :—“It may be described as a series of states throughout which the same object is presented in consciousness. Each state of consciousness lasts only for a moment, a kshana or unit of appreciable time, and perishes. A second state then succeeds and is lost, and so on.” We find the expression (in the plural) in the Prakaranapancikad, page 127, and as धारावाहिकन्लानानि on page 40; whilst, on page 56, the author 65 refers to it ag the धारावाहिकन्याय. 1 have met with it elsewhere also. Yet, strange to say, the Vacaspatyam is the only Sanskrit dictionary that contains it. The term धारावाहिन्‌ (with "वाहिक also) is there defined as “सन
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 63

gloss_en: The simile of a dancer.
explanation: The simile of a dancer. One dancer gives pleasure to many spectators, just as one lamp gives light to many persons. It oceurs in Jaiminiyanyayamalavistara 11. 1. 10 as follows:— ^्रलयङ्गिकायौण्यज्गानि तच््रेणोता ङ्गिनो यतः । एकैकस्योपकारस्वं तस्मास्प्रयङ्गयनुष्टितिः ॥ विधानवदनुष्ठानं सङ्ृदेवोपकारकम्‌ | तदेशकार्कणामेकत्वान्नतैकादिवत्‌”” ॥ The last pada is thus explained:—“ यथा agat पुरतो नृलयन्नर्तकः सर्वेषां सुखं युगपन्निर्वतेयति aga” | Of the same kind, apparently, is the following expression of the Nydyavartika i. 10 (page 70) :-—“gaaearat बृद्धानामेकस्मिन्नर्तकीञरक्षेपे युगपदनेकम्र्ययवत्‌ `” which is reproduced in the a Nydyavirtikatatparyatika, page 354 (line 9 from bottom ) in these words:—“ उक्तमेतद्‌ यथा नर्तकीश्रुलताभङ्गे एकस्मिन्वहूनां प्रति- सन्धानमिति. 67
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 66

gloss_en: The simile of a camel with a rope fastened in its nostrils.
explanation: The simile of a camel with a rope fastened in its nostrils. It is found in verse 78 of Tattvamuktavali -- “माया यस्य वशं गता बलवती त्रैकोक्यसंमोटिनी विज्ञेयः प्रभुरीश्वरः स भगवानानन्द्सचिद्धनः | यस्तस्या TATA: खल नसि रोतोरकल्पः सदा ज्ञातव्यः स हि जीव इत्थमनयोरस्त्येव भेदो महान्‌ * ॥ “He under whose control is that mighty illusion which deceives the three worlds, He is to be recognized as the Supreme Lord, the adorable, essentially thought, existence, and joy ; but he who is himself always under her control, like a camel drawn by a string through his nostrils, is to be recognized as the individual soul,—vast indeed is the difference between the two.” The translation is Prof. Cowell's. The dictionaries give references to three works where the figure is used, but always as नस्योत, and, either in text or comment, applied to a bullock, not toacamel, The passages will be found in Tait. Samhita 2. 1. 1. 2, Vanaparva xxx. 26, and Bhagavata Purana 6. 3. 12. The second one reads thus :-—“ ईश्वरस्य वदो तिष्े्नान्येषां नात्मनः ry | मणिः सूत्र इव प्रोतो नस्योत इव aaa” ॥ Compare, too, Magha xii. 10, with its description of a vain attempt to load a pack-bullock though held by its nose-cord (नस्या ). ॥
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 67

gloss_en: One does not need a looking-glass in order to look at a bracelet on the wrist.
explanation: One does not need a looking-glass in order to look at a bracelet on the wrist. The nearest approach to this that I have met with is in the Karpiramanjar?, page 17, line 2:—“atgar इस्थकंकणं किं दप्पणेण Gaeta,” which is, in Sanskrit, ^ अथवा हस्तकंकणं करं द््पणेन द्यते. 68
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 67

gloss_en: If aman has lost a cowrie he does not spend a karshapana in the search for it.
explanation: If aman has lost a cowrie he does not spend a karshapana in the search for it. That would be like expending a sovereign in order to recover a farthing! It occurs in the following portion of S’abara’s bhashya 4. 3. 39:—“ न हि ears नष्टे तदन्वेषणार्थां gfeden । न हि काकिन्यां नष्टायां तदन्वेषणं काषीपणेन क्रियते" ॥ न हि कचिद्श्रवणमन्यत्र श्रुतं निवारयितुसुत्सहते ॥ A thing's not being heard in one place does not get rid of the fact of its being heard elsewhere. This is applied by Sankara in Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 4.1 (page 681) as follows:—“ag केषुचित्परदेशेषु न भ्राणानासुत्पत्तिः श्रूयत इत्युक्तं तदयुक्तं अरदेशान्तरेषु श्रवणात्‌ । न हि क्चिदृश्रवणमन्यत्र ad निवारयितुसुष्सहते.” It is quoted as a nyaya in Vaiydsikanydyamala 2. 3. 2 (sitra 8). Compare with it S'‘lokavartika iv. 161:—“a क्रचि- स्संकराभावात्सतरैव निवतेते । afer संकरं दष्टा संकरोऽन्यत्र कटप्यते, and see Dr. Ganganath Jha’s rendering of that and the previous verse. Compare too a saying of Vacaspatimis'ra’s on Yogabhdsya ii, 22:—“q हि रूपमन्धेन न द्यत इति चक्ुष्मतापि दर्यमानमभावग्रापं भवति.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 68

gloss_en: An Iguana creeping along does not on that account become a snake, This is found in Mahabhdsya 1.
explanation: An Iguana creeping along does not on that account become a snake, This is found in Mahabhdsya 1. 1. 23 (4) as follows:— “qT खल्वप्यन्यस््करृतमनुवतैनादन्यद्धवति न हि गोधा स्॑न्ती सर्पणादहिभैवति । "यदप्युच्यते न खल्वन्यस्मकरृतमनुवर्तनादन्यद्धवति न हि गोधा सप॑न्ती सर्पणादहिभ॑वति मवेद्रव्येष्वेतदेवं स्यात्‌ । शब्दस्तु ag येन येन विरोषेणा- भिसंबध्यते तस्य तस्य विरोषको wala.” The same passage reappears in 1. 3. 12 (5), and in 6. 1. 50. 69
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 68

gloss_en: A man who is already ina village does not express a longing to get there, such as a man might who was out in the jungle! This is found in, Brikad@ranyakabhasya 1.
explanation: A man who is already ina village does not express a longing to get there, such as a man might who was out in the jungle! This is found in, Brikad@ranyakabhasya 1. 3. 28, page 87:— “a हि प्राणात्मन्युत्पन्नात्माभिमानस तस्प्र्यादंसनं सम्भवति | न हि म्रामस्थः कदा भ्रामं प्रा्ुयामिव्यरणस्थ gant | असंनिङृष्टविषये aad न तत्स्वात्मनि सम्भवति.” न हि fagat faga इति कथ्यते ॥ A man who has three sons could not be designated as a man with two sons. Therefore, if an order were given to bring the father of two sons, it would not do to bring one who had three! This nyaya of Raghunathavarman’s, taken from the Kas'ikavriiti ( with the substitution of कथ्यते for व्यपदिङ्यते ), is based on the words “a हि ya आनीयतामित्युक्ते fga आनीयते? which form part of Patanjali’s comment on Panini’s sitra 6. 4. 96 (छदेर्वेऽब्यपसर्गस्य). The meaning of that siitra is that when the affix च 6 i. ९ the kritaffix अ ) follows, the root (technically described as छादि ) is shortened to gz when there are not. two upasargas. So we say yxgq, and दृन्तच्छद्‌ ; but ससुपच्छाद्‌ because there are two upasargas. But would the root vowei be shortened if there were more than two upasargas ? No; for we say समुपाभिच्छाद्‌. Yet the siitra seems to make no provision for this. Patanjali comments on it as follows :— ““अद्विम्रशव्युपसर्गस्येति वक्तव्यम्‌ । इहापि यथा स्यात्‌ | ससुपाभिच्छाद्‌ इति । तत्त्दिं वक्तव्यम्‌ । न वक्तव्यम्‌ । यत्र त्रिप्रश्तयः सन्ति द्वावपि aa स्तस्त- , mae सिद्धम्‌ | न वा एष रोके सम्प्रययः | न हि eye आनीयता- fat fags आनीयते । तस्मादद्धिभ्रश्युपसरगस्येति वक्तव्यम्‌” ॥ Patanjali was fond of this kind of illustration. The following one is employed again and again in various parts of the bhasya, and always in connection with the paribhdsa ननिवयुक्तमन्यसदशाधिकरणे तथा aime: । Tt reads 118 :--“अत्राह्मणमानयेल्युक्त 70 बाह्मणसददा आनीयते नासौ रोष्टमानीय कृती भवति.” It occurs in 3. 1. 12 ( ण्ठा, 4); 8. 3. 19; 6. 1. 45; 6. 1. 71 (vart. 7); 6. 1. 135 (vart. 12); 6. 8. 34 ( णव, 7); 7. 1. 37 (ण्डा 7). The words अब्राह्यणमानय appear also in 2.2.6. The paribhasa just quoted is rendered by Prof. Kielhorn thus :—*( An expression ) formed by the addition of the negative azz or (of the particle of comparison ) gq ( to some word or other ) denotes something which 8 is different from and yet similar to (what is denoted by the latter), because it is so that (such expressions) are (generally ) understood (in ordinary life).” With the paribhasa, Nagoji has also taken the illustration. In Kaiyata’s comment ona portion of the bhasya under sitra 1, 1. 57 we find the expression “a हि Groat द्विषुत्रन्यपदेदो waa.” न हि नारिकेरद्वीपवासिनोऽप्रसिद्धगोश्रवणात्कङुदा- दिमदर्थरतिपत्ति्भवति ॥ From hearing the unfamiliar word ‘cow’, an inhabitant of Cocoanut Island would gain no perception of an object possessed of a hump &c. This sentence is found in the Nydyavartika on stra 3.1. 67. Again, in Pras‘astapada’s Vais'esika- : bhasya, page 182, we read :--“अनुमानविषयेऽपि नारिकेरद्वीपवासिनः साखामात्रददौनात्को नु खल्वयं राणी स्यादित्यनध्यवसायो भवति. The commentator, S'ridhara, remarks on #5 :--“नारिकिर्द्रीपे गवामभावात्तत्र्ो Sreisafeataraa:.”” With some hesitation I have admitted the above into my collection of ‘inferences from familiar instances, on account of the reference to the unfamiliar Cocoanut Island, and its cow-less condition! The following passage from Professor Beal’s translation of Hiuen Thsang’s work ( Vol. ii. page 252) is suppossed to refer to this island:—“The people of this island are small of stature, about three feet high ; their bodies are those of men, but they have the beaks of birds; they grow no grain, but live only 71 on cocoanuts.” The editor and translator of Itsing’s Record thinks that the island in question is one described by that pilgrim as lying to the north of Sumatra, and therefore probably one of the Nicobar group. See page xxxviii of that work. Jayanta Bhatta refers more than once to the ignorance of these islanders. See Vydyamanjart page 118, line 5; page 121, li
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 69

gloss_en: Not even toa man whose sight is defective does a lotus on the ground appear to be in the sky! It appears in Citsukhi i.
explanation: Not even toa man whose sight is defective does a lotus on the ground appear to be in the sky! It appears in Citsukhi i. 17 (Pandit, vol. iv, page 594) :-—“ न च देशान्तरे सतवादन्रासत्वाचच ख्यातिवबाधयोरन्यथाप्युपपत्तिः । अन्यत्र सत्ताया इह प्रतीयहेतुस्वात्‌ । न हि मूमावम्मोरहं सदिति दु्टाक्षस्यापि नभसि तद्वभासते. 1 Ae! न हि यहेवदत्तस्य युध्यमानस्य स्थानमवगतं तदेव ुज्ञानस्यापि भवति ॥ The position occupied by Devadatta when eating is not that which he occupies when fighting! The force of this dristanta is best seen from the darstantika in Tantravartika, p. 575 — «“एकत्वेऽपि हि शब्दस्य शक्तिभेदः प्रयोजने । तत्र कार्यान्तरस्थानं न स्यात्कायौन्तेरेष्वपि ॥ न शरब्देकत्वेन यक्किञ्चित्का्ये स्थानं coach तदेवान्यत्रापीति शक्यते वक्तम्‌ । न हि यद्ेवदत्तस्य युध्यमानस्य स्थानमवगतं तदेव भुज्जानस्यापि भवतीति गम्यते | कायैप्रयुक्ता हि स्थानविोषादयो न ETAT” ॥ 10 74 विधिशतेनापि प्रवर्तते न हि विधिशतेनापि तथा पुरुषः gaat यथा खोभेन ॥ Not even ¢ hundred injunctions could move aman to action as readily as the desire for gain does. This is found in Twntravartika 3. 4. 34 ( page 999 ):— “स्वर्यो दक्षिणां दद्यादिति दानं बिधीयते । रोभादेवाजैनाङ्गत्वास्प्ाठसतेषां TATE: ॥ न हि दद्यादिलयस्य प्रतिगृह्णीयादिलययमर्थो भवति | भवेदपि सामर्यां्यदि. तदा- क्षेपमन्तरेण दानविधिर्नोपपद्ते | स तु रोभभ्रा्षप्रतिग्रहवछेनोपप्यमानो न शक्तोलयाक्षेठुम्‌ | न हि बिधिकशतेनापि तथा पुरुषः प्रवर्तते यथा रोभेन' ॥ Jayanta Bhatta (on page 361) puts the matter quaintly thus:— “ चपेटापरिहाराय मोदकम्राक्चयेऽपि वा । nada agalat जहुधीति नियोगतः” ॥ Compare Nydyakanika, page 407:—“a च विधिमाच्रमपि प्रवृत्तिहेतुः | अनिच्छतो विधीनां रातेनाप्यम्नवृतेः ” ॥ See, too, verses 1040 and 1041 of Sambandhavartika, and Anubhitiprakas'a xiii. 277. But many centuries before any of these worthies the same _ View had been expressed by Patanjali in Mahabhdsya 1. 3 72:— क्रियाफलं कतौरमभिप्रेति । याजका यजन्ति गा रष्स्यामह इति । कर्मकराः कृर्वन्ति पादिकमहरुष्स्यामह इति. See another quotation from the same source under the nyaya ^ प्रयोजनमनुदिदय We.” Ss (9 a न हि aa: स्वै जानाति ॥ Everybody does not know everything. Near the end of the Laukikanydyasangraha Raghunatha describes his effort in the following verse :— “aed न्यायदन्दं निजमतिमनतिक्रम्य यावन्मयाष्ं पारं प्रासं तु शक्तो न हि भवति गुरुः कः पुनमौदशो ना । सर्वैः सर्वै न वेत्ति प्रथितमिदमतो नासि मेऽत्रापराधः रिष्टा ेयाः स्वयं वे सुविमरुमतिभिः सम्यगाराधितारयैः" ॥ He then connects various nyayas with the padas of the 75 above; namely, यावत्तैलं तावद्ाख्यानम्‌ and यावत्छ्ाता तावस्पुण्यम्‌ with the first pada, and, the maxim which we are now considering, with the third, I think the second of these should read arazata. In the larger work the reading is यावत्ल्ञातं. Our present nyfya is found in Upamitibhavaprapancad Katha, page 501, as follows:—“ fag: प्राह नैवात्र कोपः कार्यस्त्वया यतः | सर्वः सर्वै न जानीते सिद्धमेतज्गच्रये ?2॥ On the other hand, we have the following query in Atmatattvaviveka, page 94:—* तथापि घ्रानुभवकल्पनायां सर्वः सर्वदा सवै जानाति न तु निश्चिनोतीति क्रं न स्यात्‌." न हि खतोऽसती शक्तिः कलुंमन्येन शक्यते ॥ If a power is not of itself present [ in a person or thing], it cannot be supplied by another. In Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 1. 24 we have part of a discussion as to Brahman’s ability to produce the world without extraneous aid. It is urged that he has this power in himself, just as the power to produce curds resides in milk. The objection is then raised that since curds are not produced without the action of heat, milk is not independent of other agencies, and so the argument breaks down. The reply is as follows:—“ यदि स्वयं दधिमावशीखता न स्याजनैवौप्ण्यादिनापि बराहधिभावमापद्येत । न हि वायुराकाशो वौष्ण्यादिना व्रलाहधिभावमापयते | साधनसामग्या च तस्य पूणता संपाद्यते.” On this last clause Anandagiri says “साधनेति | न हि स्वतोऽसती शक्तेः करतु- मन्येन शक्यत इति न्यायाद्वि्मानैव क्षीरादिशक्तिः साधनसामग्योपचीयत इत्यर्थः.” The nyaya is taken from S‘lokavdrtika ii. 47 (page 59), of which verse it forms the second line. The first line is स्वतः सर्वप्रमाणान
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 73

gloss_en: A blind man is not qualified for work involving the examination of butter.
explanation: A blind man is not qualified for work involving the examination of butter. This is found in Vaiydsikanyayamala 3. 4. 2(sitras 18-20), where it is followed by “नापि पङ्गोविष्णुक्रमाद्यु- पेते कर्मण्यधिकारः.” It was doubtless taken from Tantravartika .1 4. 24 (page 332), where we read :- नन्वनेनैव न्यायेन यथा दाक्रया- दिति वाक्यदोषादन्धादीनामाज्यावेक्षणादिरदहितः कर्माधिकारः स्यात्‌. This sutra 24 is Jaimini’s sitra 30, under which we have S’abara’s “ आख्यातशब्दानामर्थं बुवतां शाक्तिः सहकारिणी `? which see above. न ह्यन्यस्य वितथभावेऽन्यस्य वैतथ्यं भवितुमर्हति ॥ The falseness of one person does not prove somebody else to be false. This is from S/abara 1. 1. 2 (page 5, line 6 ):—“sgसामान्यतोदृष्टं पौरूपेयं वचनं वितथसुपलभ्य वचनसाम्यादिदमपि वितथमवगम्यते । न अन्यत्वात्‌ | न ह्यन्यस्य वितथभावेऽन्यस्य वैतथ्यं मवितुमहैति । अन्यस्वादेव | न हि देवदत्तस्य इ्यामत्वे यक्तदन्तस्यापि इयामत्वं भवितुम्हतिः॥ See the same in verse in Slokavartika, page 100; on which Parthasarathi says:—“a द्यनाक्चवाक्यसख वैतथ्ये सलयाक्षवाक्यस्यापि वै- तथ्यम्‌.” That Kumirila, however, had a poor opinion of human veracity in general, is shown from the following verse on page 88:—“adar चापि पुरूषाः प्रायेणानृतवादिनः | यथाद्यत्वे a विखं- मस्तथातीतार्थकीर्तने.”' Again, on page 178: न च पुंवचनं aa स- ्यत्वेनावगम्यते | atte श्रूयते यस्मासप्रायादनृतवादिनी.” Compare with this nyaya “न हि खदिरगोचरे wat vert दर धीमावो wala,” and “अन्यवेदमस्थिताद्धूमान्न वेदमान्तरमभ्निमत्‌.*” न ह्येष स्थाणोरपराधो यदेनमन्धो न पदयति ॥ It is not the fault of the post that a blind man cannot see it. Vacaspatimis’ra quotes this on page 87 of his Tatparyatikd, prefacing it with the words “ FAST.” It will be found on page 112 of the second volume of the Virukta. We meet with it again in the opening sentence of Kusuwmdnjolt १. :-“ नन्वीश्वरे sania सत्यां सर्वमेतदेवं स्यात्तदेव तु न पश्याम इति चेत्‌ । न aa स्थाणोरपराधो यदेनमन्धो न प्यति.” 77
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 76

gloss_en: Particular [or distinguishing ] knowledge does not arise until that which particularizes [or defines, the object in question ] has been grasped.
explanation: Particular [or distinguishing ] knowledge does not arise until that which particularizes [or defines, the object in question ] has been grasped. Itis in this form that the nyaya is found in Kusumdnjali iii 21 ( page 527), but in Tantravartika, page 258 it appears in the contracted form ^ नागृही- तविरोषणेतिन्यायेन,? and, on page 287, as “ अगृहीतविशेषणा विशिष्टबु- fet दृष्टा.” In Madhava’s Nydyamdlavisiara 3. 1. 6, it is quoted as ^ नागृहीतविदोषणा fRregfe:,’? and in Saptapadarthi, page 2, line 6, as ^ नागृहीतविशेषणा विशेष्यबुद्धिः."” In his commentary on Téarkikaraksé, Mallinatha twice (namely, on pages 47 and 107) cites the maxim in the contracted form adopted by Kumarila, whilst Raghunathavarman’s version of it is “ नान्नातविदेषणा विदि्टबुद्धिरविशेष्यं संक्रामति." In Nydyasitravritti ii. 126 (=2. 2. 58) it appears as नागृहयीतविरोषणान्याय, and Dr. Ballantyne renders it, “Cognition which does not apprehend the distinction, cannot infer [the nature of] what is to be distinguished.” The nyaya occurs five times in Nydyamanjari, and each time in a different form! The references are as follows:—page 320, line 19; 483, line 4 from bottom; 449, line 3 from bottom; 588, line 6; and 543, line 7. In Sir Monier Williams’ Sanskrit Dictionary विरिष्टवुद्धि is defined as “differenced or distinguishing knowledge (९ g. the knowledge of ‘a man carrying a staff’ which distinguishes him from an ordinary man)”; whilst Molesworth explains विरिष्टक्ञान as “knowledge of an object distinguished or characterized by something ( whether a property or an accident) standing out in some speciality (inherent or attached ).” Identical in meaning with the above, though differing somewhat in form, is S’abara’s ५ न्‌ दयप्रतीते विशेषणे विरिष्टं के चन भ्रत्येतु- मह॑न्ति' (1. 8. 33, page 82). : 78
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 77

gloss_en: That which itself is not an accomplished fact cannot be an instrument [with which to bring about some other result}.
explanation: That which itself is not an accomplished fact cannot be an instrument [with which to bring about some other result}. In the Mimansaparibhasa, page 31, this nyaya is quoted in the discussion on the expression ^जच्चिहोत्रे जुहोति". The passage is as follows:—“q च होमस्य प्रल्ययवाच्यायासथंभावनायां करणत्वात्तन्ना- washer तृतीया स्याज्योतिष्टोमेनेतिवदिति वाच्यं द्वितीयाया एव लक्षणया करणा्थकत्वात्‌ । नासाधितं करणमिति न्यायेनासाधितस्य करणत्वा- योगात्‌.” ॥ एणः a rendering of this, see page xxxi of Prof. Ganganatha Jha’s Introduction to his translation of Slokavartika, Krishna Yajvan perhaps took the nyaya from the Jaiminiyanyayamalavistara, where, in connection with the same subject, under the तस्रख्यन्यायः we find the following karika :— ५ नासाधिते हि धात्वर्थे करणत्वं ततोऽस्य सा । साध्यतां वक्ति संस्कारो wares: क्रियात्वतः'? ॥ atari कर्णमूखकर्पणन्यायः ॥ The figure of pulling the root of the ear with the tip of one’s nose! It occurs in Sures'vara's Brihadaranyakavartika 4. 3, 1184, as follows -- “ समस्तव्यस्ततामेवं सति व्याचक्षतेऽत्र ये । कर्षन्ति नासिकाम्रेण args सुखेन ते" ॥ On which Anandagiri remarks:—‘aeapfariazat आन्तस्वं दृष्टान्तेनाह कर्षन्तीति. It may be compared with the acrobat’s sitting on his own shoulders, and with the edge of a sword’s cutting itself. 79 निरामयस्य किमायुर्वेदविदा ॥ | What need has a healthy man of one skilled in the science of medicine? “They that are whole need not a physician, but they that are sick.” In the Prabandhacintémani ( pages 106-7 ) there is a story about the poet Bana and his brother-inlaw the poet Mayira, who were favourites of the King Bhoja. The former, owing to the curse pronounced by his sister (the wife of the latter), was attacked by leprosy ; but being healed by the intervention of the Sun, he appeared at court—to quote from Mr. Tawney’s translation—* with his body anointed with golden sandal-wood, and clothed in a magnificent white garment. When the king saw the healthy condition of his body, Mayira represented that it was all due to the favour of the Sun-god. Then Bana pierced him in a vital spot with an arrow-like speech, ‘If the propitiating of a god is an easy matter then do you also display some wonderful performance in this line.” Mayira replied as follows :— ५ निरामयस्य किमायुर्वेदविदा तथापि तव वचः सव्यापयितुं निजपादौ पाणी च gat Bert स्वया षष्टे काव्ये qi: परितोषितोऽहं लु पूर्वस्य काव्यस्य पष्टेऽक्षरे भवानीं परितोषयामीति प्रतिश्वुय सुखासनमासीनश्वण्डिकाप्रासादपश्चाद्धागे निविष्टो “मा मांकषीविंभ्रमम्‌" इति षषठऽक्षरे प्रयक्षीकृतचण्डिकाभ्रसा- दास्रलयम्रप्रथमानवपुःपह्छवः Bags च तव्परसाद्मारोक्याभिसुखागतेरैपति- भरसुखराजकोकैः कृतजयजयारवो महता महेन पुरं प्राविक्षत्‌.” As pointed out by Mr. Tawney in a footnote, the poem here ascribed to Mayira is the Candis’ataka of which Bana was the author. It was published in the Kavyamala for 1887, and the first verse commences thus :— “at भांक्षीरविभ्रमं श्ररधर विधुरता केयमासयास्यरागं पाणे प्राण्येव नायं कलयसि कर्हश्रद्धया किं त्रिद्यूटम्‌ * | 80 ` निषादस्थपतिन्यायः ॥ The topic of a king who is a कत्ल by caste. This forms the subject of Jaimini 6. 1. 51, 52 and is thus expounded by Kunte (in Saddars‘anacintanika, page 1788):—“The term Nishddasthapati occurs in the Veda. The question is—how is it to be interpreted? Is the term Nishddasthapati a Karmadharaya compound or Tatpurusa compound? The Karmadharaya overrules the Tatpurusa; because, in the latter, a case not directly seen has to be understood, and because metonymy is thus involved. A direct statement is always to be preferred to metonymy. The Karmadharaya makes a direct statement and therefore does not involve metonymy. He who is a Nisada is a Sthapati; and therefore a superior Nisada is entitled to perform the Raudra sacrifice.” In other words, the compound when dissolved is not निषादानां स्थपतिः but निषाद्‌ एव स्थपतिः. In explaining the term बह्मलोक in S‘ankara’s bhisya on Vedantasitra 1. 3. 15, Vacaspatimis'ra says (On page 213; line 3 from bottom ):—“aq तावन्निषादस्थपतिन्यायेन षष्टीसमा
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 78

gloss_en: The simile of a bird let loose from its cage.
explanation: The simile of a bird let loose from its cage. Used to illustrate the upward flight of the soul when released from the body. It occurs in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 443, as follows :—“ ऊर्ध्वगमन जीवस्य स्वभावः । देहे स्थितिस्तस्प्रतिबन्धककर्माधी- नेति मतमाभरिलयोक्तम्‌ | केचित्तु चिरकारुशषरीरावस्थितग्रयुक्तबन्धसुक्तावृ्वगमनं मन्यन्ते । ते खल्वेवमाडुः | बन्धसुक्तसयोध्वैगमनं दष्टं यथा ways यथा वा वारिनिर्भिन्नपरिणतेरण्डबीजस्य यथा वा दढपंकटिक्तजरनिमजनम्रक्षीणपंकरेपञ्युष्कारावूफरस्य.? It will be noticed that there are three illustrations in the last clause—the third being really the awegtaerrara, which see above.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 81

gloss_en: Sir M.
explanation: Sir M. Monier-Williams defines it thus:—* The performance of one ritual act for all objects in orderly succession before performing another act for all objects in the same order.” When it is not a matter of sacrificial ritual, but merely of the bestowal of gifts, the definition given by Captain Molesworth is more suitable; namely—* The order or method ( when a set or number of things is to be given to each individual of a multitude ) of giving first one article of the set to each person all round; going round again with another article; and again with a third ; and thus, until all the articles composing the set shall have been received by each person.” The nyaya sums marizes the teaching of Jaimini 5, 2, 1, 2. See काण्डानुसमयन्याय, and Karkacarya on Katyayana’s S’rautasitra i. 114, aca बहिर्मनः ॥ As to eaternals mind is dependent on others. This oft-quoted nyaya would seem to have originated with Mandanamis’ra. It is found in the following verse of his Vidhiviveka, page 114:— j 11 82 “देत्वभावे फलाभावाव्प्रमाणे सति न अमा । चश्ुराचुक्तविषयं परतनं बहिर्मनः ” ॥ Students of the Sarvadars'anasangraha will remember that the second line is quoted ( without mention of its source ) in the first chapter of that work, and Prof. Cowell translated it thus—‘The eye &. have their objects as described, but mind externally is dependent on the others.” The dependence of manas is pointed out in three other passages of Vidhiviveka, on pages 120, 161, and 178. The nyaya is found in Citsukht i. 12 (The Pandit, vol. iv, page 513) in the words “qa च मनसो wee: सम्बन्धः wat aaa इति =a.” It is employed also by the author of Vidydsdgart on Khandanakhandakhddya, page 307 :—“ रजतज्ञानयैन्द्रियकल्ववादी वक्तव्यः किंतन्मानससुत बहिरश्चजम्‌ | नाद्यो मनसा बदिरर्थैऽस्वातञ्यादेतच्च तत््वविवेके सर्वज्ञदूषणप्रस्तावे मण्डनमिश्रेरभिदितम्‌.' He then cites the verse “हेत्वभावे We.” Is this Tattvaviveka identical with Mandana’s Vidhiviveka, or is it the name given to a section of it?
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 81

gloss_en: This is the first line of Kuswmdnjali iii.
explanation: This is the first line of Kuswmdnjali iii. 8, the second being ५ जैकतापि विरुद्धानासुक्तिमात्रविरोधतः ° ॥ The following is Professor Cowell’s translation of the verse and of a portion of Haridasa’s explanatory comment:—‘In the case of contradictories, there can be no middle course ; nor can you assume the two contradictories to be identical, because the fact of their contradiction is directly asserted.” ‘ ‘There can be no middle course, ‰ ¢. you cannot make some third supposition different from either, from the very fact that they are contradictories [and therefore the one or the other must be true]; nor can you assume them to be identical.” Raghunatha’s explanation of the first line, in his larger work, coincides with this. He says:— “a TERS ताभ्यामन्यस्य Tee स्थितिः किन्तु agra” ॥ 88 The nyaya is quoted in the vritti on Tattvamuktaikalapa iii. 48, and in the last sentence of Khandanakhandakhddya iii ( page 561 ); and the whole verse in the commentary on Udayana’s Laksandvali, page 47, and in the second chapter of Sarvadarsanasangraha. In Tattvadipana, page 234, line 13, it appears in a somewhat modified form, namely, “परस्परप्रति- स्पर्धिनोरन्यतरनिषेधेऽन्यतरव्यवस्थेति न्यायात्‌.” पर्जन्यवत्‌ ॥ Like the rain [ which falls on all places alike ]. It is found in the following passage of Mahabhdsya 1. 2. 9 and 6. 1. 127:— “कृतकारि खल्वपि area पर्जन्यवत्‌ | तद्यथा । पर्जन्यो यावदूनं पूर्ण च सर्व॑- armada?! This is quoted by Nagojibhatta under (कपो) cxi—qgeqaganat:,” which Professor Kielhorn translates thus :—“ The rules of grammar are like the rain [in this that they are] applied [both where they produce a change and where they do not; just as the rain falls upon that which already is full of water as well as upon that which is empty |.” There is another example of this in the Panini chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha:—* अ्रकृत्यादिविभागकल्पनावस्सु werg सा- मान्यविरोषरूपाणां लक्षणानां पर्जन्यवत्सक्ृदेव NT बहूनां शब्दानामनु- शासनोपकंभाच.” “And again, since general and special rules apply at once to many examples, when these are divided into ` the artificial parts called roots &c., (just as one cloud rains over many spots of ground ), in this way we can’easily comprehend an exposition of many words.” The translation is Prof. Cowell’s. We meet with it again in a most interesting passage of Brahmasitrabhagya 2. 8. 42, where Sankara tells us that just as the rain causes the production of barley, rice, shrubs, &c., by its action on the seeds, so God, making use of men’s previous works, impels them to either good or evil! There can be little doubt that the mischievous saying, “eat आणि करविता gaz आहे? current among the Marathis, was derived from this source, 84 On page 226 of Merutunga’s work the following verse is quoted as from Kamandakiyanitiséra, It is not to be found, however, in the printed edition of that work, but stands as verse 161 of Hitopades'a i:—“qurq इव भूतानामाधारः एूथिवीपतिः। विकलेऽपि हि पर्जन्ये जीव्यते न तु भूपतौ 2 ॥ “The king is the main. stay of creatures, like the rain-cloud. For even if the raincloud be somewhat wanting, it is possible to live, but not if the king is wanting in any respect” (Tawney, page 188 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 82

gloss_en: The simile of [ the spoon ] made of the Parna wood.
explanation: The simile of [ the spoon ] made of the Parna wood. Various spoons are used in the sacrifices, as described in the footnote to Professor Eggeling's translation of S’atapatha Brahmana 1, 8, 1.1; and, of these, the juha is always made of the wood of the Parna (7%. ९. the Palas’a ) tree. This is in accordance with Tatttiriya Samhita 8. 5, 7, where the praises of that tree are sung, and blessings promised in connection with the use of the juwhi made of its wood, as pointed out in S’abara on Jaimini 3. 6, 1-8, The qufmefrea of the yg employed in the sacrifices is therefore used to illustrate something invariably present, in contradistinction to that which is so occasionally, as in the case of the godohana. For a passage containing both of these, see under गोदोहनन्याय. Other examples will be found in Bhamati 8, 8. 61; Parimala, pages 624 to 626; and S’alika page 157.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 84

gloss_en: Seeing resemblance to an elephant in a heap of straw.
explanation: Seeing resemblance to an elephant in a heap of straw. An illustration of mere semblance of resemblance ( q@regararaat ). A real likeness is said to be that which stands the test of a close examination. The figure is contained in Kumirila’s S’lokavartika, page 446. 85 Cam त्वसद्ादेव कल्पयित्वोपजायते | साददयग्रययस्तत्र तदाभासत्वकल्पना ॥ ४० ॥ ; बाधकम्रल्ययाचेषा साददयाभासता मता | यथा TSR TERT कुञ्जरादिना ॥ ४१॥ समीपस्थोऽपि जानाति rest नेति तत्र हि न वाध्यते समीपादौ यत्तु साददयमेव तत्‌” ॥ ॥४२ ॥ Prof. Ganganath Jha renders the passage thus:— “40. In a case where a notion of similarity is brought about by means of objects that are not really similar, we have only a (false ) semblance of similarity. 41—42. This is said to be a false semblance of similarity, because it is subsequently set aside by an idea to the contrary, ९. g. the similarity of an elephant in a stack of hay; in which case when one is sufficiently near the stack, he realizes that there is no real similarity between the stack and the elephant. That notion of similarity which is not set aside even on close proximity to the object, is a case of real similarity,” Compare the दूरस्थवनस्पतिन्याय. पर्यस्यद्रौ SST न पुनः पादयोरधः ॥ Thow seest the fire burning on the mountain, but not that which is under thy very feet! “Why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?” The above is the first line of Hemacandra’s Parisistaparvan i. 382, the second line being “ यत्परं रिक्षयस्येवं न खं शिक्षयसि स्वयम्‌. The next verse continues the rebuke:—* का हि युगणना तेषां येऽन्यशिक्षाविचक्षणाः । ये स्वं शिक्षयितुं दक्षास्तेषां पुगणना नृणाम्‌.” The teaching here is akin to that of the भूरिङ्गल्यायः namely “ Practise what you preach,” 86
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 84

gloss_en: Poisoned wounds will not heal without the use of the lancet.
explanation: Poisoned wounds will not heal without the use of the lancet. This is found in Jaiminiyanyayamaldvistara 3. 8. 4, as follows :-—“a हि दुःखरूपं तपो विना दुःखप्रदं पापं नश्यति । यथा रोके पाटनमन्तरेण विषव्रणानां नोपशां तिः” ॥ This is, perhaps, the source of Raghun&tha’s qo शिदामयिषोः राखम्रहणन्यायः which he makes use of thus :—“fgrat दुःखमयसंसारपरिदत्तिभ्नान्तानां जीवानां कंचित्कालं विक्षेपविगमससुह्छसदात्मानन्दाजुभवेन विश्रान्ति संपादयितुकामस्य तस्य संहा- राय तमोग्रहणस्य दुश्चिकरित्यत्रणपीडितानां चरणं freak: जखग्रहणन्यायेन ‘Marre भिषजो रूणर्हिसां प्रयुञ्जतः । न कंचिदपि नषण्यं घृणेवात्र प्रयोजिका" ॥ इति शिवपुराणे व्याख्यातेन निरतिशयकरुणाम्बुधित्वरिङ्गात्‌” ॥ 770 under. stand this somewhat involved sentence, it is necessary to bear in mind that संपादयितुकामस्य refers to 8४8, whose benevolent intentions towards humanity are the subject of the immediate context. Contrast with the above the following saying of Sures’vara’s (in Brihaddranyakavartika 4. 3.176 ), “a दाहज्वरgeaften स्याद्धिषक््क्रिया.” पाठटक्रमन्यायः ॥ The law of teatual sequence. It forms the subject of Jaimini 5, 1. 47, According to the Mimarsa, there are six kinds of sequence ; namely, (1)S’rutikrama, or ‘direct sequence,’ which is treated of in the first siitra of this chapter. It is known as the श्रुतिबरीयस्स्वन्यायः and is regarded as the strongest of the six. Then (2) Arthakrama, or ‘logical sequence, which is discussed in siitra 2, and in which the sequence is determined by the sense rather than by the order of the text. For example, take the sentence “अद्चिहोत्रे जहोत्योदनं पचति,” where the boiling of the rice must necessarily precede the offering of the agnihotra, although the former is mentioned first. Again when we read “जाते वरं ददाति, जातमज्जलिना गृह्णाति, जातमभि- 87 प्राणिति, the logical sequence, as S’abara shows, is in the reverse order of the textual. Then comes (8) Pathakrama, as above, which S‘abara illustrates by the following Vedie passage:-— “समिधो यजति तनूनपातं यजति इडो यजति वबर्हियैजति स्वाहाकारं यजति इति.” To quote Kunte:—These are what are called the five Prayaja oblations; and the sequence in which these texts occur shows the order in which they are to be offered. This is the application of what is called Pathakrama, or textual sequence.” Under the राजयपुरप्रवेदान्याय in the Second Handful, will be found a passage from Mahabhdsya 1. 1. 58 (vart. 1) which furnishes a good example of the supersession of this kind of sequence by अर्थक्रम. The next is (4) Pravrittikrama, which forms the subject of siitras 8-12. Kunte calls it ‘practical sequence.’ “In a series of acts, to be performed upon a series of objects, a beginning is made from some one object; then, in performing all the acts, the same object is acted upon first. This sequence is called Pravrittikrama, which signifies sequence determined by the conduct of the individual.” It is known as {16 प्रावर्तिकक्रमन्याय. The remaining two varieties are the स्थानक्रम and सुख्यक्रम. Kunte’s notes on this portion are well worth a 16616066; and much help, too, is to be had from Dr. Thibaut’s translation of the Arthasangraha, where (on pages 11-15) the six forms of krama are explained under प्रयोगविधि. The use of these technical terms is not confined to the Mimanisakas, but is very common amongst writers on Vedanta also. Four of the above varieties of /rama are mentioned in Bhadmati 1. 1. 1, pages 47-49, beginning with the words “oy भूदशचिहोत्रयवागूपाकवदार्थः क्रमः &९.. and enlarged upon in Vedantakalpataru, pages 32-34; then, in Bhamati 4. 3, 6, we read “पाटक्रमादरथकमो aware यथार्थक्रमं पच्यन्ते सूत्राणि,” and -so ` stitra 12 is next explained, and afterwards sitra 7. For the pathakrama only, see Anandagiri on Brahmasitrabhasya 2, 8. 15 (page 620), \ 88 पुरस्तादपवादा अनन्तरान्विधीन्बाधन्ते नोत्तरान्‌ ॥ ` This grammatical nyaya is paribhdsé नड in Nagojibhatta’s treatise, and is taken from Mahabhasya 6. 1. 89 (under vartika 2). Professor Kielhorn translates as follows:—“Apavddas that precede [ the rules whi
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 86

gloss_en: This is another of Raghunatha’s samples of grammatical rules and is closely connected with पूवं द्यपवादाः &c.
explanation: This is another of Raghunatha’s samples of grammatical rules and is closely connected with पूवं द्यपवादाः &c., to which it forms an alternative. Dr. Kielhorn’s translation of it, in the Paribhasendus'ekhara, is as follows:—“Or (we may say that ) first all forms which fall under the Apavdda are set aside, and that subsequently the general rule is employed (in the formation of the remaining forms).’ It is found in Mahabhdsya 2, 4. 85 (vart. 11); 8. 1. 3. (vart, 10 ); 3, 2. 124 (vart. 10); 6. 1. 5. (vart, 2); 6. 1. 161 ; and 6. 1. 186. In each case it is im. mediately preceded by the paribhas& पूर्व द्यपवादाः &c., the latter standing alone only in 4, 1, 89 ( vart. 2.), 89
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 88

gloss_en: The base and the suffia jointly convey the meaning which is understood’ [from the word].
explanation: The base and the suffia jointly convey the meaning which is understood’ [from the word]. This is found in Patanjali 8. 1. 67, vartika 2, and the rendering is that which was given me (in 1903) by my learned friend Dr. Kielhorn, who also explained that year is equivalent to प्रतीयमानार्थ. This seems to be the only reasonable explanation of the term. The nyaya is used in the above form by S‘abara on Jaimini 3. 4. 18. (page 320 ), and 10. 8. 24 ( page 677 ), and appears also in Tantravartika 3. 1. 12 (page 686). It is quoted, however, with a slight addition in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 4, line 14, where it becomes “श्रक्रृतिप्र्ययौ nea सह qa: प्राधान्येनेति न्यायेन”, and (in Indian Thought for January 1907, page 51) it is rendered by Dr. Thibaut, “According to the principle ‘that the root and the affix of a yerb conjointly signify principally what the affix denotes’,’"—a rendering which differs materially from that given above. In this altered form it is found again in the Ramanuja section of Sarvadars'anasangraha, where Mr. Gough gives as its English equivalent “The base and the suffix convey the meaning conjointly, and of these the meaning of the suffix takes the lead.” This would seem to be based on an expression of Madhava’s_ . in Nydyamalavistara 3. 4, 8, namely, ध्र्काकिप्रययोौ TAA सह बूतस्तयोस्तु प्रत्ययः भराधान्येनेति शाब्दिकैरुद्ोषणात्‌.” To say that whilst the root and the suflix unitedly convey the meaning, the latter is the more important factor, is a very different thing from asserting that the two parts together convey the meaning of one of them! Jayanta Bhatta, on page 403 of the Nydyamanjar%, says truly, “Etre परस्परपेश्षम्थमभिदधाते न च ape प्रययार्थोऽभिधीयते नियोगस्य धातुवाच्यत्वान्न च प्रलयेन प्रकृलर्थोऽभिधीयते यागादेः fee वाच्यत्वानुपपत्तेः | न च तौ परथक्णथक्‌ स्वकार्यं कुरुतः” ॥ One more example of the nyaya may be adduced from Tantravartike 2. 1, 1 ( page 348 ), with Prof. Gangainath Jha’s 12 90 translation :—“qrat तु ada प्रत्ययार्थो भावनेति व्यवहारः । तत्रायमभिप्रायः। भरत्ययाथ सह ब्रूतः प्रकृतिप्रलयौ सदा । , भ्राधान्याद्धावना तेन प्रययार्थोऽवधार्येते” ॥ “In the Mimamsa S‘astra, however, the Bhavana is always held to be signified by the affix. The sense of this theory may be thus briefly explained. The sense of the affix is always expressed by the root and affix taken together; and as the Bhavana is the most important factor in this joint signification, it is held to be signified by the affix.” The word भावना means ‘a creative energy, or ‘productive energy’, or ‘tendency to realize something’. So Dr. Thibaut.
