Inthe Arthasangraha (page 2) it is definedas “भवितुमैवनानु- कूलो भावयितुव्यीपारविरोषः'?, “the particular activity of some productive agent (bhdvayitri) which tends to bring about the existence of something which is going to be (bhavitri); which is capable of future existence” ( Trans p. 3). It is further declared to be twofold, as शाब्दी and र्थी. For the meaning of these, see, specially, Professor Cowell’s translation of the Jaimini section of Sarvadars'anasangraha, page 182.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 89

gloss_en: The rule as to the subatitution [ of one material for another, in a sacrifice ].
explanation: The rule as to the subatitution [ of one material for another, in a sacrifice ]. This subject is dealt with in Jaimini 6, 3. 1817, the five sitras being styled “ननिलयकर्मणोऽनिलयग्रारन्धकर्मणश्च मरति- निधिना aarcanterna.’? Other aspects of प्रतिनिधान are discussed in all the subsequent siitras of the pada. Kunte’s summary of the teaching of this pada is well worth reading. The nyaya occurs in the following passage of S’ankara’s bhasya on Brahmasitra 3. 3. 40, and I append Dr. Thibaut’s ध्ण88 0 ;--“मोजनकोपेऽप्यद्धिवौन्येन वा दरव्येणाविर्द्धेन प्रतिनिधान91 न्यायेन भ्राणाच्िहोत्रस्यानुष्टानमिि.? “Even in the case of the omis. sion of eating, the agnihotra offered to the Prinas has to be performed by means of water or some other not altogether unsuitable material, according to the Mimarsa principle that in the absence of the prescribed material some other suitable material may be substituted.” It must not, however, be supposed that the choice of the “suitable material” was left to the sacrificer; the substitute was as rigidly prescribed as that for which it might be substituted. or instance, the juice of the Piitika plant was the only allowable substitute for that of the Soma plant, and Niviara for rice, Ramanuja mentions both in S'ribhasya, page 508, and Patanjali speaks of one, in his discussion of axe, in Mahabhasya 1. 1. 56 (vart.13). The passages from the two authors stand thus:—“@raram च पूतीकग्रहणं श्रुतिचोदितं सोमावयवसद्धावादिति न्यायविदो विदुः । व्रीद्यभावे च नीवारग्रहणं बीहिभावतः” ॥ CR) ५ वेदेऽपि सोमस्य स्थाने पूतीकतृणान्यभिषुणुयादिव्युच्यते” ॥ ( ? ) प्रति प्रधानं गुण आवर्तनीयः ॥ A subordinate act is to be repeated im the case of each principle thing. The nyaya in this form is found in Jaiminiyanydyamalavistara 3. 1. 7,and in the Mimamsdparibhasa, page 36, in connection with what is termed the अहेकत्वन्याय which is based on the Vedic injunction “दृक्ञापवित्रेण अहं aané.” Madhava says:—‘oefafa द्वितीयया अहस्योदे्यतया भ्रयो- जनवत्तया च प्राधान्यं गम्यते । ae प्रति गुणः संमागैः । प्रतिप्रधानं च गुण आवर्तनीय इति न्यायेन यावन्तो ger: सन्ति ते समार्जनीयाः? ॥ In Jaiminiyasitravritt the nyaya is quoted as ^प्रतिग्रधानमङ्गावृत्तिः?. Prof. Ganganatha’s rendering (on page xxx iiiof the Introduction to his translation of S'lokavartika ) is, “with regard to each Primary, the Secondary is to be repeated”. See the म्रहेकत्वन्याय in connection with this, 92
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 90

gloss_en: When there is sense-perception, what need is there of inference? So Raghunatha; but I have nowhere met with the saying in this form.
explanation: When there is sense-perception, what need is there of inference? So Raghunatha; but I have nowhere met with the saying in this form. It is a well-known principle however. Kumarila says in Tantravirtika, page 87 :--“वेदवाक्यानुमानं हि तावदेव रवतते । तदरथविषयं यावस्मत्यक्षं नोपलभ्यते ॥ seer श्रूयमाणे तु न विदयेतानुमानिकम्‌ । न हि हस्तिनि दृष्टेऽपि तत्पदेनानुमिष्यते'” ॥ Amalananda, too, in Veddntakalpataru, page 368:—“ प्रत्यक्षे च यागविधावानुमानिकविधिकव्पनाऽनुपपन्नाः. In S‘abara 3. 1. 12 (page 216) we read प्रल्यक्षमनुमानाद्लीयः, but Patanjali points out (in Mahabhasya 3. 2.124) that this is not always the case. He says:—* भवति वे प्रयक्षादप्यनुमानबली- यस्स्वम्‌ | तद्यथा | अलातचक्रं प्रत्यक्षं दर्यतेऽनुमानाच्च गम्यते नतदस्तीति. There are two references to this in Nyayamanjari. On page 461 (line 7 from bottom ) we read:—“gqafy च नैष नियमः भरयक्षा- जुमानयोर्विरोधे saad बलीय इति त्वरिततरपरिश्रमितचक्रीभवदलातग्राहिणः प्रलक्षस्यानुमानबाधितत्वदशनादिति &e.”. Andon page 609 (line 6 from bottom ):—“‘agq aga नियम एवैष प्रलक्षानुमानयोर्विरोधे प्रयक्षमेव adia इति तदेदानुमानस्य बाधकसुचितं नानुमानान्तरमिति | तदसत्‌ । AVATAR परलयक्चषमप्यनुमानेनानन्यथासिद्धेन बाध्यत एव | ननु भरमणविरतो परिमितपरिमाणो्मुकम्राहि प्रलक्षमेव तत्र waa बाधकं नानुमानमिति । मैवम्‌ | अनवरतपरिभ्रमणससुद्धतचक्रावभाससमय एवानुमानेन तद्धान्ततानि- श्चयाव्‌ `? ॥ For अरातचक्र see also Vydyasiitra 3. 2. 59; 21111८7, page 378, line 15; S’alika, page 36 ; and Vakyapadiya,i. 131. . With the quotation from Kumarila compare the following from Nydyakanikaé page 268, and Tatparyatika, page 27:— ५न हि प्रयक्षेण करिणि दृष्टेऽपि चीत्कारेण तमनुमिमते प्रेक्षावन्तः?” ॥ Sankara 1118719 cites this in his comment on 172258८7 3. 2, 10. 98
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 92

gloss_en: The simile of sherbet.
explanation: The simile of sherbet. Used to illustrate the production of some new thing by the union of others, just as sherbet is the result of the commingling of various ingredients. It appears in Sahityadarpana, 46, as follows:—* प्रतीयमानः प्रथमं प्रयेकं हेतरुच्यते | ततः संवलितः सर्वो विभावादिः सचेतसाम्‌ । प्रपानकरसन्यायाचर्व्यमाणे रसो भवेत्‌ ॥ यथा खण्डमरिचादीनां सम्मेखनादपूर्व इव कश्चिदास्वादः प्पानकरसे सज्जायते विभावादिसम्मेनादिहापि तथेदयर्थः ”. This is meant to show how Flavour is single, though spoken of as resulting from a composition of causes. Dr, Ballantyne translates thus:—‘“First each reason is mentioned separately as being percei ved; and [then we say ] let all this commingled—the Excitants and the rest—constitute, like the [composite] Flavour of sherbet, the flavour tasted by the intelligent. As from the commingling of sugar, pepper &c., a certain unprecedented relish is produced in the shape of the flavour of the sherbet, so is it here also, from the commingling of the Excitants &c., such is the meaning.” The same illustration is found in Nydyamanjart (page 872) with पानक instead of प्रपानक. “ पदार्थभ्योऽन्य एव वाक्यार्थः पानकादिवत्‌ | यथा पानकं दक॑रानागकेशरमरिचादिभ्योऽथौन्तरमेव यथा च सिन्दूरहरितार्लाक्षादिभ्योऽथौन्तरमेव चित्रं ...तथा पदेभ्यो वाक्यं पदाथभ्यो वाक्यार्थः? ॥ ^180 Tatparyatikd, page 219, line 26,
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 93

gloss_en: Who can resist a stream [of argument] flowing [steadily on ] because established by proof? This seems to be the sense of the ny&ya as quoted at the beginning of the Arhata section of Sarvadars'anasangraha.
explanation: Who can resist a stream [of argument] flowing [steadily on ] because established by proof? This seems to be the sense of the ny&ya as quoted at the beginning of the Arhata section of Sarvadars'anasangraha. The passage is as follows :—* अथ मन्येथाः प्रमाणवचादायातः प्रवाहः केन वार्यत इति न्यायेन यत्सत्ततक्षणिकभिलयादिना प्रमाणेन शक्षणिकतायाः भ्रमिततया &९. Professor Cowell renders it thus:—* But the opponent may maintain ‘The un94 broken stream (of momentary sensations) has been fairly proved by argument, so who can prevent it? In this way, since our tenet has been demonstrated by the argument, whatever is, is momentary &.’” Ina footnote to page 62 of his translation of this portion of Sarvadars‘anasangraha in Le Bouddhisme @apres les sources brahmaniques, Professor L. de la Vallée Poussin has recorded Professor Leumann’s comment on the above rendering which he considers inaccurate in respect of the nyaya. The criticism is just,—but, unfortunately, the printer has made a mess of the rendering which the critic proposes to substitute for Mr. Cowell’s. प्रमाणवन्त्यदृष्टानि He सुबहन्यपि ॥ Unseen influences [ springing from actions, and eventually producing certain effects ], however numerous, may be assumed [as the causes of those effects ], af of established credibility. This nyiya is the first line of a verse in Tantravartika 2. 1, 5, where the important dogma of the existence of apurva is discussed. The second line is “अदृषटडातभागोऽपि न कल्प्यो द्यप्रमा- णकः”? ॥ ‘The whole verse is quoted in Sures’vara’s Brihad- ~ Granyakavartika, page 1124, and again on page 1797; whilst the first line is found in Tatparyatika, page 487, as follows :— (aq चानेकादृष्टकटपनाभयान्सुख्यार्थपरिव्यागो न्याय्यः प्रमाणसिद्धे Para चयोगाजुपपत्तेः | ag: | श्रतसिख्यथैमश्चतोपरन्धो यलवता भवितव्यं न त॒ श्ुतदोथिल्यमाद्रणीयमिति । तथा प्रमाणवन्यदृष्टानि कट्प्यानि सुबहून्यपि.” There is another example in Citsukhi i. 23 ( Pandit, vol. v, page 27 ):--“ एतेनोभयपदलक्षणास्वीकारे गौरवदोषो निरस्तो वेदितव्यः । बुुत्सिताथप्रतिपादनभ्रयोजनतया गोरवस्यैवोचितःत्वात््रमाणवन्त्यद्टानि कट्प्या- नि सुबहून्यपीति न्यायात्‌.” A third will be found in Khandana, page 74, on which the commentator says, “qares प्रमाणं प्रवतेते तददष्टमपि...ामाणिकेरम्युपगम्यते. See, too, Tattvadipana, page 416, and Bhamati, page 463. 98 Raghunathavarman gives the verse in a different form. According to him, the first line is “ बालाग्रदतभागोऽपि न कल्प्यो निप््रमाणकः, ”? whilst “ प्रमाणवन्ति &९. 18 the second. In the numbered part of his larger work it is called the arermararaisit nyaya; but, towards the end of the volume, he quotes ( without acknowledgment ) the above-cited passage of Citsukhi ( together with a good deal of the context) which contains the nyaya in its proper form. On page 514 of his translation of the Tantravartika, Prof. Ganganatha Jha renders the whole verse thus :—* It is a rule, in all cases, that a large number of unseen agencies may always be assumed, when all of them are justified by some authority ; while even the hundredth part of an unseen agency should not be assumed, if there is no authority for it.” It may be well to call attention here to this transcendental power adrista, or apirva, invented by the philosophers in order to account for present things without divine intervention. In his article on Mimanuisa ( Hssays, vol. i, page 343 ), Colebrooke says:—“ The subject which most engages attention throughout the Minanisd, recurring at every turn, is the invisible or spiritual operation of an act of merit. The action ceases, yet the consequence does not immediately ensue. A virtue meantime subsists, unseen, but efficacious to connect the consequence with its past and remote cause, and to bring about at a distant period, or in another world, therelative effect. That unseen virtue is termed apirva, being ४ relation superinduced, not before possessed.” Goldstucker ( 8. v. अपूव ) quotes Kumarila to the effect that Mimamsakas apply that term ewclusively to the unseen influence which follow
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 93

gloss_en: Even a stupid person does not adopt a course of action without a motive.
explanation: Even a stupid person does not adopt a course of action without a motive. This oft-quoted line is found in the S'lokavartika, (page 653) in connection with an argument regarding _ a Creator of the universe. The need of a motive for action is pointed out on page 4 also of the same work. The following ` passage from Vydyamanjari, page 191, is a reminiscence of Kumiarila’s argument that if the Creator acted without a motive his intelligence would be at fault:—“f& किमपि भ्रयोजनमजुसन्धाय saat waa प्रजापतिरेव वा । निष्मयोजनायां अदृत्ताव्रे्षापूर्वकारित्वादुन्मत्ततुल्योऽसौ भवेत्‌ ` ॥ 0" page 339 of the Nydyakanika Vacaspati 11878 says:—“Seraat हि safe: प्रयोजनवत्ताव्याप्ता भ्रयोजनवत्ताभावे न भवति दिदापावच्वमिव तरुत्वाभाव इयर्थः "` ॥ We find the nyaya quoted in full by Anandagiri in his comment on Brahmasiitrabhdsya 2.2.1. The two passages are the following:—“qfaqu sam शद्र दचेतनं चेतनस्य पुरुषस्यार्थ साधयितुं स्वभावेनैव विचित्रेण विकारात्मना fade इति u” ^“ चेतनसयेति । अर्थशब्दो भोगापवगांथः । अचेतनस्य प्रयोजनपरिक्तानाभावादप्रदत्तिः । भ्रयोजनमनुदिर्य मन्दोऽपि न प्रवतैत इति न्यायादिल्या्चक्याह स्वभावेनेति.” ४ 97 Patanjali (in Mahabhasya 3.1. 26, vart. 14) tells us that with whole classes of people the motive which actuates them is a purely selfish one. He says:— “ag कश्चित्परोऽनुग्रहीतव्य इति sada । स्वै इमे स्वभूते प्रवर्तन्ते । ये तावदेते गुरुशुश्रूषवो नाम तेऽपि स्वभू्य्ेमेव भ्रवतैन्ते पारलोकिकं नो भवि- ष्यतीह च नः श्रीतो गुररध्यापयिष्यतीति । तथा यदेतदासकर्मकरं नामैतेऽपि स्वभूयर्थमेव प्रवतैन्ते भक्तं Fs च रप्यामहे परिमाषाश्च न नो भविष्यन्तीति । तथा य एते शिल्पिनो नाम तेऽपि स्वभूयर्थमेव प्रवर्तन्ते वेतनं च रप्स्यामहे मित्राणि च नो भविष्यन्तीति ” ॥ Compare a passage from the same source under the nyaya “a हि विधितेनापि ke.” Bhartrihari reproduces Patanjalis view in the following verse ( Vakyapadiya iii. page 255 ):— % निमित्तेभ्यः प्रवतैन्ते सर्वं एव सखभूतये | अभिप्रायानुरोधेऽपि स्वार्थस्यैव प्रसिद्धये” ॥ प्रस्तरप्रहरणन्यायः ॥ The rule as to the throwing into the sacrificial fire of a handful of Darbha grass. Kunte says:—“The Prastarapraharananyaya is well known among the Mimanisakas. Prastara isa handful of Darbha-grass ready for use before a sacrifice is begun. It is spread on the sacrificial ground (Vedi) and serves as a seat for the sacrificial vessels. When a sacrifice is finished it is thrown into the sacrificial fire as an offering. A Vaidika text states—‘ The handful of grass is to be thrown into the fire with the Suktavaka,’” The nyaya forms the subject of Jaimini 3. 2. 11-14 whichis otherwise termed “ सृक्तवाकस्य प्रसतरप्रहरणाङ्गताधिकरणम्‌. ”? The question which has to be decided is thus put by the author of the S’dstradipika:— « दुदौपूणैमासयोः श्रूयते सूक्तवाकेन प्रस्तरं प्रहरतीति । तत्र॒ संरायः । किं भ सूक्तवाकस्य च कारार्थः संयेगोऽधवाङ्गाङ्गिमावः "2 । 98 The decision is that the Siktavaka mantra is subordinate to the. act of offering up the grass, whilst the latter serves the double purpose of a resting-place for the vessels and an offering to the gods. The term प्रतिपत्ति which is found in siitra 14 is thus explains ed by Kunte:—“The rule is that all things connected with a sacrifice are somehow or other to be used in performing the same; nothing is to be thrown away, nothing is to be preserved, The final disposal of sacrificial things for the sake of getting rid of them is called Pratipatti.” Lass)
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 96

gloss_en: See this explained under पाठक्रमन्याय.
explanation: See this explained under पाठक्रमन्याय. ROAST: ॥ The simile of a fruitful mango-tree. Such a tree not only produces luscious fruit, but also affords shade and fragrance for the weary traveller in the hot season. Some of us know from experience how charming a camping-ground a mango-grove is! Raghunatha’s explanation of the nyaya in connection with the worship of Ganes‘a is as follows:—“ एकफलाकांश्षया तदाराधनमन्यata we प्रयच्छतीति विवक्षायां तु फल्वत्सहकारन्यायः | यथा सहकारं STARA रसालोऽसौ सहकारोऽतिखोरभः इत्यभिधानादतिसोरभ आश्नव्रक्षोऽति- मधुरपक्रफल्नमितश्चाखः स्वसुपसन्नाय छायार्थिने जनाय we परिमरुं चाप्रा- तमपि ददाति तथा प्रकृतेऽपीलयर्थः ॥* The quotation here is from Amarakosa, 1१४. 33 ( page 87). The thought expressed in the above seems to be that of Apastambadharmasitra 1. 7. 20. 3. viz. “ar wert निमिते छाया गन्ध इतयनू्पयेते* which see above. 99 बरहि्यायः॥ ` ia The question of Kus'a grass. This is based on the sentence “बर्हिर्देवसदनं afer,’ “I cut grass as a seat for the gods,” which forms the subject of Jaimini 3. 2. 1. 2. The question is whether the word बर्हिस्‌ is to be taken im its primary sense or in a secondary one; and the conclusion 18 ५मुख्यगौणयोसुख्ये कार्यसंप्रययः, which see above in the form aorgeqat: &e.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 98

gloss_en: The mind of even those who are highly educated is distrustful of itself.
explanation: The mind of even those who are highly educated is distrustful of itself. This is the second line of the second verse in S‘akuntalanatak. In Tarkikaraks& (page 208), in an exposition of वाद्‌, we find the following:—“ वादे तु दैवादागताः सदस्या वादिभ्रतिवादिभ्यां संप्रतिपत्या प्रामादिककथाभासज्ेकाव्यावतेनायाङ्गी- क्रियन्ते न जल्पवितण्डयोरिव प्रमेयादिव्यवस्थापनार्थमङ्गत्वेनोपादीयन्ते ॥ On which, Mallinatha comments as follows:—“ वादे विदोषमाह वादे स्विति । दैवादागतानां वा करं प्रयोजनमत आह प्रामादिकेति । बरुबद्पि शिक्षितानामात्मन्यप्रययं चेत इति न्यायादिति ara: ॥ For the benefit of any who may consult the original, I may add that the quotation from the Nydyavartika which immediately follows in Varadaraja’s text, is found on page 161 of that work; and that from Vacaspatimis’ra’s tika, on page 224,
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 99

gloss_en: The simile of a country with several kings [ working in opposition to one another].
explanation: The simile of a country with several kings [ working in opposition to one another]. It occurs in chap. x1x of the Anubhitiprakasa, which deals with the Kena Upanisad. The passage is contained in verses 12 and 18 :— : «८ वाकश्ुःश्रोत्रसुख्यानि प्रेरयेत्करणानि कः । ईश्वरशरत्किमेकोऽसौ बहवोऽमीव्युतेयैताम्‌ ॥ | भ्वर्त्यानामनन्तत्वाद्विलक्षण्याच्च नैकता | Wane बहुत्वे सयाद्रहुराजकदेशवत्‌" ॥ ` 100 The पव is sufficiently explained by the example. Raghunatha links it with the बहुशरकाङृष्टगन्याय which is otherwise unknown to me.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 99

gloss_en: The association of many is good policy.
explanation: The association of many is good policy. Raghunathavarman explains thus:—“ अनुग्रहः साहाय्यम्‌ । न्याय्यो न्यायादनपेतोऽर्थात्तदुपेतः का्यसाधक इति यावत्‌; `” and then quotes Pancatantra i. 331 by way of illustration:—‘agarmranit मेखनं का्यसाधकम्‌। go: संपादयते रजस्तया नागोपि बध्यते ?› ॥ For the many variants of this verse see Indische Spruche 4425, and Peterson’s Subhdshitavali 2742. The maxim is found in Jaiminiyanydyamalavistara 7. 1. 5, and in Nages’a on Kaiyata, page 16. Wea प्रदीपककिकाक्रीडयैव नगरदाहः ॥ The burning of a city just by a child’s playing with the wick of a lamp. “Behold how great a matter a little fire kindleth.” I assume that कलिका means the bud-like charred excresence which often forms on the topof a wick in an open oil-light. The saying 18 found in Kusumdnjali v. 3, page 89:— “ag तस्य सर्वदा सर्वैत्राविशोषे arr सवैदोत्पत्तिप्रसंग इति निरपेक्षश्वरपक्षे दोषः, aaa उपेक्षणीय एवास्त्विति बालस्य प्रदीपकटिकाक्रीडयेव नगरदाहः परन्तु तन्न स्थेमभाजो जगत एवाकारणस्वग्रसङ्कखात्‌."” Udayana seems to use the phrase ironically, but I do not quite grasp the drift of this. We meet with प्रदीपकलिका again in Syddvadamanjart, page 157:—“ar च anaratrerestaaftrat भ्रदीपकलिकावन्नवनवोत्पद्यमाना." बुभुक्षितस्य किं निमन्रणाग्रह उत्कण्ठितस्य किं केकारवश्रावणम्‌ ॥ What need has a hungry man of a pressing invitation [ to eat]; why direct the attention of a longing one to the cry of 101 the peacock? In the Kumarapala chapter of Prabandhacintamani, page 212, we are told that that king having given Hemacandra (a Jain) a pressing invitation to join him in a pilgrimage to the temple of Somanatha (dedicated to 84४8), the hermit replied as follows:—‘gyfwaer किं निमच्रणाग्मह उत्कfare किं केकारवश्रावणमिति रोकरूढेस्तपस्िनामधिकृततीथौधिकाराणां को नाम नृपतेरत्र निर्बन्धः `` ॥ “What need is there to show much zeal about inviting one who is hungry? Why make one who is longing, listen to the ery of the peacock? So runs the popular proverb, and, in accordance with it, I ask, why does your Majesty exert yourself to press hermits, whose very profession is the visiting of sacred places?” This is Mr. Tawney’s rendering, on page 130 of his translation. Indian writers often tell us that the cries of the peacock intensify the longings of separated lovers! For example, Raghwarhs'a xiii 27:— “fara केका; शिखिनां बभूवुयैरिमिन्नसह्यानि विना त्वया मे. ब्राह्मणय्ामन्यायः || The figure of a village in which Brahmans abound. This is in Raghunatha’s list, but hardly deserves a place amongst nyiiyas. In Veddéntakalpataruparimala, page 188, a distinction is drawn between the expression प्रचुरब्राह्मणो ara: and ब्राह्मणप्रचुरो भामः, the meaning, in the former case, being a village in which Brahmans are more numerous than in some other village, and, in the latter, a village in which the Brahmans outnumber the other castes. The passage is as follows:— “ng धर्मिविशेषणव्वेन निर्देश एव व्यधिकरणसजातीयाल्पत्वस्य निरू- पकस्वं दृष्टम्‌ । यथा प्रचुरत्राह्यणो राम इत्र प्राचुयैस्य आमान्तरगतव्राद्यणाद्प्वं निरूपकं दृष्टमिति । तस्य स्वधर्भिविरोष्यत्वे तु समानाधिकरणविजातीयाव्पस्वमेव निरूपकमत एव ब्राह्यणप्रचुरो भाम इति प्रयोगे तद्भामगत्चुद्रार्पस्वापेक्ष प्राच्य प्रतीयते.” See also Tantravartika, p. 1066 line 2. Akin to this is the मद्यम्रामन्याय. 102 Hera ॥ The illustration afforded by 21701. We learn from the commentary on Sanksepas'Griraka 1. 14 that Bharchu was a Brahman and highly esteemed by the king of his country. This, however, brought him into disfavour with the jealous hangers-on at the royal court ; and, to get rid of him, they one day blind-folded him and carried him off to a forest; at the same time telling the king that he was dead and had become a goblin! After a long time he returned to the town in company with some foresters, but was prevented by his enemies from entering it. On one occasion, when in a pleasure-ground outside the city, the king actually saw him; but, in consequence of the false statement that had been made, he supposed it to be his ghost! Eventually the wh
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 100

gloss_en: Offering clarified butter on ashes [ instead of on the sacri.
explanation: Offering clarified butter on ashes [ instead of on the sacri. ficial fire]. An illustration of wasted, or misdirected, effort. Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, page 240:—“ अकार्यवारणोदयुक्तो मूढे यः परिखिद्यते । वाग्विस्तरो am तस्य भस्मन्याञ्याइतिथैथा ॥ नोपदेशशतेनापि मूढोऽकायौन्निव््यते । शीतांशु्रसनात्केन राइवाक्यैर्निवारितः ॥ There is another instance in Hemacandra’s Paris'istaparvan. i, 58“ दध्यौ चेवं स राजधिरहो तेषां ऊमन्रिणाम्‌ । सन्मानो यो मयाकारि स भस्मनि इतं ध्रवम्‌.” The nyaya was doubtless derived from Chhandogya Upani+ shad 5, 24. 1:—‘@ य इदमविद्वानचिहोत्रं जहोति यथाङ्गारानपोद्य भस्मनि जहुयात्तादक्तस्यात्‌.* It is found also in Naiskarmyasiddhi 1. 16:— ६ % अन्तरेण विधिं मोहाः यौत्सास्परायिकम्‌ | ॥ न तत्स्यादुपकाराय भस्मनीव हुतं तरिः” ॥ भाण्डानुसारि सरेहवत्‌ ॥ The simile of [ a remnant of ] oi adhering to [ the sides of ] a vessel [ out of which oil has been poured]. In the bhasya on Brahmasitra 3. 1. 8, there is a lengthy and important dis. cussion as to whether, on returning to earth, in some new birth, after a residence in the moon, a man brings with him a remnant of the works which took him there,—this remnant being techni 104 cally styled anus‘aya. S'ankara affirms that he does, and says :-- “a: पुनरनुद्ायो नामेति | केचित्तावदाहुः । स्व्गा्थस्य कर्मेणो सुक्तफरस्यावदोषः कश्चिदनुशयो नाम भाण्डानुसारिनेहवत्‌ | यथा हि aad रिच्यमानं न सवौत्मना रिच्यते भाण्डानुसायव कश्चित्लेहरोषोऽवतिष्ठते तथानुशायोऽपीति"' ॥ The question is then asked, why does he not remain in that blissful region until the whole of his merit is exhausted? The answer is, that, just as a servant who has long served in a king’s household, finds his wardrobe at last reduced to the slender proportions of a pair of shoes and an umbrella, and is therefore unfit to continue in that exalted position, so, too, a man is unworthy of a residence in the moon who has only a small balance of merit remaining to his credit! Here is this unique reply in S‘ankara’s own words:—“ ननु निरवदोषकर्मफरो- पमोगाय चन्द्रमण्डलमारूढाः | बाढम्‌ । तथापि खल्पकमौवदोषमात्रेण तत्रावस्थातुं न भ्यते | यथा करल कश्चित्सेवकः सकः सेवोपकरणे राजकुलमुपख्षश्चिरप्रवासात्यरिक्षीणवहू पकरणद्छत्रपादुकादिमात्रावरोषो न॒ राजङ्कटेऽवस्थातुं शक्रोति | एवमनुदायमात्रपरिग्रहो न चन्द्रमण्डलेऽवस्थातं शक्रोतीति,” This is a portion of the system which is regarded as the highest flight of the Indian mind, and to which some restless folk in Europe and America are betaking themselves, in order to find rest for their souls! There are not a few in India to-day, however, who have found that rest by turning from these gropings in the dark, to the midday light afforded by an accredited revelation. To quote a modern writer:—‘The painful, toilsome, searching of the creature into things too high for it, only ends in perplexity and bitter disappointment.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 103

gloss_en: The simile of the design on a jar.
explanation: The simile of the design on a jar. Used, apparently, of something variable at will. It is found in Khandanckhandakhadya (page 289 of Medical Hall Press edition) in the following passage :— ४५ अथान्यत्रास्तु यद्वा agi करणं प्रमाबिवक्षितजातिविरोषव्यपदेशकं शरमाणम्‌ | 105 ava: खदिवमाः प्रयक्षादिग्रमितयो fragheartaas: । न च प्रमाता wat वा तद्धेदहेतः । प्रमाणानि तु यथायथं चतसृष्वसाधारणानीति भिन्नबुद्धि- व्यपदेहञानिवन्धनानीति | मेवम्‌ | विवक्षितपदं तावक्षणे भाण्डाठेख्यमिव पुरुपेच्छानामनियतविषयस्वात्‌? ॥ The commentator S’ankara Mis‘ra explains the nyaya thus:— “भाण्डाटेख्यमिवेति | यथाछेख्यं रेखोपरेखादि सर्वभाण्डसाधारणं न भाण्डवि- Rawat तथा पुरूषाधीन बिवक्षापि न विरोषिकेलय्थः' ॥ In the edition of S’ri-Harsa’s work, however, now in course of publication (together with the commentary Vidydsdgari ) in the Chaukhamba Sanskrit Series (page 528), the form of the simile differs, and a different explanation of it is given:— ‘Sates । भण्डो विप्ररुम्भकस्तस्याछेख्यं wetsery । देवदत्त जाया किंमपलयं जनयिष्यतीति पृष्टे पुत्रो न पुत्रीति विप्रखम्भकस ऊुढ्योपयौटेख्यं न बिषयविरोषनियतं निषेधस्योभयथा योजयितुं शक्यत्वात्तथा बिवश्ितपदमपि युरुषेच्छानामनियतत्वेन चतुष्टयजातीयस्य नियमेन प्रव्येतुमरक्यत्वात्रस्यचिचतखः श्रमितयो विवक्षिताः कस्यचित्पञ्चेति"" ॥ The simile is found in Tatparyattka ( page 496, last line ) ४8 भाण्डालेख्य.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 104

gloss_en: It is declared (by Mimamsakas] that an accomplished [ sacrificial ] wct is [ not an end in itself, but] for the bringing about of a result in the futwre [such as the attainment of Heaven &c.
explanation: It is declared (by Mimamsakas] that an accomplished [ sacrificial ] wct is [ not an end in itself, but] for the bringing about of a result in the futwre [such as the attainment of Heaven &c.]. And this, they say, necessitates belief in an Apirva as a connecting link between the two. See the arguments for and against this theory, in Professor Ganganatha Jha’s translation of Tantravdrtika 2.1.5. Most of us will be thankful for the aid of so able an interpreter of the teaching of that terribly difficult book ! The nyaya is found, in slightly different form, in the 14 106 Sanksepas'ariraka i. 143; and, in its usual form, in the commentary. I quote only the second half of the verse :— “ad भव्यग्रधानं भवति हि न पुनः erat कदाचि- च्छाखस्थाः शाब्दशाक्तिस्थितिनिपुणधियो विस्तरादेवमाुः" ॥ “aa भव्यायोपदिद्यत इति जेमिनिरशावरस्वामिनोर्मतं तो च वेदार्थनि्णयेऽधि- कृताविति तदुक्तमेव अहीतुखुचितमित्यभिग्रलयाह । भूतमिति । Meret | भव्य साध्यं प्रधानं यस भव्यशेषमिय्थेः"' ॥ In verse 312 of the same chapter we meet with it again, and also in the altered form in which a Vedantist would apply it to his system :— ५ भव्याय भूतमिति किंच विधिप्रधाने काण्डे नयोऽयमिह तद्विपरीतमाहः | भूताय भव्यमिति भूतपरं हि सवै वेदावसानमिति सूत्रकृदाचचक्चे"" ॥ “ भव्याय भूतमिति । विधिः प्रधानं प्रतिपिपाद्यिषितो यस्मिन्‌ काण्डे तस्मिन्‌ । इह ब्रह्मकाण्डे | तद्विपरीतं भूतभव्यन्यायविपरीतम्‌ | तद्वेपरीलयमेवाह | भूताय भव्यमिति । हिर्हेतौ । यतः भूतपरं सत्यसिद्धव्रह्मपरं वेदावसानं वेदान्तं सूत्रकृदा- चचक्षे समन्वयसूत्रप्रसुखैः सूतररुक्तवानतो भाप्यकारादय एवमाइरिलय्थः” ॥ The above extracts will be found in The Pandit, vol. iv, page 583; and vol. v. page 473. The nyaya occurs again in i. 395 ( Pandit, vol. vi. p. 167) in a context dealing with bhdvand, that topic so dear to the Mimamsaka, for a right understanding of which nothing could be better than the perusal of Dr. Thibaut’s translation of the Arthasangraha, pp. 3-5. Verse 482 of the same chapter contains a fourth example. It appears also in Zattvadipana, page 377, line 7 from bottom, and page 427, line 9 from bottom ; also in the Mimamsanydyaprakas'a, page 16, section 36. 107
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 105

gloss_en: The simile of the man who [in order to become proficient ] makes drawings of a war-chariot on the ground.
explanation: The simile of the man who [in order to become proficient ] makes drawings of a war-chariot on the ground. This nyaya is found in S‘abara’s bhashya on Jaimini 7. 2. 15, and again (in conjunction with gea@ftrqr ) in 9. 2.13. The latter passage is as follows :—* यत्तावदुपाध्यायः रिष्यसन्निधावधीते तद्भहणार्थम्‌ | यच्छिष्यस्तद्धारणार्थम्‌ | ्रहणधारणे प्रयोगार्थं भूमिरथिकवत्‌ खुष्केष्टिवद्वा । तद्यथा मू- मिरथिको भूमौ carver शिक्षां करोति dora प्रा्भावो भवितेति यथा च छात्रः शुष्केष्टीः प्रयुक्ते प्रयोगे प्रा्ुकमा भवितासीति एवमेतद्रषटव्यम्‌” ॥ In his Laukikanydyaratnakara (page 186 b of India office MS. 582) Raghunathavarman applies these two nyayas and the राकुनिग्राहकगतिन्याय as follows:—“qaataraet च रिष्याणामभ्यासदार्व्वेनासं भावनादिससुच्छेदाय न तु परदेषेणेति विवक्षायां भूमिरथिकन्यायः शुष्केषटिन्यायः शकुनिमराहकगतिन्यायश्च sada ” ॥ His explanations of the three are taken from Jaiminiyanydyamdlavistara 9.1. 6 and 9. 2. 2.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 107

gloss_en: The law that [something does not take place] when the occasion [for its taking place] has once gone.
explanation: The law that [something does not take place] when the occasion [for its taking place] has once gone. This is Prof. Kielhorn’s rendering of the nyaya as it occurs under Nagojibhatta’s Paribhdsa@ lxiv. The sentence is as follows—“ aq एव निर्देशाद्धष्टावसरन्यायस्याच्र शादे नाश्रयणम्‌ । ध्वनितं चेदमिको गुण इति सूत्रे भाष्य इति भाष्यमप्रदीपोहयोते निरूपितम्‌ *॥ The reference is to the Uddyota on 1. 1. 3, and the nyaya will be found in vol. i. pages 185, 186, 190. There is an instance of it in Tantravartika 3. 5. 46 (page 1060 ):—* यदपि यजमानस्य अष्टावसरं क्रियमाणे विगुणं भवतीति तदपि गुणलोपे सुख्यस्येत्यनेन विरुद्धम्‌. 2 The nyaya is expressive of a lost opportunity. 108
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 107

gloss_en: The simile of the power of an intowicant.
explanation: The simile of the power of an intowicant. It is employed by S‘ankara in his bhasya on Brahmasitra 3. 3. 53:—“#3 देहमाच्रात्मददिीनो रोकायतिका देहव्यतिरिक्तस्या्मनोऽभावं मन्यमानाः समस्तव्यस्तेषु बाह्येषु एथिव्यादिष्वदृष्टमपि चेतन्यं दारीराकारपरिणतेषु भूतेषु स्यादिति संभावयन्तस्तेभ्यश्चेतन्यं मदशक्तिवद्विज्ञानं चेतन्यविरिष्टः कायः पुरुष इति चाहुः" ॥ Anandagiri comments on the latter part thus :—“ qaf | यथेकैकसमात्ताम्बूलादेरदष्टापि मदशाक्तिः संघाते द्यते तथेदं ज्ञानमेकैकस्मिन्ञदृष्टमपि देहाकारपरिणतमूतेषु संहतेषु भवतीति चाडूरिति योजना. In the Lokayatika section of Saddars'anasamuccaya, karika 94 reads thus:—“ एश्व्यादिभूतसहत्यां तथा देहादिसंभवः। agate: greet agugheraraar.” See, too, S'alika, page 146, line 7 from bottom. The illustration is found also in Nydyamanjart (page 439, line 4 from bottom ):— यत्तु मदशक्तिवदि्युक्तं aa मदशक्तष्टत्वादभ्यु- पगमो न तु WAS तत्र द्दीनम्‌.” मधु wale ae प्रपातं नानुपदयसि ॥ O foolish one! thow scest the honey but dost not see the preci. pice. This is the second line of S’antiparva ccexi. 7. (ecex Bombay edn. ), the first being “ स्वाटुकामुक कामानां वैवृष्ण्यं किन गच्छसि. Anandabodhacarya quotes it in his Nydyamakaranda (page 77) as “ag पयसि gde प्रपातं किं न पदयसि. The editor of that work was apparently unaware of the existence of the passage in the Moksadharma, for he considered the आभाणक to be based on the following verse of the Devibhdga०८० :—“ag पद्यति मूढात्मा aad नैव परयति । करोति निन्दितं कर्म नरकान्न बिभेति च.” Thanks to the St. Petersburg Lexicon, we can refer to five other passages of the Mahabharat where the same illustration 109 is found. In Vanaparva ccexxxv. 21 (Cale), we read :-- “मधु प्रपश्यन्ति न त॒ प्रपातं यहयूतमारुब्य हरन्ति tery.” In Udyogaparva 1 27 :--“ विषमं नावमन्यन्ते ward Agate: | संयुगं ये गमिष्यन्ति नररूपेण aga.” In १०२८८१०८ 11. 11 :—“ न लब्धो बुध्यते दोषान्मोहाल्लोभात्परवतेते | मधुरिप्सुर्िं नापर्यं प्रपातमहमीदशम्‌.*' Again in 0. 10:—“ धनं धनेश्वरस्येव हृत्वा पार्थस्य मे सुतः । मधुगरप्सुरिवा- बुद्धिः प्रपातं नावबुध्यते. Lastly, in Striparva i 37 :-- “ay यः केवरं egt प्रपातं नानुपर्यति । स wel मधुरोभेन शोचत्येवं यथा भवान्‌.” Compare the following from Sastradipika 3. 6. 3, page 340:-- cg हि मधुन्येव quefegdat शाखामधिरोहति तस्य विनिपात एव भवति । तद्वदिहापि | “ मधुदृ्टिवदेवासख quad प्रपद्यतः । क्रियाफरविनाश्चात्मा विनिपातः प्रसज्यते.” मध्येऽपवादाः पूर्वान्विधीन्वाधन्ते नोत्तरान्‌ ॥ In the Paribhdsendus'ekhara, this [एष्णणह8त stands between पुरस्तादपवादाः &c., and अनन्तरस्य विधिवौ &८. which see above. Professor Kielhorn’s translation of the present one is as follows:—* Apavddas that are surrounded ( by rules which teach operations that have to be superseded by the Apaviada-operations ), supersede only those rules that precede, not those that follow, them.” “The reason for (the validity of) this Paribhasa is this that (an Apavdda, when it has become effective ) by superseding the rule which presents itself first, no longer wants (to supersede something else ).” It appears in Mahabhasya 8. 2. 1 (vart. 6); 4.1. 55 (vart. 4); 4. 1. 114 (vart. 4 and 6); 4. 3. 182 (vart. 6); 4. 3. 156 (vart. 7); 6. 1. 102 (vart. 6); 6. 1. 166; 6. 8, 68 (vart. 5); 6. 4, 148 (vart. 5); and 7. 2. 44 (vart. 4). In no case, however, are the words नोत्तरान्‌ found in the paribhasa. 110
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 108

gloss_en: The simile of a slow-poison.
explanation: The simile of a slow-poison. It forms nyfya 150 of the Pirvabhaga of Laukikanyayaratnakara, and is used in opposition to तीव्रविषन्याय as follows:—“uq हि संद्रायादिनिवरत्तिमेन्दविषन्यायेन सुकरा | ata प्रतिवाद्यापादितं त॒ dees date दुष्परिहरं am । सयोभुक्तं विषं मन्दमन्यथा dq” 1८ occurs in S‘astradipika 1. 3.4 (page 148, line 2 from bottom ):—* इत्थं च संमवति प्रामाण्ये नाप्रामाण्यं युक्तमिति मवति केषांचिदाकांक्षा सापि मन्द्‌- विषन्यायेन निराकतेव्येव्येवम्थमिदमधिकरणम्‌. महतापि प्रयल्ञेन तमिखायां परामृशन्‌ । RTE विवेकं हि न कश्चिदधिगच्छति ॥ Not even by the most thorough examination, could one distinguish between black and white, in intense darkness. The verse is Kumarila’s, and is used by him (in Tantravartika 1. 3. 1) to illustrate the impossibility of tracing the sources from which Manu and other Smriti-authors derived their laws. Just before the s'loka he says :--“ न च afgaraa Hlemgaes मन्वा- दिभिः प्रतिपन्नं किं विधिपरादुताथैवादरूपादिति. ” Then, immediately after the ४९86 :--“ न च मन्वादिवचनाद्वेदमूख्त्वं निश्चिनुमः. This latter clause, as the Nydyasudha points out, is with reference to Manu’s own statement, in chapter ii 7, to the effect that every precept of his was deduced from the Veda. = ६4 ba
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 110

gloss_en: The very same face uhich looks large [when seen] in a large mirror, appears small [ when reflected ] in the pupil of the eye.
explanation: The very same face uhich looks large [when seen] in a large mirror, appears small [ when reflected ] in the pupil of the eye. The illustration is found in Tatparyagika, page 137 line 16 :-“ एकमपि व्यज्ञकभेदादुत्कर्ैवन्निकर्षवदृष्टं यथा महति दर्पणे nega तदेव कनीनिकायामण्विति.” 111
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 110

gloss_en: This very curious simile is found in the commentary on S‘antideva’s Bodhicarydvatara i.
explanation: This very curious simile is found in the commentary on S‘antideva’s Bodhicarydvatara i. 4,the first half of which reads (पऽ :--“ क्षणसंपदियं सुदु रभा प्रतिरब्धा gendaradt.” The tika commences as {0108 --^ अषटाक्षणविनियक्तस्य क्षणस्य संपत्तिः समग्रता | इयं gear । सुष्टु दुःखन wera इति कथच्चि्पराप्या । महा्णवयुगच्छिद्रकूममीवापंणोपमा.” In a footnote, the editor tells us that Professor Kern was unable to get any satisfactory meaning out of the nyaya, but proposed the following:—“ As the entering of the tortoise’s neck into the hole of the yoke formed by the great ocean.” Before him, Burnouf, who was equally puzzled, suggested, “It is as unlikly to happen as if a tortoise should put its neck into a hole opening every yuga in the world’s ocean.” This was all that I knew when writing on it early in 1904. A note, however, contributed to the Journal of the Pali Text Society for 1906-1907, by Mr. Harinath De, M. A, threw considerable light on the subject. He gave extracts from three Pali works in which the simile is more or less directly referred to, and one of them, namely that from the Majjhima Nikaya, is said by him 60 be “the original passage in which the comparison first occurred.” Mr. De did not translate it, but I take the following to be the sense of it. “Ifa man were to throw into the sea a one-holed yoke, and it were tossed to and fro between north and south, and east and west, and if, once in a hundred years, a tortoise, blind of one eye, were to rise to the surface, would it be likely that its neck would enter that yoke?” It was not until after व had made use of this in a note on the simile which I contributed to the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society in October 1909, that I became aware that my friend Mr. F. W. Thomas had really explained it six years previously ! It came about on this wise. In the September number of the Indian Antiquary for 1903, in an article entitled 112 Matriceta and the Maharajakanikalekha, he quoted a verse from a Tibetan work, and gave the following translation :— “When like the neck of a tortoise, entering the hole of a yoke in the ocean, I had obtained the state of man, attended with the great festival of the good religion.” He then added this explanatory remark:—“The reference to the blind tortoise, which rises from the bottom of the ocean once in a hundred years, and by a rare chance happens to insert his neck into a yoke floating on the surface of the ocean, is used to illustrate the extremely rare chance by which a living creature is born asa human being.” After pointing out the recurrence of the simile in two other Buddhist works, he added:—‘I have noted also a fourth recurrence of it in the Tibetan version of a work entitled Subhdsitaratnakarandakakatha, and ascribed to Stra. This reference will now be familiar to M. Lévi, who has himself discovered in Nepal the Sanskrit text of the work... The Sanskrit original here reads,,.as follows :— ata evaha bhagavin manusyam atidurlabham , maharnavayugachhidre kirmagrivarpanopamam ॥ Is it not probable that we have here a saying ascribed to Buddha, which we may hope also to find in the Pali literature? [ I now learn from Prof. Rhys Davids that it does occur in the Majjhima Nikdya: see the edition of Mr, Chalmers iii. page 169 }.” This is the passage of the Nikaya quoted by Mr. Harinatha De, but as he makes no reference to Mr. Thomas’ article I infer that he, too, in 1907, was as unacquainted with it as myself. Orthodox writers, such 88 Kumarila, Vacaspati Mis'ra and others, have not hesitated to make use of illustrations employed by Buddhist writers, and that now under consideration forms no exception. I have recently met with a slightly modified form of it in the Bodhasaéra, a modern Vedantic work by S'ri-Narahari, published, together with a commentary, in the 113 Benares Sanskrit Series in 1906. On page 228 the author quotes a verse from “ Vasistha” ( probably the Yogavdsistha) which J subjoin together with the comment :— “ चलार्णवयुगच्छिद्रकूमैम्
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 111

gloss_en: The illustration of a devotee who was hindered [in the religious life ] by the affection which he had for w courtezun [ when he was a householder].
explanation: The illustration of a devotee who was hindered [in the religious life ] by the affection which he had for w courtezun [ when he was a householder]. Itis said to be the subject of a Gatha, and is cited to show that the past, equally with the present or future, may injuriously influence the seeker after the knowledge which leads to emancipation. It is found in Pancadas't ix, 41;— “ अतीतेनापि महिषीखेहेन प्रतिबन्धतः | fagerd न वेदेति गाथा रोके प्रगीयते ` ॥ We are told in the next verse that the Guru, making use of that self-same incident as an instrument for the conveyance of Brahmajndna, brought about its removal and secured the man’s emancipation ! The commentator puts it thus:—“ गुरुस्तस्य तत्वोपदेष्टा तदीयं afettaeaqaa तस्यामेव महिष्यां तच्वं॑तन्महिष्युपा- धिकं बरह्मोक्तवान्‌ ततः सोऽपि महिषीसेहलक्षणग्रतिबन्धकापगमेन गुरूपदिष्टं तत्वं यथावत्‌ शाखखोक्तप्रकारेणेव ज्ातवानिलर्थः ” ॥ The three verses immediately preceding the above will be found under the हिरण्यनिधिदृष्टान्त. मुनिर्मनुते मूर्खो सुच्यते ॥ A sage meditates [on Brahman ] and a fool is emancipated ! An impossible sequence. Compare Ezekiel xviii. 2:—“The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children’s teeth are set on edge.” The nyaya forms part of an interesting passage on page 37 of Veddniatattvaviveka :— एतेनेदमपास्तं सुनिमनुते मूर्खो सुच्यत इल्येतच्छाख्फरं भ्रयोक्तरीति न्यायविर्टम्‌ ॥ तथा चाभाणकः । काचिन्निषादी तनयं भ्रसूते कश्चिक्निषादस्तु कषायपायीति । सुनिकवैकश्रवणादि- विधिफल्सय साक्षात्कारस्य मूखेऽनभ्युपगमात्‌ । तत्फरस्याप्यविद्यानिवृ्तेमनि- ग्र्च्छात्रगाततया तावन्मात्रतया मूखैनिष्ठत्वाभावात्‌ | इष्टफखानां यथाददौसुपgua.” For the nyaya aera प्रयोक्तरि, see below. 115 य एव करोति स एव Be ॥ He who performs an action will himself reap the fruit thereof [ whether in the form of reward or retribution ]. This doctrine, common to all the orthodox schools, is found in Nyayavartika 3. 1. 4 and is directed against the belief that the body is the soul, and that when the body is cremated, the man, with all his deeds, ceases to exist. This is reasserted in T'atparyatikd, page 403, thus :—“q एव कर्मणः कर्ता स एव तत्फरुस्य भोक्तेति सवैरा- स्तिकपथानुसारिभिरम्युपेयम्‌.*” The emphasis, therefore, here is not so much on the fact that whatsoever a man soweth that shall he also reap, as that whosoever soweth the same shall also reap. This, of course, implies that the reaper will be conscious of the fact that he was the sower, for otherwise the precept would be of no moral value. It is difficult to see how any one can hold with the above, and at the same time be a believer in the doctrine of transmigration, the advocates of which are compelled to admit that the subjects of those repeated births have absolutely no consciousness of previous existences. I am not unmindful of the fact that the followers of the Yoga system [ sittra iii. 16 ] profess to attain to a knowledge of the past and the future by means of संयम ( that is, by धारणा, ध्यान, and समाधि collectively ); but, even if that were so, the number affected would be infinitesimal.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 114

gloss_en: That for which there is no room on a camel's back is tied to his neck! Illustrates the piling up of misfortunes almost beyond endurance.
explanation: That for which there is no room on a camel's back is tied to his neck! Illustrates the piling up of misfortunes almost beyond endurance. Perhaps akin to our “It is the last straw which breaks the camel’s back.” It occurs in Upamitibhavaprapancd Katha, page 394:—* मया चिन्तितम्‌ । अहो हती देवेन वयं मन्दभाग्याः | तदिदमामाणकमायातम्र्‌ | यदुत ASCH BE न माति तत्कण्ठे 116 निबध्यत इति । तथाहि वैश्वानरपापमित्रयोगेणेव ऊमारस्य गादसुदरेजिता वयं यावतेयमपरा कृत्येवास्य भायौ areata.” Kritya is a female deity who is invoked for evil purposes. There is another example on page 895 of the same :— “ महाभारसमाक्रान्तमूर्तेराराटिकारिणः । Rae माति नोष्टस्य Tes तन्निबध्यते ?' ॥ यत्राकृतिस्तत्र गुणाः ॥ Where there is a good outward appewrance, there also are good qualities, This is found in Hemacandra’s Paris'istapurven ऋ. 288 :--“ अस्याकरलनुसारेण गुणानपि हि निश्चिनु । यत्राकृति- स्तत्र गुणा इति लोकेऽपि गीयते.” It is contained also in verse 5076 of Indische Spriiche, but whether as a quotation or not, I can” not 88 :--“ यत्राकृतिस्तत्र गुणा वसंति नैतद्धि सम्यकविभिः प्रणीतम्‌ | केनातिचार्वग्यपि मे हृदिस्था दुनाति गात्रं विरहे भयास.” Professor Béhtlingk took it from Kosegarten’s edition of the Pancatantra (i. 208), but I cannot find it in the Bombay edition. Some work on श्ङ्गाररस is a much more probabie source,
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 115

gloss_en: Say what you wish to say about a thing, but tis real value will be shown by the advantage derived from it.
explanation: Say what you wish to say about a thing, but tis real value will be shown by the advantage derived from it. In the opening part of the Tarkikaraksad the author says:—“ प्रारिप्सितस्य अन्थस्य प्रेक्षावदुपादित्सभ्रयोजिकामभिमतफर्साधनतामभिधाय श्रोतृढुद्धिमनु- दख्यन्वाकि्यमाणमेवा्रे दद्रीयति | निः्रेयसफलं ्राटुर्षां तत्वावधारणम्‌ | म्रमाणादिपदाथौस्ते रक्ष्यन्ते नातिविस्तरम्‌ ॥* On which Mallinatha comments thus :— “ag यदभिधिष्सितं तदभिधीयतां फटे व्यक्तिभैविष्य॑तीति arenes wat वैक््यमाणार्थप्रतिन्ञाडंबरविलम्बैरिलयायदोकाक्षेपमादोक्य समाधत्ते प्रारिष्सितस्येति । ्श्चावतां धीमताञुपादित्सा attra तत्र प्रयोजिकां हेत॒भूतामि य्थः। Raat: प्रयोज नक्ञानाधीनत्वात्तञ््ापनायाभर भ्रति्वा arate भावः ॥ 11 age यदपेक्षं चश्ुस्तद भावग्रहेऽपि तदपेक्षते ॥ That on which the eye depends to perceive an object, it must also depend on to perceive that objects absence, This is Professor Cowell’s rendering of the nyaya in the Aulukya chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 126, Jivainanda’s edn. ):—“ न चारोकाभावस्य घटाद्यभाववद्रूपवदभावत्वेनारोकसापेक्षचक्चु्जम्यज्ञानविषयत्वं स्यादिव्येषितव्यं । यद्रहे यदपेक्षं चश्चुस्तदभावम्रहेऽपि तदपेक्षत इति न्यायेनारोकग्रहे आलोकापेक्षाया अभावेन तद्भावग्रहेऽपि तद्पेक्षाया अभावात्‌.” “And you need not assert that this absence of light must be the object of a cognition produced by the eye in dependence on light, since it is the absence of an object possessing colour [ 4. ९. light possesses colour, and we cannot see a jar’s absence in the dark ], as we see in the case of a jar’s absence; because, by the very rule on which you rely, namely, that on which the eye depends to perceive an object, it must also depend on to perceive that object’s absence, it follows that as there is no dependence of the eye on light to perceive light, it need. not depend thereon to perceive this light’s absence.” Most probably Madhava took this from Udayana’s Kirandvali where it stands (on page 18 ) in a similar context. It occurs also in Laksandvalitiha, page 12. यववराहाधिकरणन्यायः ॥ + In Anandagiri on Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 8. 45 we read :-- ५ यववराहाधिकरणन्यायेन रोकप्रसिद्धिः शाखरीयप्रसिच्ा बाध्येलयाह.* There is no adhikarana of this name in Mimarnsa or Vedanta, but . the reference is doubtless to the शाखप्रसिद्धार्थप्रामाण्याधिकरण, otherwise styled the आर्यम्लेच्छाधिकरण, which comprises Jaimini’s stitras 1, 3. 8 and 9, under which the words यव, वराह) and others having a double meaning, are discussed by the bhasyakara. These two siitras are quoted by S’ankara on Brahmasitra 3. 4. 42, and explained by Anandagiri. The matter is well put 118 in Colebrooke’s Essay on Mimanisa (page 339 ) :--५.4 very curious disquisition occurs in this part of the Mimanisa, on the acceptation of words in correct language and barbaric dialects, and on the use of terms taken from either. Instances alleged are yava, signifying in Sanskrit, barley, but in the barbaric tongue, the plant named priyangu ; varaha in the one a hog, and in the other a cow [or, rather, a crow]; pilu, a certain tree, but among barbarians an elephant; vefasa,a ratten cane and a citron [or, rose-apple, jamtbu]. The Mimamsa concludes, that in such instances of words having two acceptations, that in which it is received by the civilized (Aryas), or which is countenanced by use in sacred books, is to be preferred to the practice of barbarians ( 1176९000 ), who are apt to confound words or their meanings.” The above is of importance to students of Vedanta; for, in addition to the passages named above, it is discussed in Bhamati 3. 3. 52, and enlarged upon in Veddintakalpataru, pages 461, 462. The brief allusion, too, to the same thing, in Bhamati 1. 3. 22, in the words:—“q हि गावो वराहमनुधावन्तीति छष्णाविहङ्गानुधावनसुपपद्यते गवामपि तु तादा सूकरानुधावनम्‌*” is quite unintelligible alone. See too, S’alika page 192; Tatparyatika, page 292; Kusumanjali, vol. 2, pages ° 130, 154; and Nydyamanjari, page 288, line. 26.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 116

gloss_en: When the same fault attaches to both sides of an argument at cannot be urged against one alone.
explanation: When the same fault attaches to both sides of an argument at cannot be urged against one alone. This is Professor Cowell's rendering of the nyfaya in the Panini chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 142, Bib. Ind. and 161 Jivananda’s edn.) It originated, however, with Patanjali, and is found in Mahabhasya 6.1.9 ( vart.2) as “qatraareiat न तमेकश्वो- द्यो भवति. S‘abara ( on siitra 8. 3. 14) quotes it as “ यश्चोभयो- 119 aia नासावेकस्य वाच्यः. In a form differing slightly from these it is quoted in Nydyakanika ( page 225, line 4 from bottom ), and is still further changed in the following verse of Aniruddha’s on Sdnkhyasiitra i. 6 :— “यत्रोभयोः समो दोषः परिहारोऽपि तादः | नेकः पयनुयोक्तव्यस्तादगर्थविचारणे” ॥ For other references to the nyaya, see Tantravartika, page 947 ; Nydyamanjari, page 95, line 10 from bottom ; and (in the poetical form) Tarkabhdsa, page 88. The Khandanakara cites the first two words of the nyaya, on page 531, and ascribes it to Bhatta (Kumarila). ` यस्य नालि gat न तस्य पुत्रस्य कीडनकानि क्रियन्ते ॥ Toys are not made for the son of a man who has no son! This is used by S‘abara to illustrate Jaimini’s sutra 10. 3. 5— ` “a चाङ्गविधिरनङ्गे स्यात्‌.” He says:—“aaay कर्मण्यङ्गस्य विदोषः विधिः स्यात्‌। मवति च विदोषविधिः “आश्ववालः प्रस्तरः इति । न ह्यसति प्रस्तरे भ्रस्तरविशेषः Reta यथा यस्थ नासि gat न तस्य पुत्रस्य क्रीडनकानि क्रियन्ते." Then, in Parthasarathi’s Nydyaratnamala, page 111, we find the expression स्वैमिदमजातपुत्रकरीडनकमापद्यते.” यस्याज्ञानं MAA Vea: सम्यक्‌ च वेत्ति सः ॥ He who has Nescience [as an upddhi] is the subject of delusion ; [ but though ] deluded he is also possessed of sound knowledge. This is affirmed of the individuated Self, who, though a portion ‘as it were’ of the undivided and indivisible Self, is also the आश्रय of Nescience. This is one of the mysteries of the advattavida, For a full description of जीव as found in S’ankaracarya’s famous bhasya, see Notes to my edition of the Veddntasdra, == ` The above nyaya is found in Raghunatha’s two treatises, but his exposition of it is extremely meagre since he ignores the 120 second pada of the line. He says :--“ यस्यानात्मनि देहादावाष्मश्नमो- sf aenarafe कल्प्यते.” The verse of which it forms the first line is quoted in full by Ramatirtha in his discussion of the term adhydropa in Section 6 of Vedantasara. In the Calcutta editions of 1829 and 1886, the reading of the second pada is “ ज्रान्तः सम्यक्ररोति सः+? whilst Prof, A. E. Gough, in his translation published in The Pandit of August 1872, followed the reading “ आजान्तोऽसम्यद्धरोति a:.” In my edition of 1894 (page 104) I adopted the reading “ आन्तः सम्यङ् वेत्ति सः,” but I now see that in so doing I was exemplifying the truth of the first pada! There was manuscript authority for it, however, and 80 Vidydsdgav%, p. 443. In its true form the nyaya is found in the commentary on Sanksepas Griraka iii. 8 (The Pandit, vol viii. page 660), and again in Tattvadipana, page 179 (with the mislection आजान्ति- for area:). The former passage is as follows :— Sag मिभ्यातत्वक्ञानयोर्विषयवेषम्येऽपि TAA AAAS HT: सम्यक्‌ च वेत्ति स इति न्यायाद्ञान्नान्तितत््वबोधानामेकाश्रयत्वनियमादक्तानाश्रयचिन्मा- त्राश्रयत्वमेव आन्त्यादेः स्यात्‌ &e.” If any one can trace Ramatirtha’s verse to its source I shall be glad. The Yogavdsistha is a very likely dkara; but with the weight of seventy summers upon me I am not myself prepared to join in the search. यस्योन्मूकनाय यस्य प्रसक्तिर्भवति ततस्तस्य TATA ॥ He who is bent on destroying another must be stronger than he. This is contained in an extract ( given by Prof. Kielhorn ) from Bhairavamis’ra’s comment on Nagoji Bhatta’s paribhasa exii, namely निषेधाश्च admia: (= प्रतिषेधाश्च बलीयांसो भवन्ति of Patanjali 1. 1. 63, vartika 6), which runs thus:—“ इयं च परि- माषा रोकसिद्धन्यायमूखिकेत्याह | विध्युनमूखनायेति । seer विधेनिंवतेनाये- aa: एतेन येन नाप्राप्तन्यायेन विधिद्याखरस्य Rae बाध इति gai तम्‌ । लोकेऽपि यस्योन्मूखनाय यस्य ्रसक्तिैवति ततस्तस्य बलवत्त्वं कंसा 121 सकृष्णस्येव. T
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 118

gloss_en: The rule as to a night-saerifice.
explanation: The rule as to a night-saerifice. This is the topic of Jaimini 4. 3. 17-19, and is largely used by writers on Vedanta asa type of scripture-passage conveying no direct promise of reward, and therefore dependent on an arthavada-passage for such promise. Though S‘ankara does not mention the nyaya in his bhasya on Brahmasitra 3. 8. 38, it is evident that he has it in view, and Anandagiri, and Amalananda (in the Kalpataru), expressly connect his remarks with it. So also the Kalpatarwparimala, The last-mentioned work quotes it again on page 255 (in connection with Brahmasitra 1. 2. 24):—* तथा सर्वपापग्रदाहोऽपि बरह्मङिङ्गगरश्नोत्तराभ्यां प्राधान्येन प्रतिपिपादयिषिततयोपकरान्तस्योपासनस्य फला- sian राच्रिसत्रन्यायेना्थवादिकफलर्बिपरिणामे कर्तव्ये प्रधानार्थवाद इवाङ्गाथवादे श्रुत्यापि wee अहणोचिलयात्‌,” The nyaya is found also in Pancapddikavivarana, page 122, line 8 from bottom, and again on page 134, line 9 from the bottom. The latter passage is as follows :—“ ननु रात्निसत्रन्यायेनार्थवाद्गतमेव मोक्षं ब्रह्मज्ञानं वा प्रयोजनं साध्यत्वेन परिणमय्य मोक्षकामो बद्यज्ञानकामो वा विचारयेत्‌ &०. See, too, 31140, p. 7 and 157 ; and Tattvamuktakalapa ए. 81.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 123

gloss_en: The simile of piercing the central figure of a target.
explanation: The simile of piercing the central figure of a target. That 18, hitting the bull’s eye. It is used of something difficult of accomplishment, and requiring great skill. “In Prakrit the radha is generally called puttaliyd, literally ‘a little figure, as apparently a little human figure was painted in the middle of the butt.” This note, contributed by Professor Leumann to Mr. Tawney’s translation of Merutunga’s work, is probably a correct explanation of the word राधा, rather than the dictionary meaning, “an attitude in shooting.” The illustration appears on pages 412, 420, and 434 of Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha, as follows:—“ सा चेयती viewer arts सुदुरुभा | राधवेधोपमानेन Ta 124 भरा्िः प्रकीर्तिता ॥ “od संसारविस्तारं विरुध्य कथमप्यदः । मानुष्यं प्राप्य दुष्प्रापं राधावेधोपमं जनः ” ॥ at भव्याः aera मोहलछितं युष्माभिरा- कण्यैतामेकान्तेन हितं मदीयवचनं gat विशुद्धं मनः । राधावेधसमं कथंचिद्‌- रुं watt मानुष्यकं हिंसाक्रोधवदशानुगैरिदमहो जीवैः पुरा हारितम्‌.” Two more examples will be found on pages 575 and 981 of the same, The above meaning of rddha@ fully explains the epithet radhabhedin as applied to the renowned archer Arjuna.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 123

gloss_en: On acquiring the mountain Rohana one acquires the wealth of gems contained in tt.
explanation: On acquiring the mountain Rohana one acquires the wealth of gems contained in tt. This occurs 7 the Pratyabhijni-section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 106 of Jivananda’s edn.):—“‘q@ragarerat हि सर्वाः सम्पदस्तन्निष्यन्दमय्यः सम्पन्ना एव रोहणाचरुलाभे रलसंपद्‌ इव । एवं परमेश्वरताराभे किमmeray । agaguert: | “भक्तिरक्ष्मीसधद्धानां किमन्यदुपयाचि- wal एनया वा दरिद्राणां किमन्यदुपयाचितमिति." ` Professor Gough renders it thus:—* For when the nature of the Supreme Being is attained, all felicities, which are but the efflux thereof, are overtaken ; as if a man acquired the mountain Rohana ( Adam’s Peak), he would acquire all the treasures it contains. If a man acquire the divine nature, what else is there that he can ask for? Accordingly Utpalacarya says—‘ What more can they ask who are rich in the wealth of devotion? What else can they ask who are poor in this?’” For a story in connection with the mountain Rohana as a mine of wealth, see Prabandhacintamani, page 3. 125
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 124

gloss_en: The belief as to a Yaksa in a Banyan tree.
explanation: The belief as to a Yaksa in a Banyan tree. A popular belief, based solely on the tradition of the elders, that a Yaksa, or goblin, lives in every Banyan tree. It is used as an illustration of देतिद्य ( ‘tradition’ ), which some regard as a pramana, but which Gautama rejects as such. See Nydyasitra 2.2.1. The Nyayasiitraviviérana expounds the term thus :—“aRafte zat यक्षः प्रतिवसतीति रोकम्रसिद्धिः । तत्र मूलवज्कनिरदैरोनाप्ोक्त्वानिश्चयेन ताददा- Ramer शाब्दबोधहेतुतया नास्य शब्दग्रमाणेऽन्तभावः ` ॥ This is the position of the objector who maintains that tradition is a distinct pramaina; the refutation is contained in siitra 2. In Ballantyne’s Aphorisms of Nydya Philosophy, Book ii, page 66, we have the following rendering of a portion of the Vyayasiitravritti :—“A rumour (aitihya) is what is expressed in this way—'thus indeed people say’ Wc. for it is an assertion which has come from one to another, without any first assertor being indicated :—for example, ‘In every Bengal fig-tree there is a goblin, and the like.” In a foot-note to page 329 of Colebrooke’s Essays, vol. i, Professor Cowell gives to aitihya the meaning of “fallible testimony (as opposed to infallible s‘ruti),’ whilst Colebrooke himself, on page 427, renders it by ‘tradition.’ In Nydyamanjari, page 194, the nyaya is applied in the following way by the disbeliever in the existence of God:—q च प्रसिद्धि- मात्रेण युक्तमेतस्य कल्पनम्‌ | निमूरत्वात्तथा चोक्त प्रसिद्धिवैटयक्चवत्‌. '* And at the top of page 64, there is the following, forming the second half of a verse :— “tft तु न सत्यमत्र हि वटे यक्षोऽस्ति वा नेति वा को जानाति कदा च केन कलितं यक्षस्य कीदर्वपुः ॥ In S'lokavartika (page 492 ) we read ^ जगति ag न तथ्यं नियमैतिद्यसुक्तं मवति तु यदि ae नागमाद्धिदयते ag” which Prof. Ganganatha Jha renders by “ Much of what is known in the world as 126 ‘ Tradition * is not always true; and whatever happens to be true that does not differ from ‘Valid Testimony.” Parthasarathi’s comment on this is—“ पुरुषवचनपरंपरा एेतिद्यं वटे az वैश्रवण इत्यादि | तच्चानिर्णायकत्वास्रमाणमेव न भवति । तद्धावेऽप्यागमान्तर्मावात्‌.” See, too, Tarkikaraksd, page 117.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 125

gloss_en: As Raghunathavarman had the temerity to include this in his list of nyayas, I introduce it in order to show its origin and its worthlessness.
explanation: As Raghunathavarman had the temerity to include this in his list of nyayas, I introduce it in order to show its origin and its worthlessness. At the bottom of page 53 of the Benares edition of Laukikanydyasangraha, it stands thus:—“ तथा च ^ वध्यतां वध्यतां बालो ननेना्थोंऽस्ति जीवता । स्वपक्षहानिकैत्वाद्यः ऊुखाङ्गारतां Ta’ इति न्यायविषयतां नातिवर्तते. This verse, and the words which follow it, are taken bodily, from Citsukhi i. 16 (The Pandit, vol. iv, page 584); but the real source of the sloka is Vishnu Purdna 1. 17. 31, where it reads “ दुरात्मा वध्यतामेषः??? the remainder being the same as the above. I got the clue from the Laukikanydyaratnakara, where Raghunatha apologetically says:—“ इद विष्णुपुराणे प्रह्वादसुदिद्य हिरण्या्चवचनं पूैन्यांयत्वेनोदाहतत्वात्तच्वेनोदाहतम्‌.” whi वर्चोल्यायः ॥ The topic of glory [or splendour]. This forms the topic of Jaimini 3. 8. 25-27. The point discussed is whether in using the mantra “ ममान्न वर्चो fexereg,” “ Fire! let there be glory for me in the offerings,” the officiating priest (at the new and full moon sacrifices) is to enjoy the fruit, or whether it falls to the sacrificer. The pirvapaksin holds the former view, but the decision is that it goes to the latter. In this adhikarana there is mention of karana-mantras, but the term is not explained. Kunte says that a karana-mantra is that which regulates sacrificial operations; and that one which is merely chanted in the course of a sacrificial operation is called akarma-karanamantra, 127
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 126

gloss_en: Showing ¢ looking-glass to a man whose nose has been cut of! An incitement to wrath! It occurs in Prabandhacintamani, page 291, as follows:—“ प्रायः सम्प्रतिकोपाय सन्मार्गस्योपदेशनम्‌ । विलूलनासिकस्येव ageiestaetra’’? “As a general rule, pointing out the right way leads to immediate wrath, as the showing of a mirror to one whose nose has been cut off.
explanation: Showing ¢ looking-glass to a man whose nose has been cut of! An incitement to wrath! It occurs in Prabandhacintamani, page 291, as follows:—“ प्रायः सम्प्रतिकोपाय सन्मार्गस्योपदेशनम्‌ । विलूलनासिकस्येव ageiestaetra’’? “As a general rule, pointing out the right way leads to immediate wrath, as the showing of a mirror to one whose nose has been cut off.” This is Mr. Tawney’s rendering, and I have adopted the reading of his manuscripts in the first line. In the second line, the Mss. read विञुद्धादशेदशशनम्‌. s
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 127

gloss_en: The law regarding the Vis'vajit-sacrifice.
explanation: The law regarding the Vis'vajit-sacrifice. It forms part of the great sacrifice called Gavaém ayana which lasts for a year; for a description of which see Dr. Eggeling’s translation of S’atapatha-Brahmana, vol. 2, page 427. A quite new explanation of this sacrificial session is propounded in a book by the learned Librarian of the Mysore Govt. Oriental Library, entitled Gavam Ayana, the Vedic era. It was favourably reviewed by Prof. L. D. Barnettin the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society for April 1909. The Vis'vajit is discussed in Jaimini 4. 3. 10-16, and 6. 7. 1-20. In the former, the question is raised of the reward which is,to follow the offering of the sacrifice, since none is mentioned in the scripture prescribing it; and the decision is that in this, and in all similar cases, heaven is to be understood to be the reward. This is somewhat quaintly put by S‘abara under sitra 16:—“ अनादिष्टफठे कर्मणि स्वगैः फलम्‌ । इति प्रययो रोके । caged । आरामक्ृदेवदन्तो नियतोऽस्य स्वर्गः | तडागक्रदेवदत्तो नियतोऽस्य स्वग इति । इत्थमनेन न्यायेन स्वगे संप्रययो भवति यस्मास्स्वगैफटेषु कर्मसु sty फर्वचनं नेवोचारयन्ति गम्यत एवेति । तस्मादप्यवगच्छाम एवेजातीयकेषु सर्गः Geeta.” The offerer of the Vis’vajit is required to surrender all his property to the sacrificing priests, a point which gives rise to a very curious 128 discussion in the Sixth Book. Is he then to give away his father and mother; or, if a reigning monarch, is he to part with all the lands of his kingdom? These and many other interesting points are fully argued out by S‘abara; and, for some of them, the English reader may with advantage refer to Colebrooke’s Essay on the Mimamsa, vol. i., page 345. This nyaya is very frequently quoted by writers on Vedanta and Nyaya. The following are examples. Bhaimati, page 86 :— “a 3 ब्रह्मभूयादन्यदद्धतस्वमार्थवादिकै किञ्चिदस्ति येन तत्काम उपासना- यामधिक्रियेत, Raters तु स्वर्गकल्पनायां तस्य सातिशयववं क्षयित्वं चेति न नित्यफल्त्वमुपासनायाः१ Veddntakalpataru, page 430:--““सन्नि- हितकरणोपकारे संभवति न afer स्वर्मकल्पना नापि ददरौपूणमासफलस्वगैस्याजुषङ्गः"* | See, too, Pancapadikavivarana, pages 134 (line 9), 137 (line 13), and 164 (line 6 from bottom). Nydyamanjari, page 524, line 13 from bottom :—* परप्रकरणपरिपिखणfaa नास्य संपदादिविधिवत्प्रधानाधिकारनिवेित्वमतो विश्वजिद्धिकरणन्यायेन सखर्भकाममधिकारिणमिह यावदुपात्तमध्यवसामस्तावदेव च न पुनरावर्तते &.” There is another good example of it in the early part of the Jaimini chapter of Sarvadars'anasangraha.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 127

gloss_en: A bowl of poison with milk on the surface, A wolf in sheep's clothing.
explanation: A bowl of poison with milk on the surface, A wolf in sheep's clothing. The illustration is found in Merutunga’s work the Prabandhacintémani, page 153:—“ परोक्षे कार्यहन्तारं weet प्रियवादिनम्‌ | वर्जयेत्तादशं मित्रं Raed पयोमुखम्‌. “The friend who behind one’s back tries to impede one’s business, but in one’s presence speaks kindly, such a friend one should avoid, a bowl of poison with milk on the surface.” This is Mr. Tawney’s rendering ( on page 92), and he points out in a footnote that the verse is quoted in Béhtlingk’s Indische Spriiche, and ascribed by him to Canakya. 129
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 128

gloss_en: This highly technical nyaya is found in both of Raghunathavarman’s works.
explanation: This highly technical nyaya is found in both of Raghunathavarman’s works. I have taken it from a passage in the Bauddha section of Sarvadars'anasangraha (page 11 in Jivananda’s edition), and subjoin Professor Gough’s rendering ( italicizing the words which represent the maxim):—“aq क्षणिकत्वं नीटादि- क्षणानां सच्वेनानुमातव्यम्‌ । यत्सत्तरक्षणिकं यथा जरधरपटलम्‌ । सन्तश्चामी भावा इति । न चायमसिद्धो 29: | अर्थक्रियाकारित्वरश्षणस्य सत्वस्य नीखादि- क्षणानां TOASTS, । व्यापकन्याच्रत्या उ्याप्यव्याचृ्तिन्यायेन व्यापकक्रमा- कमव्यावृत्तावक्षणिकात्सत्वव्याटृ्तेः सिद्धत्वाच्च. “Of these points of view, the momentariness of fleeting things, blue and so forth, is to be inferred from their existence; thus, whatever is is momentary ( or fluxional ) like a bank of clouds, and all these things are. Nor may any one object that the middle term (existence) is unestablished ; for an existence consisting of practical efficiency is established by perception to belong to the blue and other momentary things ; and the exclusion of existence from that which is not momentary is established, provided that we exclude from it the non-momentary succession and simultaneity, according to the rule that exclusion of the continent is exclusion of the contained.” In S’alika page 119, line 14, we find the nyaya as ^ व्यापकनिव्रत्ति्िं व्याप्यनिवृत्त्या sarat,”” and, on page 67, line 2 “व्यापकाभावे व्याप्यं नास्ति.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 129

gloss_en: The simile of the movements of a bird-catcher.
explanation: The simile of the movements of a bird-catcher. It is found in S‘abara on Jaimini 9. 1. 22, as follows:—“‘qar राकुनिग्राहकस्य wat जिघृक्षतदछद्मना गतिर्भवति aa: पदन्यासो इ्टिधरणिधानमरब्दकरणच्च कथमनवञुदधः शकुनिगदयेतेति एवमिहाप्यनवबुद्धमिव अदीत ज्ञं भ्रच्छन्नगति- wig नाम । यथा श्ुनि्राहकस्य यस्मिन्देशे शनः पदन्यासो न स तदेशा- थोऽपि त॒ तदेश्ाभिगतस्य शकुनेस्थैन क्रियत एवमिहप्टुपांश॒स्वं न तदेशानां पदाथौनामथैन क्रियते तदेशाभिगतस्य यन्ञस्यार्थेन गम्यते `?॥ See also भूमिरथिकन्याय. 17 130
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 129

gloss_en: The simile of [the time for sounding] the conch-shell.
explanation: The simile of [the time for sounding] the conch-shell. An offering called उपांशुयाज, or ‘low-voiced offering,’ is performed between the cake-oblation to Agni and that to Agni-Soma at the full-moon, and between the cake-oblation to Agni and that to Indra-Agni at the new-moon (Dr. Eggling’s trans: of Satapatha-Brahmana, vol. 1. page 192). Sometimes, however, the second oblation is omitted; and then the question arises how is the Upanis‘uyaja to be offered, since that comes between the two १ The subject is discussed in Jaimini 10. 8, 62-70, and the above question is answered by S'abara as follows:—“qt हि द्वयोः पुरोडा- शयो्मध्य इति कालोऽवगम्यते स एव wenger इति । एवं चे्यचप्येकपुरो- saat gt पुरोडाशो न स्तस्तथापि स पूव आभ्नेयोऽसि तस्मादुत्तरः स एव कारो योऽसावन्तराटेन रक्षितः । यथा “नागवेखायामागन्तव्यं, ‘state यामागन्तव्यं, “पटहवेखायामागन्तव्यं ' इति यस्मिन्‌ आमे न नागान tar न पटहस्तस्मिन्नपि स॒ एव काटस्तत्र ह्यागमनं क्रियते । एवमिहापि दरष्टग्यम्‌ । तस्मादेकुरोडाशायासुपांश॒याजः कतैन्य इति.” The simile might well be called ज्ञेखध्वनिवेखान्यायः, but I have adopted the name given in the Nyayamalavistara on this adhikarana, which also explains it more fully :-—“ ज्ञंखन्यायेनोपलक्चकस्याश्नीषोमीयपुरोडाज्सेवाभावेऽप्युपलक्षये ae यागोऽस्ति । यथा रोखध्वनि- jet राजसेवार्थं प्रतिदिनमागन्तव्यमिव्युक्ते कर्सिमश्चिदिने तं ae धमतः पुरूषस्याभावेनोपलक्चकस्य ध्वनेरभवेऽप्युपलक्षिते तस्मिन्काठे सेवकाः समागच्छन्ति तथात्रापि इष्टव्यम्‌.” This is very clear, but what is the meaning of नागवेला १ To be in harmony with the rest of the sentence it ought to indicate something which, like the sounding of the s‘ankha or the beating of the pataha, takes place regularly every day; but how can any such sense be got out of naga, unless it refers to the trumpeting of the royal elephants at some particular time 131 of the day? The S’astradipika, Nyayamalavistara, and Jaiminiyasiitravritti ignore the expression altogether. In Appai Diksita’s Vidhirasdyana, page 22, we have an instance of the gpqeqrz as follows:—“ उपलक्षणापायेऽप्युपलक्ष्यानपायस्य प्रतिदिनं शंखवेरायामागन्तन्यमिव्यादौ भ्रसिदधत्वात्‌ । न हि कारविरोषो- पलक्षणतयोपात्ते शंखध्वनौ क्चिदिवसे दैवादृते सति तदुपलक्षितः कारो नास्तीति नावगम्यते. See also Vidhiviveka, page 7. Compare the expression “Cockshut-time,” in Shakespeare’s Richard iii (Act V. Scene 3):—*Thomas the Earl of Surrey, and himself, Much about cock-shut time, from troop to troop. Went through the army, cheering up the soldiers.” In Chambers’ Twentieth Century Dictionary the expression is said to mean “Twilight; probably referring to the time when poultry are shut up.” Webster's International Dictionary gives a different explanation.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 130

gloss_en: The illustration of the moth.
explanation: The illustration of the moth. The destruction of the silly moth by flying into a lighted lamp is a figure often met with in Sanskrit works; as for example in S’is'‘wpalavadha ii. 117, Kumdarasambhava iv. 40, Réjatarangint vii. 875, and Kamandaki i. 43. According to Merutunga, however, the poor moth is moved with envy at the brilliance of the light, and so seeks to diminish it! He says (on page 211, at the bottom ):— “snqaymmnged gat ze न कथमपि क्षमते । दग्ध्वा तनुमपि शकम दीप्रं दीपार्चिषं हरति. “The mean man cannot anyhow endure to behold the exaltation of the man of radiant merit; the moth even burns its own body to extinguish the bright flame of the candle.” (Tawney’s translation, page 130 ). 189
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 131

gloss_en: When the [ prescribed ] ritual [ for the removal or prevention of evil] is at an end, wp comes a goblin! This implies ultimate failure in spite of effort.
explanation: When the [ prescribed ] ritual [ for the removal or prevention of evil] is at an end, wp comes a goblin! This implies ultimate failure in spite of effort. It occurs in Citsukha Muni’s comment on Wydyamakaranda, page 16, as follows:—*aq सोऽयं शान्ते कर्मणि वेतारोदयोऽभेदं साधयितुं वृत्तेन भेदश्चतेरदाहृतत्वात्‌.> In 21571, page 93, line 17, the nyaya is quoted as “ ज्ञान्ति- कर्मणि वेतारोदयः,?? which makes the goblin appear during the performance of the arita, the rite to avert evil. So, too, in Mandana-Mis'ra’s Vidhiviveka, page 210. The purport, however, is the same as in the other case. In As'valiyana’s Grihyasitra 4. 6. 1, शान्तिकर्म is prescribed when a guru dies, or on the loss of a son or of cattle.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 132

gloss_en: Verbal expectancy is satisfied [or fulfilled] by words only.
explanation: Verbal expectancy is satisfied [or fulfilled] by words only. This ny&ya of Raghunatha’s is very frequently met with, It occurs in the last chapter of Sarvadars‘anasangraha (page 157 of Bib. Ind. and 177 of Jivananda’s edn.) with qzqf as the last word; in Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 680, line 7; in Vaidyanatha’s comment on Kavyapradipa page 232 (in the erroneous form ज्ञाब्दा साकांश्चा); in Haridasa’s vritti on Kusumanjals iii. 15 ( page 35 ), also-in Rucidatta’s commentary on the same portion (page 478); and, finally, in Sahityadarpana ॐ. 18 ( with प्रपूर्यते ) In paragraph 70 of the Tarkasangraha we are told that there are three requisites to the intelligibility of a sentence, namely, expectancy (akdnksé ), compatibility ( yogyata), and juxtaposition (sannidhi). In para 71, the first of these is thus explained:—* Expectancy means a word's incapacity to convey a complete meaning, this being occasioned by the 133 absence of another word [which, when it comes as expected, will complete the construction and the sense].” Then in para 72 we read:—“a collection of words devoid of expectancy &c., is no instrument of right knowledge; for example, ‘cow, horse man, elephant, gives no information, from the absence of expectancy*; [ the words having no reference one to another, and not looking out for one another].” This is Dr. Ballantyne’s translation, accompanying the text; and the same subject is explained by him in his lucid rendering of Sahityadarpana i. 6. शास्यादिविषयस्य FASS: करणस्य इयामाकेऽभिहतिभेवति ॥ A blow with an instrument such as a pestle, directed towards the rice, falls instead on the s'yamaka grain. Aiming, at a pigeon and killing a crow. It occurs in Nydyavartika, page 46 :--“कथं युनरन्यविषयं करणम .यविषयां क्रियां करोति । शाल्यादि- विषयसख quae: करणस्य इयामाकेऽमिहतिर्भवति । नानियमादिति यद्विषयं करणं तद्विषया क्रियेति न नियमोऽस्ति । दृष्टा हि वृक्षादिविषयस्य छेदनस्यावयवक्रियेति । ब्रृक्षभ्छि्यतेऽवयवे क्रियेति "` ॥ MAS प्रयोक्तरि ॥ The fruit promised in Scripture [in connection with a sacrificial or other act ] is for the performer [of that act]. These are the first words of Jaimini’s sitra 8. 7, 18. They are quoted as a nyadya in a passage of Veddntatattvaviveka, for which see ^ सुनिमैनुते, Fal qeaa.” It is cited by Vacaspati Mis’ra, also, in Tatparyatika, page 296, line 6 from bottom, and page 408, line 4, and in his Bhamati, pages 28 and 492. Also in S'rvbadsya 2. 8. 33 (p. 1688 ), and 3, 4. 45 (p. 2028), where Dr. Thibaut renders it, “the fruit of the injunction belongs to the agent.” The first part of Tattvamuktakalapa ii. 59 reads thus:— 134 ५अन्यश्चेद्न्यकर्मप्रजनितफलभुक्‌ शाखवेयाऊुरी स्यात्‌ &c.,” on which the author comments as follows :--“अन्यस्यान्यकृतकर्मफलमोगे शाख्रफलं भ्रयोक्तरीति व्यवस्थाभङ्गाच्छाखस्य sareeat स्यात्‌ &. Compare Patanjali on Panini 1. 8. 72.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 132

gloss_en: The simile of men carrying a palanquin.
explanation: The simile of men carrying a palanquin. Used by Jayanta Bhatta to show how all the words in a sentence wnitedly convey the sense of the latter. It occurs in Vyayamanjari, page 397, line 12:—“gay हि वाद्यानि करणानि काष्टादीनि पाके व्याप्रियन्ते यथा च रिबिकाया vara: सर्वे रिबिकामुद्यच्छन्ति यथा त्रयोऽपि वाण उखां बिश्रति तथा सवौण्येव पदानि वाक्याथैमववबोधयन्ति.*” Again, on page 400, line 11 from bottom:—* fifierrsaacaala पदानि कर्ये संहत्य व्याप्नि- यन्ते इत्येतदपि aaa.” Cf. Brihadarnyavartika, 1. 4. 1600. fracas नासिकास्पर्शन्यायः ॥ The simile of touching the nose by encircling the head [ with one’s arm]. That is, putting the arm round the head instead of bringing it directly to the face. Raghunathavarma classes it with nyayas expressive of a round-about way of doing things. It is quoted by Vijnanabhiksu on Brahmasitra 3. 8. 87, in the form हिरोवेष्टनेनां गुल्या नासिकाम्रवेशवत्‌. Raghunatha tells us that, by some, it is styled द्रविडप्राणा- यामन्याय, and in Molesworth’s Marathi Dictionary we find द्वाविडग्राणायाम defined as “a circuitous or devious mode of speaking or acting, ambages, tortuous procedure.” This is not the first time that this fine dictionary has come to our aid when the more-pretentious Sanskrit lexicons have failed us! But we should like to know how the expression came to have the meaning here assigned to it. Doubtless hereby hangs a tale ; can our Indian pandits throw light on it? 185
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 134

gloss_en: The simile of the shining-forth of a thousand lamps standing in the midst of solid rock! It occurs in Brahmasitrabhasya 2.
explanation: The simile of the shining-forth of a thousand lamps standing in the midst of solid rock!
