It occurs in Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 2. 28, near the end:—* क्रिचान्यत्प्रदीपवद्धिक्ञानमवभासकान्तरनिरपेक्षं स्वयमेव प्रथत इति वुवताप्रमाणगम्यं विक्ञानमनवगन्तृकमिद्ुक्तं स्यात्‌ | शिराघनमध्यस्थप्रदीपसहसखप्रथनवत्‌.*› Dr. Thibaut renders it thus :— Moreover, if you maintain that the idea, lamplike, manifests itself without standing in need of a further principle to illuminate it, you maintain thereby that ideas exist which are not apprehended by any of the means of knowledge, and which are without a knowing being; which is no better than to assert that a thousand lamps burning inside some impenetrable mass of rocks manifest themselves.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 135

gloss_en: The figure of a sham-sacrifice, That is, the performance of sacrificial ceremonies, by a pupil, with a view to his becoming proficient in them, without the offering of a real sacrifice.
explanation: The figure of a sham-sacrifice, That is, the performance of sacrificial ceremonies, by a pupil, with a view to his becoming proficient in them, without the offering of a real sacrifice. This is classed with भूमिरथिक in S'abara’s bhashya on Jaimini 9. 2. 18, and an extract from the passage will be found under that nyaya. The term gesfe, as adopted in Marathi, is thus explained by Molesworth :—* Dry exercise or blank practising; performance or doing, antecedently to the occasion, of a work or matter in which the performer is ignorant or inexpert (in order that the necessary knowledge or ability may be acquired in provision for occasions anticipated ).” afeactaeaeara: ॥ See this explained under पाठक्रमन्याय. 136 श्वः कार्यमद्य कुवीत ॥ . One should do to-day that which one intends to do tomorrow, “Boast not thyself of tomorrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth.” The verse containing this nyaya of Raghunatha’s occurs three times in S'déntiparva, namely in chapters CLXXV, CCLXXvil, and cccxx1m of Calcutta edition. It reads as follows :-- 4 4 श्वःकार्यमद्य कुर्वीत Tale चापराह्िकम्‌ | a हि प्रतीक्षते aq: कृतं वास्य न वा कृतम्‌ ॥ It is quoted in the Prabandhacintémani, page 111, and Mr. Tawney (on page 68 of his translation ) renders it thus:— “One should do to-day the duty of tomorrow, and in the forenoon the duty of the afternoon, for death will not consider whether one has done one’s work or not.” Compare the following well-known story. “An old Rabbi was once asked by his pupil when he should fulfil a certain precept of the law, and the answer was ‘The day before you die.’ ‘But, said the disciple, ‘I may die tomorrow.’ ‘Then, said the master, ‘ do it to-day.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 135

gloss_en: The simile of the attempt to straighten a dog’s tail.
explanation: The simile of the attempt to straighten a dog’s tail. An illustration of wasted effort. It occurs in the following verse of the Upamitibhavaprapancaé Katha, page 448 :— न चैष शाक्यते कर्तु नश्नो यलदातेरपि । को हि स्वेद्ञतेनापि ages नामयिष्यति ॥ See also under अरण्यरोदनन्याय in the second Handful. ल "4 . ate 137
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 136

gloss_en: Like a milky preparation that has been licked by a dog.
explanation: Like a milky preparation that has been licked by a dog. Used of something which has become impure and therefore unacceptable. It is found in the following verse of the last chapter of the Sarvadars‘anasangraha :--“फृराभिसन्धरूपघातकत्वमभिहितं भगवद्धिर्नीरुकण्ठभारतीश्रीचरणे : | अपि प्रयलसम्पन्नं कामेनोपहतं तपः । न qed महेशस्य श्वलीढमिव पायसम्‌ ॥ Compare with this the nyaya “a हि पूतं स्यादोक्षीरं शवदतो aaa.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 137

gloss_en: A dog, when an ear or its tail has been cut off is still a dog, not a horse or a donkey! This 18 Patanjali’s illustration of the vartika एकदेदाविक्रृतमनन्यवत्‌ (which see above) and is referred to by Nages’a in his comment on Kaiyata ( under S‘iva siitra 2, vartika 4), as the fag=sacerra.
explanation: A dog, when an ear or its tail has been cut off is still a dog, not a horse or a donkey! This 18 Patanjali’s illustration of the vartika एकदेदाविक्रृतमनन्यवत्‌ (which see above) and is referred to by Nages’a in his comment on Kaiyata ( under S‘iva siitra 2, vartika 4), as the fag=sacerra. Compare also Nagojibhatta’s एषणा xxxvii. Akin to this illustration is Kumarila’s “a हि गोडनि जाते विषाणे वा भन्ने गोत्वं तिरोधीयते, which is found in Tantravartika 2,1. 34, page 418; and “a हि केवरभोजी देवदनत्तोऽन्येः सह deat अुञ्ञानोऽन्यत्वं wea,” on page 617.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 137

gloss_en: The rule as to the use or omission of the Sodas‘istotra [at the Atiratra-sacrifice ].
explanation: The rule as to the use or omission of the Sodas‘istotra [at the Atiratra-sacrifice ]. In very common use as an indication of option being allowable in regard to something. From the introduction to the third volume of Dr. Eggeling’s translation of the S’atapatha Brahmana, we learn that “the distinctive feature of the Atiratra-sacrifice, as the name itself indicates, is an ‘overnight’ performance of chants and recitation...At the end of each round, libations are offered, followed by the inevitable potations of Soma-liquor...and the performance partook largely of the character of a regular nocturnal carousal.” Then, as to the 18 188 &^०१०७१, he says ( page xviii )-448 regards the ceremonies preceding the night-performance, there is a difference of opinion among ritualists as to whether the S’odas'i-stotra is or is not a necessary element of the Atiratra...As’valayana ( 5. 11.1) refers incidentally to the S’odas‘in, as part of the Atiratra, though it is not quite clear from the text of the sitra whether it is meant to be a necessary or only an optional feature of that sacrifice.” There can be little doubt, however, that the learned writers who use the nyaya, regard the use of the stotra as optional. For example, as illustrations of option in action, S‘ankara, in his bhasya on siittra 1. 1. 2 (page 87), and again on sitra 2. 1. 27 (page 471), quotes the Vedic sentences “अतिरात्रे षोडशिनं gaa” “नातिरात्रे षोडशिनं गृहवति. Then at the close of 1. 4. 13, he says:—attaraera समानेऽपि aa ज्योतिषो अरहणाम्रहणे । यथा समानेऽप्यतिरात्रे वचनभेदात्षोडदिनो ग्रहणाग्रहणे तद्वत्‌”; and in 3.3. 2, “a दहि षोडशिग्रहणाम्रहणयोरतिरात्रो भिद्यते.” ‘In his comment on 8. 8. 26 ( page 898, line 5 from bottom), Anandagiri makes use of the expression “षोडदिग्रहणाग्रहणवद्विकस्पे sa,” and repeats it three lines lower down. In Veddntakalpataruparimala, page 539 (line 7 from bottom), we read “qreRmeneataa विकल्पो भविष्यति," and on page 656 (line 4), षोडरचिग्रहणाब्रहणन्यायसञ्चारणे तथेव विरोधपरिहाराय विकल्पोऽम्युपगन्तव्यः.” The optional character of the stotra is made use of by Laugaksibhaskar also, in Arthasangraha, page 24, from line 14; and by Nages'abhatta in the Pradipoddyota on Mahabhasya 1. 1. 44 ( vart, 7 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 137

gloss_en: This is Nagojibhatta’s shortened form of the paribhasi सङद्रतौ विप्रतिषेधे यद्वाधितं तद्वाधितमेव, which Professor Kielhorn renders thus:—*When (two rules), while they apply (simultaneously ), mutually prohibit each other, that which is once superseded is superseded altogether.
explanation: This is Nagojibhatta’s shortened form of the paribhasi सङद्रतौ विप्रतिषेधे यद्वाधितं तद्वाधितमेव, which Professor Kielhorn renders thus:—*When (two rules), while they apply (simultaneously ), mutually prohibit each other, that which is once superseded is superseded altogether.” ‘Thisis illustrated by the following from ordinary life-—‘qur gerawritte: प्रेष्यो भवति स तयोः पर्यायेण कार्य 139 करोति यदा aga युगपस्मेषयतो wake च कायै तदोभयोर्न करोति यौगपद्यासंभवात्‌'* ॥ The paribhasa is found in Mahabhdsya 1. 1. 56 ( vart, 25, 26, 27); 1. 4. 2 (vart. 7); 6. 8. 42 (vart, 5); 6. 8 189; 6. 4. 62 ( vart. 2); 7. 1, 26; 7. 1. 54; and 7.1. 78. The illustration is met with in 1, 4. 2 (vart. 5), and 6. 1, 85 (vart. 8 ).
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 138

gloss_en: The rule regarding a sacrificial session.
explanation: The rule regarding a sacrificial session. For this kind of sacrifice, lasting several days, not less than seventeen sacrificers are absolutely necessary. But what if one of them should leave or die before the completion of the ceremony? In sucha case he must be replaced by a substitute, or the whole thing becomes null and void. This, however, cannot be done when there is only one sacrificer engaged in a sacrifice. The nyaya is the subject of Jaimini 6. 3.22. In siitras 23 to 26 itis laid down that the substitute does not reap the benefit of the sacrifice,— but that it goes to the man whose place he has taken, It is very clear, from the above, that the mention of the सत्रन्याय on page 430, line 5 of Veddntakalpataru, is wrong, and that the reference is really to the रात्रिसच्रन्याय which see above.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 139

gloss_en: The meaning of an ambiguous capression is to be determined { from the context.
explanation: The meaning of an ambiguous capression is to be determined { from the context. In Brahmasitrabhasya 1. 3. 14 there is a discussion as to the meaning of the ‘small ether’ of Chhandogya Upanisad 8. 1. 1, and Anandagiri makes the following comment on the closing part of it:—“qyeafa. संदिग्धस्य aerated ' इति न्यायादादौ तस्मिन्यदन्तरिति तच्छब्दोऽनन्तरमप्याकाशमभिरुष्य हतपुण्डfe पराति aa यदन्तराकादो तदन्वेष्टव्यं विजिज्ञासितव्यं चेत्युपसंहरति.” The nyaya is quoted again in his tiké on 8, 8. 52. It is taken from Jaimini’s sitra 1.4.29, “संदिग्धेषु areretara,”’ which is quoted and applied by the author of the S’astradipika, in his 140 discussion, under 1. 8. 8, of such words as यव, वराह, पीलु and others, to which the Aryas attach one meaning and the Mlecchas ‘another. We find it, too, in Kumiarila’s lengthy exposition of the same portion, in the ०08: “संदिग्धेषु च सर्वेषु वाक्यरोषेण निणैयम्‌ ?› (page 148); and again under 8. 4. 36 (page-1003):— «संदिग्धं वाक्यदोषेण निणैयमवधारितम्‌ । विध्युदेदोन निर्णीते कं त॒ दोषः करिष्यति. See also Bhamati 3. 3. 34 (page 641). समुदाये वाक्यपरिसमाधिः ॥ For this paribhasa, see under गरदातदण्डनन्याय.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 139

gloss_en: When a sentence can suitably be regarded as one, it is not right to divide it.
explanation: When a sentence can suitably be regarded as one, it is not right to divide it. This oft-quoted line of Kumarila’s was directed against an older writer, named Bhavadasa (so Parthasarathi tells us ), who proposed to divide Jaimini’s siitra 1. 1. 4 into two parts. The line is found in S'lokavartika, page 135. It is quoted in Bhdmati 1. 1. 28 (page 159), 1. 2. 18 (page 206), 1. 4. 3 (page 286), 1. 4. 16 (page 308), 3. 3. 57 (page 668), and 3. 4. 20 ( page 678). In Anandagiri on Brahmasiatrabhasya 1. 2. 15, we २८०१.“ वक्तुेदेऽप्येकवाक्यता साकाक्षतवासपूर्वोत्तरवाक्ययो- tides वाक्थैक्यसंभवे तद्धेदस्यायोगादिल्यर्थः, and, in the latter part of the bhasya cn 1. 4, 3, S’ankara himself has a good deal to say on एकवाक्यता. Then Anandagiri quotes the nyaya in his comment on 2. 8. 2 and 3.3.14. “A vakyabheda—split of the sentence-takes place according to the Mimamsa when one and the same sentence contains two new statements which are different.” (Dr. Thibaut’s Translation of Sankara’s bhasya, vol. i. page 177 note). See, too, Prof. Cowell’s long note on page 68 of his Translation of S’andilya sitras. 141 be’
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 140

gloss_en: A qualifying word is of use when it is appropriate [ that is, when it suits the विजञेष्य ], and when [ withoutit] a wrong meaning would be conveyed.
explanation: A qualifying word is of use when it is appropriate [ that is, when it suits the विजञेष्य ], and when [ withoutit] a wrong meaning would be conveyed. The nyaya is quoted as above in the commentary on Sanksepas'ariraka i. 347 (The Pandit, vol. ए. page 676), and on page 401 of Vidydsdgart(a comment on Khandanakhandakhadya); whilst, on page 215 of the latter, it appears without स्यात्‌. There is another good example on p. 592. I have traced it, however, as far back as Kumarila, but cannot say whether he was its author or not. In Tantravdrtika 1. 3. 18 (=Jaimini 1. 3. 24.) there is a discussion (as a purvapaksa ) of the reasons assigned by Patanjali for the study of grammar. One of these is that, without a knowledge of grammar, the performance of the injunction ^ ब्राह्मणेन निष्का- रणोः धर्मः षडङ्गो वेदोऽध्येयः would be impossible; on which the purvapaksin says :— ^ षडङ्गो वेद इत्युक्तं श्रतिलिङ्गाद्येश्षया | तैः off: प्रविभक्तः सन्स हि क्मविबोधनः ॥ नलु बादयाङ्गानपक्ष्वे वेदस्वरूपान्तरीतश्रुलाद्यपेक्षया विरोषणमनथकं भ्रसभ्यते | तथाहि । संभवव्यभिचाराभ्यां स्याद्विशेषणसंभवः | शरुलयाचम्यभिचारातत ay: किं विरोष्यते"' ॥ Dr. Ganganath Jha renders this (and the preceding clause ) as follows :— “We must explain the expression ‘the Veda with its six subsidiary sciences’ as referring to its constituent parts, in the following manner. The ‘six subsidiaries’ referred to must be taken to be the six means of interpretation—Direct Assertion &c. ; as it is only when interpreted through these that the Veda becomes capable of rightly pointing out Dharma, An objec142 tion is here raised :—If the subsidiaries referred to be taken as those contained in the Veda itself (7. ¢. Direct Assertion, &.), and not anything outside it (as grammar, Nirukta, We. ), then in that case the qualification with the six subsidiaries would be absolutely meaningless. Specially as we can have a qualification, only when such a one is possible, and when a qualification is actually needed for the purpose of setting aside certain incongruities (or contradictions ); and as there is certainly no incongruity in the Veda with regard to Direct Assertion &c. what could be specified by a qualification of these subsidiaries? [That is to say, Grammar not being invariably concomitant with the Veda, a qualification is needed in order to make it an object of study together with the Veda ; while Direct Assertion &९., are always contained in the Veda, and hence any qualification of these would be absolutely meaningless }.” Page 281. Another good example is furnished by Sures’vara in his vartika on Brihaddranyakopanisadbhasya. At the beginning of the second Brahmana of the sixth Chapter, referring to the prayer “sii नय सुपथा राये We.,” at the end of the seventh chapter of the dranyaka (the fifth of the Upanisad), he says:— ¢ “सक्तमावसितावुक्तं मारगप्राथनमभ्नितः। सुपथेति ga aa set मागैविरोषणम्‌ ॥ २ ॥ संभवे व्यभिचारे च विरोषणविरोभ्ययोः ze विरोषणं रोके यथेहापि तथेक्ष्यताम्‌" ॥ ३॥ Anandagiri explains verse 8 as follows:— “संभव इति । fteguetea विरोषणविशोप्ययोः संभवे Agta Rar Riera भ्यभिचारे प्रसक्ते नीरमिति विदोषणमर्थवदृ्टम्‌ । तथा सुपथा नयेलत्रापि व्यभिचारसंभवे विशेषणमर्थवञ्ज्ञेयमियर्थः' ॥ ३ ॥ The fourth verse of the vartika still further elucidates it:— “सुपथेति ततो युक्तं संभवे भूयसां पथाम्‌ । विङ्ञेषणमतो वाच्याः पन्थानः BARAT” ॥ ४ ॥ 143
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 141

gloss_en: Everything is switable [or proper] for the strong.
explanation: Everything is switable [or proper] for the strong. “Might is right.” In other words, a strong man may be lawless with. impunity; or, to quote Prof. Ganganatha Jha’s explanation of it, “for a pious man all actions are equally lawful.” This is quite in accord with the teaching of the Bhagavata Purdna x, 38, 30, Si: { ^ ध्मैव्यतिक्रमो दृष्ट ईश्वराणां च साहसम्‌ । तेजीयसां न दोषाय वहेः सर्वभुजो यथा ॥ ॥ नेतत्समाचरेजातु मनसापि ह्यनीश्वरः | विनर्यल्याचरन्मौब्या्यथारद्रोऽब्धिजं विषम्‌ ॥ Kumarila quotes the nyaya (in Yantravartika, page 134, line 14) in the course of a long explanation of the evil doings attributed to certain holy personages; but, in order to discourage persons of less piety from imitating them, adds:—“ मन्दतपसां गजैरिव महावटकाष्टादिभक्षणमात्मविनाद्चायैव स्यात्‌.” In the opening part of the Tatparyatika, Vacaspati 21818 reproduces Kumarila’s warning in the following words :--^तपःप्रभाव एव हि तादशस्तेषां यत एवंविधाः पाप्मानो विद्ीयन्त इति । न चास्मदादीनां मन्दतपसामयं प्रसंगः । न हि गजानासुद््यं तेजो वटकाष्टमश्ितं पचतीलयस्मदा- दीनामप्युदुर्ख्यण तेजसा तथा भवितव्यम्‌.” सर्वशाखाप्रत्ययमेकं कर्म ॥ All the different schools of a Veda acknowledge one and the same sacrificial action. The followers of the Mimams@ evident. ly regard this as an important point, for Jaimini devotes 25 ~ siltras, viz. 2. 4. 8-82, to the discussion of it. Kunte’s remarks on the bearings of the question, in his Saddars'anacintanika, are worthy of perusal, The nyaya is frequently quoted in the philosophical works. Instances of it will be found in Tantra144 vartika, page 84, line 7; in Pancapadikavivarana, page 167, line 3 from bottom; in Nydyamanjari, page 256, line 16; in Vivaranaprameyasangraha, page 169, line 17; and in Sribhasya 3. 3. 58. Raghunathavarman makes use of the nyaya but does not include it in his numbered list.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 143

gloss_en: The law relating to the Sakamedha offerings.
explanation: The law relating to the Sakamedha offerings. This is the topic of Jaimini 5. 1. 19-22. The group of offerings called Sakam-edhah form the third of the three seasonal, or fourmonthly (caturmasya), sacrifices which are performed at the parvans (or commencement of the spring, rainy, and autumn seasons ), and which, in this case, last for two days; three of the group (consisting of seven ) being offered on the first day, and the remainder on the second day. An objector urges that two days are required for each of the group, but this is set aside, and the ruling is as above. For a full description of these fourmonthly sacrifices, see Dr. Eggeling’s translation of the S’atapatha-Brahmana, vol. i. pages 383 and 408,
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 144

gloss_en: If a man with eyes is led by somebody else, it is clear that he does not see with his eyes! This is found in S’abara on Jaimini 1.
explanation: If a man with eyes is led by somebody else, it is clear that he does not see with his eyes! This is found in S’abara on Jaimini 1. 2.31 and is used by an objector to illustrate his argument that it is not necessary to understand the meaning of Vedic sentences employed in sacrificial rites, since the way in which they are to be used is clearly laid down in works prepared for the purpose. The illustration is quoted by Jayanta Bhatta in Nydyamanjari, page 286, line 12. 145
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 144

gloss_en: An injunction in general terms is indefinite.
explanation: An injunction in general terms is indefinite. It appears as a nyaya in the second part of the Laukikanydyaratnakara (1. O. MS, page 319 a), where Raghunath applies it thus:— ¢ रोके कंचिदशं जिगमिषुं प्रति तत्रल्ानि वस्तृन्यानेयानीति सामान्यतो विधा- येदमानेयमिदमानेयमिति स्पष्टीक्रियते. It is doubtless derived from the following verse in Tantravartika 3. 4. 47 (page 1020) :— “सामान्यविधिरस्पष्टः संद्ियेत विरोषतः | स्पष्टस्य तु विधेनौन्धेरपसंहारसं भवः” ॥ The verse is cited in Veddntakalpataruparimala (page 253), where the second line reads ^ स्पष्टस्य तु विधेरनैवसुपसंहार इष्यते, and the first line is quoted by the author of the Nyayasudha in his comment on Tantravartika 1. 2. 42 which defines the term परिसंख्या (‘limitation, or ‘exclusive specification, as Dr. Thibaut and M. M. Kunte respectively render it). The lack of definiteness in general statements is alluded to by S‘abara, also, on Jaimini 10. 8. 16, where he says «न हि सामा- न्यवाची शाब्दो erararada,”” but Kumarila points out (on page 1027) that the fa%rq requires the सामान्य. He says:—‘q a निःसामान्यः कश्चिद्विरोष उपपद्यते । ततश्च gaara रिरशपामिलयविरोधा- व्पश्वादुच्यमानं न विरुध्यते." निरवकारायोर्निरवकाञ्चो सावका वरीयान्‌ ॥ That [injunction] which leaves no room [for others] is stronger than one which does. For example, an injunction directing animal sacrifice [“arftafri पञ्यमारूभेत" ] and which leaves no room for option, overpowers the more general one forbidding the taking of life [५ न हिस्यात्सवौ भूतानि ” ]. In this way one Smriti may prevail over another. The nyaya is found in Raghunatha’s list, and is applied by him as follows:— 19 Tien hel peter (1 146 «न चानुभवेन gaara: शक्यः sehr । निरवकाशस्य च सावका- छनिरवकादायोर्भैरवकादो बरीयानिति न्यायाद्वाधकस्वोपपत्तेः"॥ ++ reference ` #0 Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 1. 1 will fully explain the two terms of this expression. In his comment on it Anandagiri quotes the nyaya twice, and again under 2. 1. 4, 6, and 13. In im. mediate connection with the first of the five, Anandagiri quotes also the nyaya ^ सपिक्षनिरपेक्षयोर्निरपेश्चस्य aaa,” and the two occur together in the following verses of Yamunacarya’s Agamapramainya, page 68 :--“ सापेश्चनिरपेश्चत्वे न हि बाधस्य कारणम्‌ । शक्तौ रजतबोधस्य निरपेक्षस्य बाधकम्‌ ॥ नेदं रजतविज्ञानं तस्सापेश्षमपीप्यते । सेयं ञवाङेति संवित्तेसेर्वर्तिविनाशजा ॥ अनुमा बाधिका दष्टा सपेश्षाप्यक्चजन्मनः। अतो निरवकारेन सावका निषिध्यते ` ॥ See also Citsukha Muni on Nyaéyamakaranda, pages 7 and 148; and ^ सापिश्चमaaa wala” in Mahabhasya 2. 1. 69 ( vart. 6) and Syadvadamanjari, page 19.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 145

gloss_en: Like a lion's first step.
explanation: Like a lion's first step. This obscure nyaya occurs in Merutunga’s work, page 278:—“ विचार्याविचार्य वा कृतग्रयाणोऽयं महानरे- नद्रश्वालितः | सिंहस्यैकपदं यथेति sraratea एव aaa.” Mr. Tawney renders it thus (on page 174 ) :—* Whether with due consideration or not, this great king has been set in motion, and has started on his expedition; on the principle of the lion’s first step, he cuts a good figure onthe march.” Does the illustration mean that a lion in motion presents a finer appearance than one at rest?
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 146

gloss_en: The simile of ८ well [ dug ] in sandy soil [the sides of which are incessantly falling in].
explanation: The simile of ८ well [ dug ] in sandy soil [the sides of which are incessantly falling in]. Used of an argument that will not hold water. It is found in Brahmasitrabhasya 2. 2. 32:— “fe aga सर्वप्रकारेण यथा यथायं वैनाशचिकसमय उपपत्तिमर्वाय परीक्ष्यते तथा तथा सिकतादूपवद्विदीर्यत एव । न कांचिदत्नोपपत्ति पयामः” ॥ 147 +
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 146

gloss_en: The figure of oil from sand.
explanation: The figure of oil from sand. A non-entity like a hare’s horn. The following is from Bhartrihari’s Witis‘ataka (verse 5) “ota सिकतासु तैलमपि aaa: पीडयन्‌ fra खगतृष्णिकासु alee पिपासार्दितः । कदाचिदपि पयैटन्छरविषाणमासादये- त्र तु प्रतिनिविषटमूखैजनचित्तमाराधयेत्‌"? ॥ In Brahmasitrabhdsya 2. 1. 16, we read:—* यच्च यदात्मना यत्र न वर्तते न तत्तत उत्पद्यते यथा ease.” Compare with this, Yogavasistha 2. 5. 23, “a यत्नेनापि महता प्राप्यते रत्मदमतः??, where तेरमदमतः is given as a variant. American rock-oil was not + known in those days! There are two good examples of this illustration in Vyaéyamanjari. On page 493, line 1:—aeneff हि तिलसर्षपानुपादत्ते न सिकताः | waa च तैरस्य को विदोषः सर्षपाणां सिकताभ्यः ° ॥ On page 494 :--“ तैलार्थीं सिकताः कश्चिदाददानो न इङ्यते। अदृष्टा चाद्य नान्योऽपि तदथीं तासु धावति.” Compare also Paris'istaparvan viii. 152 -- “भ्याहार्षन्सुनिरप्येवं sate खगरोचने | अस्मासु भवति द्रव्यं किं ae वालकास्िव ॥ सुक्तवाकन्यायः ॥ The law as to the Saktavaka [or song of praise]. This is the topic of Jaimini 3. 2. 15-18, and immediately follows the Prastara-praharananydya with which it is closely connected. Both form a part of the New and Full Moon sacrifices in connection with which there is the direction ^ सूक्तवाकेन प्रस्तरं प्रहरति." The question then arises as to whether the whole of the mantras which comprise the Siktavaka are to be repeated on each of the two occasions, or only a part. S‘abara’s argument is thus paraphrased by Kunte :—“ Though the Veda mentions positively 148 that certain mantras are to be used in certain sacrifices, yet they are not to be so used blindly. The mantra which serves some purpose of a sacrifice is to be used. Though the Veda prescribes the use of the whole mantra, yet it is not to be obstinately maintained that the whole is to be recited. A whole mantra like the Siktavaka, or a part only, is to be recited according as it is necessary. This is to be ascertained by the sacrificer himself. Hence it cannot be said that the whole Siktavaka is to be recited on the occasion either of the new or of the full moon day.” Again :—“That portion of the Siktavaka which is recited at the new or full moon sacrifice is the whole of it in reference to the sacrifice itself; because the Veda never prescribes a certain text as constituting the Suktavaka, and because the Veda simply states that the Stktavaka, is to be recited... The Siktavaka is not one text only, but is composed of different texts. The principal god connected with a sacrifice is mentioned in the middle of the Siktavaka, while something connected with the sacrifice to be performed is described at its beginning and end......All that is sought is the accomplishment of the new or full moon sacrifice. Hence there is no necessity for discussing whether the whole Siktavaka or a portion of it is to be recited; because whatever mantras are sufficient to accomplish a sacrifice constitute the whole Siktavaka so far as the sacrifice is concerned.”
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 147

gloss_en: The simile of a bird tied by a cord, It is found in the Chhandogya Upanisad 6.
explanation: The simile of a bird tied by a cord, It is found in the Chhandogya Upanisad 6. 8. 2, namely “स यथा शकुनिः सूत्रेण प्रबद्धो दिदं दिशं पतित्वान्यत्रायतनमर्ञ्ध्वा बन्धनमेवोपश्रयत एवमेव ag सोम्य तन्मनो दिद दिदं पतित्वान्यत्रायतनमरन्ध्वा प्राणमेवोपश्रयते प्राणबन्धनं हि सोम्य मन इति.” 149 11.11. 11.11 (iii. 81) and Pancadas’t. (xi. 47) respectively as गिा०क8:-- “gait: सूत्रबद्धो यः स गच्छन्विविधा दिशः । अरूब्ध्वाधारमाकादो बन्धनस्थानमाव्रजेत्‌"' ॥ “nat: सूत्रबद्धः सन्दिक्षु sage विश्रमम्‌ | ॥ अलब्ध्वा बन्धनस्थानं हस्तस्तम्भादुपाश्रयेत्‌"” ॥ I have omitted, in each case, a second verse relating to the darstantika.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 148

gloss_en: The simile of the thread [about to be woven into a] gar-— ment [and already regarded as a garment].
explanation: The simile of the thread [about to be woven into a] gar-— ment [and already regarded as a garment]. Raghunatha explains it thus:—“ यत्र तु भाविसंज्ञया निदेशो यथा नार्द्रो वसेत्काङ्या- मिलयत्र aa सूत्रशाटिकान्यायावतारः । सूत्रस्य शाटिकां वापयतीलत्र यथा सूत्रावस्थायां माबिन्या शािकितिसंक्ञया निर्देशस्तथा दा्ठीन्तिकेऽपीति बोध्यम्‌. It is no doubt derived from the following passage in the Mahabhasya 1. 8. 12 (vartika 2 ):-“ आत्मनेपदेषु चापि नेतरेतराश्रयं भवति । कथम्‌ । भाविनी संज्ञा विज्ञास्यते सूत्रशाटकवत्‌ | तद्यथा | कश्चित्कं- चित्तन्तुवायमाह अस्य सूत्रस्य शाटकं वयेति । ख परयति यदि शाटको न वातभ्योऽथ वातव्यो न शाटकः शाटको वातव्यश्चेति विप्रतिषिद्धम्‌ । भाविनी खल्वस्य संज्ञाभिप्रेता स मन्ये वातव्यो यस्िननुते शाटक इलयेतद्धवतीति.” This is repeated in 2. 1. 51 (vartika 4). Kumarila employs the illustration in Tantravartika 8. 7. 33 (page 1145 ):—“qdare सूत्रस्य शाटकं वयेल्यक्ते वानेन शाटकः क्रियत इति हि भाविसंज्ञाविक्ञानादवि- रोधो विह्वायते तथैवात्र प्रयेतव्यम्‌.” Light is thrown-upon this by the following extract from Ballantyne’s Aphorisms of the Nydya, ii. 127. The siitra.so numbered is ^ सहचरणस्थानतादर्थ्यं &e.” “Though its meaning be not so and so, it is figuratively so employed in the case of a Brahman, a scaffold, “a mat......in consideration of association, 150 place, design......‘Though it be not so and so,’ ९ ९, though such be not the direct meaning of the word, it is figuratively employed ; for example, the word ‘staff’ &c., is employed for a Brahman &c., because of association......In like manner...... from the ‘design’ (tddarthya), ‘He makes a mat’ (kata ) implies his aiming after a mat; for the mat, inasmuch as it is a thing non-existent [ wntil made] can have [at the time when one is spoken of as making it] no maker.” Again, under siitra 4. 1. 50 [aféfae तु तदसत्‌ ], the author of the vritti says (as interpretted by Dr. Ballantyne ):—“The weaver sets himself to work, having considered, that, ‘ In these threads [ ‰ ९, constituted by these threads] there will be a web, but not with the understanding that ‘there is a web’; for, if that were the case, then, the product being supposed extant, there would be no setting one’s self to work, because desire [ precluded by possession ] would be absent.” See also a passage in Tatparyatika, page 254, beginning at line 14; and Sankhyatativakaunvudi on karika 9, pages 52, 53. सोपानत्के पादे द्वितीयाञुपानहमशक्यत्वान्चोपादत्ते ॥ ८ A man does not [attempt to] put a second shoe on a foot already shod, for it would be an impossibility. This is found in the bhasya on Jaimini 1. 2. 33, where the pirvapakshin objects to certain Vedic texts as unnecessarily setting forth things already known.
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 149

gloss_en: The simile of vegetable [ or mineral] poison and animal 007 son [जङ्गमविष].
explanation: The simile of vegetable [ or mineral] poison and animal 007 son [जङ्गमविष]. An illustration of one thing being counteracted by another. In his smaller work Raghunathavarman places this amongst the purely grammatical nyayas, immediately after the 151 पूर्वात्परबलीयसत्वन्याय, and describes it as follows:—“ स्थावरजङ्गमविषन्यायश्वेह बोध्यः | रजतादिक्ञानतद्वाधक्ञानयोः सर्पवत्सनाभादिरूपजङ्गमस्थावरविषयोश्चोत्तरेण wars: प्रसिद्धो यथा तथा प्रकृतेऽपि. ॥ In the larger work, it stands amongst miscellaneous nyayas near the end of the uttarabhaga, and is numbered 242. I extract from it the following: —“zaritn वत्सनाभादिविषेण जङ्गमस्य सपैविषादेर्बाधो जङ्गमेन च स्थावरस्येति प्रसिद्धम्‌ | सामान्येन परस्परबाध्यवाधकभावविवक्षायां सुन्दोपसुन्दन्याय विषयेऽस्य प्रवृत्तिः । पुत्र निवल्यौन्यस्य स्वयमेव act विव feat दग्धेन्धनवहिन्यायविषयेऽस्यावतरणम्‌ । परेण पूैवाधमाच्रविवक्षायां पू्वास्परबरीयसू्वन्यायसेति बोध्यम्‌ । अपच्छेदन्यायस्त्वस्पष्टसुदाहरणसुभयत्रा- नियतपूवौपरीभावेनानियतवबाध्यवाधकभावात्‌” ॥ An example of animal poison proving an antidote to the other kind is found in Adiparva, chap. exxviii (Cale). The wicked Duryodhana mixed some kélakitaka in Bhima’s food and, when he had eaten it and become unconscious, threw him into the water. The story then continues thus:—“‘@ निःसंज्ञो जलस्यान्तमथ वै पाण्डवोऽविशत्‌। आक्रामन्नागभवने तदा नागऊमारकान्‌ ॥ ततः समेत्य बहुभिस्तदा नगे्मृहाविधैः | अदश्यत शशं भीमो महादर्विषोख्वणेः ॥ ततोऽस्य द्ङ्यमानस्य तद्विषं कालक््टकम्‌ | हृतं सषैविपेणेव स्थावरं जङ्गमेन TN Compare the विषनाराकविषन्याय in Sarvarthasiddht on Tattvamuktakalapa ii. 58. स्वाङ्ककिज्वाखया परं fag: स परं दहेद्वा न वा खा- इकिदाहमडभवति ॥ A man who tries to burn his enemy by setting fire to his own fingers, may or may not burn the enemy but certainly burns his own fingers! It occurs in. Nydyavartika 2. 1. 12 in reference to a person who denies the validity of Proof. Siitras 8 and 12, as translated by Dr. Ballantyne, are as follows :—“[ Perhaps some one will say] the nature of a Proof 3" 5 4 me ५/5 Oo: ६ ^ Rs 152 does not belong to sense &c., for it cannot be so at any of the three times [into which Time is divided ].” “[If there be no such thing as Proof] because [ forsooth ] nothing can be such at any of the three times, then the objection itself cannot be established.” On this the author of the vartika says:—“qag त्रिष्वपि कालेषु न साधकं तदसाधनमिति aaa प्रतिषेधस्यासाधकत्वं स्ववा- चैवाभ्युपगतं भवति | यथा कश्चित्सवाङ्गलिञ्वाख्या परं दिधक्षुः स च परं दहेद्रा न वा स्वाङ्कुछिदाहमदुभवति,” स्वेदजनिमित्तेन शाटकल्यागन्यायः ॥ The illustration of throwing away a garment because of a louse in it! It occurs in Upamitibhavaprapanca Katha (page 160 line 10) :--“यतोऽहमनन्तापल्यापि दुजनचक्षुदौषभयादविवेकादिभिमंत्रिभि- areata प्रख्यापिता रोके मभमैवापत्यान्यन्यजनापत्यतया गीयन्ते | सोऽयं स्वेद्‌- जनिमित्तेन शाटकल्यागन्यायः?* ॥ (0717876 Raghunatha’s यूकाभिया कन्थालागन्यायः ॥ हिरण्यनिधिदष्टान्तः ॥ The illustration afforded by buried treaswre [ over which men may walk again and again, unconscious of its existence ]}. It is found in the Chhandogya Upanisad 8. 8. 2 as follows:— ‘aunty हिरण्यनिधिं Manda उपयुपरि सञ्चरन्तो न विन्देयुरेवमेवेमाः सवौः प्रजा अहरहगैच्छत्य एतं ब्रह्मलोकं न विन्दन्त्यनृतेन हि म्रसयूढाः" ॥ Sures’varacarya makes use of it in Sambandhavéartika, verses 294 and 295 :— ^“ कतस्तञ््ञानमिति tafe बन्धपरिक्षयात्‌ | असावपि च भूतो वा भावी वा वर्ततेऽथवा ॥ अधीतवेदवेदार्थोऽप्यत एव न सुच्यते । | हिरण्यनिधिदृष्टन्तादिदमेव च iar” ॥ 153 The translator of the vartika, Mr. S. Venkataramana Aiyar, gives “the illustration of the golden mine,” as the rendering of the nyaéya in verse 295; and adds in a footnote, “people, other than professional detectives of mines, will not discover a rich mine of gold hidden deep beneath the surface of the earth”; but I think that my rendering is more in accord with S‘ankaracirya’s interpretation of it in the Vedic passage. He says :— “Roam feria पुनभ्रैहणाय निधातृभिनिधीयत इति fafa हिरण्यनिधिं निहितं भूमेरधस्ताज्निक्षिप्तम्‌” ॥ There is no thought he
source: Jacob, A Third Handful of Popular Maxims (Laukikanyayanjali), Bombay (Nirnaya-Sagara Press), 1911 (Third Handful), UC Libraries scan, p. 150
